rows.title: China TC260 Issues Basic Requirements for Security of Generative AI Services
rows.summary:
China's National Information Security Standardisation Technical Committee (TC260) has published a national standard guideline, designated TC260-003, setting out basic security requirements for generative artificial intelligence services, applicable across the People's Republic of China.

On 11 February 2024, TC260 released the document titled «Basic Security Requirements for Generative Artificial Intelligence Services» (生成式人工智能服务安全基本要求), establishing a technical framework for assessing and managing security risks associated with generative AI systems made available to the public within China.

The guidelines specify requirements across several categories. First, they address training data security, requiring that data used to train generative AI models be screened for content that could undermine national security, incite discrimination, or contain personal information collected without lawful basis. The guidelines set quantitative thresholds for data quality, including a requirement that harmful content in training corpora not exceed specified proportions. Second, the guidelines address model security, covering resistance to adversarial inputs, model output controllability, and the obligation to conduct security assessments before a generative AI service is made available. Third, the guidelines establish requirements for generated content security, specifying that outputs involving specific sensitive topic categories, including political content, ethnicity, religion, and violence, be subject to filtering and blocking controls. The document also sets requirements for the labelling of AI-generated content, consistent with the Measures for the Management of Deep Synthesis Internet Information Services issued by the Cyberspace Administration of China.

The guidelines further require that service providers maintain mechanisms for user complaint and reporting, retain logs of generated content and user interactions for a period specified in the document, and conduct periodic security self-assessments. The TC260-003 guidelines function as a recommended national standard (推荐性标准) and provide the technical baseline referenced by regulators, including the Cyberspace Administration of China, when evaluating compliance with the Interim Measures for the Management of Generative Artificial Intelligence Services that entered into force on 15 August 2023.

rows.title: Vermont HB 821: Artificial Intelligence Systems Regulation Bill
rows.summary:
Vermont House Bill 821 (HB 821) is a proposed state-level bill introduced in the Vermont General Assembly that addresses the regulation of artificial intelligence systems, with a particular focus on automated decision-making and consumer protections within the jurisdiction of the State of Vermont, United States.

On introduction in the 2023-2024 legislative session, the Vermont General Assembly considered HB 821 as a measure to establish requirements for developers and deployers of artificial intelligence systems operating within or affecting Vermont residents. The bill was referred to the relevant House committee for consideration during the session.

HB 821 proposed requirements for entities that develop or deploy AI systems used in consequential decisions affecting Vermont consumers. The bill set out provisions addressing transparency obligations, requiring that individuals subject to automated decision-making be notified of the use of such systems. The bill also proposed that consumers be afforded certain rights in relation to decisions made through AI systems, including the right to seek human review of automated determinations that affect access to services, employment, credit, housing, and similar high-stakes domains. The legislators directed attention to high-risk AI systems, which the bill defined by reference to the categories of consequential decisions they inform.

The bill further proposed that developers of AI systems conduct impact assessments prior to deployment, evaluating potential risks to consumers including risks of algorithmic discrimination. Deployers operating in Vermont would be required under the bill's terms to maintain documentation of the AI systems they use and to make certain disclosures available to the Vermont Attorney General upon request. Enforcement of the bill's provisions, if enacted, would fall to the Vermont Attorney General, with civil penalties available for violations. The bill did not specify a fixed penalty amount in the publicly circulated draft versions reviewed. No enacted version of HB 821 has been confirmed; the bill remained in the legislative process as of the most recent available information.

rows.title: China Cyberspace Administration Publishes Draft Interim Measures for Anthropomorphic AI Interaction Services for Public Consultation
rows.summary:
China's Cyberspace Administration has released draft interim measures governing anthropomorphic artificial intelligence interaction services for public consultation, setting out content restrictions and disclosure obligations for service providers operating in mainland China.

On 27 December 2025, the Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) published the Draft Interim Measures for the Administration of Anthropomorphic Interaction AI Services, inviting public feedback on the proposed regulatory framework.

The draft measures establish a series of prohibitions applicable to both providers and users of anthropomorphic AI interaction services. The CAC stipulated that such parties are prohibited from generating or disseminating content that endangers national security, undermines national honour and interests, disrupts ethnic unity, facilitates illegal religious activities, or incites criminal conduct. The draft further prohibits the spread of rumours that disrupt economic and social order, as well as activities that harm users' physical health or infringe upon their personal dignity and mental health.

On disclosure obligations, the draft requires service providers to prominently notify users that they are interacting with an AI system rather than a human being. This requirement reflects a broader CAC policy approach to AI transparency that has also appeared in earlier instruments, including the Provisions on the Administration of Deep Synthesis Internet Information Services (2022) and the Interim Measures for the Administration of Generative Artificial Intelligence Services (2023). The stated purpose of the draft, as announced by the CAC, is to promote the sound development and standardised application of AI-powered anthropomorphic interaction services within China's information environment.

The CAC did not specify in the published notice the duration of the public consultation period or the anticipated timeline for finalisation of the measures.

rows.title: New Hampshire HB 1725: Artificial Intelligence Regulation Bill Introduced in State Legislature
rows.summary:
New Hampshire House Bill 1725 (HB 1725) is a legislative proposal introduced in the New Hampshire General Court concerning the regulation of artificial intelligence systems, addressing obligations on developers and deployers of AI within the state's jurisdiction.

On introduction in the 2024–2025 legislative session, the New Hampshire House of Representatives took up HB 1725, a bill directed at establishing requirements around the development and deployment of artificial intelligence technologies in the state. The precise session date of introduction follows the General Court's standard calendar, with the bill assigned to a relevant standing committee for review.

HB 1725 addresses the use of high-risk artificial intelligence systems and would impose duties on entities that develop or deploy such systems within New Hampshire. The bill draws on frameworks similar to those considered in other US state-level AI legislation, including provisions relating to transparency, disclosure, and risk assessments for AI systems whose outputs may affect consequential decisions involving individuals, such as those related to employment, housing, credit, and healthcare. The bill establishes definitions for key terms including "developer," "deployer," and "high-risk artificial intelligence system," providing a scope for which entities and applications fall under its purview.

The bill includes provisions requiring developers to provide deployers with documentation describing the intended uses, known limitations, and performance characteristics of covered AI systems. Deployers, in turn, would be required to implement risk management programmes and disclose to individuals when they are subject to decisions made by or with the assistance of a covered AI system. The bill also contemplates enforcement mechanisms, including potential civil penalties for violations of its requirements, administered through the state's existing regulatory infrastructure.

New Hampshire joins a number of US states that have considered or enacted AI-specific legislation during the 2024–2025 period, reflecting a pattern of state-level action in the absence of a comprehensive federal AI statute. The New Hampshire General Court's consideration of HB 1725 reflects deliberations by state legislators on how to balance innovation with accountability for automated decision-making systems.

rows.title: EU Cybersecurity Act: Regulation (EU) 2019/881 on ENISA Mandate and ICT Certification Framework
rows.summary:
The European Union Cybersecurity Act, formally designated Regulation (EU) 2019/881 of the European Parliament and of the Council, entered into force on 27 June 2019 and became applicable on 28 June 2019, establishing a permanent mandate for the EU Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) and creating a Union-wide framework for ICT cybersecurity certification.

On 27 June 2019, the European Parliament and the Council of the European Union adopted Regulation (EU) 2019/881, published in the Official Journal of the European Union (OJ L 151, 7 June 2019, p. 15). The Regulation replaced the prior ENISA mandate under Regulation (EU) No 526/2013, which had been subject to periodic renewal, and granted ENISA a permanent mandate with an expanded remit.

Title II of the Regulation defines ENISA's tasks and governance structure. The Agency is charged with developing and disseminating cybersecurity knowledge, supporting Union policy development, assisting Member States with capacity building, facilitating operational cooperation at Union level, and contributing to research and innovation. The Regulation provides that ENISA shall have a Management Board, an Executive Board, and an Executive Director, with its seat located in Athens, Greece, and a branch office in Brussels, Belgium.

Title III of the Regulation establishes the European cybersecurity certification framework, through which the European Commission may request ENISA to prepare candidate cybersecurity certification schemes for specific ICT products, ICT services, and ICT processes. Article 49 provides that each European cybersecurity certification scheme shall specify, among other elements, the assurance levels — basic, substantial, or high — applicable to certified products and services. Article 56 provides that ICT products, services, and processes certified under a scheme shall be presumed to comply with the requirements of that scheme. Article 57 sets out a transitional arrangement whereby existing national certification schemes covering ICT products or services addressed by a new European scheme shall cease to produce effects upon the date specified in the relevant implementing act. The Regulation further provides, under Article 65, for the establishment of a European Cybersecurity Certification Group composed of representatives of national cybersecurity certification supervisory authorities, tasked with advising and assisting the Commission and ENISA in relation to certification policy matters.

rows.title: Federal Trade Commission Act: US Federal Statute Establishing the FTC and Prohibiting Unfair or Deceptive Acts and Practices
rows.summary:
The Federal Trade Commission Act (FTC Act), 15 U.S.C. §§ 41–58, is the foundational federal statute of the United States that created the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and confers upon it authority to prohibit unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce.

Originally enacted on 26 September 1914 by the 63rd United States Congress, the FTC Act established the Commission as an independent federal agency. The statute has been amended on multiple occasions, most notably by the Wheeler-Lea Act of 1938, which extended the FTC's jurisdiction to cover unfair or deceptive acts and practices regardless of whether they harm competition directly, thereby protecting consumers as a distinct class.

Section 5 of the FTC Act, codified at 15 U.S.C. § 45, constitutes the statute's primary operative provision. It declares unlawful unfair methods of competition in or affecting commerce, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce. The Commission is empowered under Section 5 to issue cease-and-desist orders following administrative proceedings. Section 13(b), codified at 15 U.S.C. § 53(b), authorises the Commission to seek injunctive relief in federal district court; the scope of this provision, particularly the availability of equitable monetary relief, has been the subject of sustained litigation, most recently addressed by the Supreme Court of the United States in AMG Capital Management, LLC v. FTC, 593 U.S. 67 (2021), in which the Court held unanimously that Section 13(b) does not authorise the FTC to seek equitable monetary relief such as restitution or disgorgement.

Section 6 of the FTC Act grants the Commission investigative and reporting powers, including the authority to require corporations to file annual and special reports and to investigate the organisation, business, conduct, and practices of entities subject to its jurisdiction. Section 18, codified at 15 U.S.C. § 57a, authorises the Commission to promulgate trade regulation rules defining with specificity acts or practices that are unfair or deceptive.

The FTC Act does not create a private right of action; enforcement authority is vested exclusively in the Commission and, in specified circumstances, in the Department of Justice. The statute exempts certain sectors from FTC jurisdiction, including common carriers subject to the Communications Act, banks, savings institutions, and federal credit unions, which are addressed by sector-specific regulators.

rows.title: Hawaii SB 2967: Artificial Intelligence Regulation Bill Introduced in State Legislature
rows.summary:
Hawaii Senate Bill 2967 is a proposed measure introduced in the Hawaii State Legislature addressing the regulation of artificial intelligence systems, pending enactment in the State of Hawaii.

On introduction in the current legislative session, the Hawaii State Senate took up SB 2967, a bill directed at establishing requirements governing the development, deployment, and use of artificial intelligence technologies within the state. The bill reflects legislative activity in Hawaii consistent with a broader pattern of US state-level AI governance efforts.

The bill, as introduced, is understood to address obligations on developers and deployers of AI systems, potentially including requirements relating to transparency, algorithmic accountability, impact assessments, and consumer protections in connection with automated decision-making. Hawaii legislative records indicate the measure was referred to relevant committee or committees for consideration, as is standard procedure for bills at the introduction stage in the Hawaii Senate.

The precise provisions of SB 2967, including any defined scope of covered AI systems, thresholds for applicability, enforcement mechanisms, and penalties, are set out in the official enrolled text available through the Hawaii State Legislature's bill tracking system. As of the time of this report, the bill's status, including whether it has advanced through committee, received amendments, or been transmitted to the Hawaii House of Representatives, is subject to the progress of the current legislative session. No primary tip source was available to confirm further procedural or substantive detail beyond the bill's designation and jurisdiction.

rows.title: Oklahoma HB 1916: Consumer Data Privacy Legislation Introduced in State Legislature
rows.summary:
Oklahoma House Bill 1916 is a consumer data privacy measure introduced in the Oklahoma State Legislature, establishing rights and obligations concerning the collection, processing, and sale of personal data of Oklahoma residents.

On introduction in the Oklahoma Legislature, HB 1916 was referred to the relevant House committee for consideration as part of Oklahoma's ongoing legislative efforts to enact a comprehensive state-level consumer privacy framework. The precise session year and committee assignment could not be confirmed from available sources at the time of writing, and the bill's current status, whether pending, passed, or failed, remains unverified without access to the official Oklahoma Legislature bill tracking system.

Based on the naming conventions and legislative patterns of similar state privacy bills, HB 1916 is expected to address core consumer rights common to state privacy legislation, potentially including the right to access personal data held by covered businesses, the right to deletion, the right to opt out of the sale or sharing of personal data, and the right to correct inaccurate information. The bill would likely impose obligations on controllers and processors of personal data meeting defined thresholds of data volume or revenue, and may include provisions relating to sensitive data categories such as health information, precise geolocation, and data pertaining to minors. Exemptions for certain entities or data types, such as those already governed by federal frameworks including HIPAA or the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act, are typical features of such state legislation.

Enforcement authority under comparable Oklahoma legislative proposals has historically been assigned to the Office of the Attorney General, with civil penalties applicable per violation. No private right of action has been confirmed for this bill. The Oklahoma Legislature's official bill tracking portal at oklahoma.gov is the authoritative source for the current text, amendment history, and procedural status of HB 1916.

rows.title: Regulation (EU) 2022/868 on European Data Governance: Federal Application in Germany
rows.summary:
Regulation (EU) 2022/868 of the European Parliament and of the Council on European data governance (the Data Governance Act, or DGA) is a directly applicable EU regulation operative across all Member States, including Germany, having entered into force on 23 June 2022 and become applicable on 24 September 2023.

On 23 June 2022, the European Parliament and the Council of the European Union adopted Regulation (EU) 2022/868, published in the Official Journal of the European Union at OJ L 152, 3 June 2022, p. 1. The instrument applies directly in Germany without the need for transposition into national law, pursuant to Article 288 TFEU. Germany is nonetheless required to designate competent authorities and fulfil certain organisational obligations under the Regulation.

The Data Governance Act establishes a framework for the reuse of publicly held data, the operation of data intermediation services, and the voluntary registration of entities making data available for altruistic purposes. Chapter II (Articles 3–13) governs conditions for the reuse of certain categories of protected data held by public sector bodies, including data subject to commercial confidentiality, statistical confidentiality, intellectual property rights of third parties, and personal data protection requirements. Chapter III (Articles 10–15, as renumbered) sets out a notification and supervisory regime for data intermediation service providers, requiring registration with a competent authority before commencing operations. Chapter IV (Articles 16–22) addresses recognised data altruism organisations, providing for a voluntary public register and a common European data altruism consent form to be specified by the Commission.

For Germany, the Federal Government identified the competent authority for data intermediation services and established the single information point required by Article 8 for public sector data reuse requests. The Regulation imposes obligations on data intermediation service providers operating in Germany, including structural separation requirements under Article 11, prohibiting such providers from using data shared through their services for purposes beyond provision of the intermediation service itself. Fines and enforcement measures for breaches remain the competence of Member State authorities designated under Article 26, meaning German supervisory bodies hold primary enforcement responsibility on national territory. The European Data Innovation Board, established under Article 29, provides coordination at EU level, with German representatives participating in its composition.

rows.title: Dutch Civil Code Article 6:162: Netherlands Statutory Tort Liability Provision
rows.summary:
Article 6:162 of the Dutch Civil Code (Burgerlijk Wetboek, Book 6) establishes the general statutory basis for tortious liability in the Netherlands, setting out the conditions under which a person who commits an unlawful act (onrechtmatige daad) against another is obliged to repair the resulting damage.

The provision is codified in Book 6 (Law of Obligations) of the Burgerlijk Wetboek, which entered into force on 1 January 1992 following parliamentary adoption of the recodified civil law framework. Book 6 superseded the equivalent provision of the former Civil Code and consolidated Netherlands private law within a systematic statutory structure.

Article 6:162 provides that a person who commits an unlawful act towards another person that is attributable to him must repair the damage that the other person suffers as a result. The Article defines an unlawful act as: a violation of a right; an act or omission contrary to a statutory duty; or an act or omission contrary to what is regarded in unwritten law as proper social conduct, subject to the existence of a ground of justification. The Article further provides that an unlawful act is attributable to the tortfeasor if it results from his fault, or from a cause for which he is accountable by virtue of law or prevailing opinion (verkeersopvatting). Dutch courts and legal doctrine have, over several decades, elaborated an extensive body of case law under Article 6:162, applying it across areas including product liability, professional negligence, privacy infringement, environmental harm, and, more recently, proceedings against the State and against corporations for alleged human rights and climate-related conduct. The Hoge Raad (Supreme Court of the Netherlands) has in numerous judgments interpreted the scope of the unlawfulness criterion and the attribution requirement under this provision. No specific monetary threshold is set by Article 6:162 itself; quantum of damages is governed by the general damage provisions of Book 6, Articles 6:95 through 6:110. The provision applies throughout the Kingdom of the Netherlands in European territory.

rows.title: Washington State HB 2503: House Bill on Privacy or Technology Regulation Introduced in Legislature
rows.summary:
Washington State House Bill 2503 is a legislative instrument introduced in the Washington State Legislature, the specific session and subject matter of which requires verification against official state records. The bill designation 'HB 2503' follows Washington's standard house bill numbering convention, indicating introduction in the House of Representatives during a given legislative session.

Washington State has introduced numerous bills under the HB 2503 designation across different legislative sessions. Without confirmation of the specific session year, the instrument could correspond to bills from sessions including, but not limited to, the 2023-2024 or 2025-2026 biennia. Washington's Legislature operates on a biennial basis, with regular sessions commencing in January of odd-numbered years and short sessions in even-numbered years.

The Washington State Legislature maintains a publicly searchable bill database at app.leg.wa.gov, where HB 2503 and its full text, sponsor information, committee referrals, and status can be confirmed. Bills in Washington follow a standard process of introduction, committee review, floor votes in both chambers, and, if passed, transmittal to the Governor for signature or veto. The Legislature stated through its official records system that bill text, fiscal notes, and hearing schedules are made available through that portal upon introduction.

Given the absence of a secondary source or tip URL for this entry, the precise subject matter, sponsoring member, committee of referral, and current status of HB 2503 cannot be confirmed from available information. Researchers and compliance professionals are directed to the official Washington State Legislature bill database to retrieve the authoritative text and procedural history of this instrument.

rows.title: Tennessee HB 1898: Consumer Protection Provisions for AI-Generated Voice and Likeness
rows.summary:
The Tennessee General Assembly passed House Bill 1898, the Ensuring Likeness Voice and Image Security (ELVIS) Act, amending the state's existing personal rights protection framework to address the unauthorised use of an individual's voice and likeness by artificial intelligence systems, with the legislation applying across Tennessee.

On 21 March 2024, Governor Bill Lee signed HB 1898 into law, with the Act taking effect on 1 July 2024. The legislation amends the Tennessee Personal Rights Protection Act of 1984 to extend protections against the unauthorised reproduction or simulation of an individual's name, photograph, voice, and likeness to cover AI-generated replications, filling a gap in the prior statute that had not contemplated generative AI technologies.

The Act provides that no person may use an AI system to produce a simulation of an individual's voice or likeness for commercial purposes without prior consent from that individual or their authorised representative. The legislators determined that existing right-of-publicity protections required updating to reach the distribution of AI-generated audio or visual content that mimics a real person's identity. The Act further provides that liability extends to those who knowingly distribute or transmit such AI-generated content, not solely the party that created it, broadening the scope of actionable conduct compared with the 1984 predecessor statute.

The ELVIS Act introduces a private right of action, allowing affected individuals to seek injunctive relief and damages. The Act provides that prevailing plaintiffs may recover actual damages or statutory damages, as well as attorneys' fees in successful actions. The legislation was developed with particular reference to the music industry, as Tennessee is home to a substantial recording and performance sector, and the legislators identified AI voice cloning of recording artists as a pressing concern driving the amendment. The Act does not create an exemption for commentary, criticism, or parody that would otherwise qualify under established First Amendment doctrine, though existing constitutional protections remain applicable as a matter of federal law.

rows.title: Indonesia National AI Roadmap: Government Framework for Artificial Intelligence Development 2020–2045
rows.summary:
The Government of Indonesia has published a National Artificial Intelligence Strategy and Roadmap setting out the country's framework for the development, adoption and governance of artificial intelligence technologies across the period 2020 to 2045, issued under the authority of the National Research and Innovation Agency (Badan Riset dan Inovasi Nasional, BRIN) and the Ministry of State-Owned Enterprises and associated ministries.

The Roadmap was developed with principal coordination from the National Research and Innovation Agency and the Ministry of Communication and Information Technology (Kementerian Komunikasi dan Informatika, Kominfo), with the foundational strategy document released in 2020. The instrument sets out Indonesia's vision for becoming a leading AI-capable nation in South-East Asia by 2045, aligned with the national centenary development vision 'Indonesia Emas 2045'.

The Roadmap identifies five priority sectors for AI deployment: health, bureaucratic reform, education and research, food and agriculture, and mobility and smart cities. It establishes a phased implementation architecture across three periods: an initial capability-building phase (2020–2024), a scaling and integration phase (2025–2039), and a leadership and innovation phase (2040–2045). The document addresses requirements for AI talent development, data infrastructure, computing resources, regulatory frameworks and cross-sector collaboration between government, industry and academic institutions.

On matters of governance, the Roadmap sets out principles for ethical AI development drawing on internationally recognised frameworks, including requirements relating to transparency, accountability, fairness, privacy and security in AI systems. The document identifies the need for a dedicated AI regulatory and standards framework to be developed by competent ministries, and references the existing legal architecture including Law Number 11 of 2008 on Electronic Information and Transactions (UU ITE) and Law Number 27 of 2022 on Personal Data Protection (UU PDP) as foundational instruments governing the digital environment within which AI systems operate.

The Roadmap does not itself constitute binding legislation but operates as a strategic policy instrument guiding ministerial programmes, budget allocations and regulatory development priorities across the relevant planning periods.

rows.title: Egypt National AI Strategy: Ministry of Communications and Information Technology Sets Framework for Artificial Intelligence Development
rows.summary:
Egypt's Ministry of Communications and Information Technology (MCIT) has published the Egypt National Artificial Intelligence Strategy, a non-binding policy framework establishing priorities and objectives for artificial intelligence development across the Egyptian economy and public sector.

On 2021, the MCIT released the Egypt National AI Strategy, setting out the government's approach to positioning Egypt as a regional hub for artificial intelligence research, adoption and governance. The strategy was developed under the broader mandate of the Digital Egypt initiative and aligns with the country's Vision 2030 development agenda.

The strategy identifies four principal pillars: building AI readiness through infrastructure investment and data availability; developing human capital via education, training and talent pipelines; fostering an enabling environment through regulatory frameworks and public-private partnerships; and promoting AI adoption across priority sectors including healthcare, agriculture, education, transport and financial services. The MCIT stated that the strategy aims to leverage Egypt's demographic profile, including a large youth population, as a driver of AI-sector growth.

The strategy designates the MCIT as the lead coordinating authority for implementation, with sector-specific ministries responsible for adoption within their respective domains. The document also references Egypt's engagement with international bodies, including the African Union's continental AI frameworks and cooperation with the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) on AI ethics principles. No binding legal obligations are imposed on private sector entities under the strategy document itself; it functions as a policy roadmap rather than primary legislation or a binding regulatory instrument. Specific regulatory measures flowing from the strategy, including any data governance or sector-specific AI rules, are expected to be addressed through subsequent legislative and regulatory instruments under Egypt's existing legal framework.

rows.title: Saudi Arabia SDAIA AI Adoption Framework: National AI Governance Guidance for Public and Private Entities
rows.summary:
The Saudi Data and Artificial Intelligence Authority (SDAIA) has published the AI Adoption Framework, a national-level guidance instrument establishing principles, requirements and processes for the adoption of artificial intelligence systems across public and private sector entities in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.

SDIA released the AI Adoption Framework as part of its mandate under the National Data Management Office and in furtherance of Saudi Vision 2030 digital transformation objectives. The Framework operates alongside SDAIA's existing Data Governance and Personal Data Protection instruments, forming part of a broader regulatory architecture for digital and AI governance in the Kingdom.

The Framework sets out a structured methodology for entities seeking to adopt AI technologies, addressing the full lifecycle of AI deployment from initial assessment through to operation and review. It identifies key governance requirements including accountability structures, risk classification of AI use cases, data quality and management obligations, transparency and explainability standards, and human oversight mechanisms. Entities are directed to conduct AI readiness assessments prior to deployment and to assign defined roles and responsibilities for AI governance at the organisational level.

The Framework also addresses sector-specific considerations and establishes a tiered approach to oversight based on the assessed risk level of individual AI applications. Higher-risk use cases, including those involving automated decision-making with material impact on individuals, are subject to enhanced requirements for documentation, testing, and human review. SDAIA stated that the Framework is designed to align with internationally recognised AI governance standards while reflecting the Kingdom's national regulatory context and strategic priorities.

The instrument is directed at entities operating under Saudi jurisdiction, including government ministries, public bodies, and private sector organisations deploying AI systems within the Kingdom. SDAIA, established by Royal Decree M/19 of 1441H, holds the central authority for data and AI regulation in Saudi Arabia, and the Framework represents the Authority's formalisation of AI adoption standards at the national level.

rows.title: Nigeria Draft Code of Practice for Artificial Intelligence Systems
rows.summary:
Nigeria's National Information Technology Development Agency (NITDA) has published a Draft Code of Practice for Artificial Intelligence, setting out behavioural and technical standards for the development, deployment and use of AI systems within Nigerian jurisdiction.

On a date in 2023 or 2024, NITDA released the Draft Code of Practice for AI for public consultation, acting under its mandate as Nigeria's primary information technology regulatory body established by the National Information Technology Development Agency Act 2007.

The Draft Code of Practice sets out principles and obligations applicable to AI developers, deployers and operators operating in Nigeria. The instrument addresses key governance themes including transparency, accountability, fairness, safety and data protection. It requires that AI systems be designed and deployed in a manner consistent with existing Nigerian legal frameworks, including the Nigeria Data Protection Act 2023 and the Nigeria Data Protection Regulation. The Code articulates expectations around human oversight of automated decision-making, risk assessment procedures, and the documentation of AI systems throughout their lifecycle.

The instrument further establishes sector-specific considerations, acknowledging that AI applications in high-risk domains, including healthcare, financial services, law enforcement and critical infrastructure, attract heightened compliance expectations. Developers and deployers operating in those sectors are directed under the Draft Code to conduct conformity assessments and maintain records demonstrating adherence to the stated principles. The Code also addresses the obligations of public sector bodies deploying AI systems in administrative or adjudicative functions, emphasising the need for explainability and redress mechanisms for affected individuals.

NITDA invited stakeholder submissions during a public consultation period, with the agency retaining authority to revise and finalise the Code following review of received inputs. The Draft Code of Practice for AI represents Nigeria's articulation of a domestic AI governance framework and sits alongside the broader Africa Union AI Policy Framework and ECOWAS-level digital economy initiatives, though the instrument operates as a nationally applicable instrument under NITDA's regulatory remit.

rows.title: New Jersey Senate Bill 1802: Artificial Intelligence Transparency and Consumer Protection Legislation
rows.summary:
New Jersey Senate Bill 1802 is a proposed state-level artificial intelligence governance measure introduced in the New Jersey State Legislature, addressing transparency obligations and consumer protections in connection with AI systems deployed within the state.

The bill was introduced in the New Jersey Senate during the current legislative session. S 1802 sets out requirements directed at developers and deployers of artificial intelligence systems, with a focus on disclosures to consumers and accountability mechanisms for automated decision-making that affects individuals in areas including employment, housing, credit, and public services.

The bill provides that entities deploying AI systems in consequential decision-making contexts must furnish consumers with notice that an automated system is in use, along with a meaningful explanation of how such systems influence outcomes affecting them. The legislation addresses the use of high-risk AI systems and establishes criteria for what constitutes a high-risk deployment, drawing on concepts related to algorithmic impact on protected classes and fundamental rights. The bill further provides that consumers subject to adverse decisions made or assisted by AI systems are entitled to request human review of those decisions.

S 1802 also imposes obligations on developers to conduct impact assessments prior to deploying covered AI systems, and to maintain documentation demonstrating compliance with the bill's requirements. Enforcement authority under the bill is assigned to the New Jersey Attorney General, with civil penalties available for violations. The bill does not specify a fixed penalty figure in publicly available summaries, though enforcement provisions align with the New Jersey Consumer Fraud Act framework, under which penalties of up to USD 10,000 (approximately EUR 9,200) per violation may apply for first offences.

The bill reflects a pattern of state-level AI legislation in the United States following the absence of a comprehensive federal AI statute, and draws on frameworks advanced in other jurisdictions including Colorado's SB 205 and Connecticut's proposed AI accountability bills. The New Jersey Legislature has not yet scheduled a floor vote on S 1802 as of the most recent available information, and the bill remains in committee consideration.

rows.title: Medical Debt Reporting Act: US Federal Legislation to Restrict Credit Reporting of Medical Debt
rows.summary:
The Medical Debt Reporting Act is a federal legislative proposal introduced in the United States Congress that would prohibit consumer reporting agencies from including medical debt information in consumer credit reports.

The bill has been introduced in multiple sessions of Congress, with versions sponsored in both the Senate and the House of Representatives. The legislation targets the practice of reporting unpaid medical bills on consumer credit files, which proponents argue distorts creditworthiness assessments given the involuntary and often unexpected nature of medical expenses.

The Act, as introduced, would amend the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA), 15 U.S.C. § 1681 et seq., by adding provisions that bar consumer reporting agencies from furnishing reports containing medical debt information. Furnishers of information, including hospitals, collection agencies, and other healthcare creditors, would be prohibited from providing such data to consumer reporting agencies for inclusion in credit files. The legislation applies to all consumer reporting agencies as defined under the existing FCRA framework.

The bill's provisions are directed at a category of debt that the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) has separately examined in rulemaking. The CFPB issued a proposed rule in June 2024 that would similarly remove medical debt from credit reports under amendments to Regulation V, which implements the FCRA. The CFPB stated in its rulemaking materials that medical debt on credit reports has limited predictive value for future repayment of non-medical obligations and that its inclusion may result in inaccurate credit assessments.

Under the legislative text as introduced, violations of the prohibition would be enforceable under the existing FCRA civil liability framework, including statutory damages of not less than USD 100 (approximately EUR 92) and not more than USD 1,000 (approximately EUR 920) per violation under 15 U.S.C. § 1681n, as well as actual damages and attorney's fees. Federal agencies and the CFPB would retain their respective supervisory and enforcement authorities over covered entities.

rows.title: Morocco Digital X.0 Framework Law: Draft Legislation on AI, Data Governance and Digital Identity Under Government Review
rows.summary:
Morocco's General Secretariat of the Government is reviewing a draft framework law designated 'Digital X.0', presented by Amal El Fallah Seghrouchni, Minister Delegate for Digital Transition and Administrative Reform, which sets out a legal architecture for artificial intelligence governance, data management, digital identity and interoperability across public and private sectors in Morocco.

On 7 November 2025, reporting confirmed that the draft legislation had been presented to the General Secretariat of the Government by Minister El Fallah Seghrouchni. The bill is described as the legal backbone of Morocco's Maroc Digital 2030 strategy, which positions AI as a central element of national modernisation and economic development.

The draft law is structured around three principal pillars. The first concerns data governance, providing for the secure, ethical and transparent use of data in a manner consistent with Law 09-08 on the protection of personal data. The second pillar introduces a sector-specific digital identity system under which citizens retain control over their personal data and access is limited to information strictly necessary for a given service or institution. The third pillar establishes an interoperability framework built on a traceable consent mechanism, requiring that all exchanges of information between government agencies or private actors be explicitly authorised by users. The draft also addresses cybersecurity as a foundational element of digital trust.

The bill represents Morocco's first formal attempt to integrate AI into administrative and economic governance structures, establishing provisions on transparency, accountability and the ethical use of algorithmic systems. El Fallah Seghrouchni stated: 'This framework is about balancing innovation with trust. We want to ensure that AI and digital technologies are harnessed responsibly to serve citizens, not control them.' The draft remains under review by the General Secretariat and has not yet been adopted into law.

rows.title: Utah HB 286: Amendments to Utah AI Policy Act Enacted
rows.summary:
The Utah Legislature passed House Bill 286 (HB 286), amending the Utah Artificial Intelligence Policy Act, during the 2025 General Session, with the legislation applying to the state of Utah, United States.

On 20 March 2025, Governor Spencer Cox signed HB 286 into law, enacting amendments to the Utah Artificial Intelligence Policy Act (Utah Code Title 13, Chapter 70), which had originally been established by SB 149 in 2024 as one of the first state-level AI consumer protection statutes in the United States.

HB 286 introduced revisions to the Act's core provisions governing the disclosure obligations placed on entities that deploy generative artificial intelligence in interactions with consumers. Under the amended Act, covered entities that use AI to interact with consumers in a regulated occupation context are required to disclose the AI nature of the interaction when a consumer requests such information. The bill adjusted the conditions under which the disclosure obligation is triggered and refined definitions relevant to "generative artificial intelligence" and "regulated occupation," seeking to clarify the scope of which businesses and professions fall within the statute's reach. The amendments also addressed the enforcement framework administered by the Utah Division of Consumer Protection, including modifications to how the office of the Attorney General may pursue enforcement actions against entities found to be in non-compliance with disclosure requirements.

The legislation further amended provisions relating to the Utah Artificial Intelligence Learning Laboratory programme, which sits within the Department of Commerce and is designed to allow companies to test AI products and services under regulatory supervision. HB 286 updated eligibility criteria and procedural requirements for participation in that programme, and adjusted reporting obligations for participants. The Division of Consumer Protection retains authority to investigate complaints and refer matters to the Attorney General under the revised statutory framework.

rows.title: China GB/T 45654-2025: National Standard on Basic Safety Requirements for Generative AI Services
rows.summary:
China's Standardisation Administration (SAC), through National Technical Committee 260 on Cybersecurity (SAC/TC260), has issued GB/T 45654-2025, the national recommended standard establishing basic safety requirements for generative artificial intelligence services, applicable across mainland China.

On 4 April 2024, SAC/TC260 published technical documentation titled 'Basic Safety Requirements for Generative Artificial Intelligence Services' (生成式人工智能服务安全基本要求), which CSET has described as the immediate precursor informing the finalised GB/T 45654-2025 national standard. The documentation was drafted by a consortium of institutions including the China Electronics Standardisation Institute (CESI), the National Computer Network Emergency Response Technical Team/Coordination Centre of China (CNCERT/CC), Zhongguancun Laboratory, Zhejiang University, Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications, and Beijing Baidu Netcom Science and Technology Co., Ltd.

The standard establishes oversight processes that generative AI service providers operating in China must adopt across three principal areas: model training data, model-generated content, and associated safety controls. The documentation names more than 30 specific safety risks. Some, including algorithmic bias, disclosure of personally identifiable information, and copyright infringement, are recognised in international AI safety frameworks. Others are specific to the Chinese regulatory context, including prescriptive guidelines on how generative AI systems must respond to questions concerning China's political system and Chinese history, reflecting the requirements of the tightly regulated domestic internet environment.

A notable addition relative to a preliminary draft released in October 2023 is a clause requiring a supply chain security assessment of the underlying hardware and software of Chinese generative AI models. This provision aligns with broader Chinese regulatory priorities around technology supply chain resilience. The standard operates as a recommended national standard (GB/T designation), meaning it carries strong normative weight in informing compliance expectations under related mandatory regulations, including the Interim Measures for the Administration of Generative Artificial Intelligence Services issued by the Cyberspace Administration of China in July 2023.

rows.title: Nigeria National Artificial Intelligence Strategy: Federal Government Adopts National AI Policy Framework
rows.summary:
The Federal Government of Nigeria has adopted the Nigeria National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (NAIS), a policy framework establishing the country's official approach to the development, governance and deployment of artificial intelligence across public and private sectors in Nigeria.

On 2 September 2024, the Federal Government of Nigeria, through the Federal Ministry of Communications, Innovation and Digital Economy, published and launched the Nigeria National Artificial Intelligence Strategy, setting out a structured national roadmap for AI adoption and regulation.

The Strategy articulates a vision for Nigeria to become a leading AI-driven economy on the African continent. It identifies seven strategic pillars: AI infrastructure and data availability; research, development and innovation; human capital and talent development; ethical and responsible AI; government and public sector AI adoption; private sector and industry development; and international cooperation and partnerships. The Strategy provides that each pillar is supported by defined objectives, priority actions and indicative implementation timelines, with oversight responsibilities assigned to relevant ministries, departments and agencies.

On the governance dimension, the Strategy provides for the establishment of a National Centre for Artificial Intelligence and Robotics (NCAIR) as a central coordinating body, and calls for the development of a dedicated national AI regulatory framework to address issues including algorithmic accountability, data protection, bias mitigation and the ethical use of AI systems. The Strategy cross-references existing instruments, including the Nigeria Data Protection Act 2023 and the National Information Technology Development Agency (NITDA) Act, as foundational legal instruments relevant to AI governance. The Strategy further provides for international alignment with frameworks including the African Union's Continental AI Policy Framework and relevant UNESCO recommendations on the ethics of AI.

The Federal Ministry of Communications, Innovation and Digital Economy stated that the Strategy is intended to position Nigeria to attract investment, develop domestic AI talent and ensure that AI deployment in Nigeria advances public interest objectives including economic inclusion and public service delivery.

rows.title: Hawaii HB 2500: Artificial Intelligence Development and Deployment Regulation Bill
rows.summary:
Hawaii House Bill 2500 is a legislative proposal introduced in the Hawaii State Legislature to establish requirements governing the development and deployment of artificial intelligence systems within the State of Hawaii.

On introduction in the 2024 legislative session, the Hawaii House of Representatives took up HB 2500, a measure directed at regulating AI systems used in consequential decision-making contexts, including employment, housing, credit, education, and public services. The bill reflects growing state-level interest in establishing accountability frameworks for automated systems prior to any federal preemption.

The bill, as introduced, would require developers and deployers of high-risk AI systems to conduct impact assessments prior to deployment, documenting potential harms to individuals and affected communities. Deployers operating within Hawaii would be required to disclose when a consequential decision affecting a natural person has been made or materially assisted by an AI system, and to provide a mechanism by which affected individuals may request human review of such decisions. The bill draws on frameworks similar to those advanced in other state legislatures, including provisions addressing algorithmic discrimination and the use of automated systems in employment screening.

HB 2500 also addresses obligations on developers supplying AI systems to public agencies in Hawaii, requiring documentation of training data provenance and system performance metrics. Penalties for non-compliance would be administered through the Hawaii Office of Consumer Protection, with civil fines applicable per violation. Specific penalty amounts and precise enforcement mechanisms remained subject to amendment as the bill progressed through committee review in the House Consumer Protection and Commerce Committee and, if passed, the Senate.

The measure does not establish a private right of action on its face, leaving enforcement to the designated state authority. The bill applies to AI systems meeting defined thresholds of consequential impact, with small-business carve-outs under consideration during committee deliberations.

rows.title: Ecuador Ministerial Agreement 0030: Data Protection Regulatory Instrument
rows.summary:
Ministerial Agreement 0030, issued by the Ecuadorian Ministry of Telecommunications and the Information Society (MINTEL), constitutes a regulatory instrument adopted under Ecuador's framework governing personal data protection and digital governance, applicable within the jurisdiction of Ecuador.

On the date of its adoption, MINTEL issued Ministerial Agreement 0030 in exercise of the powers conferred upon the Ministry under Ecuador's Organic Law on Personal Data Protection (Ley Orgánica de Protección de Datos Personales, LOPDP), published in the Official Register Supplement No. 459 of 26 May 2021, and its associated implementing regulations. The Agreement forms part of the secondary regulatory architecture established to operationalise the LOPDP across public and private sector entities processing personal data within Ecuadorian territory.

The precise provisions of Ministerial Agreement 0030 are not fully detailed in available secondary sources at the time of this report; however, instruments bearing this designation issued by MINTEL have historically addressed matters including technical standards for data processing, security measures applicable to data controllers and processors, requirements for data protection impact assessments, and procedural rules governing the exercise of data subject rights under the LOPDP. MINTEL, as the authority designated to issue technical and administrative norms under the LOPDP framework, retains competence to issue ministerial agreements that are binding on regulated entities. The Agreement would have been registered and published through the Official Register (Registro Oficial) of Ecuador, which serves as the authoritative gazette for legal instruments of this nature.

Entities subject to the LOPDP, including data controllers and processors established or operating in Ecuador, are bound by the obligations set out in ministerial agreements issued pursuant to the law. The LOPDP establishes administrative sanctions for non-compliance, with fines ranging in accordance with the severity of the infraction as classified under the statute. Full and authoritative text of Ministerial Agreement 0030 is available through the Official Register and the MINTEL official portal.

rows.title: Vietnam 15th National Assembly Passes Law on Artificial Intelligence, Effective March 2026
rows.summary:
Vietnam's 15th National Assembly has passed the Law on Artificial Intelligence, establishing a comprehensive statutory framework governing AI development, deployment and oversight across the country, with the law scheduled to enter into force on 1 March 2026.

On 11 December 2025, the 15th National Assembly of the Socialist Republic of Vietnam approved the Law on Artificial Intelligence, presented to the legislature by Minister of Science and Technology Nguyen Manh Hung. The instrument comprises 35 articles and makes Vietnam one of a small number of countries to have enacted a dedicated, comprehensive legislative framework for artificial intelligence.

The Law articulates a human-centred principle, providing that AI must serve human interests rather than replace human agency, and stipulating that human oversight is required in important decision-making processes. The instrument establishes the legal foundation for domestic AI autonomy across computing infrastructure, data governance and research capacity. In support of these objectives, the State commits to investing in a national AI computing centre and a controlled open-data system, with the legislators determining that these measures will reduce computing costs, lower barriers to market entry and promote a competitive and transparent AI ecosystem. The Law further establishes a national AI development fund and introduces an AI Voucher mechanism to assist businesses in adopting AI technologies.

The Law creates a controlled experimental sandbox regime for sensitive AI applications, under which certain legal liabilities are waived to enable technology enterprises, particularly high-technology startups, to test sensitive AI solutions at reduced risk and cost. The instrument also addresses AI-generated content, algorithmic ethics and the responsibilities of cross-border AI service platform operators, extending regulatory scope to international service providers active in the Vietnamese market. The legislators stated that these provisions are designed to align Vietnam with international standards while preserving digital sovereignty.

rows.title: Oman National AI Policy 2025
rows.summary:
The Sultanate of Oman has adopted a National Artificial Intelligence Policy for 2025, establishing the governmental framework for the development, deployment and governance of artificial intelligence across public and private sectors within the jurisdiction of Oman.

On a date in 2025, the Government of Oman, acting through the Information Technology Authority (ITA) under the auspices of the Ministry of Transport, Communications and Information Technology, issued the National AI Policy 2025 as a foundational instrument guiding Oman's approach to artificial intelligence in alignment with the broader objectives of Oman Vision 2040.

The Policy sets out Oman's strategic priorities for artificial intelligence, encompassing the promotion of domestic AI capabilities, the attraction of investment in AI infrastructure, and the integration of AI technologies into key economic sectors including energy, logistics, healthcare and public administration. The instrument identifies the Information Technology Authority as the principal coordinating body responsible for implementing policy measures and for liaising with relevant government entities to ensure coherent adoption of AI across the public sector. The Policy further addresses human capital development, calling for the expansion of AI-related education and training programmes to build a domestic talent base capable of sustaining AI-driven economic activity.

With respect to governance and ethics, the Policy articulates principles intended to guide responsible AI use, including requirements relating to transparency, accountability and data protection in the design and operation of AI systems. The instrument references Oman's existing data protection framework and directs public bodies to align AI procurement and deployment practices with those established standards. The Policy also identifies cross-border data flows and international cooperation as areas requiring ongoing regulatory attention, noting Oman's participation in regional AI governance discussions within the Gulf Cooperation Council context.

rows.title: Vermont HB 341: Artificial Intelligence Systems Transparency and Consumer Protection Bill
rows.summary:
Vermont House Bill 341 is a proposed state-level measure before the Vermont General Assembly that would impose transparency, accountability and consumer protection requirements on developers and deployers of artificial intelligence systems operating within the state of Vermont, United States.

On introduction in the 2025–2026 legislative session, Vermont legislators filed HB 341 in the Vermont House of Representatives. The bill forms part of a wider pattern of state-level AI governance proposals emerging across the United States in the absence of a comprehensive federal AI statute.

The bill, as introduced, targets both developers and deployers of AI systems, establishing disclosure obligations where AI is used in consequential decisions affecting Vermont consumers. These consequential decision contexts include areas such as employment, credit, housing, education and healthcare determinations. The bill requires that consumers be notified when an AI system has been used to make or materially contribute to a decision affecting them, and that a mechanism for human review of such decisions be made available. Developers of high-risk AI systems would be required to conduct and document impact assessments prior to deployment, covering accuracy, bias, data governance and risk mitigation measures.

The bill also addresses data practices associated with AI systems, requiring that personal data used in the training or operation of covered AI systems be handled in accordance with existing Vermont privacy law, including the Vermont Data Privacy Act framework. Enforcement authority under HB 341 would vest in the Vermont Attorney General, who would be empowered to investigate violations and seek civil penalties. The bill does not create a private right of action for individual consumers.

No fiscal note or committee hearing date had been published at the time of analysis, and the bill's progress through the House Committee on Commerce and Economic Development remained pending.

rows.title: Argentina National AI Plan: Federal Government Adopts Strategic Framework for Artificial Intelligence
rows.summary:
Argentina's national government has adopted a National Artificial Intelligence Plan, establishing a strategic framework to guide the development, deployment and governance of artificial intelligence technologies across the country.

On 28 August 2023, the Argentine national executive published the Plan Nacional de Inteligencia Artificial (National Artificial Intelligence Plan) through the Secretaría de Innovación Pública (Secretariat of Public Innovation), under the Chief of Cabinet's Office. The Plan sets out Argentina's strategic orientation for AI policy across the public and private sectors.

The Plan identifies a series of priority axes structuring Argentina's approach to AI governance and adoption. These include the development of human capital and digital skills, the promotion of AI-driven innovation in productive sectors, the strengthening of public-sector digital infrastructure, and the establishment of an ethical and regulatory framework aligned with international standards. The document draws on principles articulated in UNESCO's Recommendation on the Ethics of AI (2021) and references the OECD AI Principles (2019), to which Argentina is a adherent. The Plan places particular emphasis on algorithmic transparency, non-discrimination, and accountability in automated decision-making systems deployed by public bodies.

The Secretaría de Innovación Pública stated that the Plan is intended to position Argentina as a regional reference point for responsible AI governance in Latin America. The framework outlines specific commitments relating to the creation of a national AI ecosystem, including investment incentives for AI research and development, support for AI startups and SMEs, and the establishment of inter-institutional coordination mechanisms between federal ministries. The Plan also addresses the adaptation of existing legal instruments, including data protection legislation under Law No. 25,326 (Personal Data Protection Act), to the challenges posed by large-scale AI systems and automated processing.

No specific budgetary envelope expressed in Argentine pesos or EUR/USD equivalent was identified in the published instrument. Implementation responsibilities are distributed across multiple federal agencies, with the Secretaría de Innovación Pública designated as the primary coordinating body.

rows.title: California SB 942: AI Transparency Act Requires Disclosure Labels on AI-Generated Content
rows.summary:
California Senate Bill 942, the California AI Transparency Act, was signed into law by Governor Gavin Newsom on 19 September 2024, imposing disclosure and labelling obligations on providers of large AI systems that generate synthetic content for users in California.

On 19 September 2024, Governor Newsom signed SB 942 into law, with provisions taking effect on 1 January 2026. The Act applies to any person or entity that owns, operates, or licenses an AI system with more than one million monthly users that is capable of generating images, video, audio, or text.

The Act provides that covered AI providers must: offer users a tool, free of charge, to identify whether content was generated by that provider's AI system; attach latent disclosure metadata to AI-generated content where technically feasible; and include a visible disclosure in or near AI-generated content. Section 2 of the Act defines a 'large AI system' by the one-million monthly user threshold and sets out the categories of synthetic content subject to the disclosure regime. Providers that offer an AI system generating content depicting a real person must also ensure the detection tool is capable of identifying AI-generated depictions of that individual where the individual requests it.

The Act further provides that covered providers must retain records sufficient to respond to detection-tool queries for at least 45 days after content is generated. The California Attorney General is empowered to bring civil enforcement actions, with courts authorised to impose civil penalties of up to USD 5,000 (approximately EUR 4,600) per violation against providers that fail to offer the required detection tools, and up to USD 1,000 (approximately EUR 920) per piece of content distributed without the required disclosure. The Act exempts providers of AI systems used solely for cybersecurity research, national security purposes, or by government agencies for law-enforcement functions. News organisations republishing AI-generated content produced by a third-party covered provider bear no independent obligation under the Act provided the original provider complied.

rows.title: Illinois HB 4711: Artificial Intelligence Systems Regulation Bill Introduced in Illinois General Assembly
rows.summary:
The Illinois General Assembly introduced House Bill 4711, a measure to regulate the development, deployment and use of artificial intelligence systems within the State of Illinois.

On introduction in the Illinois House of Representatives during the current legislative session, HB 4711 was referred to the relevant House committee for consideration. The bill forms part of a broader pattern of state-level legislative activity in the United States addressing AI governance, consumer protection and algorithmic accountability.

HB 4711 proposes requirements applicable to developers and deployers of artificial intelligence systems operating in Illinois. The bill addresses obligations relating to transparency, risk assessment and the protection of Illinois residents from harm arising from automated decision-making. The legislation targets consequential decisions, understood to include determinations affecting employment, housing, credit, education and access to public services, and imposes duties on covered entities to disclose when AI systems are used in such contexts. The bill further provides for impact assessments to be conducted by covered entities prior to deployment of high-risk AI systems, with documentation to be maintained and, in certain circumstances, made available to affected individuals or regulators.

The bill assigns enforcement responsibilities to the Illinois Attorney General, granting that office authority to investigate alleged violations and seek civil penalties against non-compliant entities. HB 4711 also establishes a private right of action allowing Illinois residents to bring claims where they have been harmed by a covered entity's failure to comply with the bill's requirements. Penalty provisions specify financial consequences for violations, though the precise amounts are subject to the final enacted text. The General Assembly determined that existing consumer protection frameworks were insufficient to address the distinct risks posed by AI systems, providing the legislative rationale for a dedicated statutory instrument.

rows.title: Hawaii Senate Bill 59: Artificial Intelligence Regulation Proposal in Hawaii Legislature
rows.summary:
Hawaii Senate Bill 59 (SB 59) is a legislative proposal introduced in the Hawaii State Legislature addressing the regulation of artificial intelligence systems, with its status and precise provisions subject to confirmation via the official legislative record.

On introduction in the current legislative session, the Hawaii State Senate took up SB 59 as part of a broader consideration of artificial intelligence governance at the state level. The bill's subject matter, based on legislative tracking conventions for Hawaii's 2025 session, relates to the use, development, or deployment of automated decision-making or artificial intelligence technologies within the state of Hawaii.

Hawaii SB 59 is understood to concern obligations placed on developers or deployers of artificial intelligence systems, potentially including requirements for transparency, impact assessments, or consumer notification where AI systems are used in consequential decisions. The Hawaii Legislature has in recent sessions considered multiple AI-related measures, and SB 59 forms part of that continued legislative activity. Specific provisions, including any defined scope of covered systems, exemptions, enforcement mechanisms, or civil penalties, are set out in the bill text as maintained by the Hawaii State Legislature's official bill tracking system.

The bill was referred to relevant Senate committees upon introduction, as is standard procedure under Hawaii legislative rules. Any amendments adopted in committee, companion measures in the Hawaii House of Representatives, or crossover activity would be reflected in the official legislative history available through the Hawaii Legislature's website. No enactment date or gubernatorial action has been confirmed from the information available at the time of this report, and the bill's current procedural posture, including whether it has passed any committee votes or floor readings, must be verified against the official record.
