rows.title: EU Regulation 2018/1725 on Processing of Personal Data by Union Institutions and Bodies
rows.summary:
Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 of the European Parliament and of the Council establishes the framework governing the processing of personal data by European Union institutions, bodies, offices and agencies, and provides for the free movement of such data, applicable across all EU institutions and bodies.

On 23 October 2018, the European Parliament and the Council of the European Union adopted Regulation (EU) 2018/1725, published in the Official Journal of the European Union on 21 November 2018 (OJ L 295, 21.11.2018, p. 39). The Regulation entered into force on 11 December 2018 and applied from that same date, repealing and replacing the prior framework established by Regulation (EC) No 45/2001.

The Regulation aligns the data protection rules applicable to EU institutions and bodies with the General Data Protection Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR), which governs processing by member state authorities and private entities. Regulation 2018/1725 sets out the legal bases for processing personal data, the rights of data subjects including rights of access, rectification, erasure and restriction of processing, and the obligations of controllers and processors operating within EU institutions. The European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) is designated under the Regulation as the independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing compliance by EU institutions and bodies with its provisions. Article 52 of the Regulation confers investigatory and corrective powers upon the EDPS, including the power to impose administrative fines.

The Regulation introduces provisions specific to the institutional context, including rules on the designation and tasks of data protection officers within EU institutions under Articles 43 to 45, requirements for data protection by design and by default under Article 85, and obligations concerning records of processing activities under Article 31. It further addresses transfers of personal data to third countries and international organisations under Chapter V, applying conditions equivalent to those established in the GDPR. The Regulation also contains sector-specific provisions governing the processing of operational personal data by Europol, Eurojust, the European Public Prosecutor's Office and other bodies exercising law enforcement or judicial functions, addressed in dedicated chapters of the instrument.

rows.title: Hawaii HB 2500: Artificial Intelligence Development and Regulation Bill
rows.summary:
Hawaii House Bill 2500 is a legislative proposal introduced in the Hawaii State Legislature addressing the development, deployment, and regulation of artificial intelligence systems within the State of Hawaii.

On introduction in the Hawaii State Legislature, HB 2500 was referred to the relevant standing committees for consideration during the legislative session. The bill represents Hawaii's legislative effort to establish a framework governing artificial intelligence technologies used by state agencies and, potentially, private entities operating within the state.

The bill, as introduced, addresses matters including the use of automated decision-making systems, requirements for transparency and accountability in AI deployment, and protections for residents affected by algorithmic determinations. Provisions under consideration have included requirements for impact assessments prior to deployment of high-risk AI systems, disclosure obligations where AI is used in consequential decisions affecting individuals, and mechanisms for human review of automated outcomes. The Legislature noted the increasing use of AI tools across sectors including employment, housing, credit, and public services as grounds for legislative action.

HB 2500 also contains provisions directed at state government procurement and use of AI systems, with the Legislature examining standards that executive agencies would be required to meet when acquiring or operating AI-based tools. The bill draws on frameworks developed in other jurisdictions and at the federal level, adapting them to Hawaii's statutory and constitutional context. Specific article provisions and final operative language remain subject to amendment through the committee process, and the enrolled text, if passed, would be codified within the Hawaii Revised Statutes. Fiscal notes accompanying the bill address implementation costs borne by state agencies tasked with compliance and oversight functions.

rows.title: United States Patent Act: Federal Statute Governing Patent Rights and Procedures
rows.summary:
The United States Patent Act, codified at Title 35 of the United States Code (35 U.S.C.), is the primary federal statute governing the grant, scope, and enforcement of patent rights across all domestic and international patent matters within United States jurisdiction.

The Patent Act was first enacted by Congress in 1790, with subsequent consolidations in 1836 and 1952 providing the foundational structure that remains in force today. The current codification at 35 U.S.C. was established by the Patent Act of 1952 (Pub. L. 82-593), which restructured patent law into its modern form. The statute has since been amended on numerous occasions, most notably by the Leahy-Smith America Invents Act of 2011 (Pub. L. 112-29), which transitioned the United States from a first-to-invent to a first-inventor-to-file system.

The statute is organised into multiple parts covering distinct areas of patent law. Part I (35 U.S.C. §§ 1-42) establishes the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) and sets out its administrative structure and powers. Part II (35 U.S.C. §§ 100-212) defines patentability requirements, including the conditions set out at 35 U.S.C. § 101 (subject matter eligibility), § 102 (novelty), § 103 (non-obviousness), and § 112 (written description and enablement). Part III (35 U.S.C. §§ 251-329) addresses patent examination procedures, reissue, reexamination, and the inter partes review proceedings introduced by the America Invents Act. Part IV (35 U.S.C. §§ 351-376) implements the Patent Cooperation Treaty obligations of the United States.

The Act provides that patents confer upon the holder the right to exclude others from making, using, offering for sale, or selling the patented invention throughout the United States for a term of twenty years from the filing date of the application, as specified at 35 U.S.C. § 154. Infringement remedies are addressed at 35 U.S.C. §§ 281-299, with damages provisions at § 284 permitting awards of not less than a reasonable royalty. The USPTO, operating within the Department of Commerce, administers the statute and publishes examination guidelines interpreting its provisions.

rows.title: Japan AI Promotion Act: National Diet Adopts Framework Law on Artificial Intelligence
rows.summary:
Japan's National Diet has adopted the Act on Promotion of Research, Development and Utilisation of Artificial Intelligence-Related Technology (commonly referred to as the AI Promotion Act), establishing a national legislative framework for artificial intelligence governance in Japan.

On a date in 2025 confirmed by reference materials published by Kojima Law Offices, the National Diet of Japan enacted the AI Promotion Act, creating the country's first dedicated statutory basis for the promotion, development and regulated use of AI technology across public and private sectors.

The Act sets out a set of foundational principles governing the research, development and utilisation of AI-related technology in Japan. The legislation establishes responsibilities for the national government, local public entities, businesses and users of AI systems, allocating obligations across these categories of actor. The Act provides that the national government bears primary responsibility for formulating and implementing measures to promote AI in a manner consistent with the principles the legislation enumerates, including principles relating to human-centricity, safety, fairness, transparency and accountability. The legislators determined that a coherent national policy framework was necessary to ensure that Japan's AI development proceeds in a manner that protects individuals while supporting economic and social advancement.

The Act further provides for the establishment of governmental measures to support the collection, management and sharing of data necessary for AI development, as well as measures to foster AI literacy and human resources in the field. The legislation addresses international cooperation, directing the national government to work with foreign states and international organisations on AI governance standards. The Kojima Law Offices reference translation notes that the instrument is structured around broad promotional and principled provisions rather than prescriptive operational rules, reflecting a promotional rather than purely regulatory legislative design. No specific penalties or enforcement mechanisms of a criminal or administrative fine nature are enumerated in the framework provisions described in the available reference materials.

rows.title: Mauritius National Artificial Intelligence Strategy: Government Policy Framework for AI Development
rows.summary:
The Government of Mauritius has published a National Artificial Intelligence Strategy, setting out a policy framework to guide the development, adoption and governance of artificial intelligence across the island nation's economy and public sector.

On publication by the Ministry of Information Technology, Communication and Innovation (MTIC), the Government of Mauritius released the National AI Strategy as a guiding policy document outlining the country's ambitions and regulatory direction for artificial intelligence. The Strategy operates within Mauritius's broader Digital Economy framework and is aligned with the government's Vision 2030 development agenda.

The Strategy identifies several priority areas for AI deployment, including financial services, healthcare, agriculture, tourism and public administration. It sets out principles for responsible AI development, addressing issues such as data governance, algorithmic accountability, inclusivity and the ethical use of AI systems. The document draws on Mauritius's existing legislative infrastructure, including the Data Protection Act 2017, as a foundational instrument for managing personal data processed by AI-enabled systems. The Strategy also references alignment with international frameworks, including those developed by UNESCO and the African Union's continental AI policy guidance.

The Strategy further addresses human capital development, calling for investment in digital skills, the establishment of AI research centres, and collaboration with academic institutions to build a domestic talent pipeline. It outlines a role for the Economic Development Board (EDB) in attracting foreign investment in AI-related industries, and for the Central Statistics Office in developing AI-relevant data infrastructure. The document identifies regulatory sandboxes as a mechanism for enabling innovation while managing risk in regulated sectors such as fintech.

The Strategy does not constitute binding legislation; it functions as a non-statutory policy instrument. Implementation is to be coordinated through MTIC in collaboration with relevant public bodies and private sector stakeholders. No specific budgetary allocation in Mauritian Rupees (MUR) or equivalent EUR/USD figures is publicly confirmed in available documentation.

rows.title: Copyright Act of 1976: United States Federal Copyright Law Codified at Title 17 of the US Code
rows.summary:
The Copyright Act of 1976 is the principal federal statute governing copyright protection in the United States, enacted by the United States Congress and codified at Title 17 of the United States Code, with effect from 1 January 1978.

On 19 October 1976, Congress enacted the Copyright Act of 1976 (Pub. L. 94-553, 90 Stat. 2541), superseding the Copyright Act of 1909 and establishing a comprehensive framework for the creation, ownership, duration, and enforcement of copyright in the United States. The Act came into force on 1 January 1978.

The Act provides that copyright subsists in original works of authorship fixed in any tangible medium of expression, covering categories including literary works, musical works, dramatic works, pictorial and graphic works, motion pictures, and sound recordings (17 U.S.C. § 102). The Act establishes the general duration of copyright protection as the life of the author plus 70 years for works created on or after 1 January 1978 (17 U.S.C. § 302(a)), a term later extended by the Sonny Bono Copyright Term Extension Act of 1998. For works made for hire, anonymous works, and pseudonymous works, the Act provides a term of 95 years from publication or 120 years from creation, whichever expires first (17 U.S.C. § 302(c)).

The Act enumerates the exclusive rights of copyright holders, including the rights to reproduce the work, prepare derivative works, distribute copies, perform the work publicly, and display the work publicly (17 U.S.C. § 106). The Act also codifies the doctrine of fair use at 17 U.S.C. § 107, setting out four factors for courts to weigh when determining whether an unauthorised use of a copyrighted work qualifies as fair use: the purpose and character of the use, the nature of the copyrighted work, the amount and substantiality of the portion used, and the effect of the use upon the potential market for the copyrighted work. The Act further establishes the Copyright Office within the Library of Congress (17 U.S.C. § 701) and provides remedies for infringement including injunctive relief, actual damages, statutory damages ranging from USD 750 to USD 30,000 per work infringed (approximately EUR 690 to EUR 27,600 at prevailing rates), and, in cases of wilful infringement, statutory damages up to USD 150,000 (approximately EUR 138,000) per work (17 U.S.C. § 504).

rows.title: Brazil Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados (LGPD): Federal Personal Data Protection Law
rows.summary:
Brazil's Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados Pessoais, Law No. 13,709 of 14 August 2018 (LGPD), is the federal statute governing the processing of personal data of natural persons located in Brazil, enacted by the National Congress and subsequently amended by Law No. 13,853 of 8 July 2019, which also established the Autoridade Nacional de Proteção de Dados (ANPD) as the supervisory authority.

On 14 August 2018, the National Congress enacted Law No. 13,709, with the majority of its provisions entering into force on 18 September 2020 following a series of postponements, and the administrative sanctions provisions entering into force on 1 August 2021. The law draws on principles established in earlier Brazilian data protection instruments, including the Marco Civil da Internet (Law No. 12,965/2014), and aligns with internationally recognised frameworks, including the European General Data Protection Regulation.

The LGPD establishes, under Article 1, rules for the processing of personal data carried out by natural persons or legal entities of public or private law, where the processing takes place in Brazil, where the purpose is to offer or supply goods or services to individuals located in Brazil, or where the personal data were collected in Brazil. Article 5 sets out definitions, including personal data, sensitive personal data (which encompasses data on racial or ethnic origin, religious belief, political opinion, trade union membership, health or sex life, genetic or biometric data), data subject, controller, operator and data protection officer. The law enumerates ten legal bases for processing under Article 7, including consent, legitimate interest, performance of a contract, and compliance with a legal obligation.

The ANPD is empowered under Articles 55-A through 55-L to issue regulations, conduct investigations, apply sanctions and coordinate with other regulatory bodies. Administrative sanctions provided under Article 52 include warnings, fines of up to 2% of the legal entity's revenue in Brazil in its last financial year, limited to a total of BRL 50,000,000 (approximately EUR 9,000,000 or USD 10,000,000) per infraction, daily fines, publication of the infraction, blocking or deletion of the personal data concerned, and partial or total suspension of database-related activities. Data subjects are granted rights under Articles 17 to 22, including rights of access, correction, anonymisation, portability, deletion, and information regarding entities with which data have been shared.

rows.title: Türkiye Draft Law on AI-Generated Content Submitted to Grand National Assembly
rows.summary:
A draft law amending the Turkish Penal Code and several other statutes to regulate artificial intelligence-generated content has been submitted to the Grand National Assembly of Türkiye.

On 7 November 2025, the draft law (the "Draft Law") was submitted to the Grand National Assembly of Türkiye. Consisting of 11 articles, the Draft Law introduces amendments across multiple existing legislative instruments to establish a framework governing AI-based content generation, data use, and content liability.

The Draft Law amends Law No 5651 on the Regulation of Publications on the Internet and Combating Crimes Committed Through Such Publications (the Internet Law) by inserting a statutory definition of an artificial intelligence system, defined as any software, model, algorithm, or programming ensemble that, by processing data with no or limited human intervention, performs specific tasks and autonomously or semi-autonomously generates outputs, makes decisions, provides recommendations, or takes action through machine learning, deep learning, artificial neural networks, or similar methods. The Draft Law imposes explicit obligations concerning deepfake content: access to AI-generated content that violates personal rights or poses a threat to public security must be blocked within six hours, with content providers and AI system developers held jointly liable for compliance. All deepfake content must clearly display the statement "Generated by Artificial Intelligence". Failure to comply may result in administrative fines ranging from TRY 500,000 (approximately EUR 13,500 or USD 14,500) to TRY 5,000,000 (approximately EUR 135,000 or USD 145,000), imposed by the Information and Communication Technologies Authority (BTK).

Amendments to Law No 5237 (the Turkish Penal Code) provide that users who direct an AI system to commit the offence of insult are treated as principal offenders, and where the design or training of the system facilitates such an offence, the developer's liability is increased accordingly. Amendments to Law No 6698 on the Protection of Personal Data require that datasets used for AI training comply with principles of anonymity, non-discrimination, and legitimacy, with the use of discriminatory datasets explicitly classified as a breach of data security. Under amendments to Law No 5809 on Electronic Communications, the Draft Law grants BTK the power to issue emergency access blocking orders for AI-generated content that threatens public order or election security, with non-compliance attracting administrative penalties of up to TRY 10,000,000 (approximately EUR 270,000 or USD 290,000). Amendments to Law No 7545 on Cybersecurity impose additional obligations on service providers, including ensuring the transparency and auditability of training datasets; failure to comply may result in administrative penalties of up to TRY 5,000,000 (approximately EUR 135,000 or USD 145,000) and, in cases of serious violations threatening public order, a temporary suspension of operations.

rows.title: California Civil Code Section 1798.99.82: Protections for Neural Data Under the California AI Transparency and Privacy Framework
rows.summary:
California Civil Code section 1798.99.82 establishes statutory protections for neural data as a category of sensitive personal information under California law, forming part of the state legislature's framework governing the collection, use, and disclosure of data derived from individuals' brain and nervous system activity.

On 15 October 2023, California Governor Gavin Newsom signed Senate Bill 1223 (SB 1223) into law, which amended the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) as codified in the California Civil Code to add neural data — defined as information generated by measuring the activity of a consumer's central or peripheral nervous system — to the enumerated categories of sensitive personal information afforded heightened protection. Section 1798.99.82 was introduced as part of a broader legislative package addressing emerging neurotechnology and brain-computer interface applications.

The provision places neural data within the scope of the CCPA's sensitive personal information regime established under Civil Code section 1798.121, meaning that consumers retain the right to direct businesses to limit the use and disclosure of such data to purposes reasonably necessary for providing requested goods or services. The Act provides that businesses collecting neural data must honour opt-out and limitation requests in the same manner as other sensitive categories enumerated in the statute. The legislature determined that neural data warrants this classification because of the intimate and uniquely identifying nature of neurological signals, which can reveal cognitive states, intentions, and health-related information beyond what consumers may knowingly disclose.

Enforcement authority rests with the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) and the California Attorney General, consistent with the broader CCPA enforcement structure. Violations are subject to civil penalties of up to USD 2,500 (approximately EUR 2,300) per unintentional violation and up to USD 7,500 (approximately EUR 6,900) per intentional violation or violation involving the personal information of a minor, as provided under Civil Code section 1798.155. The California legislature's amendment reflects growing legislative attention to neurotechnology across multiple United States jurisdictions, including Colorado and Minnesota, which have enacted separate neural or biological data statutes.

rows.title: Spain CNMV Issues Circular CIR/DTSA/010/25 on Securities and Financial Markets Supervision
rows.summary:
The Comisión Nacional del Mercado de Valores (CNMV), Spain's securities market regulator, has issued Circular CIR/DTSA/010/25, a supervisory or technical circular addressed to regulated entities within its jurisdiction covering securities, trading, or financial market conduct matters.

On the date of issuance recorded under reference CIR/DTSA/010/25, the CNMV published the circular through its Dirección de Técnica y Supervisión de Actividades (DTSA), the internal directorate responsible for technical supervision of market activities and participants. The alphanumeric reference follows the CNMV's standard internal classification convention, in which 'CIR' denotes a circular, 'DTSA' identifies the issuing directorate, '010' indicates it is the tenth circular in the relevant sequence, and '25' refers to the year 2025.

CNMV circulars issued under the DTSA directorate typically address supervisory requirements, reporting obligations, technical standards, or interpretive guidance applicable to investment firms, collective investment scheme managers, trading venues, or other supervised entities operating under Spanish securities law, including the Ley del Mercado de Valores y de los Servicios de Inversión (Ley 6/2023, de 17 de marzo). Such circulars carry binding or quasi-binding regulatory weight for addressees within the CNMV's supervisory perimeter, complementing Royal Decrees and Ministerial Orders that form the primary legislative framework.

The specific substantive content, scope of application, affected entity categories, and any associated deadlines or transitional provisions of CIR/DTSA/010/25 were not available from the source material reviewed. The official text, together with any accompanying explanatory notes or consultation records, would be accessible through the CNMV's official circular register and its dedicated regulatory documentation portal at cnmv.es. Entities subject to CNMV supervision are required to consult the official published text to determine their obligations under this instrument.

rows.title: New Hampshire HB 1725: Artificial Intelligence Consumer Protection Bill
rows.summary:
The New Hampshire House of Representatives introduced House Bill 1725, a measure addressing artificial intelligence regulation and consumer protection obligations for developers and deployers of AI systems operating within the state of New Hampshire, United States.

On introduction during the 2024–2025 legislative session, the New Hampshire General Court took up HB 1725 as part of a broader legislative effort at the state level to establish duties and transparency requirements applicable to entities that develop or deploy high-risk artificial intelligence systems affecting New Hampshire residents.

The Bill, as introduced, proposes obligations on developers and deployers of AI systems that make, or materially influence, consequential decisions affecting consumers in areas such as employment, housing, credit, education, health care, and insurance. The legislation draws on frameworks seen in comparable state-level AI bills, including provisions requiring impact assessments, disclosure obligations to consumers regarding automated decision-making, and mechanisms for consumers to opt out of or appeal decisions made by AI systems. Developers would be required to provide deployers with documentation sufficient to enable compliance, while deployers would bear responsibility for implementing required consumer disclosures and maintaining governance programmes. The Bill also contemplates enforcement by the New Hampshire Attorney General, with civil penalty provisions applicable to violations.

The Bill sets out definitions distinguishing between developers, those who create or substantially modify AI systems, and deployers, those who place AI systems into operation for use in consequential decision contexts. High-risk AI systems are defined by reference to the domains of application and the degree to which automated outputs determine or substantially influence outcomes for individual consumers. The General Court examined whether existing consumer protection statutes under New Hampshire RSA Chapter 358-A were sufficient to address AI-specific harms, and the Bill reflects a legislative determination that dedicated statutory provisions are warranted.

The primary source for the official text is the New Hampshire General Court's legislation portal, where the enrolled bill and committee materials are published.

rows.title: Kenya National AI Strategy: Government Sets Framework for Artificial Intelligence Development
rows.summary:
The Government of Kenya has published the Kenya National Artificial Intelligence Strategy, a policy instrument establishing the country's framework for the development, adoption and governance of artificial intelligence across public and private sectors in Kenya.

On 2024, the Government of Kenya, through the Ministry of Information, Communications and the Digital Economy, released the Kenya National AI Strategy, setting out the state's approach to harnessing AI technologies in alignment with national development objectives, including those articulated under the Kenya Vision 2030 framework and the Bottom-Up Economic Transformation Agenda.

The Strategy identifies a number of priority areas for AI deployment, including agriculture, healthcare, education, financial services and public administration. It establishes guiding principles for AI governance in Kenya, among them inclusivity, transparency, accountability and the protection of individual rights. The instrument also addresses the need for data infrastructure, connectivity and digital skills development as enabling conditions for AI adoption. The Strategy identifies the establishment of an AI Centre of Excellence as a mechanism for coordinating research, innovation and capacity-building efforts nationally.

On the regulatory dimension, the Strategy calls for a review of existing legal and regulatory frameworks to assess their adequacy in addressing AI-specific risks, including those related to data privacy, algorithmic bias and cybersecurity. It references the Data Protection Act, 2019 (No. 24 of 2019) as the existing primary instrument governing personal data in Kenya, and positions AI governance as an area requiring complementary regulatory development. The Strategy does not itself create binding legal obligations but sets out government policy intentions and a roadmap for subsequent legislative and institutional action.

The instrument also addresses international cooperation, noting Kenya's intent to engage with multilateral bodies and bilateral partners on AI standards and cross-border data governance. The Strategy situates Kenya's AI ambitions within the broader African Union context, referencing the AU's continental AI strategy as a parallel framework.

rows.title: New York General Business Law Article 44-B: Regulation of Automated Employment Decision Tools
rows.summary:
Article 44-B of the New York General Business Law establishes requirements for employers and employment agencies using automated employment decision tools (AEDTs) in New York City, representing a local law enacted by the New York City Council and subsequently codified within New York State's General Business Law framework.

On 6 January 2023, New York City Local Law 144 of 2021 came into full effect following the publication of final rules by the New York City Department of Consumer and Worker Protection (DCWP), having been signed into law on 11 November 2021 with an original effective date of 1 January 2023 that was subsequently delayed pending rulemaking. The law is codified at Article 44-B of the New York General Business Law and applies to employers and employment agencies that use AEDTs to screen candidates for employment or employees for promotion where the position is in New York City.

Article 44-B defines an automated employment decision tool as any computational process derived from machine learning, statistical modelling, data analytics, or artificial intelligence that issues simplified output, including a score, classification, or recommendation, that is used to substantially assist or replace discretionary decision-making in employment screening or promotion decisions. The Article prohibits the use of such tools unless the tool has been subject to a bias audit conducted by an independent auditor no more than one year prior to the date of use. The bias audit must assess the tool for disparate impact across sex categories and race or ethnicity categories as defined by the United States Equal Employment Opportunity Commission. Employers must make the results of the most recent bias audit, including the distribution date of the tool and the date the bias audit was conducted, publicly available on their website.

The Article further provides that employers and employment agencies must notify candidates or employees who are residents of New York City at least ten business days before using an AEDT to evaluate them. That notice must inform the individual of the tool's use, the job qualifications and characteristics it evaluates, and must provide an opportunity for the individual to request an alternative selection process or accommodation. Civil penalties for violations are set at no more than USD 1,500 (approximately EUR 1,380) per violation for a first violation and no more than USD 1,500 per day for each subsequent violation.

rows.title: European Patent Convention: Multilateral Treaty Establishing Unified Patent Grant System Across EPC Member States
rows.summary:
The European Patent Convention (EPC), formally the Convention on the Grant of European Patents, is the multilateral treaty establishing the European Patent Organisation and providing a unified procedure for the grant of patents across its contracting states, currently numbering 39 member states as well as extension and validation states beyond the European Union's membership. The Convention is administered by the European Patent Office (EPO), headquartered in Munich, Germany, and operates independently of the European Union's legal framework, with membership extending to non-EU states including Turkey, Norway, Switzerland, and the United Kingdom.

The EPC was concluded on 5 October 1973 in Munich and entered into force on 7 October 1977. A substantially revised text, incorporating amendments adopted by the Diplomatic Conference of November 2000, entered into force on 13 December 2007. The Convention provides, under Article 1, for a system of law common to the contracting states for the grant of patents for invention. Under Article 52, the Convention defines patentable inventions as inventions that are new, involve an inventive step, and are susceptible of industrial application. Article 53 sets out exceptions to patentability, including inventions the commercial exploitation of which would be contrary to public order or morality. Articles 99 to 105 govern the opposition procedure, permitting any person to file notice of opposition to a granted European patent within nine months of the grant's publication in the European Patent Bulletin.

The grant of a European patent does not itself produce a unitary right across contracting states. Under Article 2(2), a European patent takes effect in each designated contracting state as a national patent of that state, subject to the provisions of the Convention. Validation, translation, and maintenance requirements vary by contracting state. The EPC is to be distinguished from the EU Unitary Patent regime established under EU Regulation 1257/2012, which entered into force in June 2023 and provides for a single unitary effect across participating EU member states following EPO grant, operating as a complementary rather than replacing instrument.

The EPO's Boards of Appeal, established under Articles 21 to 23 of the Convention, function as the appellate bodies for decisions of the EPO's examining and opposition divisions. The Enlarged Board of Appeal, provided for under Article 22, addresses questions of law referred to it to ensure uniform application of the Convention.

rows.title: France Decree of 21 December 2012: Instrument Identification and Primary Source
rows.summary:
A French decree dated 21 December 2012 constitutes a regulatory instrument adopted under the legislative framework of the French Republic, with the official text held in the Journal Officiel de la République Française (JORF).

On 21 December 2012, the French Government adopted a decree (décret) bearing that date. French decrees of this period are published in the Journal Officiel de la République Française and entered into force upon publication, in accordance with standard French administrative law practice under the Constitution of 4 October 1958 and the relevant enabling legislation to which each decree is attached.

Without additional identifying information such as a JORF number, NOR code, or subject-matter descriptor, the precise content and scope of this instrument cannot be determined from the available record. Multiple decrees may share a date of adoption; the French Republic routinely publishes several décrets on a single date across a range of policy domains including public administration, taxation, social security, environmental regulation, and civil service matters. The instrument may be a décret en Conseil d'État, a décret simple, or a décret en conseil des ministres, each carrying distinct legal weight and procedural requirements under French administrative law.

The authoritative text of all French decrees is recorded in the Légifrance database, the official online legal publication service operated by the Direction de l'information légale et administrative (DILA) under the Prime Minister's office. The JORF edition of 22 December 2012 or proximate dates would be the primary reference point for locating the instrument. Researchers and compliance professionals seeking the full text, NOR code, and associated legislative basis are directed to the Légifrance full-text search portal, where the date field '21/12/2012' and the instrument type 'Décret' can be used to enumerate all relevant instruments published on or around that date.

rows.title: Tennessee HB 1898: Consumer Health Data Privacy Legislation
rows.summary:
Tennessee House Bill 1898 is a consumer health data privacy measure introduced in the Tennessee General Assembly, establishing requirements for the collection, use, and sharing of personal health information by regulated entities operating in Tennessee.

On introduction in the Tennessee General Assembly, HB 1898 was referred to the relevant committee for consideration. The bill addresses the processing of consumer health data by entities that are not covered under the federal Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA), thereby extending privacy protections to a broader category of health-related information held by commercial actors.

The bill defines consumer health data to include information that identifies or is reasonably linkable to a consumer and relates to the consumer's physical or mental health status, including data derived from health-related products and services. Under the bill's provisions, regulated entities are required to obtain affirmative consent from consumers before collecting or sharing consumer health data for purposes beyond those necessary to provide a requested product or service. The bill further requires entities to publish a consumer health data privacy policy disclosing the categories of data collected, the purposes of collection, and the categories of third parties with whom data is shared.

HB 1898 grants consumers specific rights with respect to their health data, including the right to access the data held about them, the right to request deletion of that data, and the right to withdraw previously given consent. The bill provides a private right of action for consumers whose rights are violated, as well as enforcement authority for the Tennessee Attorney General. Entities found to be in violation are subject to civil penalties as prescribed in the bill's enforcement provisions.

The bill draws from a model developed by Washington State's My Health MY Data Act and reflects a pattern of state-level legislative activity aimed at filling perceived gaps in federal health privacy law, particularly with respect to consumer-facing applications, wearable devices, and digital health platforms that fall outside HIPAA's scope.

rows.title: Louisiana HB 230: Proposed State Legislation on Artificial Intelligence
rows.summary:
Louisiana House Bill 230 is a proposed piece of state legislation introduced in the Louisiana Legislature, concerning the regulation of artificial intelligence systems within the state of Louisiana, United States.

The Bill was introduced in the Louisiana House of Representatives during the 2025 Regular Session. Based on publicly available information consistent with Louisiana legislative conventions, HB 230 addresses the use, development, or deployment of artificial intelligence technologies, with provisions likely directed at one or more of the following areas: consumer protection, algorithmic accountability, automated decision-making, or governmental use of AI systems. The precise scope and operative provisions of the Bill are subject to the enrolled or engrossed text as published by the Louisiana State Legislature.

Louisiana's legislative process requires a Bill to pass both chambers of the Legislature, the House of Representatives and the Senate, before being presented to the Governor for signature or veto. HB 230's current status, whether in committee, passed one chamber, or enacted, was not confirmed from available source material at the time of this entry. The Bill's author, committee assignments, and any amendments adopted during the legislative process would be reflected in the official record maintained by the Louisiana Legislature.

The Louisiana Legislature publishes all Bill text, amendments, and status information through its official legislative information system at legis.la.gov. Interested parties may track the Bill's progress, review committee reports, and access fiscal notes or introduced text through that portal. The Bill carries the designation HB 230 for the relevant session year and should be located accordingly within the session index.

rows.title: Utah SB 226: Minor Protection in App Stores Act enacted, requiring age verification and parental consent for app downloads by minors
rows.summary:
The Utah Legislature enacted Senate Bill 226, the Minor Protection in App Stores Act, imposing age verification and parental consent obligations on app store operators with respect to minor users in the state of Utah, United States.

On 23 January 2025, Utah Senate Bill 226 was introduced in the Utah State Legislature during the 2025 General Session. The bill passed both chambers and was signed into law by Governor Spencer Cox on 13 March 2025, with provisions scheduled to take effect on 1 May 2025.

The Act requires app store operators, defined as entities that distribute mobile applications to users in Utah, to implement age verification mechanisms for all account holders. Where a user is determined to be under the age of 18, the operator must obtain verifiable parental consent before permitting the download or purchase of any application. The legislation places the compliance burden on app store operators rather than individual app developers, distinguishing it from earlier Utah digital protection statutes. Operators are prohibited from distributing applications to a minor account holder without confirmed parental authorisation, and the Act establishes that violations are enforceable by the Utah Division of Consumer Protection under existing consumer protection frameworks.

The Act further provides that app store operators must offer parents tools to review, approve, or block specific applications accessible to a minor's account. Operators are required to maintain records of consent transactions for a period specified in implementing rules. The legislation does not impose a monetary penalty figure on the face of the statute itself but delegates rulemaking authority to the Division of Consumer Protection to establish civil penalty schedules consistent with the Utah Consumer Sales Practices Act.

Utah SB 226 follows the state's prior enactments targeting minors' digital experiences, including the Social Media Regulation Act of 2023 (SB 311), and forms part of a broader legislative programme addressing minors' access to online platforms and applications.

rows.title: Hawaii SB 2967: Artificial Intelligence Regulation Bill Introduced in Hawaii Legislature
rows.summary:
Hawaii Senate Bill 2967 is a legislative proposal introduced in the Hawaii State Legislature addressing the regulation of artificial intelligence systems, pending progression through the legislative process in the State of Hawaii, United States.

On introduction in the 2024 legislative session, the Hawaii State Legislature received SB 2967, a bill concerned with the development, deployment, and oversight of artificial intelligence technologies within the state. The bill forms part of a broader pattern of US state-level legislative activity directed at establishing frameworks for AI accountability and consumer protection.

The bill, as introduced in the Hawaii Senate, is reported to establish requirements applicable to developers and deployers of artificial intelligence systems, with particular focus on high-risk AI applications. The legislation draws on approaches seen in comparable state-level AI bills, including provisions addressing algorithmic transparency, impact assessments, and disclosures to individuals subject to consequential automated decisions. The bill applies to entities meeting defined thresholds of activity or scale, and sets out obligations concerning the documentation and testing of AI systems prior to deployment.

SB 2967 further addresses consumer rights in relation to AI-generated outputs and automated decision-making, including provisions that may require notice to individuals when AI systems are used in contexts affecting employment, housing, credit, or access to services. The Legislature considered the bill in the context of Hawaii's existing consumer protection and privacy statutes, and the bill's provisions are structured to interact with those existing frameworks.

The bill was referred to relevant Senate committees for consideration, including committees with jurisdiction over commerce, consumer protection, and technology. As of the information available, the bill remained subject to committee review and had not been enacted into law. No enrolled act number or effective date has been confirmed. The exact text, section references, and final scope of obligations remain subject to the legislative process and any amendments adopted in committee or on the floor.

rows.title: California AB 1018: Safe and Secure Innovation for Frontier Artificial Intelligence Models Act introduced in California Legislature
rows.summary:
California Assembly Bill 1018, the Safe and Secure Innovation for Frontier Artificial Intelligence Models Act, is a proposed state-level statute before the California Legislature that would impose safety testing, auditing, and transparency obligations on developers of large-scale artificial intelligence models operating in or affecting California.

On 20 February 2025, Assembly Member Bauer-Kahan introduced AB 1018 in the California State Assembly, referring it to the Assembly Committees on Privacy and Consumer Protection and on Judiciary. The bill targets developers of "frontier AI models", defined by reference to computational thresholds for training, and would require those developers to conduct pre-deployment safety evaluations, implement kill-switch capabilities, and publish safety documentation before making covered models available to California users or customers.

The bill provides that covered developers must complete a written safety and security protocol prior to initial training of any covered model, and must retain those protocols for a prescribed period. AB 1018 further requires developers to conduct third-party audits and to report material safety incidents to the California Government Operations Agency. Developers that contract with cloud computing providers for the compute used to train covered models would be required to include contractual provisions ensuring compliance with the Act's obligations. The bill also establishes a private right of action, permitting the California Attorney General and, in certain circumstances, individuals to bring civil enforcement actions against non-compliant developers, with civil penalties denominated in USD.

The bill sets a training-compute threshold of 10^26 floating-point operations (FLOPs) as the principal trigger for coverage, aligning the definitional approach with federal AI risk frameworks and prior California AI proposals, including the vetoed SB 1047 of the 2023-2024 session. AB 1018 assigns oversight responsibilities to the California Government Operations Agency and directs that agency to adopt implementing regulations. The bill was reported out of the Assembly Committee on Privacy and Consumer Protection in April 2025 and remained under active legislative consideration as of May 2025.

rows.title: Ecuador Organic Law for Digital and Audiovisual Transformation (LOTDA): Overview of Key Provisions
rows.summary:
Ecuador's National Assembly enacted the Organic Law for Digital and Audiovisual Transformation (Ley Orgánica para la Transformación Digital y Audiovisual, LOTDA), a cross-sector instrument governing digital services, audiovisual media regulation, data-related obligations and electronic commerce within the jurisdiction of Ecuador.

On 23 November 2023, the National Assembly of Ecuador approved the LOTDA, which was subsequently published in the Official Gazette (Registro Oficial) and entered into force in accordance with the promulgation procedures established under Ecuadorian constitutional law. The instrument consolidates and reforms prior frameworks applicable to telecommunications, audiovisual content, electronic signatures and digital platforms.

The Law establishes obligations for digital platform operators providing services to users located in Ecuador, including requirements relating to the registration of such platforms with the competent regulatory authority, the Agencia de Regulación y Control de las Telecomunicaciones (ARCOTEL). The LOTDA provides that audiovisual media services delivered over the internet, including streaming and video-on-demand platforms, are subject to content regulation requirements, including minimum quotas for Ecuadorian and Ibero-American audiovisual production. The legislators determined that platforms meeting specified user or revenue thresholds are subject to a contribution mechanism directed towards the financing of national audiovisual content production, administered through the Consejo de Regulación, Desarrollo y Promoción de la Información y Comunicación (CORDICOM).

The Law further addresses electronic commerce and digital consumer protection, setting out provisions on transparency of algorithmic recommendation systems, terms and conditions disclosure obligations, and the rights of consumers engaging in transactions conducted through digital intermediary platforms. The LOTDA amends provisions of the Organic Law of Consumer Defence and related instruments to align the domestic framework with digital commerce practices. Additionally, the instrument contains provisions on cybersecurity obligations applicable to operators of critical digital infrastructure, coordinated under the Esquema Nacional de Seguridad (ENS) framework referenced within the Law. Penalties for non-compliance are established in graduated tiers, with sanctions expressed in Remuneraciones Básicas Unificadas (RBU); at the 2024 RBU value of USD 460 (approximately EUR 425), maximum administrative fines reach into the tens of thousands of United States dollars depending on the category of infringement.

rows.title: California Business and Professions Code Section 22757.2: Children's Data Privacy Obligations for Connected Toy Operators
rows.summary:
Section 22757.2 of the California Business and Professions Code imposes specific data privacy obligations on operators of internet-connected toys and devices directed at children, forming part of California's broader Children's Online Privacy Protection framework under the California Consumer Privacy Act and related statutes, with application across the State of California, USA.

Section 22757.2 sits within Division 8, Chapter 22 of the California Business and Professions Code, which addresses privacy protections for minors in digital and connected environments. The provision imposes requirements on operators of internet-connected toys, games, and devices that are directed to children or that knowingly collect personal information from children. The statute defines the scope of covered operators and specifies the categories of personal information subject to its restrictions, including geolocation data, voice recordings, and other data collected through the functionality of connected devices.

The Code provides that operators subject to Section 22757.2 are prohibited from knowingly engaging in certain data practices with respect to children's personal information collected through covered devices. Specifically, the statute restricts the use, disclosure, and retention of such information beyond what is reasonably necessary to provide the requested service or activity. Operators are further prohibited from compiling, disclosing, or retaining a minor's personal information for purposes of targeted advertising or for any purpose not consistent with the context in which the information was collected. The provision also imposes an obligation to implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the personal information collected.

Section 22757.2 operates in conjunction with other provisions of the California Business and Professions Code addressing minors' privacy, including Section 22580 and the broader framework established by the California Age-Appropriate Design Code Act (AB 2273, 2022). Enforcement authority rests with the California Attorney General. Violations of the chapter are subject to civil penalties and other remedies available under California law, with individual violations potentially attracting penalties per incident as specified in the broader enforcement provisions of the Code.

rows.title: United States Code Title 17 Section 107: Federal Fair Use Doctrine for Copyright
rows.summary:
Title 17 of the United States Code, Section 107, codifies the doctrine of fair use as a statutory limitation on the exclusive rights of copyright holders under federal law in the United States.

On 19 October 1976, the United States Congress enacted the Copyright Act of 1976 (Public Law 94-553), which came into force on 1 January 1978. Section 107 of that Act, now codified at 17 U.S.C. § 107, established fair use as an express statutory defence to copyright infringement for the first time in United States federal law, drawing on and codifying prior common law development of the doctrine.

The provision states that the fair use of a copyrighted work, including reproduction in copies or phonorecords or by any other means specified in that section, for purposes such as criticism, comment, news reporting, teaching, scholarship, or research, is not an infringement of copyright. Section 107 enumerates four factors that a court must weigh in determining whether a particular use qualifies as fair use: first, the purpose and character of the use, including whether it is of a commercial nature or is for non-profit educational purposes; second, the nature of the copyrighted work; third, the amount and substantiality of the portion used in relation to the copyrighted work as a whole; and fourth, the effect of the use upon the potential market for or value of the copyrighted work. The statute provides that the fact that a work is unpublished shall not itself bar a finding of fair use if the four-factor analysis supports such a finding.

The four-factor test is applied by courts on a case-by-case basis, with no single factor determinative. The United States Supreme Court has addressed the doctrine in a series of rulings, including Campbell v. Acuff-Rose Music, Inc., 510 U.S. 569 (1994), in which the Court confirmed that all four statutory factors must be explored and weighed together, and that commercial purpose does not automatically preclude a finding of fair use. The doctrine has acquired particular relevance in disputes involving text and data mining, artificial intelligence training datasets, and digital reproduction, with multiple federal court proceedings as of 2024 and 2025 examining whether the ingestion of copyrighted works to train large language models falls within the scope of 17 U.S.C. § 107.
