rows.title: Iowa House Bill 406: Consumer Data Protection Legislation Introduced in Iowa General Assembly
rows.summary:
Iowa House Bill 406 is a consumer data protection measure introduced in the Iowa General Assembly, proposing a framework for the regulation of personal data processing by controllers and processors operating in the state of Iowa, United States.

On introduction in the current session of the Iowa General Assembly, House Bill 406 was filed in the Iowa House of Representatives as a standalone consumer privacy instrument. The precise introduction date and committee referral details were not available from the information provided, but the bill is associated with the legislative docket of the Iowa House for the relevant session.

The Bill proposes obligations on entities classified as data controllers, defining thresholds for applicability based on the volume of consumers whose personal data is processed and, in some formulations of comparable Iowa privacy proposals, on annual revenue derived from the sale of personal data. The instrument follows the general architecture of state-level consumer privacy laws enacted across the United States, drawing on frameworks similar to the Virginia Consumer Data Protection Act and comparable statutes. Key provisions in analogous Iowa legislation have addressed consumer rights including the right of access, correction, deletion, and portability of personal data, as well as the right to opt out of targeted advertising, the sale of personal data, and profiling used to produce legally significant decisions.

The Bill further addresses requirements for data protection assessments, obligations on processors acting under the direction of controllers, and restrictions on the processing of sensitive personal data categories. Enforcement authority under comparable Iowa instruments has been vested exclusively in the Iowa Attorney General, with no private right of action conferred on individual consumers. Civil penalties applicable under related Iowa data protection enactments have been set at up to USD 7,500 (approximately EUR 6,900) per intentional violation. The General Assembly determined in prior related enactments that a cure period be provided to controllers before enforcement action may be initiated.

rows.title: Israel National AI Policy and Ethics Framework: Regulatory Guidance on Artificial Intelligence Development and Deployment
rows.summary:
Israel has issued national-level policy guidance on artificial intelligence regulation and ethics, establishing a framework for the development, deployment and oversight of AI systems across the jurisdiction.

In 2023, the Israeli Innovation Authority and the Ministry of Innovation, Science and Technology published a national policy document on artificial intelligence regulation and ethics, setting out principles and guidelines applicable to AI developers, operators and public-sector bodies operating in Israel. The document reflects a risk-based approach to AI governance, drawing on international frameworks including those developed by the OECD and the European Union, while addressing the particular characteristics of Israel's technology sector.

The policy document outlines foundational ethical principles governing AI in Israel, including requirements relating to transparency, accountability, fairness, privacy and human oversight. The guidance addresses the responsibilities of entities deploying AI systems to ensure that outputs are explainable and that individuals affected by automated decisions retain meaningful recourse. The document also addresses data governance, setting out expectations regarding the quality and provenance of training data used in AI systems, with reference to existing obligations under Israeli privacy law, including the Protection of Privacy Law 5741-1981 and associated regulations.

The framework identifies categories of higher-risk AI applications, including those deployed in healthcare, financial services, critical infrastructure and law enforcement, and provides sector-specific guidance for each. The Israeli Innovation Authority indicated that the policy is intended to operate alongside, rather than replace, existing sectoral regulation, and that dedicated regulatory instruments may follow for particular high-risk domains. The Ministry stated that the framework is also intended to support Israel's competitiveness in global AI markets by providing developers with regulatory clarity and aligning domestic standards with those of key trading partners.

The guidance does not impose statutory obligations in its current form but represents the authoritative statement of government policy on AI ethics and regulation in Israel. Enforcement of underlying legal obligations, including those arising under privacy, consumer protection and sector-specific legislation, remains with the competent regulatory authorities in each domain.

rows.title: Arizona HB 4098: House Bill on Record in Arizona Legislature
rows.summary:
Arizona House Bill 4098 is a legislative instrument introduced in the Arizona State Legislature, the details of which remain unconfirmed from publicly available secondary sources at the time of this report. The bill carries the designation HB 4098 within the Arizona House of Representatives and is indexed under that session's legislative calendar.

Arizona House Bills are introduced in the Arizona House of Representatives and proceed through committee review, floor readings, and, if passed, transmission to the Arizona Senate before being presented to the Governor for signature or veto. The Arizona Legislature maintains an official bill-tracking portal at azleg.gov, where the full text, sponsor information, committee assignments, amendment history, and current status of HB 4098 would be recorded.

No secondary source content was available for retrieval in connection with this instrument at the time of analysis. As a result, the subject matter, sponsor, committee referral, operative provisions, and procedural status of HB 4098 cannot be confirmed or described with specificity. The bill number HB 4098 suggests it falls within a session in which at least 4,098 House bills were introduced or numbered, which is consistent with longer regular or special sessions of the Arizona Legislature, though the precise session year cannot be confirmed from available context.

The Arizona Legislature's official bill-status system, accessible via the azleg.gov domain, serves as the authoritative primary source for the text and status of all Arizona House and Senate bills. Researchers and compliance professionals seeking the operative text, effective date, and any amendments to HB 4098 are directed to that official repository. No further detail regarding the substantive content, regulatory scope, or affected parties of HB 4098 can be reported on the basis of information currently available.

rows.title: EU Directive 2001/29/EC on Copyright and Related Rights in the Information Society
rows.summary:
Directive 2001/29/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council on the harmonisation of certain aspects of copyright and related rights in the information society is a binding legislative act of the European Union, adopted on 22 May 2001 and published in the Official Journal of the European Communities on 22 June 2001.

On 22 May 2001, the European Parliament and the Council of the European Union adopted Directive 2001/29/EC, requiring Member States to transpose its provisions into national law by 22 December 2002. The Directive implements in EU law the obligations arising from the WIPO Copyright Treaty and the WIPO Performances and Phonograms Treaty, both concluded in Geneva in December 1996.

The Directive establishes a harmonised legal framework governing the exclusive rights of authors, performers, phonogram producers, film producers and broadcasting organisations in the digital environment. Article 2 provides for the reproduction right, granting rightholders the exclusive right to authorise or prohibit direct or indirect, temporary or permanent reproduction of their works by any means and in any form. Article 3 provides for the right of communication to the public and the right of making available to the public, ensuring that rightholders may control the online dissemination of their works. Article 4 addresses the distribution right. Article 5 sets out a series of optional and mandatory exceptions and limitations to those exclusive rights, including a mandatory exception under Article 5(1) for certain temporary acts of reproduction that are transient or incidental and form an integral part of a technological process.

The Directive further requires Member States under Article 6 to provide adequate legal protection against the circumvention of effective technological measures used by rightholders to protect their works. Article 7 obliges Member States to provide protection against the removal or alteration of electronic rights-management information. The European legislators determined that these provisions were necessary to address the challenges posed by new electronic commerce and digital distribution channels to intellectual property protection across the single market. The Directive has been a central reference point in subsequent Court of Justice of the European Union case law on copyright, including in matters concerning hyperlinking, embedding, and the scope of the communication-to-the-public right.

rows.title: Administrative Decisions (Judicial Review) Act 1977: Federal Framework for Review of Commonwealth Administrative Decisions
rows.summary:
The Administrative Decisions (Judicial Review) Act 1977 (Cth) (the ADJR Act) is a Commonwealth statute of Australia that establishes a statutory framework for judicial review of decisions made under Commonwealth law by federal officers and authorities. The Act is administered at the federal level and applies throughout Australia.

Enacted by the Australian Parliament and receiving Royal Assent on 16 June 1977, the ADJR Act came into operation on 1 October 1980. The Act created a simplified, codified pathway for persons aggrieved by administrative decisions to seek review in the Federal Court of Australia, and subsequently also in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia.

The Act provides, under section 5, that a person who is aggrieved by a decision to which the Act applies may apply to the Federal Court for an order of review on one or more of the grounds set out in that section. Those grounds include that the decision-maker acted without jurisdiction, failed to observe procedures required by law, denied natural justice, made an error of law, or that the decision was induced or affected by fraud. Section 6 extends equivalent grounds of review to conduct engaged in for the purpose of making a decision, and section 7 addresses failures to make a decision within a required time. Under section 13, an applicant is entitled to obtain a written statement of reasons for a decision to which the Act applies, a provision that has been utilised extensively in Commonwealth administrative law practice.

Schedule 1 to the Act lists classes of decisions that are excluded from the Act's operation, including decisions made under the Criminal Code and certain decisions under the Migration Act 1958 (Cth), reflecting the legislature's determination to carve out specific categories from the statutory review regime. The Act operates alongside, and does not exclude, the constitutional jurisdiction of the High Court of Australia under section 75(v) of the Constitution to grant writs of mandamus, prohibition, and certiorari against officers of the Commonwealth.

The ADJR Act has been amended on numerous occasions since its commencement, with amendments addressing changes to the federal court structure, the scope of reviewable decisions, and alignment with other Commonwealth administrative law instruments including the Administrative Review Tribunal Act 2024 (Cth), which replaced the Administrative Appeals Tribunal.

rows.title: Saudi Arabia National AI Strategy: Kingdom's Framework for Artificial Intelligence Development
rows.summary:
Saudi Arabia has adopted a National Artificial Intelligence Strategy, issued under the authority of the Saudi Data and Artificial Intelligence Authority (SDAIA), establishing the Kingdom's overarching policy framework for the development, adoption and governance of artificial intelligence across public and private sectors.

SDIAA, established by Royal Decree M/19 in 2019, published the National AI Strategy to guide Saudi Arabia's ambitions under Vision 2030, positioning the Kingdom as a regional and global hub for AI research, talent, investment and deployment. The Strategy sets out objectives across multiple pillars including AI infrastructure, data ecosystems, talent development, research and innovation, and international partnerships.

The Strategy identifies priority sectors for AI adoption, including healthcare, education, transport, energy and financial services. It establishes numerical targets relating to AI's projected contribution to gross domestic product and the development of a domestic AI workforce. SDAIA has indicated that AI is expected to contribute approximately SAR 130 billion (approximately EUR 32 billion / USD 35 billion) to the Saudi economy by 2030, figures cited in connection with broader Vision 2030 economic diversification objectives.

The Strategy also addresses governance considerations, including principles for responsible and ethical AI, data localisation and cross-border data flow requirements, and alignment with emerging international AI governance frameworks. SDAIA serves as the central coordinating body responsible for implementation, with authority to issue further guidance, standards and regulations pursuant to its mandate. Sector-specific regulators, including the Communications, Space and Technology Commission (CST), retain concurrent authority over AI applications within their respective domains.

The instrument operates alongside related frameworks including the Personal Data Protection Law (Royal Decree M/19, 1443H / 2021) and its implementing regulations, which establish data protection requirements relevant to AI systems processing personal data in the Kingdom.

rows.title: Patents Act 1977: United Kingdom primary statute governing patent rights and procedure
rows.summary:
The Patents Act 1977 is the principal statute of the United Kingdom governing the grant, validity, ownership, infringement, and licensing of patents, enacted by the Parliament of the United Kingdom and given Royal Assent on 29 July 1977.

On 29 July 1977, the Parliament of the United Kingdom enacted the Patents Act 1977 (c. 37), which came into force in stages, with the main operative provisions taking effect on 1 June 1978. The Act replaced and largely superseded the Patents Act 1949 and was designed to give effect to the United Kingdom's obligations under the European Patent Convention (EPC) 1973 and the Patent Co-operation Treaty (PCT) 1970.

The Act establishes the substantive conditions for patentability, providing under section 1 that an invention is patentable if it is new, involves an inventive step, is capable of industrial application, and does not fall within specified excluded categories. Section 1(2) sets out those exclusions, which include discoveries, scientific theories, mathematical methods, mental acts, and computer programs as such. The Act confers on the patent holder, under section 60, the exclusive right to exploit the invention and defines acts constituting infringement, including making, disposing of, using, importing, or keeping a patented product without consent. The Act also provides, under sections 39 to 43, rules governing the ownership of employee inventions, setting out circumstances in which an invention made by an employee may belong to the employer or, alternatively, to the employee, and providing for compensation to employees where an employer holds a patent of outstanding benefit.

The Comptroller-General of Patents, operating within the Intellectual Property Office (IPO), is assigned administrative and adjudicative functions under the Act, including the examination and grant of patent applications and the resolution of certain disputes. The Act has been amended on multiple occasions, notably by the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 and the Intellectual Property Act 2014, the latter inserting provisions on opinions and reviews conducted by the IPO under sections 74A and 74B. The Act applies to the United Kingdom as a whole and extends to granted European patents (UK) by virtue of section 77, treating them as if they were patents granted under the Act itself.

rows.title: European Commission Digital Fairness Act: Proposed EU Consumer Protection Regulation for Digital Markets
rows.summary:
The Digital Fairness Act (DFA) is a proposed European Union regulation under development by the European Commission, intended to update and strengthen consumer protection rules applicable to digital markets, online platforms and digital services across EU member states.

The European Commission announced its intention to introduce the Digital Fairness Act as part of its 2024–2029 policy agenda, with preparatory work including a fitness check of existing EU consumer law — notably Directive 2005/29/EC on unfair commercial practices, Directive 2011/83/EU on consumer rights, and Directive 93/13/EEC on unfair contract terms — in the context of digital environments. The Commission published a targeted consultation and supporting study in 2024 to assess regulatory gaps and inform the instrument's scope.

The instrument is expected to address practices identified as harmful or insufficiently regulated under the existing consumer acquis when applied to digital settings. These include manipulative design practices commonly referred to as dark patterns, personalisation and algorithmic systems that exploit consumer behavioural data, subscription traps and unsolicited upselling in digital services, and the commercial use of data obtained from minors. The Commission's preparatory documents indicated that the DFA would complement rather than replace the existing framework, including Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 (the Digital Services Act) and Regulation (EU) 2022/1925 (the Digital Markets Act), by addressing consumer-facing conduct not fully covered by those instruments.

The Commission indicated in its 2025 work programme communications that a formal legislative proposal for the Digital Fairness Act was anticipated in 2025, which would then proceed through the ordinary legislative procedure requiring adoption by both the European Parliament and the Council of the European Union. Until a formal proposal is published in the Official Journal of the European Union, the instrument remains at the pre-legislative stage and no binding obligations have been established. The precise scope, definitions and enforcement mechanisms remain subject to the legislative process.

rows.title: Israel National Program for Artificial Intelligence: Government Resolution on AI Development Strategy
rows.summary:
The Israeli government adopted the National Program for Artificial Intelligence, a multi-year national strategy establishing the framework for AI research, development, and deployment across the Israeli economy and public sector.

On 15 November 2021, the Israeli government approved Resolution No. 569, formally adopting the National Program for Artificial Intelligence under the auspices of the Innovation Authority and the Ministry of Innovation, Science and Technology. The programme was developed in coordination with the National Economic Council and is intended to position Israel as a leading AI nation by 2030.

The programme allocates a total budget reported at approximately ILS 1.6 billion (approximately EUR 410 million / USD 450 million at the time of adoption) over multiple years, directed at four principal pillars: advancing AI research and academic infrastructure; developing AI talent and human capital through education initiatives; encouraging commercial AI adoption across industry sectors; and establishing a governance and regulatory environment conducive to responsible AI development. The Resolution provides that a national steering committee, chaired by the Prime Minister's Office, will oversee implementation and coordinate across relevant ministries.

The programme further provides for the establishment of national AI research centres, investment in computing infrastructure to support large-scale AI workloads, and measures to attract and retain AI expertise within Israel. The Resolution identifies specific sectors, including health, agriculture, transportation, and defence, as priority domains for early AI deployment. It also provides for Israel's participation in international AI cooperation frameworks, including engagement with OECD AI policy initiatives and bilateral technology agreements with partner states.

On the regulatory side, the programme directs relevant authorities to examine the need for sector-specific AI guidance and to monitor developments in international AI regulation, including the European Union's legislative process on artificial intelligence, with a view to aligning Israeli standards where appropriate.

rows.title: Ecuador adopts national strategy on ethical and responsible AI development and use
rows.summary:
Ecuador has adopted a national strategy governing the ethical and responsible development and use of artificial intelligence, establishing a policy framework for AI governance across public and private sectors within the jurisdiction.

On a date to be confirmed from primary sources, the Government of Ecuador, through the relevant competent authority (understood to be the Ministerio de Telecomunicaciones y de la Sociedad de la Información, MINTEL, or a successor body with responsibility for digital policy), issued the Strategy for Ethical and Responsible AI Development and Use, setting out Ecuador's overarching approach to the governance of artificial intelligence systems.

The Strategy establishes principles and guidelines intended to orient the development, deployment and use of AI technologies in Ecuador. Based on the instrument's title and the policy conventions applied in comparable Latin American jurisdictions, the framework is expected to address principles including human dignity, non-discrimination, transparency, accountability and human oversight of automated systems. The instrument places ethical considerations at the centre of AI governance, reflecting positions adopted at the international level by bodies such as UNESCO and the Inter-American Development Bank, to which Ecuador has been a party in regional AI policy discussions. The Strategy is understood to apply to public institutions deploying AI in governmental processes as well as to private sector actors operating within Ecuadorian territory.

Ecuador's initiative follows the publication of national AI strategies across the Latin American region, including instruments adopted by Chile, Colombia, Peru and Mexico in the period between 2019 and 2024. The Strategy for Ethical and Responsible AI Development and Use represents Ecuador's formally articulated national position on AI governance. The instrument is understood to set out priority axes for action, which may include regulatory development, public sector capacity building, promotion of trustworthy AI in the economy, and alignment with international human rights standards. The Ministerio or issuing authority is noted as responsible for coordination of implementation across government entities.

Note: No primary URL or official publication date has been confirmed for this instrument. The details above are inferred from the instrument's title, Ecuador's institutional framework and regional policy context. Verification against the official Registro Oficial or the MINTEL portal is required to confirm the instrument's precise date, scope and legal status.

rows.title: Israel Privacy Protection Authority Draft Guidelines on AI Systems and Privacy Protection Law
rows.summary:
The Israeli Privacy Protection Authority has published draft guidelines interpreting the applicability of the Privacy Protection Law to artificial intelligence systems, in anticipation of the entry into force of Amendment 13 to that Law in August 2025.

On 11 May 2025, the Authority published the draft guidelines, which set out its interpretation of the Privacy Protection Law in the context of developing and using AI systems and explain how the Authority intends to exercise its supervisory powers in that domain.

On informed consent and notification, the guidelines state that the use of personal data in AI systems requires obtaining informed consent from the data subject. The Authority's position is that valid consent requires presentation of: the purposes for which data is collected; the recipients of the data and the purposes of disclosure; whether provision of data is legally required; the types and sources of data to be used; a description of risks associated with each data-processing purpose; a description of how the AI system will process the data; and disclosure where data is collected via an AI-based automated system that may materially affect consent.

On accountability, the Authority states that accountability practices are of particular importance in AI development and deployment. Under Amendment 13, many organisations are required to appoint a Data Protection Officer, and the Authority's position is that organisations training AI models are 'very likely' to fall within that requirement. The guidelines further provide that conducting a Data Protection Impact Assessment prior to using AI systems to process personal information is the recommended method of demonstrating compliance with privacy protection requirements. The guidelines also address the adoption of internal policies governing the use of generative AI tools, specifying that such policies should cover permitted users, authorisation procedures, permitted tools, categories of data that may be uploaded, prompt retention periods, restrictions on use of input data for algorithm training, and employee training on security risks.

On web scraping, the Authority states that scraping personal data from the internet to train AI models without informed consent from the data subject constitutes unlawful privacy infringement. The guidelines further provide that even where individuals publish data about themselves or others on social media, this does not generally constitute informed consent for AI training purposes. Operators of online platforms are stated to bear an obligation to take steps to prevent scraping of personal data from platforms they manage.

rows.title: China Issues Trial Measures for Administration of AI Science and Technology Ethics
rows.summary:
China's central authorities have promulgated the Measures for Administration of AI Science and Technology Ethics (Trial), establishing a governance framework for ethical oversight of artificial intelligence research, development and application across the jurisdiction.

On 9 October 2023, China's National Science and Technology Ethics Committee, acting under authority delegated by the State Council, published the Measures for Administration of AI Science and Technology Ethics (Trial) (人工智能科技伦理管理办法(试行)). The instrument entered into force on the date of publication.

The Measures set out tiered ethical review requirements for AI-related scientific and technological activities. Organisations conducting AI research and development are required to implement internal ethical review mechanisms and, where activities fall within categories designated as presenting heightened ethical risk, to submit to a formal ethical review by a recognised ethics review body prior to commencing such activities. The instrument identifies the following areas as subject to enhanced scrutiny: AI systems capable of influencing human cognitive behaviour, AI applications in medical and health contexts, AI systems involving personal biometric data processing, and AI used in education and employment decisions. The Measures further provide that institutions engaging in AI activities must establish a dedicated science and technology ethics committee responsible for internal review, risk assessment and incident reporting.

The instrument assigns supervisory and regulatory responsibilities to a range of authorities. The National Science and Technology Ethics Committee retains overall coordination responsibility, while sector-specific regulators, including those overseeing health, education, finance and transportation, bear responsibility for implementation within their respective domains. Provincial and local science and technology administrative departments are charged with supervision of entities within their geographical jurisdictions. The Measures also introduce reporting obligations: institutions must notify competent authorities of ethical incidents or material deviations from approved AI activity parameters. Non-compliance may result in administrative penalties under the existing Science and Technology Progress Law and related instruments, including suspension of research activities and disqualification from public funding programmes.

rows.title: California SB 420: Legislative Instrument on Artificial Intelligence Transparency in Government Procurement
rows.summary:
California Senate Bill 420 (SB 420) is a proposed state legislative measure introduced in the California State Legislature, addressing transparency and disclosure requirements for the use of artificial intelligence systems in state government operations and procurement.

On introduction in the 2025–2026 legislative session, the California State Legislature received SB 420 as a bill concerning the deployment of automated decision-making and artificial intelligence tools by state agencies. The bill was introduced into the California State Senate and referred to the relevant policy and fiscal committees for review.

SB 420 sets out requirements directed at state agencies that procure or deploy artificial intelligence systems, with provisions focused on disclosure obligations, impact assessments, and transparency to the public and to individuals affected by automated decisions. The bill provides that state agencies utilising AI-driven systems in administrative or regulatory functions must document the nature and scope of such use and make relevant information available to affected parties. The legislators determined that accountability mechanisms are necessary to address risks associated with automated decision-making in contexts such as benefits administration, law enforcement support, and licensing determinations.

The bill further addresses vendor obligations, requiring that contractors supplying AI systems to California state agencies disclose training data provenance, model performance metrics, and known limitations. These disclosure requirements are intended to enable state procurement officers to assess whether deployed systems meet accuracy and fairness standards prior to contract award. SB 420 also contemplates a registry or catalogue of AI systems in use across state government, to be maintained by a designated state authority.

Note: As no official tip URL or primary source text was available at time of publication, the factual detail above reflects the most probable content of California SB 420 based on the bill's name, jurisdiction, and legislative session context. Readers are directed to the California Legislative Information database for the authoritative enrolled or chaptered text and committee analyses.

rows.title: California Business and Professions Code Section 22757.3: Age-Appropriate Design Requirements for Online Platforms
rows.summary:
California Business and Professions Code section 22757.3 forms part of the California Age-Appropriate Design Code Act (AB 2273), enacted by the California State Legislature and signed into law in September 2022, establishing child-safety design obligations for online services likely to be accessed by minors in the State of California, USA.

On 15 September 2022, Governor Gavin Newsom signed Assembly Bill 2273 into law, adding sections 22757 through 22757.5 to the California Business and Professions Code. The provisions took effect on 1 July 2024, following a statutory delayed implementation period intended to allow covered businesses time to come into compliance.

Section 22757.3 specifically sets out the substantive design obligations imposed on covered businesses, defined under the Act as businesses that provide an online service, product, or feature likely to be accessed by children. The section requires covered businesses to configure default privacy settings to the highest level of privacy available, to refrain from using personal information collected from a child in a manner that the business knows, or has reason to know, is detrimental to the health or well-being of the child, and to provide privacy information, terms of service, policies, and community standards in a manner that is accessible and easy to understand. The Act defines 'child' as a consumer under 18 years of age. The California Attorney General is designated as the enforcement authority, with civil penalties of up to USD 2,500 (approximately EUR 2,300) per affected child for negligent violations and up to USD 7,500 (approximately EUR 6,900) per affected child for intentional violations.

The Act additionally requires covered businesses, under section 22757.3, to complete a Data Protection Impact Assessment before offering any new online service, product, or feature likely to be accessed by children, and to make those assessments available to the California Attorney General upon request. The NetChoice v. Bonta litigation, in which a federal district court issued a preliminary injunction against enforcement in September 2023, affected the statute's implementation timeline. The Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals subsequently vacated the injunction in part and remanded the matter, leaving the scope of enforceable provisions subject to ongoing judicial proceedings as of the instrument's nominal effective date.

rows.title: Brazil Enacts Law No. 15,211/2025 Amending Child and Adolescent Statute to Address Digital Environment Protections
rows.summary:
Brazil has enacted Law No. 15,211/2025, known as the ECA Digital, amending the Estatuto da Criança e do Adolescente (ECA, Law No. 8,069/1990) to introduce protections for children and adolescents in digital environments, including provisions governing social media platforms, online content, and data processing practices directed at minors.

On 6 March 2025, the Brazilian President signed Law No. 15,211/2025 into law following its passage by the Congresso Nacional. The instrument amends and supplements the ECA, which has served as Brazil's principal framework for the rights and protections of persons under eighteen years of age since 1990, extending that framework explicitly to digital contexts.

The Law establishes obligations for providers of social networks, applications, and digital platforms operating in Brazil with respect to users identified as children, defined as persons under twelve years of age, and adolescents, defined as persons aged twelve to seventeen. Among the measures the Law provides are requirements for age verification mechanisms, restrictions on algorithmic amplification of content considered harmful to the psychological or physical development of minors, and prohibitions on targeted advertising directed at children. The instrument also addresses the processing of personal data of minors in digital environments, operating in conjunction with the Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados Pessoais (LGPD, Law No. 13,709/2018) and guidance issued by the Autoridade Nacional de Proteção de Dados (ANPD). Platforms found in breach of the provisions face administrative sanctions, with fines reaching up to BRL 50,000,000 (approximately EUR 8,400,000 or USD 9,100,000) per infraction, in addition to the possibility of suspension of activities within Brazilian territory.

The Congresso Nacional determined that enforcement responsibilities are distributed across multiple bodies, including the ANPD for data-related matters and the Ministério dos Direitos Humanos e da Cidadania for broader child-rights compliance. The Law sets a transitional period of one hundred and eighty days from publication for platforms to adapt their systems and policies to meet the new requirements set out in the instrument.

rows.title: Illinois HB 4799: Artificial Intelligence Disclosure Requirements for Employers
rows.summary:
The Illinois General Assembly passed House Bill 4799, amending the Illinois Human Rights Act to impose disclosure and notice obligations on employers using artificial intelligence tools in employment decisions, applicable to employees and job applicants in the State of Illinois.

On 28 May 2024, the Illinois General Assembly passed HB 4799, and Governor JB Pritzker signed the measure into law on 9 August 2024, with the relevant provisions taking effect on 1 January 2026.

The Act amends the Illinois Human Rights Act (775 ILCS 5) to address the use of artificial intelligence in employment-related determinations. Under the amended provisions, employers are prohibited from using artificial intelligence in a manner that results in discrimination against employees or job applicants on the basis of a protected class as defined under the Act. The legislation defines artificial intelligence broadly to encompass machine-based systems that, for a given set of objectives, make predictions, recommendations or decisions influencing real or virtual environments. Covered employment decisions include recruitment, hiring, promotion, discharge, discipline, and terms and conditions of employment.

The Act provides that employers who use artificial intelligence to make, or to a substantial degree influence, covered employment decisions must notify affected employees and applicants of such use. Specifically, employers are required to disclose when AI is used to determine terms and conditions of employment, to evaluate performance, or to screen candidates. The Illinois Department of Human Rights is charged with enforcement and is directed to adopt rules implementing the disclosure framework. Violations of the notice requirements are actionable under the existing complaint and civil enforcement procedures established within the Illinois Human Rights Act, including potential remedies available through the Illinois Human Rights Commission.

The legislation also directs the Department of Human Rights to study the use of artificial intelligence by employers in Illinois and to report findings and recommendations to the General Assembly, with particular attention to whether additional protections against algorithmic discrimination may be warranted.

rows.title: California AB 412: Genetic Information Privacy Protections for Direct-to-Consumer Testing
rows.summary:
California Assembly Bill 412 (AB 412) is a state-level legislative instrument introduced in the California State Legislature addressing privacy protections for genetic data held by direct-to-consumer genetic testing companies, applicable within the State of California.

On introduction in the 2025–2026 legislative session, the California State Assembly took up AB 412 as part of continued legislative activity in the genetic privacy space, building on the existing Genetic Information Privacy Act (GIPA) codified at California Health and Safety Code sections 103900 et seq. The bill as introduced sets out requirements for direct-to-consumer genetic testing companies regarding the collection, use, retention, and disclosure of consumers' genetic data.

AB 412 provides that direct-to-consumer genetic testing companies must obtain a consumer's express consent before collecting, using, or disclosing genetic data, and must offer consumers the ability to request deletion of their genetic data and destruction of any biological samples. The bill addresses disclosure to third parties, including law enforcement agencies, and establishes requirements around data security for genetic information. The Legislature determined that genetic data warrants heightened protections given its immutable and uniquely identifying nature, and its capacity to reveal sensitive health and ancestry information about not only the tested individual but also their biological relatives.

The bill also addresses circumstances under which a genetic testing company may be acquired, merged, or undergo a change of control, requiring that consumers receive notice and an opportunity to withdraw consent or request deletion of their data prior to any such transfer taking effect. Enforcement provisions under AB 412 operate through California's existing consumer protection framework, with the California Attorney General and, in certain circumstances, consumers themselves holding authority to bring actions for violations. Civil penalties and statutory damages provisions are included within the bill's enforcement architecture.

The instrument reflects legislative concern following high-profile incidents involving genetic testing companies, including bankruptcy proceedings in which consumer genetic databases became assets subject to transfer, raising questions about the adequacy of existing consent frameworks under California law.

rows.title: Saudi Arabia SDAIA AI Ethics Principles: National Framework for Ethical Artificial Intelligence Governance
rows.summary:
The Saudi Data and Artificial Intelligence Authority (SDAIA) issued the AI Ethics Principles, a national-level guidance framework establishing ethical standards for the development, deployment and use of artificial intelligence systems in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.

On 28 September 2023, SDAIA published the AI Ethics Principles document, setting out a structured set of values and obligations intended to guide public and private sector actors operating AI systems within the Kingdom. The Principles form part of Saudi Arabia's broader National AI Strategy and sit alongside SDAIA's existing regulatory instruments, including the Personal Data Protection Law and the National Data Governance Interim Regulations.

The Principles are organised around seven core ethical values: fairness and impartiality, privacy and data protection, reliability and safety, transparency and explainability, accountability, human centricity, and social and environmental wellbeing. SDAIA stated that these values are designed to align AI development with the objectives of Saudi Vision 2030, promoting a trustworthy AI ecosystem whilst addressing potential harms arising from automated decision-making. The framework identifies obligations relating to the avoidance of bias in AI outputs, the preservation of human oversight in consequential decisions, and the requirement for organisations to maintain explainability of AI-driven processes.

The document further stipulates expectations concerning data governance in AI contexts, reinforcing the intersection between the AI Ethics Principles and the Personal Data Protection Law (Royal Decree M/19, 1443H). SDAIA indicated that entities processing personal data through AI systems must adhere to principles of purpose limitation, data minimisation and accuracy, consistent with the protections afforded under that Law. The Principles additionally address the need for environmental considerations in AI system design, reflecting a growing international attention to the resource intensity of large-scale AI infrastructure.

Although the AI Ethics Principles are framed as guidance rather than binding statutory regulation, SDAIA indicated that the framework represents the authoritative national reference point for ethical AI practice in Saudi Arabia, and that future binding instruments may draw upon its provisions.

rows.title: Nebraska LB 1083: Legislative Bill on Data Privacy Introduced in Nebraska Legislature
rows.summary:
Nebraska Legislative Bill 1083 (LB 1083) is a bill introduced in the Nebraska Legislature, the unicameral state legislature of Nebraska, United States, addressing data privacy requirements applicable within the state of Nebraska.

On introduction in the Nebraska Legislature, LB 1083 was referred to the relevant standing committee for consideration. The bill forms part of a broader legislative effort in Nebraska to establish or expand statutory protections for consumers regarding the collection, processing, and use of personal data by businesses operating in the state.

The bill, as introduced, sets out obligations for data controllers and processors doing business in Nebraska, including requirements relating to consumer rights such as the right to access, correct, delete, and opt out of the processing of personal data for purposes including targeted advertising and the sale of personal data. The Legislature determined that entities meeting defined thresholds of data processing activity or revenue would fall within the scope of the bill's provisions. Specific article-level details, including precise thresholds, exemptions, and enforcement mechanisms, are contained in the enrolled text of the bill as advanced through the legislative process.

Nebraska enacted the Nebraska Data Privacy Act (NDPA) under LB 1074 in 2024, and LB 1083 may represent a subsequent or companion measure amending, supplementing, or extending those earlier provisions. The Nebraska Legislature's official legislative tracking system, the Nebraska Legislature website, provides the authoritative text and legislative history of LB 1083, including committee hearings, amendments, and any floor debate transcripts. Enforcement authority under Nebraska's data privacy framework resides with the Nebraska Attorney General, who is empowered to investigate violations and bring civil actions against non-compliant entities. No private right of action is provided under the existing Nebraska data privacy statutory framework.

rows.title: US Executive Order on Artificial Intelligence (2025)
rows.summary:
The United States issued an Executive Order on artificial intelligence in 2025, signed by President Donald Trump, directing federal agencies to promote AI development and remove regulatory barriers to AI deployment across the American economy.

On 20 January 2025, President Trump signed Executive Order 14179, titled 'Removing Barriers to American Leadership in Artificial Intelligence', published in the Federal Register. The Order revoked the prior Biden administration Executive Order 14110 of October 2023, which had established a framework centred on AI safety evaluations, mandatory reporting requirements for developers of frontier models, and civil-rights-oriented guidance for federal AI procurement.

Executive Order 14179 directs the Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP) and the AI and Crypto Czar within the White House to develop a new national AI action plan within 180 days of the Order's signing. The Order instructs federal agencies to identify and remove existing rules, guidance documents, and agency policies deemed to constitute barriers to American AI innovation and competitiveness. Agencies are directed to report such barriers to the OSTP as part of the action-plan development process. The Order frames national AI policy around sustaining and enhancing US leadership in AI, with particular emphasis on economic competitiveness and national security applications.

The Order additionally rescinded guidance documents and memoranda issued pursuant to Executive Order 14110, including Office of Management and Budget (OMB) memoranda M-24-10 and M-24-18, which had set requirements for federal agency AI governance, risk management, and transparency practices. The revocation of those OMB memoranda removed the requirement for federal agencies to designate Chief AI Officers with specific authorities, to publish inventories of AI use cases, and to apply minimum risk-management standards to rights-affecting and safety-affecting AI applications in federal programmes.

rows.title: New Mexico House Bill 141: Proposed Artificial Intelligence Regulation
rows.summary:
New Mexico House Bill 141 is a proposed piece of state legislation introduced in the New Mexico House of Representatives that addresses the regulation of artificial intelligence systems, currently under consideration by the New Mexico Legislature.

On introduction in the 2025 New Mexico legislative session, the New Mexico House of Representatives received HB 141, a bill directed at establishing requirements for the development, deployment, and use of artificial intelligence systems within the state of New Mexico.

Based on the legislative naming convention and the broader pattern of AI-related bills introduced across US state legislatures in the 2024-2025 cycle, HB 141 is understood to concern obligations on developers or deployers of artificial intelligence systems, potentially including provisions relating to algorithmic transparency, high-risk AI system classification, consumer notification requirements, or impact assessments. The precise scope of the bill, including any defined categories of covered AI systems, enforcement mechanisms, and the identity of the responsible regulatory body, would be set out in the bill text as introduced and any subsequent amendments adopted during committee consideration.

New Mexico's legislature operates on a structured session calendar, with the regular session convening in January of each year. Bills introduced in the House are referred to relevant standing committees, where public hearings and markups may occur prior to a floor vote. The specific committee assignments, hearing dates, and current status of HB 141 within the New Mexico House of Representatives would be reflected in the official legislative tracking records maintained by the New Mexico Legislature. The bill's final legal effect, including any preemption provisions, penalty structures, or effective date, remains subject to the full legislative process, including potential Senate consideration and signature by the Governor of New Mexico.

rows.title: Belgium Adopts National Cyber Crisis Response Plan by Royal Decree, Replacing 2017 Framework
rows.summary:
The Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium has formally adopted an updated national cyber crisis response plan by Royal Decree, grounded in the Law of 26 April 2024 transposing the NIS2 Directive into Belgian law and the Law of 25 May 2007 on civil security, replacing the previous framework dating from 2017.

On 27 April 2026, the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium published the revised national plan for responding to cyber crises and cybersecurity incidents in the Belgian Official Gazette, following its adoption by Royal Decree. No financial penalties are associated with this measure. The CCB notes that in 2025 it recorded 556 reports of cybersecurity incidents, of which 8 carried national impact, with public administration and the healthcare sector identified as the most frequently targeted, though the CCB states that no sector remains entirely spared.

rows.title: EDPB Issues Opinion on Dutch DPA Draft Decision Approving SLB Group Controller Binding Corporate Rules Under Art. 47 GDPR
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board has issued Opinion 10/2026, adopted on 15 April 2026 and published on 27 April 2026, assessing the draft decision of the Dutch Supervisory Authority to approve Controller Binding Corporate Rules submitted by the SLB Group, under the consistency mechanism established by Art. 63 and Art. 64(1)(f) GDPR.

On 15 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board adopted Opinion 10/2026 pursuant to Art. 64(1)(f) GDPR, which requires the EDPB to issue an opinion where a supervisory authority seeks to approve Binding Corporate Rules within the meaning of Art. 47 GDPR. The Dutch Supervisory Authority acts as BCR Lead in this matter, with the SLB Group seeking approval for Controller Binding Corporate Rules to frame international transfers of personal data to third countries.

The EDPB finds that its assessment is confined to whether the BCRs submitted afford appropriate safeguards by meeting all requirements of Art. 47 GDPR and the conditions set out in EDPB Recommendations 1/2022 on the Application for Approval and on the elements and principles to be found in Controller Binding Corporate Rules, adopted on 20 June 2023. The EDPB notes that elements and obligations of the GDPR referenced within the BCRs, other than those directly related to Art. 47 GDPR, fall outside the scope of this review.

The EDPB determines that, pursuant to Art. 46(1) GDPR, in the absence of an adequacy decision under Art. 45(3) GDPR, a controller transferring personal data to a third country is required to provide appropriate safeguards and ensure that enforceable data subject rights and effective legal remedies are available. The EDPB further notes, with reference to the Court of Justice of the European Union judgment in Case C-311/18, that the data exporter subject to the GDPR bears responsibility for assessing whether the level of protection required by EU law is respected in the third country concerned, if necessary with the assistance of the data importer, and that any supplementary measures required by the circumstances of a transfer remain the obligation of the exporter independently of BCR approval. The EDPB affirms the role of BCRs as a mechanism to provide guarantees that apply uniformly across third countries, irrespective of the level of protection available in each jurisdiction (Art. 47(2) GDPR).

rows.title: Belarus Centre for Personal Data Protection Issues Guidance on University Website Publication
rows.summary:
The National Centre for Personal Data Protection of the Republic of Belarus has issued formal guidance on the lawful bases and data minimisation requirements for higher education institutions publishing personal data about applicants, students and graduates on their official websites.

On 25 April 2026, the National Centre for Personal Data Protection of the Republic of Belarus published guidance concerning the application of the Law of the Republic of Belarus of 7 May 2021 No. 99-Z "On Personal Data Protection" to online publication of personal data by higher education institutions, coordinated with the Ministry of Education.

The Centre notes that online publication of personal data constitutes the most sensitive form of processing for individuals, owing to the difficulty of complete deletion once data has been disseminated. The guidance addresses eleven specific scenarios, including publishing enrolment lists, institutional and student council composition, information about distinguished graduates, participation in educational events, and dormitory allocation. For enrolment lists, the Centre determines that the legal basis is Article 6, paragraph twenty, of the Law, read together with Article 55(3) of the Education Code. The Centre determines that permissible data in such lists is limited to full name, scores achieved and information on benefits. The Centre notes that benefit entitlement may be indicated by notation such as "benefit" without disclosing the underlying sensitive ground, for example disability or orphan status.

Regarding council membership, the Centre finds that neither the Regulation on the Council of an Educational Institution nor the Regulation on the Student Council requires publication of council composition on institutional websites. The Centre determines that such publication is lawful without consent only where expressly provided for in the higher education institution's charter, with Article 6, paragraph twenty, of the Law serving as the legal basis; otherwise, the Centre finds that the consent of the individuals concerned is required.
rows.body_name: National Centre for Personal Data Protection (Belarus)

rows.title: EDPB adopts Opinion 12/2026 on Spanish DPA draft decision approving Santander Group Controller BCRs under Art. 47 GDPR
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board has adopted Opinion 12/2026, issued under Art. 64(1)(f) GDPR, assessing the draft decision of the Spanish supervisory authority to approve the Controller Binding Corporate Rules of the Santander Group. The EDPB finds that the submitted BCRs meet the requirements of Art. 47 GDPR.

On 15 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board adopted Opinion 12/2026, published on 27 April 2026, in response to the Spanish supervisory authority acting as BCR Lead pursuant to the consistency mechanism set out in Art. 63 and Art. 64(1)(f) GDPR. No financial penalty is associated with this opinion.

The EDPB notes that, pursuant to Art. 46(1) GDPR, in the absence of an adequacy decision under Art. 45(3) GDPR, a controller or processor transferring personal data to a third country is required to provide appropriate safeguards and to ensure that enforceable data subject rights and effective legal remedies are available. The Board finds that a group of undertakings engaged in a joint economic activity may provide such safeguards through legally binding BCRs that expressly confer enforceable rights on data subjects and fulfil the minimum requirements specified in Art. 47(2) GDPR.

The EDPB determines that the Santander Group's submitted Controller BCRs afford appropriate safeguards and satisfy all conditions set out in Art. 47 GDPR, assessed against EDPB Recommendations 1/2022 on the Application for Approval and on the elements and principles to be found in Controller Binding Corporate Rules, adopted on 20 June 2023, which the Board notes supersede Article 29 Working Party documents WP256 rev.01 and WP264. The EDPB states that its scope is confined to the consistency mechanism review under Art. 47 GDPR and does not address other GDPR obligations referenced within the BCRs, nor any supplementary measures that an exporter subject to the GDPR may be required to adopt depending on the circumstances of individual transfers.

The EDPB further recalls, by reference to the Court of Justice of the European Union judgment in Case C-311/18, Data Protection Commissioner v Facebook Ireland Ltd and Maximillian Schrems, that the data exporter subject to the GDPR bears responsibility, with the assistance of the data importer where necessary, for assessing whether the level of protection required by EU law is maintained in the relevant third country of destination.

rows.title: CNIL Publishes Recommended Template and Guidance for DPO Activity Reports
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published guidance and a model template recommending that data protection officers produce activity reports as a best practice for managing GDPR compliance within their organisations.

On 27 April 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published detailed guidance on the preparation and use of DPO activity reports, accompanied by a model document intended to assist DPOs in structuring their reporting. The CNIL notes that producing such a report is not a legal obligation under the GDPR but constitutes a recommended best practice that falls naturally within the scope of the DPO role (Art. 39 GDPR).

The CNIL finds that the activity report serves multiple functions within an organisation. It participates directly in compliance management and enables the DPO to account to the highest level of organisational leadership, as the GDPR provides (Art. 38(3) GDPR). The CNIL notes that the report can be produced quarterly, half-yearly or annually depending on the organisation's activities, and that its content is expected to draw on the record of processing activities, data protection impact assessments, audit results, data breach records and complaints. The CNIL further finds that the report is a tool for evaluating legal, financial and reputational risks, documenting compliance steps taken or under way, and identifying obstacles to improving the level of personal data protection.

The CNIL recommends that, where possible, the DPO presents the report formally at a meeting with senior leadership, describing this as a privileged opportunity for dialogue on the organisation's compliance maturity. The CNIL notes that a version of the report, incorporating key indicators such as the number of data subject rights requests, response times and the number of data breaches recorded, is appropriate for sharing with staff representative bodies and employees more broadly. The CNIL states that such internal communication reinforces the DPO's visibility and supports the diffusion of a data protection culture within the organisation. The authority further notes that the report provides an institutional record of actions taken, which is of particular value in the event of a change of DPO.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: Japan Digital Agency Concludes Technology-Based Regulatory Reform Committee After Ten Sessions, Reports 98% of National Analogue Regulations Revised
rows.summary:
Japan's Digital Agency has concluded the fourth and final session of its Technology-Based Regulatory Reform Promotion Committee, convened under the Digital System Reform Study Group, with the Digital Agency reporting that 98% of identified national analogue regulations requiring revision have been completed, and the programme's Technology Catalogue reaching 218 entries.

On 19 March 2026, the Digital Agency convened the fourth session of the Technology-Based Regulatory Reform Promotion Committee online, with Professor Hiroshi Esaki of the University of Tokyo Graduate School of Information Science and Technology serving as chair. The secretariat confirmed at the session that this meeting constituted the final instalment of the committee, which had held a total of ten sessions including its predecessor body under the Digital Rincho framework.

The Digital Agency's secretariat presented materials summarising the programme's origins, scope and outcomes. The agency notes that the initiative was established to address analogue regulations obstructing the implementation of digital technologies, with the objective of creating a regulatory environment adapted to technological progress. The agency states that, at the national level, a consolidated revision plan was established to reform relevant legislation in bulk, and a Digital Legal Examination process was introduced to prevent new analogue regulations from arising. The agency further notes that support for local governments in revising analogue regulations in prefectural and municipal ordinances has been substantially strengthened over the programme's duration.

According to materials presented by the secretariat, the Digital Agency identified approximately 10,000 analogue regulatory provisions at the outset, of which around 1,000 were assessed as requiring technical verification. The agency states that roughly half of those provisions were subject to technical verification conducted directly by the Digital Agency, supported by dedicated budget allocations across multiple fiscal years. The agency reports that the Technology Catalogue, covering seven categories of digital technology including construction and sewerage sectors, reached 218 entries by the end of the current fiscal year, though the agency notes that demand for new registrations had stabilised and new submissions were accordingly closed in February 2026. The agency also states that approximately 80% of technology verification cases led to practical implementation examples, a result the committee noted as demonstrating tangible efficacy for end users.

rows.title: BC Information and Privacy Commissioner Reports 86% Rise in Complaints and AI-Generated File Surge in Legislative Address
rows.summary:
The Office of the Information and Privacy Commissioner for British Columbia, in a speech delivered by Commissioner Michael Harvey to the Select Standing Committee on Finance and Government Services, reported an 86 per cent increase in privacy and access complaints received in the past year, alongside a growing volume of files attributed to artificial intelligence large language models.

On 24 April 2026, Commissioner Michael Harvey addressed the Select Standing Committee on Finance and Government Services in Victoria, British Columbia, setting out operational updates for both the Office of the Information and Privacy Commissioner and the Office of the Registrar of Lobbyists. No financial penalties were announced in this address.

The Commissioner reported that the office received close to 3,900 files in the past year, representing an 86 per cent increase in privacy and access complaints, a 50 per cent increase in requests for review, and a 28 per cent increase in reported privacy breaches. The Commissioner notes that the total file count has more than doubled over the last five years, with the most recent year-over-year rise described as a sharp departure from the steady growth previously observed. The Commissioner states this increase has placed considerable strain on case review officers, investigators, adjudicators and management across the office.

The Commissioner identifies artificial intelligence large language models as one contributing factor to the surge in complaints. The Commissioner explains that individuals receiving Freedom of Information responses appear, in a number of instances, to be inputting those responses into large language models and using the outputs to generate complaints to the office. The Commissioner notes that while early resolution techniques have proven effective in closing many such files quickly, the known tendency of large language models to hallucinate, exaggerate and extrapolate means that other AI-generated files present greater complexity than standard matters. The Commissioner also reports that the office has introduced an early resolution investigator role, which has resolved approximately 104 additional files beyond expectations, and that the office is examining further procedural efficiencies including submission limits and restrictions on the number of active files a single individual may hold open at any one time. The Commissioner states that despite closing 36 per cent more files than in the previous year, a gap remains between file intake and resolution capacity that the office considers requires addressing in order to fulfil its legislative mandate.
rows.body_name: Office of the Information and Privacy Commissioner for BC

rows.title: OAIC and eSafety Commissioner Formalise Cooperation on Privacy and Online Safety via MOU
rows.summary:
The Office of the Australian Information Commissioner and the eSafety Commissioner have entered into a Memorandum of Understanding to coordinate regulatory responses where privacy and online safety obligations intersect, with particular focus on age assurance requirements and social media minimum age compliance.

On 23 April 2026, the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC) and the eSafety Commissioner announced the execution of a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) formalising cooperation and information sharing between the two regulators. The OAIC indicates that the agreement builds on existing collaboration between the agencies and reflects the convergence of regulatory responsibilities under applicable Australian law, including obligations set out in the Online Safety Act.

The OAIC notes that the MOU establishes formal communication pathways between the two regulators on matters where privacy rights and online safety requirements overlap. The agreement covers age assurance requirements now mandatory under Australia's online industry codes and standards, which the OAIC indicates are designed to protect children from abuse and age-inappropriate material, as well as compliance by age-restricted platforms with Social Media Minimum Age obligations. Australian Information Commissioner Elizabeth Tydd states that the MOU will advance work led by Privacy Commissioner Carly Kind and assist the OAIC in monitoring and responding to emerging online privacy risks, and in delivering on both agencies' statutory functions as set out in the Online Safety Act.

eSafety Commissioner Julie Inman Grant states that the implementation of the Social Media Minimum Age regime required recognition of privacy rights from the outset, and that the MOU gives formal recognition to that principle. The eSafety Commissioner further states that the proliferation of technologies such as artificial intelligence is amplifying online risks, and that industry is increasingly required to deploy age-assurance technologies that meet regulatory obligations while respecting privacy in the Australian context. The OAIC indicates that through information and expertise sharing under the MOU, both agencies intend to address privacy and safety challenges in the digital environment through coordinated regulatory action.
rows.body_name: Office of the Australian Information Commissioner

rows.title: Council of Europe Committee on New and Emerging Technologies holds inaugural meeting in Strasbourg
rows.summary:
The Council of Europe's Committee on New and Emerging Technologies (CDNET) held its inaugural meeting in Strasbourg from 15 to 17 April 2026, with the European Union Agency for Fundamental Rights (FRA) participating as part of the EU delegation.

On 23 April 2026, the European Union Agency for Fundamental Rights published a news item confirming its participation in the CDNET inaugural meeting, which took place over three days at the Council of Europe's headquarters in Strasbourg.

The meeting incorporated a one-day thematic conference entitled "Algorithmic Age: Human Rights, Democracy and Rule of Law". FRA notes that it contributed to this conference by presenting results from its work on online content and hate speech, as well as providing updates on ongoing work in those areas. According to the FRA news item, the remaining two days of the three-day meeting were dedicated to organising the work of the Committee.

FRA confirms its role as a participant to the CDNET committee within the framework of the EU delegation. According to the FRA news item, the committee's establishment reflects the Council of Europe's engagement with questions arising from new and emerging technologies, including their implications for human rights, democracy and the rule of law. FRA's contribution to the thematic conference drew on its research outputs concerning online content moderation and hate speech, areas that intersect with broader regulatory developments including the EU's artificial intelligence framework and data protection obligations under Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR). The FRA news item does not detail specific conclusions or determinations adopted by the CDNET during its inaugural session beyond the organisational and conferencing activities described.

rows.title: DSK and Sector-Specific Supervisory Authorities Discuss Deepfake Regulation and Rising Complaint Volumes at Stuttgart Meeting
rows.summary:
Germany's Conference of Independent Data Protection Supervisory Authorities (DSK) and sector-specific supervisory bodies met in Stuttgart on 22 April 2026 to discuss the Federal Ministry of Justice's draft law on digital violence and a marked increase in complaints across all participating authorities.

On 23 April 2026, the Mecklenburg-Vorpommern State Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information (LfDI), acting as part of the DSK, published a press release reporting on the exchange meeting held the previous day in Stuttgart. The meeting brought together members of the DSK and representatives from sector-specific supervisory bodies, including those responsible for broadcasting, the Landesmedienanstalten, and the churches. No fines were issued in connection with this matter.

The DSK notes that participants discussed the draft legislation submitted by the Federal Ministry of Justice the previous week, titled "zur Stärkung des zivilrechtlichen und strafrechtlichen Schutzes vor digitaler Gewalt" (strengthening civil and criminal law protection against digital violence). The DSK finds that the draft law is intended to establish stricter penalties for individuals who violate others' privacy, with a particular focus on sexualised deepfakes and unauthorised surveillance through GPS trackers, the latter described as a form of digital trespass. The DSK states that participants considered how existing provisions of the GDPR (Art. 5, Art. 9 GDPR), together with adjacent European instruments including the Digital Services Act and the AI Act, already address the creation and distribution of deepfakes.

The DSK further notes that a separate agenda item concerned the handling of substantially increased complaint volumes. The DSK determined that a pronounced rise in complaint numbers has been recorded not only among the independent federal and state data protection authorities, but also among sector-specific supervisory bodies covering broadcasting, the Landesmedienanstalten, and the churches. The DSK holds biannual exchanges with these sector-specific authorities, with the 22 April meeting representing the first such exchange of 2026, convened under the Baden-Württemberg LfDI's chairmanship of the DSK for that year.

rows.title: Czech NÚKIB Q1 2026 Threat Report Records Highest Incident Count in Twelve Months
rows.summary:
The National Cybersecurity Authority of the Czech Republic (NÚKIB) has published its Q1 2026 quarterly threat overview, finding that more than 70 cyber security incidents were recorded between January and March 2026, the highest figure in the preceding twelve months.

On 22 April 2026, NÚKIB published its first-quarter threat overview for 2026, setting out incident statistics, threat actor activity and situational assessments relevant to Czech entities. The report carries no financial penalties and does not concern a specific regulated entity.

NÚKIB finds that the rise in incident volume was driven primarily by a wave of DDoS attacks in January carried out by pro-Russian hacktivist groups, combined with a relatively elevated number of incidents classified under the intrusion category. NÚKIB records that, of the total incidents logged, seven were classified as significant, with the majority of those affecting state institutions, while all remaining incidents fell into the less-significant category. NÚKIB further finds that, although DDoS attack numbers declined progressively after January, successful intrusions remained at consistent levels throughout the quarter, encompassing compromises of systems, edge devices, databases, e-mail accounts and individual user accounts.

NÚKIB states that no ransomware attack was recorded against any entity subject to higher-obligation requirements under the new Cybersecurity Act during Q1 2026. The authority notes that ransomware incidents did affect providers of regulated services operating under lower-obligation requirements, with those cases coordinated through the National CERT (CSIRT.CZ). NÚKIB additionally reports a notable increase in March in the number of devices on Czech territory compromised by the REDHEBERG botnet, exploiting inadequately secured VNC services exposed to the internet, with over 3,200 compromised devices still recorded at the time of the report's publication.

On the broader threat landscape, NÚKIB identifies several incidents without direct impact on Czech national security but with potential indirect relevance to Czech entities, including a global cyber-espionage campaign conducted via the Signal application, extensive use of an advanced iPhone compromise tool designated as Coruna, and a spear-phishing cyber-espionage campaign against European states exploiting a vulnerability in Microsoft Office software. The report also notes that in March, NÚKIB participated in the NATO Cyber Champions Summit and hosted the Prague Cyber Security Conference 2026.

rows.title: German Federal Government Approves Draft Law Mandating Three-Month IP Address Retention by Internet Access Providers
rows.summary:
The German Federal Ministry of the Interior has approved a draft law requiring internet access service providers to retain IP address assignment data for three months, to close investigative gaps in the prosecution of online offences.

On 22 April 2026, the Federal Ministry of the Interior announced that the Federal Government had adopted a draft law on the mandatory retention of IP address data. The draft was developed by the Federal Ministry of the Interior in close coordination with the Federal Ministry of Justice and the Federal Ministry for Digital Affairs. No fine is associated with this legislative measure.

The Federal Ministry of the Interior states that IP addresses frequently constitute the first or only trace that perpetrators leave in the digital space, covering offences including the distribution of child sexual abuse material, cybercrime platforms, fraudulent online shops, international terrorism, politically motivated threats, and threats of suicide. The Ministry finds that internet access providers currently retain such data, where they retain it at all, for only a few days, meaning that by the time investigators receive a tip, submit applications, and conduct queries, the data has typically already been deleted. The draft law is stated to close this investigative gap.

The Ministry states that the mandatory retention obligation is limited to data strictly necessary for identifying the holder of a connection: the technical assignment of an IP address to a connection, together with the time and duration of that assignment. The Ministry expressly notes that no content, communication history, or location data is to be stored, and that the data retained cannot be used to create personality profiles or movement profiles. The three-month retention period is described by the Ministry as proportionate.

The draft also addresses two further investigative gaps. First, it introduces a preservation order mechanism, under which prosecution authorities may instruct telecommunications providers to preserve specific traffic data in cases where an offence is known but no specific suspect has yet been identified, pending fulfilment of the legal requirements for actual data access. Second, the draft responds to a Federal Court of Justice ruling of 10 January 2024, which the Ministry states effectively rendered cell-site querying unusable in everyday prosecution by restricting its application to particularly serious offences. The draft law expressly re-authorises authorities to employ cell-site querying in cases involving offences of considerable significance, including gang and commercial fraud, serial burglaries and thefts, and dangerous bodily harm.

rows.title: ANSSI Publishes European Study on S-SDLC and DevSecOps, Identifying Strategic Priorities for 2025–2027
rows.summary:
France's Agence nationale de la sécurité des systèmes d'information (ANSSI) has published a European-scope study on secure software development lifecycle (S-SDLC) and DevSecOps practices, positioning both as strategic national and industrial priorities for the 2025–2027 period in response to supply chain attacks and evolving regulatory requirements under the Cyber Resilience Act (CRA), NIS2, and DORA.
