On 22 April 2026, ANSSI published the study, titled "S-SDLC et DevSecOps en Europe : Enjeux actuels et perspectives d'avenir", drawing on contributions from public and private sector organisations across Europe. ANSSI notes that no financial penalties are associated with this publication.

ANSSI finds that securing the software development lifecycle is no longer an optional measure but a strategic imperative, citing the increase in software supply chain attacks, including incidents such as SolarWinds and Log4Shell, as well as the growing complexity of continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines. The study identifies the regulatory landscape, encompassing the CRA, NIS2 and DORA frameworks, as a key driver requiring organisations to reassess their development security postures.

Beyond its market analysis, ANSSI sets out strategic and industrial policy orientations intended to give lasting structure to DevSecOps approaches across organisations of varying sizes and sectors. The study also identifies the rise of artificial intelligence as an emerging concern, with ANSSI determining that AI integration into development pipelines necessitates a dedicated MLSecOps approach. This approach, the authority states, is required to embed security and compliance requirements natively, both at the level of AI models and in their operational use within CI/CD chains.

The study is accompanied by concrete deliverables, including technical reference materials, a generic risk analysis framework, and roadmaps adapted to different organisational profiles. ANSSI notes that these tools are designed to support all types of organisations in structuring their DevSecOps and S-SDLC practices in line with the identified priorities.

rows.title: Japan AI Safety Institute Publishes Second Edition of Known Attacks and Their Impacts on AI Systems
rows.summary:
Japan's AI Safety Institute (AISI Japan) has published the second edition of its technical reference document, "Known Attacks and Their Impacts on AI Systems," cataloguing twelve categories of attack specific to AI systems and mapping their effects across confidentiality, integrity, and availability dimensions.

On 22 April 2026, AISI Japan released the updated document, building on the first edition published in March 2025. The document carries no fine or enforcement mechanism and imposes no legal obligations; it is a technical reference intended to support the examination of countermeasures by developers, operators, and others involved in AI system deployment.

AISI Japan identifies twelve attack types, designated A through L, organised across four attack surfaces: Training, Model, Input, and Output. AISI Japan finds that attacks within scope are those involving intervention in an AI model's training or inference processes, or the exploitation and tampering of its inputs and outputs. AISI Japan notes that attacks that target AI model assets through conventional network vulnerabilities, without interfering with training or inference, fall outside the document's scope. AISI Japan notes that the assumed AI system encompasses both a development and training environment and an operational environment, and that the framework applies to AI models beyond large language models, including systems that incorporate plugins, internal knowledge bases, and external knowledge sources.

AISI Japan determines that the twelve attack types include Data Poisoning (D) and Model Poisoning (C) during training; Model Extraction (A), Training and Reference Information Gathering (B), Prompt Injection (H), Prompt Stealing (G), Evasion (E), and Energy-Latency attacks (F) during inference; and Side-Channel attacks (K), Post-training Model Manipulation (J), Code Injection (I), and Knowledge Poisoning (L) affecting model and knowledge components. AISI Japan states that the document maps each attack type to specific impacts, including model leakage, training data leakage, safeguard bypass, model malfunction, system compromise, computational waste, task abandonment, failure of unlearning, and unintended refusal. AISI Japan states that system compromise, classified under integrity, can extend to confidentiality and availability impacts and can enable conventional cyberattacks. AISI Japan illustrates, by way of example, that training data leakage from AI used in medical diagnosis raises privacy concerns, and that abnormal outputs in automated driving systems present safety risks.

rows.title: Romania's Constitutional Court Voids Civil Servant Asset Disclosure Rules on Privacy Grounds
rows.summary:
Romania's Constitutional Court found provisions requiring civil servants to publicly disclose the income of spouses and adult dependent children to be unconstitutional under the right to private and family life and data protection standards.

On 22 April 2026, the European Union Agency for Fundamental Rights published a case law reference relating to Romania's Constitutional Court Decision no. 297. The court assessed challenges brought by appellant D.L.C., a Romanian civil servant fined for failing to include her husband's income in a mandatory asset declaration.

The Constitutional Court finds that provisions of Romanian integrity legislation requiring civil servants to disclose spousal and dependent children's income in asset declarations are unconstitutional. The appellant argued that her husband, who was not a civil servant, was under no obligation to provide his salary information, which was treated as confidential under his employment contract. The court accepts that such obligations interfere with the right to family and private life where the declarant may lack access to required information and cannot anonymise it.

The court additionally finds that provisions requiring asset declarations to be published on the employing institution's website and the National Integrity Agency's website contravene the fundamental right to privacy under Article 26 of the Romanian Constitution, Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights, and Article 7 of the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights. The court references the Court of Justice of the European Union's judgment of 1 August 2022 in Case C-184/20, which found that EU law precludes public disclosure of personal data relating to the spouse or cohabitant of a declarant. The court notes that Article 8 of the Charter establishes an explicit right to data protection and that Article 16 of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union obliges the EU to establish data protection rules, as implemented through the GDPR.

rows.title: EDPB Adopts Guidelines on Personal Data Processing for Scientific Research
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board has adopted guidelines on the processing of personal data for scientific research purposes, together with two opinions concerning the Europrivacy certification scheme, following its most recent plenary session.

On 22 April 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés reported that the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) adopted Guidelines 1/2026 on the processing of personal data for scientific research purposes. The guidelines are open for public consultation until 25 June 2026.

The EDPB finds that the guidelines provide clarity and legal certainty for researchers and facilitate compliance with the GDPR (Regulation (EU) 2016/679), whilst ensuring protection of data subjects' fundamental rights. The EDPB identifies six indicative factors that controllers must consider when defining "scientific research": a methodical and systematic approach; adherence to ethical standards; verifiability and transparency; autonomy and independence; research objectives; and capacity to contribute to or innovate upon existing scientific knowledge. The EDPB finds that where all six criteria are met, activities may be presumed to constitute scientific research; where they are not, the controller is required to justify and demonstrate why activities qualify as scientific research.

On further processing, the EDPB determines that subsequent processing for scientific research purposes is presumed compatible with the original purpose of collection, meaning controllers are not required to conduct the purpose compatibility test under Article 6(4) GDPR, provided the legal basis for original processing remains appropriate. The EDPB clarifies that controllers may use broad consent where research purposes are not fully known at collection, subject to compliance with applicable ethical standards and implementation of additional safeguards. The EDPB notes that dynamic consent and combinations of broad and dynamic consent are permissible approaches.

Regarding data subject rights, the EDPB provides guidance on limitations applicable to the right to erasure and right to object under Articles 17 and 21 GDPR in the research context, including circumstances in which erasure would render scientific objectives impossible or seriously impaired. The EDPB addresses allocation of responsibilities between entities involved in joint research processing and assessment of appropriate technical and organisational measures, including anonymisation and pseudonymisation.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: ENISA Publishes National Cybersecurity Assessment Framework Tool Version 2.0 for EU Member States
rows.summary:
The European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) has released version 2.0 of its National Cybersecurity Assessment Framework (NCAF) tool, designed to assist EU Member States in evaluating and strengthening their national cybersecurity capabilities.

On 22 April 2026, ENISA published the NCAF Tool 2.0, an updated iteration of its structured assessment instrument directed at national authorities and policymakers across the European Union.

ENISA describes the NCAF 2.0 as a key EU instrument developed to support Member States in assessing their existing national cybersecurity capabilities. ENISA notes that the tool is positioned as a practical resource enabling national authorities to identify capability gaps and chart improvements across the dimensions of their cybersecurity posture. ENISA notes that the framework is directed at a government-level audience, indicating that its primary intended users are national cybersecurity bodies and relevant public institutions rather than private sector operators.

ENISA notes that the release of version 2.0 follows the original NCAF tool and reflects the agency's continuing mandate under Regulation (EU) 2019/881, the Cybersecurity Act, which tasks the agency with supporting Member States in building and enhancing their cybersecurity capacities. ENISA notes that its work in this area also connects to the obligations placed on Member States under Directive (EU) 2022/2555 (NIS2), which requires Member States to adopt national cybersecurity strategies and establish baseline capability standards. According to ENISA, the NCAF 2.0 tool provides a structured methodology through which Member States can measure their alignment with those requirements and related EU-level expectations. ENISA states that the framework is intended to assist in both assessment and the subsequent strengthening of capabilities, suggesting a dual function covering diagnostic evaluation and improvement planning. ENISA notes that further technical detail on the specific domains, indicators, or scoring methodology applied within the updated tool has not been published in the material released by the agency at this stage.

rows.title: Belgian DPA and ICO Sign Memorandum of Understanding on Cross-Border Data Protection Cooperation
rows.summary:
The Belgian Data Protection Authority and the United Kingdom's Information Commissioner's Office have entered into a Memorandum of Understanding establishing a framework for cross-border regulatory cooperation in the enforcement and administration of data protection law, with no financial penalty attached to the agreement.

On 22 April 2026, the Belgian Data Protection Authority (Belgian DPA) and the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) signed the Memorandum of Understanding for "Cooperation in the Regulation of Laws Protecting Personal Data" at the Privacy Symposium in Venice, Italy. The Belgian DPA and the ICO recognise, as the document states, the complexities of the international circulation and exchange of personal data and the increased need for cross-border cooperation that this creates.

The Belgian DPA and the ICO describe the MoU as a joint statement of intent providing both parties with a framework intended to foster productive and clear cooperation while adhering to their respective legal differences and statutory independence. The Belgian DPA notes that the agreement is intended to streamline regulatory approaches when addressing similar issues and to promote consistency in the administration of comparable data protection laws. The Belgian DPA states that the scope of cooperation identified in the MoU covers consultation on, and taking account of, each party's regulatory activity to better protect individuals within the scope of applicable data protection and privacy laws, as well as supporting organisations in their compliance with personal data protection obligations.

The Belgian DPA states that the MoU sets out broad principles of collaboration, identifying the role and functions of each authority and the scope of their cooperation. The Belgian DPA notes that one priority area is parallel investigations or enforcement actions, and that collaboration may include the sharing of experiences and best practices, as well as the exchange of information, expressly excluding personal data, about potential or ongoing investigations regarding potential contraventions impacting both jurisdictions. The Belgian DPA states that establishing reliable partnerships with other data protection authorities forms a key pillar of its 2026–2028 strategic plan, which also identifies international cooperation, including participation in the European Data Protection Board and bodies such as the Global Privacy Assembly and the GPEN, as a core axis of its institutional priorities.

rows.title: Australian Privacy Commissioner Finds InspectRealEstate's 2Apply Platform Breached Privacy Principles Over Excessive and Unfair Personal Information Collection
rows.summary:
The Office of the Australian Information Commissioner has determined that IRE Pty Ltd, trading as InspectRealEstate, interfered with the privacy of individuals through its 2Apply rental application platform, in breach of Australian Privacy Principles 3.2 and 3.5 under the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth), with no monetary penalty imposed but remedial declarations issued.

On 1 April 2026, Privacy Commissioner Carly Kind issued determination [2026] AICmr 24 (case reference CII25/00001), concluding a year-long commissioner-initiated investigation into IRE Pty Ltd. The determination finds that IRE's 2Apply platform interfered with the privacy of individuals whose personal information was collected between March 2020 and 18 March 2025, within the meaning of s 13(1) of the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth). No financial penalty is recorded in the determination; IRE agreed to adapt its practices on a without-admissions basis.

The Privacy Commissioner finds that 2Apply collected personal information that is not reasonably necessary for IRE's functions or activities, in breach of APP 3.2, including data on prospective renters' gender, student status, citizenship status, visa expiry date, and previous living history. The Commissioner further determines that IRE collected personal information by unfair means in breach of APP 3.5, citing the material power imbalance between renters and real estate agents, property managers, and landlords, exacerbated by the prevailing rental crisis. Under s 52(1A)(a) and s 52(1A)(b) of the Privacy Act, IRE is required to cease collecting the specified categories of personal information within 60 days of the determination date, and is required to engage an independent privacy reviewer, at its own expense, to assess its collection practices, online choice architecture, and data retention policies (APP 11.2).

In a first for the OAIC, the determination applies the concept of online choice architecture to evaluate the design and structure of the 2Apply form. The Commissioner finds that the form deployed confirmshaming, biased framing, and bundled consent, each of which the determination concludes unfairly pressured individuals into disclosing personal information inconsistent with their preferences. The Commissioner's determination emphasises that other RentTech providers are expected to align their practices with the findings, and the OAIC has provided the decision to real estate peak bodies to assist agents, property managers, and landlords in considering its implications for their own information collection practices.
rows.body_name: Office of the Australian Information Commissioner

rows.title: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada Presents Views on Bill S-5, the Connected Care for Canadians Act, to Senate Committee
rows.summary:
Deputy Commissioner Marc Chénier, representing the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (OPC), appeared before the Senate Standing Committee on Social Affairs, Science and Technology on 22 April 2026 to present the OPC's position on Bill S-5, the Connected Care for Canadians Act, raising concerns about regulatory consultation, the data-blocking prohibition, and the continued relevance of the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA).

On 22 April 2026, Deputy Commissioner Chénier delivered a statement to the Senate Standing Committee in Ottawa on behalf of Commissioner Philippe Dufresne, outlining the OPC's views on the privacy implications of Bill S-5. The OPC states that the Bill aims to accelerate the adoption of common standards to enable secure information sharing across health platforms and improve interoperability between health information systems.

The OPC notes that health information is among the most sensitive categories of personal data, and the statement observes that harms resulting from data breaches in the health sector can include potential discrimination, stigmatisation, and financial and psychological distress. The OPC finds that the Bill's interoperability provisions appropriately specify that the access, use, and exchange of personal health information is not required where doing so would be prohibited by applicable privacy laws. However, the OPC recommends that the proposed data-blocking prohibition be made subject to a similar exception, on the basis that the current drafting does not include one.

The statement further notes that Bill S-5 leaves many requirements and standards, including privacy and security requirements, to future regulations. The OPC states that it is important for the Office to be consulted on the development of any regulations pertaining to privacy and security. The OPC also identifies the need to continue collaborating with provincial and territorial counterparts to facilitate regulatory alignment across an increasingly interconnected and digitised health sector, consistent with a 2022 resolution on securing public trust in digital health care endorsed by the OPC and its provincial and territorial counterparts. The statement additionally highlights that health information technology vendors are already subject to applicable private and public-sector privacy laws, including PIPEDA, and the OPC states that modernising PIPEDA is important to ensure Canadians are protected across all sectors, including health.

rows.title: Albanian IDP Finds Media Publication of Health Data on Gjirokastër Residents Violates Law No. 124/2024
rows.summary:
Albania's Information and Data Protection Commissioner's Office has determined that the publication and circulation in the media and social networks of health status information relating to identifiable individuals in the Municipality of Gjirokastër constitutes unlawful processing of personal data under Law No. 124/2024 "On the Protection of Personal Data".

On 21 April 2026, the Information and Data Protection Commissioner's Office issued a public statement addressing the dissemination of a news item containing health information about several identifiable individuals in Gjirokastër. No financial penalties were announced in this determination.

The Commissioner's Office finds that health-related data constitute special categories of personal data, the processing of which is prohibited in principle under Article 9 of Law No. 124/2024, and is permitted only in the limited cases determined by that law. The Commissioner's Office further determines that the publications in question lack both a legal basis and a clear, concrete and overriding public interest, rendering them contrary to the legislation in force. The authority notes that the right to freedom of expression and information cannot infringe the right to respect for private life and the protection of personal data, as guaranteed by Article 35 of the Constitution of the Republic of Albania and Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights.

The Commissioner's Office finds the publications to be contrary to Article 43 of Law No. 124/2024, Instruction No. 02/2025 "On the protection of personal health data", and Instruction No. 07/2025 "On the protection of personal data in the written, electronic and/or audiovisual media". The authority notes that Instruction No. 07/2025 requires the verification of information and prohibits the publication of sensitive data without a legal basis and without a clear, proportionate and justified public interest. The Commissioner's Office calls on media outlets, online portals and social network users to verify the accuracy of information responsibly and to take immediate measures to delete from all platforms any content related to this case. The authority states that failure to comply with these obligations constitutes a violation of Law No. 124/2024 and may result in the application of administrative measures and relevant sanctions.

rows.title: EDPB Writes to European Commission Over Israel's NGO Registration Requirements and GDPR Adequacy Decision Implications
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board has written to the European Commission raising concerns that Israel's registration requirements imposed on non-governmental organisations providing humanitarian aid in the Palestinian Territories may be incompatible with the GDPR, and has requested clarification on whether those data flows fall within the scope of Israel's adequacy decision under Article 45 GDPR.

On 20 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board, represented by Chair Anu Talus, addressed a letter to Commissioner Michael McGrath and Commissioner Hadja Lahbib of the European Commission. The letter, published on 21 April 2026, concerns Israel's Guidelines for the Registration of Organisations and Issuance of Recommendations for the Foreign Employees, which are directed at international NGOs engaged in humanitarian aid activities with Palestinian residents.

The EDPB notes that several EEA-based NGOs subject to the GDPR contacted data protection authorities and the Board ahead of a hearing before the Israeli High Court on 23 March 2026. The EDPB finds that the Guidelines, issued by Israel's Ministry for Diaspora Affairs and Combating Antisemitism, require NGOs to submit, among other items, CVs, passport copies, certificates of good conduct, marital status information, and personal data relating to staff spouses and children (Art. 5(2) of the Guidelines). The EDPB further notes that under Art. 8 of the Guidelines, NGOs are required to provide a list of donors including their names, not further defined background information, and the exact amount of each donation.

The EDPB identifies potential conflicts with several GDPR obligations, including the principles of proportionality, necessity and transparency, the requirement for a valid legal basis under Article 6 GDPR, and the ability of data subjects to exercise their rights. The EDPB also notes that transfers of personal data by NGOs to Israeli authorities must comply with the conditions set out in Chapter V GDPR. Because Israel benefits from an adequacy decision under Article 45 GDPR, and because the European Commission holds a monitoring duty in relation to such decisions, the EDPB requests that the Commission indicate whether such data flows fall within the scope of the adequacy decision and whether the Commission has taken, or plans to take, any action given that the Guidelines may undermine GDPR principles.

rows.title: FCA Confirms Second Cohort of Eight Firms for AI Live Testing Programme
rows.summary:
The Financial Conduct Authority has selected eight firms, including Barclays, Experian, Lloyds Banking Group (Scottish Widows), and UBS, to participate in the second cohort of its AI Live Testing programme, operating under existing FCA regulatory frameworks rather than new AI-specific rules.

On 21 April 2026, the Financial Conduct Authority announced at UK FinTech Week that the second cohort of its AI Live Testing initiative had commenced testing, with applications having opened in January 2026. No financial penalties are associated with this announcement. The FCA confirmed it is working with technical partner Advai, a London-based specialist in automated AI assurance, to deliver the programme. The full list of participating firms comprises Aereve, Coadjute, Barclays, Experian, GoCardless, Lloyds Banking Group (Scottish Widows), UBS, and Palindrome.

The FCA states that the initiative is designed to help firms explore questions around risk management and live monitoring to support responsible AI deployment for consumers and markets. The authority notes that applications reflect a diverse range of AI models, spanning agentic AI, small language models, and neurosymbolic AI. The FCA finds that use cases under testing include AI-enabled targeted support for investments, credit score insights for consumers, agentic payments, anti-money laundering detection, and Know Your Customer processes, covering both customer-facing and business-to-business applications.

The FCA confirms that testing will conclude by the end of 2026, with an evaluation report to be published in the first quarter of 2027. The authority also states it intends to publish a good and poor practice report for AI in financial services later in 2026 to assist firms in the adoption of the technology. Separately, the FCA's Innovation Insights report, published alongside the announcement, finds that applications to the FCA's Regulatory Sandbox and Innovation Pathways increased by 49 per cent on the previous year. The FCA notes that its approach to AI relies on existing regulatory frameworks, avoiding the introduction of additional AI-specific regulation, a position the authority set out in its response to the Prime Minister's letter of January 2025.

rows.title: CERT-EU Warns AI Tools Have Collapsed Mean Time to Exploit to Negative Seven Days, Prompting Service Adaptations for Union Entities
rows.summary:
CERT-EU has published an assessment finding that AI-powered vulnerability discovery tools have fundamentally disrupted the traditional patch cycle, with mean time to exploit newly disclosed vulnerabilities now estimated at negative seven days, meaning exploitation typically occurs before a patch exists, under its mandate established by Regulation (EU, Euratom) 2023/2841.

On 21 April 2026, CERT-EU published a blog post setting out its analysis of recent developments in AI-assisted vulnerability discovery and their implications for Union entities, defined as EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies. CERT-EU documents that the assessment complements CERT-EU's Threat Landscape Report 2025, in which the authority documented exploitation of vulnerabilities in internet-facing software as the highest-impact initial access vector against Union entities for the second consecutive year.

CERT-EU finds that the capability shift accelerated materially in April 2026, when Anthropic disclosed that Claude Mythos Preview, a cybersecurity-focused model Anthropic chose not to release publicly, had autonomously discovered thousands of high- and critical-severity vulnerabilities, including previously unknown zero-days in code dating back decades. CERT-EU notes that when benchmarked against vulnerabilities in Firefox 147's JavaScript engine, Claude Mythos Preview developed working shell exploits 181 times out of several hundred attempts, compared with twice for its predecessor model, Claude Opus 4.6. Anthropic distributed the model through Project Glasswing, a controlled programme providing access to twelve launch partners and over 40 organisations maintaining critical infrastructure, exclusively for defensive security work.

CERT-EU also observes that the same period saw HackerOne suspend new submissions to its Internet Bug Bounty programme after AI-generated vulnerability reports overwhelmed triage capacity and remediation resources across the open-source ecosystem, and that the cURL project shut down its own bug bounty programme in January 2026 for similar reasons. The authority states that mean time to exploit, which stood at 63 days in 2018 according to Google's M-Trends 2026 report, has now collapsed to an estimated negative seven days, rendering the traditional cycle of discover, disclose, patch and deploy inadequate. CERT-EU further finds that open-weight models are narrowing the capability gap to frontier systems, meaning these capabilities are progressively becoming accessible to a wider range of actors. In response, CERT-EU states it has built and tested an internal AI-powered penetration testing pipeline and is integrating AI-assisted analysis across its services to support Union entities. The authority sets out eight concrete defensive actions, including reducing attack surface, maintaining security hygiene, adopting AI-powered security testing, strengthening detection and response, and accelerating zero-trust adoption.

rows.title: EDPB Adopts Guidelines on Personal Data Processing for Scientific Research
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board has adopted guidelines clarifying the processing of personal data for scientific research purposes under the GDPR and approved updated Europrivacy certification criteria as a European Data Protection Seal.

On 21 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board adopted Guidelines 1/2026 on the processing of personal data for scientific research purposes. The Board also accepted two opinions on Europrivacy certification criteria sets.

The EDPB finds that scientific research requires clarification and provides six key orientating factors to determine whether processing qualifies as such: a methodological and systematic approach; adherence to ethical standards; verifiability and transparency; autonomy and independence; research objectives; and the potential to contribute to or apply existing scientific knowledge in new ways. The Board determines that where research activities meet all six factors, they may be regarded as scientific research, and where they do not, the controller is required to justify and demonstrate why the activities nevertheless qualify.

The EDPB clarifies that further processing for scientific research purposes is considered compatible with the original purpose of collection, meaning controllers are not required to conduct a purpose compatibility test under the GDPR. The EDPB determines that controllers may rely on broad consent where research purposes are not fully known at the time of data collection, provided researchers adhere to applicable ethical standards and implement additional safeguards. The Board also clarifies that individual rights, including the right to erasure and the right to object, may be subject to restrictions where processing is necessary for scientific research.

On Europrivacy, the EDPB adopted Opinion 14/2026, approving an updated set of Europrivacy certification criteria as a European Data Protection Seal pursuant to Article 42(5) GDPR. The scientific research guidelines are open for public consultation until 25 June 2026.

rows.title: Czech National Cybersecurity Authority Launches EU-Funded Project to Support NIS2 Implementation
rows.summary:
The Czech National Cybersecurity Authority (NÚKIB) has officially launched an EU-funded project to support the implementation of the NIS2 Directive, financed through the European Commission's Technical Support Instrument and delivered by the Estonian e-Governance Academy.

On 15 April 2026, NÚKIB formally inaugurated the project titled 'Implementation of the EU Cybersecurity Directive NIS2' at a ceremony attended by the Estonian Ambassador Jana Vanaveski, NÚKIB Director Lukáš Kintr, and representatives of the European Commission and the e-Governance Academy (eGA). The project is funded by the European Union via the Technical Support Instrument (TSI), administered by the Task Force for Reform and Investment (SG REFORM) under the European Commission. NÚKIB states that no financial penalty is associated with this announcement; the project involves grant-based EU technical assistance with no stated monetary value disclosed in the source.

NÚKIB states that NIS2 Directive implementation has increased the number of regulated entities in the Czech Republic by at least fifteenfold, from approximately 400 to around 6,000. Adam Kučínský, Director of the NÚKIB Regulation Department, notes that the Directive affects nearly all sectors of the economy, including healthcare and the financial sector, both of which the authority identifies as dependent on secure and resilient digital infrastructure. NÚKIB Director Lukáš Kintr states that the scale of expansion in regulated entities makes it essential for the authority to have adequate organisational capacity and operational tools to carry out effective supervision across the enlarged scope of obligations arising under NIS2 (Directive (EU) 2022/2555).

The project is delivered by eGA in cooperation with the European Commission and is designed to strengthen NÚKIB's institutional and administrative capacities, supporting adaptation of its organisational structures and service delivery. NÚKIB states that planned outputs include sectoral analysis, current cybersecurity threat analysis, supply chain security support, change management support, and capacity-building activities. eGA Senior Expert and Project Team Leader Taimar Peterkop states that the project's objective is to translate NIS2 requirements into functional organisational processes, strengthen internal capacities, and support effective cooperation with regulated entities to enable NÚKIB to manage the extended scope of the Directive.

rows.title: NÚKIB Publishes Guidance on Documentation Requirements for Cloud Computing Catalogue Registration
rows.summary:
The National Cybersecurity Authority of the Czech Republic (NÚKIB) has published a supporting guidance document titled 'Guide to Evidencing Requirements for Registration of a Cloud Computing Service', directed at cloud computing service providers seeking entry into the national cloud computing catalogue under Decree No. 505/2025 Coll.

On 20 April 2026, NÚKIB published the Guide to assist cloud computing service providers in navigating the documentation requirements established by Decree No. 505/2025 Coll. on certain requirements for registration in the cloud computing catalogue. The Decree entered into force on 1 January 2026 and, as NÚKIB notes, sets out the technical and security requirements that providers are required to meet in order to register their cloud computing services in the catalogue.

NÚKIB states that the Guide is intended to improve comprehension of the applicable requirements and to assist providers in preparing complete and accurate applications at the point of first submission. The authority notes that the document introduces providers to key terminology, the legal framework, and the general principles of the Decree, and identifies the most frequent deficiencies that NÚKIB finds lead to delays or complications in the registration process.

NÚKIB states that the Guide further addresses the requirements applicable to individual cloud computing security levels. NÚKIB states that these level-specific requirements are set out in separate documents created for each security level individually, providing providers with clear support material when preparing documentation corresponding to the specific security level of the service being offered. NÚKIB has made the Guide and the accompanying security-level documents available on its regulatory support materials webpage at nukib.gov.cz.

rows.title: EDPS Reprimands Frontex for Unlawful Data Sharing with Europol Under Frontex Regulation
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has issued a reprimand to Frontex, the European Border and Coast Guard Agency, for transmitting personal data of suspects of cross-border crimes to Europol without the necessity assessments required by Article 90(2)(a) of Regulation (EU) 2019/1896. No fine was imposed.

On 20 April 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor published its decision concluding an investigation into Frontex's data-sharing practices. The investigation was opened following an audit conducted by the EDPS in October 2022, which examined Frontex's activities in assisting Member States at EU external borders during joint operations, with a particular focus on debriefing interviews conducted with individuals intercepted while crossing those borders.

The EDPS finds that during debriefing interviews, Frontex collected information on individuals reported as suspects of cross-border crime based on interviewees' testimony, and then shared that information systematically and proactively with Europol. The EDPS determines that Frontex performed no assessment of whether such sharing was strictly necessary for Europol to carry out its mandate, contrary to the requirement imposed by Article 90(2)(a) of Regulation (EU) 2019/1896. The EDPS notes that the processing of data in an EU law enforcement database carries the risk that individuals may be wrongfully linked to criminal activity across the EU, with consequent damage to personal and family life, freedom of movement and occupation, particularly where the underlying information proves unreliable or inaccurate.

The EDPS characterises the infringement as a severe breach of Regulation (EU) 2019/1896 and determines that the exercise of its powers is to be limited to a reprimand. The decision notes several mitigating circumstances: five days after the adoption of the EDPS audit report in May 2023, Frontex interrupted its sharing of suspect data with Europol; on the single occasion since that point where Frontex shared such data, it did so following an individual, precise and specific necessity assessment; and Frontex has engaged with Europol in discussions to establish criteria and detailed rules governing any future resumption of information exchanges. The investigation was conducted in accordance with Article 57(1)(f) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

rows.title: EDPS Concludes Ten Prior Consultations for Europol and Eurojust Within Legal Deadlines in 2025
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has concluded all ten prior consultations submitted by Europol and Eurojust in 2025 within the applicable legal deadlines, applying the prior consultation framework established under the Europol Regulation, the Eurojust Regulation, and Regulation (EU) 2018/1725, as well as Directive (EU) 2016/680 governing law enforcement data processing.

On 20 April 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor published a blog post setting out the legal basis and practical operation of prior consultations in the Area of Freedom, Security and Justice, with particular reference to processing conducted by Europol and Eurojust during 2025. The EDPS notes that prior consultation is required either where a Data Protection Impact Assessment indicates a high risk, or where proposed processing, in particular through the use of new technologies, mechanisms, or procedures, involves a high risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals (Art. 5 Europol Regulation; Chapter IX, Regulation (EU) 2018/1725).

The EDPS finds that prior consultations serve several functions within this framework. The authority notes they enhance legal certainty by providing guidance before complex or high-risk processing activities commence, allow risks to individuals to be addressed proactively rather than reactively, and facilitate a consistent interpretation of data protection principles across EU institutions, bodies, offices, and agencies. The EDPS further finds that in law enforcement and judicial cooperation contexts, where agencies handle sensitive data and deploy advanced analytical tools in cross-border operations, the prior consultation mechanism carries particular weight given the potential consequences for individuals.

The EDPS states that all ten prior consultations submitted by Europol and Eurojust in 2025 were assessed and concluded within the prescribed deadlines, namely six weeks under the Europol Regulation, extendable by one additional month, and two months under the Eurojust Regulation. The authority finds that timely delivery of opinions is itself an essential component of legal certainty, as delays can stall operational projects or create compliance uncertainty. The EDPS also notes that the Europol Regulation includes an urgency procedure permitting Europol to initiate processing after a prior consultation has been launched but before the standard deadline expires, provided the EDPS is notified in advance and its subsequent opinion is taken into account, with processing adjusted where necessary. The EDPS finds this mechanism preserves independent supervision even in time-critical operational circumstances.

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion on European Biotech Act, Calling for Safeguards on Health Data in Clinical Trials
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor have jointly adopted an opinion on the European Commission's proposed European Biotech Act, expressing qualified support for regulatory harmonisation of clinical trials whilst identifying a series of data protection requirements for health and genetic data processed in that context.

On 12 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) and the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) published their joint opinion on the Commission's proposal to strengthen Europe's biotechnology and biomanufacturing sectors. The opinion addresses how the proposal interacts with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and the Clinical Trials Regulation (CTR), and sets out recommendations to ensure that simplification measures do not reduce the level of protection afforded to clinical trial participants.

The EDPB and EDPS find that the proposal's objective of addressing fragmentation in the application of the CTR is welcome, and the two authorities express support for the aim of establishing a single legal basis for the processing of personal data by sponsors and investigators. However, the joint opinion identifies several areas requiring clarification. On controller roles, the opinion finds that the proposal is required to specify whether actors involved in funding and conducting clinical trials act as sole or joint data controllers, to ensure clear allocation of responsibilities under the GDPR. On data retention, the EDPB and EDPS find that the mandatory 25-year minimum retention period is required to apply only to the clinical trial master file, rather than to all personal data processed during a trial. Where the Biotech Act provides a legal basis for further processing of trial data, the Act is required to clearly define the purposes and specific safeguards for such processing (Art. 9(2) GDPR).

The joint opinion also finds that the Biotech Act is required to ensure coherence with obligations under the AI Act where artificial intelligence is used in biotechnology contexts. Additionally, the EDPB and EDPS find that the CTR is required to explicitly mandate pseudonymisation where directly identifiable personal data need not be processed (Art. 32 GDPR). On regulatory sandboxes, the opinion states that any Commission implementing acts must provide a legal basis for the processing of personal data and, where sensitive data are involved, an applicable derogation under Art. 9(2) GDPR.

rows.title: EDPS Issues Supervisory Guidance on Data Protection Officer Roles in EU Institutions Under Regulation 2018/1725
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has published supervisory guidance setting out its interpretation of the role, position, and tasks of Data Protection Officers in EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

On 18 December 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor published its Supervisory Guidance on the Role of Data Protection Officers in EU Institutions, Bodies, Offices and Agencies, addressing the requirements placed on those entities by Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. The EDPS states that no financial penalties are associated with this guidance document.

The EDPS states that the guidance sets out its interpretation of how the DPO role is to be understood across EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies, referred to collectively as EUIs, with the aim of supporting those entities in ensuring effective, independent, and consistent application of Union data protection law. The EDPS finds that the DPO functions as a key internal safeguard for the protection of personal data within EUIs, and the guidance is directed at reinforcing that position in practice.

The EDPS notes that a related EDPS Decision, adopting rules on the application of the requirement of prior EDPS consent for the dismissal of DPOs, is scheduled for publication in the Official Journal of the European Union at the start of 2026. The EDPS states that this Decision is required to enter into force on the twentieth day following its publication in the Official Journal. The EDPS notes that the guidance references this forthcoming Decision in the context of the protections afforded to DPOs against removal from their posts, a matter governed by Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 and directly relevant to the independence requirements the EDPS finds central to the DPO role.

The EDPS determines that consistent and effective application of data protection obligations within EUIs depends on DPOs being positioned with sufficient authority and independence to carry out their tasks, and the guidance addresses the structural and organisational conditions the EDPS considers necessary to achieve that outcome under the applicable legal framework.

rows.title: EDPS Opinion 7/2026: Extension of CSAM Interim Rules Requires Safeguards Against Indiscriminate Scanning
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has issued Opinion 7/2026 on the European Commission's proposal to extend Regulation (EU) 2021/1232, the interim framework permitting voluntary provider measures to combat child sexual abuse material online, finding that the extension must address existing shortcomings and include effective safeguards against indiscriminate scanning, on the basis of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 and Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR).

On 17 February 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor published Opinion 7/2026, responding to the Commission's proposal to extend the application of Regulation (EU) 2021/1232 from its expiry date of 3 April 2026 until 3 April 2028. The interim rules permit providers to take voluntary measures to detect and report child sexual abuse material (CSAM) while inter-institutional negotiations on a longer-term legislative framework remain ongoing.

The EDPS finds that the renewal of the interim framework presents a clear opportunity to remedy shortcomings in the existing Regulation (EU) 2021/1232. The Opinion sets out specific recommendations to ensure greater legal certainty, in particular concerning the lawfulness of personal data processing within the meaning of the GDPR (Art. 6 GDPR). The EDPS further finds that effective safeguards against general and indiscriminate scanning are required, consistent with the principles of necessity and proportionality as recognised under EU data protection law. Supervisor Wojciech Wiewiórowski stated that temporary measures must not bypass fundamental rights, and that the extension is the appropriate moment to address concerns raised during discussions on a permanent regulation.

The EDPS recalls its prior opinions issued in 2020 and 2024, as well as a 2022 Joint Opinion issued together with the European Data Protection Board (EDPB), all of which the authority notes reiterate that any technical solution deployed to detect illegal content is required to be targeted and to take careful account of the privacy rights of all users. The EDPS notes that the Opinion does not address financial penalties and no fine has been imposed in connection with this matter.

rows.title: EDPS Publishes Right of Access Findings Following EDPB Coordinated Enforcement Action on EU Institutions
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has published its findings on the enforcement of the right of access to personal data held by EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies, as part of the European Data Protection Board's Coordinated Enforcement Framework action initiated in February 2024, under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

On 20 April 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor released the results of a detailed survey of EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies, examining how those entities handle individuals' access requests under Art. 17 of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. The findings form part of a broader aggregated report produced by the EDPB, incorporating results from participating data protection authorities across the EU and European Economic Area. No financial penalties are reported in connection with this action.

The EDPS identifies five key findings across the 63 surveyed entities. First, the EDPS finds that request volumes are low, with 58 out of 63 respondents reporting between zero and 25 access requests received in 2023, a pattern the EDPS attributes in part to the availability of self-service tools allowing individuals, particularly staff members, to retrieve their own data directly. Second, the EDPS finds that many entities lack centralised systems for managing access requests, which the EDPS determines creates potential inconsistencies and difficulties in demonstrating compliance during audits or litigation. Third, the EDPS finds that entities face difficulties distinguishing access requests from other request types, including requests for public access to documents and complaints.

The EDPS further finds that identity verification practices sometimes result in excessive or unnecessary processing of personal data, including data falling within sensitive categories, raising concerns about proportionality under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. The EDPS also identifies practical challenges in the provision of copies of personal data where entities are required to balance that obligation against the rights and freedoms of third parties. The EDPS states that these findings will inform its future supervisory and enforcement priorities in relation to the right of access across EU institutions. The 2025 Coordinated Enforcement Framework cycle, separately, addresses the right to erasure.

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion on Digital Omnibus Proposal for GDPR and Data Acquis Simplification
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor have adopted Joint Opinion 2/2026 on the European Commission's Digital Omnibus proposal, identifying a series of data protection concerns across proposed amendments to the GDPR, the ePrivacy Directive, and the broader data legislative framework.

On 10 February 2026, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor adopted their joint opinion on the Proposal for a Regulation as regards the simplification of the digital legislative framework, known as the Digital Omnibus. The opinion addresses proposed changes spanning the definition of personal data, scientific research exemptions, biometric data processing, artificial intelligence, data subject rights, data breaches, and data protection impact assessments.

On the definition of personal data, the EDPB and EDPS find that the proposed amendments, including the introduction of implementing acts to clarify whether pseudonymised data constitutes personal data for certain entities, raise concerns about legal certainty and the consistent application of (Art. 4(1) GDPR). The EDPB and EDPS find that carving out categories of entities from the standard definition risks undermining the foundational scope of data protection law. On artificial intelligence, the EDPB and EDPS find that the proposal's introduction of a legitimate interest basis for AI model development and an additional exemption permitting incidental and residual processing of special categories of personal data (Art. 9 GDPR) lack sufficient safeguards and could create broad derogations inconsistent with the risk-based framework established under the GDPR.

Regarding data subject rights, the EDPB and EDPS find that proposed limitations on access requests (Art. 15 GDPR) and new exemptions from transparency obligations where personal data are collected directly from the data subject (Art. 13 GDPR) require careful scrutiny, as the authorities find the stated simplification rationale does not adequately justify restricting established individual rights. On ePrivacy, the EDPB and EDPS find that proposed changes to the protection of terminal equipment, including provisions for automated and machine-readable indications of data subject choices, require alignment with the consent framework under the ePrivacy Directive and the GDPR. The authorities also find that the proposal's approach to data availability in public emergencies and the shift from mandatory prior notification to voluntary registration for data intermediation services under the Data Governance Act raises questions about oversight and accountability within the data acquis.

rows.title: EDPS Issues Opinion on EPPO and OLAF Access to VAT Data Under Proposed Amendment to Regulation (EU) No 904/2010
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has issued an opinion supporting limited direct access by the European Public Prosecutor's Office and the European Anti-Fraud Office to VAT information at EU level, while cautioning that the proposal inadequately distinguishes between administrative and criminal law enforcement data processing regimes under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

On 8 January 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor published Opinion 1/2026 on the European Commission's proposal to amend Regulation (EU) No 904/2010, which would grant the EPPO and OLAF specific, centralised direct access to VAT data in order to combat intra-Community VAT fraud, in particular Missing Trader Intra-Community schemes.

The EDPS finds that targeted and limited direct access to certain VAT information, primarily VAT identification numbers and business-to-business transaction data, can be necessary to support effective cross-border investigations and enforcement. The EDPS acknowledges the objectives of the proposal and recognises the losses to public finances across the Union attributable to large-scale VAT fraud. The EDPS determines that the proposal sits at a crossroads between administrative VAT cooperation and criminal law enforcement, and finds that processing personal data for administrative purposes and for criminal enforcement purposes follows distinct legal regimes that the proposal does not sufficiently reflect.

The EDPS considers that the proposal requires more explicit clarification of the exceptional nature of granting law enforcement bodies direct access to administrative databases, warning that without such clarification the proposal risks being misconstrued or relied upon as precedent for broader or routine access arrangements. Supervisor Wojciech Wiewiórowski stated that exceptional access to administrative databases for law enforcement purposes must remain precisely that, exceptional, clearly circumscribed and carefully safeguarded.

On safeguards, the EDPS finds that core legal, technical and organisational parameters governing the access must be defined in the basic legislative act itself, rather than deferred to implementing acts, in order to ensure compliance with the principles of purpose limitation, data minimisation and accountability applicable to EU institutions under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. The EDPS finds that delegating these core parameters to secondary measures would be insufficient to meet the standards required by EU data protection law.

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion on Proposed GDPR Simplification Measures for SMEs and SMCs, Focusing on Article 30(5) Record-Keeping Derogation
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor have jointly issued Opinion 01/2025 on the European Commission's proposal to amend the GDPR by extending simplification measures to small mid-cap enterprises and modifying the record-keeping derogation under Art. 30(5) GDPR, raising concerns about the absence of a fundamental rights impact assessment.

On 8 July 2025, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor adopted Joint Opinion 01/2025 following a formal consultation by the European Commission under Art. 42(2) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. The Commission's underlying proposal, issued on 21 May 2025, would amend the GDPR to extend certain mitigating measures previously available only to small and medium-sized enterprises to small mid-cap enterprises, introduce definitions of both categories into Art. 4 GDPR, and modify the derogation from the obligation to maintain records of processing activities under Art. 30(5) GDPR.

The EDPB and the EDPS find that, under the proposed modification to Art. 30(5) GDPR, the record-keeping obligation would not apply to any enterprise or organisation employing fewer than 750 persons, unless the processing carried out is likely to result in a high risk to data subjects' rights and freedoms within the meaning of Art. 35 GDPR. The joint opinion notes that the proposed modifications to the GDPR are targeted and limited in nature and do not affect core principles or other obligations under the GDPR. The EDPB and EDPS welcome the clarification that the derogation would not apply where processing is likely to result in a high risk, and propose to the co-legislators that the recitals clarify that a record of processing activities remains mandatory only for those processing activities meeting that threshold.

The EDPB and the EDPS recall that, in accordance with Art. 52 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union, any simplification is required to be proportionate, balanced and based on necessity. The joint opinion finds that the Commission's proposal does not include an assessment of the consequences on fundamental rights of the proposed changes to the GDPR, which the EDPB and the EDPS consider ought to have been conducted. The opinion also addresses the proposal's extension of the scope of Arts. 40(1) and 42(1) GDPR, concerning codes of conduct and certification mechanisms, to small mid-cap enterprises, and raises questions regarding the precise terminology used in the proposal when referring to enterprises and organisations employing fewer than 750 persons.

rows.title: EDPS Joins EDPB Fourth Coordinated Enforcement Action on Right to Erasure Under EUDPR and GDPR
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has joined the European Data Protection Board's fourth Coordinated Enforcement Action, examining how EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies apply the right to erasure of personal data under Article 19 of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 (EUDPR) and Article 17 of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

On 7 April 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor announced its participation in the coordinated action alongside 30 data protection authorities of the European Economic Area. The action is conducted under the Coordinated Enforcement Framework, which the EDPB launched in 2022. Prior coordinated actions addressed the role of data protection officers, the use of cloud services in the public sector, and the right of access to personal data within EU institutions.

The EDPS finds that the right to erasure, also referred to as the right to be forgotten, is the most frequent right that individuals seek to exercise. The EDPS notes that the right is not absolute: exceptions exist under both the EUDPR and the GDPR, including compliance with a legal obligation and processing carried out in the public interest. The EDPS states that each erasure request is required to be analysed on a case-by-case basis by controllers, applying justified and lawful reasoning. The EDPS further notes that the right to erasure raises questions concerning the balance between individual data protection rights and the collective memory of society.

To carry out the action, the EDPS states it will conduct a fact-finding exercise comprising a questionnaire directed at controllers within EU institutions, informed by an analysis of complaints received from individuals regarding the right to erasure. The EDPS states it will subsequently summarise its findings, draw conclusions on best and worst practices identified, and set out areas for improvement. The EDPS notes that results will be analysed in a coordinated manner with the other participating data protection authorities across the EU and EEA, with a view to determining whether further supervisory or enforcement action is necessary. The EDPB is stated to publish a report on the outcome once the coordinated enforcement action concludes.

rows.title: EDPS Publishes Revised Guidelines on Generative AI Use by EU Institutions Under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has published revised guidelines on the use of generative artificial intelligence and the processing of personal data by EU institutions, bodies, offices, and agencies, setting out updated compliance requirements under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

On 28 October 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) released an updated version of its generative AI guidelines, applicable to EU institutions, bodies, offices, and agencies (EUIs). The EDPS notes that the revision reflects the evolving technological landscape and the challenges posed by generative AI systems, and that the guidelines are issued within its role as independent data protection supervisory authority for EUIs, and not within its capacity as a market surveillance authority under the EU Artificial Intelligence Act.

The EDPS finds that the revised guidelines offer clearer and more practical instructions for the responsible development and deployment of generative AI tools. The EDPS identifies that key updates include a refined definition of generative AI for greater clarity and consistency, and a new action-oriented compliance checklist to assist EUIs in assessing and ensuring the lawfulness of their processing activities under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. The EDPS further clarifies the roles and responsibilities of EUIs, assisting them in determining whether they act as controllers, joint controllers, or processors in the context of generative AI deployment.

The EDPS additionally provides detailed advice on lawful bases, purpose limitation, and the handling of data subjects' rights as they arise in generative AI contexts. The EDPS notes that these updates were developed in part on the basis of feedback received from EUIs following the publication of the original orientations. European Data Protection Supervisor Wojciech Wiewiórowski stated that the revision reaffirms the EDPS's dual mission of enabling human-centric innovation within the EU while safeguarding personal data, and that the guidance is intended to ensure generative AI used by EU institutions serves the public interest without compromising data protection standards. The EDPS states it will continue to monitor the evolution of generative AI and update its guidance where necessary to address emerging challenges.

rows.title: EDPS Issues Guidance on Risk Management for Artificial Intelligence Systems
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) has published guidance on the risk management of artificial intelligence systems, setting out practical recommendations for identifying and mitigating technical risks to personal data in AI deployments under the EU data protection framework.

On 11 November 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor released a guidance document titled "Guidance for Risk Management of Artificial Intelligence Systems", aimed at EU institutions and bodies subject to EDPS oversight. The document, published in English and available as a PDF, addresses common technical risks arising from the use of AI systems and their implications for personal data protection.

The EDPS finds that AI systems present distinct technical risks that require structured identification and mitigation processes in order to ensure compliance with applicable data protection obligations, including those relating to data security and integrity (Art. 33 Regulation (EU) 2018/1725). The guidance sets out a framework for recognising these risks at the design and deployment stages of AI systems, with reference to the technical and organisational measures that EU institutions are required to implement when processing personal data through automated means.

The EDPS determines that practical risk management tools and recommendations are necessary to assist data protection officers and technical teams within EU institutions in assessing AI-related threats to personal data. The guidance identifies categories of common technical vulnerabilities associated with AI systems and provides recommendations on how those risks are to be addressed within existing data protection management structures. The document does not carry binding legal force but reflects the EDPS's interpretation of risk management obligations as they apply to AI system lifecycles within EU institutions and bodies.

The guidance forms part of the EDPS's broader programme of work on artificial intelligence, building on prior publications addressing AI governance and the intersection of AI regulation with data protection law, including the requirements introduced by Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, the AI Act.

rows.title: EDPS Annual Report 2024: AI Unit Created, Record 97 Legislative Consultations Issued, Dual AI Supervisory Role Assumed
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor published its Annual Report 2024 on 23 April 2025, setting out the institution's supervisory, consultative and enforcement activities during the final year of its 2020 to 2024 mandate under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

On 23 April 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor formally presented its Annual Report 2024, concluding a five-year mandate centred on preparing EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies for an evolving digital environment. The report details a range of institutional developments, enforcement actions and international engagement activities carried out across the preceding calendar year.

The EDPS reports the creation of a dedicated Artificial Intelligence Unit during 2024, alongside the adoption of an AI Strategy structured around governance, risk management and supervision. The report notes that the EDPS has assumed a dual role under EU AI legislation: competent market surveillance authority for the supervision of AI systems used by EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies, and notified body for assessing the conformity of certain high-risk AI systems. The EDPS determines that this dual mandate is directed at ensuring that the development and deployment of AI tools by EU institutions upholds applicable EU data protection standards. The report also states that foresight activities during 2024 concentrated on examining the benefits and limitations of AI-led technologies from a data protection perspective.

From a consultative standpoint, the EDPS reports issuing 97 legislative opinions during 2024, described in the report as a record number, covering the data protection dimensions of proposed EU legislation across areas including the Digital Rulebook, health, justice and home affairs, and digital wallets. The report further notes that the EDPS doubled its supervision and enforcement activities during the period, including issuing supervisory opinions, verifying and authorising international transfers of personal data, delivering training, and facilitating data protection officer networking. The EDPS also identifies sustained collaboration with the European Data Protection Board, of which it is a member and secretariat provider, as well as participation in the G7 of data protection and privacy authorities and other international cooperation platforms, as forming part of its efforts to support consistent global application of EU data protection rules.

rows.title: Spain Supreme Court Upholds Tax Agency Use of Third-Party Personal Data in Tax Liquidations Under GDPR Article 6(1)(e)
rows.summary:
The Spanish Supreme Court has dismissed an appeal brought by a third party against the Spanish Data Protection Agency, confirming that the State Tax Administration Agency's inclusion of her personal data in tax liquidation documents constituted lawful processing under Article 6(1)(e) of the GDPR and did not breach Article 8 of the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights.

On 20 April 2026, the Spanish Supreme Court issued judgment STS 4096/2025, referenced by the EU Agency for Fundamental Rights, dismissing X's appeal on points of law. No financial penalty was imposed in this matter. The case concerned the State Tax Administration Agency's inclusion of X's name, familial relation, and role as administrator of Jofimoser Els Pins S.L. within tax liquidation agreements addressed to Y, a separate taxpayer. The Tax Agency relied on this data to reject expense invoices submitted by Y and to substantiate tax liability under Article 102.2(c) of the General Tax Law, arguing that Jofimoser Els Pins S.L. had been instrumentalised by family members, including X, to fraudulently reduce tax liability.

X, who was not the subject of the tax proceedings but a third party, contended that disclosure of her personal data to Y lacked her consent and any valid legal basis, in violation of her fundamental right to data protection under Article 8 of the Charter and Article 18 of the Spanish Constitution. The Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD) had previously deemed X's complaint inadmissible, a decision X then challenged before the Supreme Court.

The Supreme Court finds that the Tax Agency's use of X's personal data constituted processing of personal data rather than a prohibited transfer or disclosure under the GDPR and the Organic Law 3/2018 (LOPDGDD). The Court further finds that such processing was legitimate where it is founded on fulfilment of a legal obligation applicable to the Tax Agency, including the requirement to justify tax liquidations under Article 102.2(c) of the General Tax Law; where it pursues a public interest in combating fraud; and where the data included are strictly necessary and proportionate, limited to information adequate, pertinent, and necessary for determining the relevant facts and motivating the decision (Art. 5(1)(c) GDPR). The Court concludes that X's data were essential to establish the familial and professional connections between X and the taxpayer that justified rejection of the taxpayer's expense deductions. The Court confirms the AEPD's inadmissibility decision and dismisses X's appeal in its entirety.

rows.title: EDPS Issues Opinion on Proposed EU Return Regulation, Calling for Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment and Data Protection Safeguards
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has published an Opinion on the European Commission's proposed Regulation establishing a common system for the return of third-country nationals staying illegally in the EU, finding that the proposal requires an in-depth fundamental rights impact assessment and a series of targeted data protection safeguards.

On 28 May 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor published Opinion EDPS/2025/06 on the Proposal for a Regulation establishing a common system for the return of third-country nationals staying illegally in the EU. The proposed Regulation aims to ensure the effective return and re-admission of third-country nationals illegally present in EU Member States by providing simplified and common rules across the bloc.

The EDPS considers that, given the proposal's impact on the fundamental rights of concerned individuals, including the rights to privacy and to the protection of personal data as enshrined in the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights, an in-depth fundamental rights impact assessment is required to identify and mitigate potential risks before the Regulation is adopted. The EDPS finds that the proposal must be aligned with applicable EU data protection legislation and other legal acts linked to the Pact on Migration and Asylum. The Opinion further recommends that the proposal explicitly outline the legal conditions under which disclosure of information about the reasons justifying a return decision may be limited, with the EDPS finding that any such limitation is to apply only in exceptional cases where strictly necessary, such as where disclosure would be contrary to State security interests.

The EDPS also finds that the EDPS recommends third-country nationals subject to a return procedure are required to receive clear information about their rights as data subjects under applicable EU data protection law. On international data transfers, the EDPS recommends that transfers of personal data relating to criminal convictions of third-country nationals be subject to a strict necessity test and must not lead to the handing down or execution of a death penalty or any form of cruel or inhuman treatment. The EDPS further recommends that the conditions governing transfers of children's personal data to the country of return be specified explicitly, and that any such transfer follow a thorough assessment confirming it is in the minor's best interest and will not endanger their well-being.

rows.title: EDPS Issues Opinion on EU-US Data Sharing Framework for Security Screenings, Calls for Strict Safeguards and Proportionality
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has issued an Opinion on the negotiating mandate for a proposed framework agreement between the European Union and the United States on the exchange of personal data for security screenings and identity verifications, finding that comprehensive and effective safeguards are required to protect fundamental rights under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

On 17 September 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) published Opinion 24/2025 on the European Commission's Recommendation to the Council to authorise the opening of negotiations for a framework agreement between the EU and the United States of America. The Opinion addresses a data-sharing arrangement linked to the US Visa Waiver Program, under which partner countries are required to conclude an Enhanced Border Security Partnership with the US Department of Homeland Security.

The EDPS finds that the proposed framework, once finalised, would establish a precedent as the first EU agreement to entail large-scale sharing of personal data, including biometric data such as fingerprints, for the purpose of border and immigration control by a third country. The EDPS stresses that the envisaged processing of personal data must not exceed what is strictly necessary and proportionate, and recommends that both the personal and material scope of the data-sharing arrangement be defined as narrowly as possible. The EDPS further finds that the seriousness of the interference with fundamental rights to data protection and privacy arising from the proposed transfers is comparable to interference caused by exchanges of data for law enforcement purposes.

The EDPS determines that any direct or indirect sharing or transfer of data from EU large-scale IT systems in the area of justice and home affairs, in particular those related to migration and asylum, is required to be strictly excluded, given the specific prohibitions on certain data transfers laid down in EU law. The EDPS Opinion sets out additional recommendations covering accountability mechanisms, including the need for clear and specific justification of each data query, transparency obligations on both US and EU competent authorities, and the availability of judicial redress in the United States for all individuals regardless of their citizenship or nationality.

rows.title: EDPS publishes Compass document setting out AI Act supervisory strategy for EU institutions, 2026–2027
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has published its Compass document outlining how it will exercise its role as market surveillance authority and notified body for AI systems used or put into service by European Union institutions, bodies, offices and agencies under Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (the AI Act).

On 17 March 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor released the document, titled "Towards Trustworthy AI in the EU Public Administration: The EDPS Compass for its new role under the AI Act 2026–2027", setting out its supervisory strategy for the two-year period covering 2026 and 2027. The EDPS states that no fine or sanction is involved in this publication. The document is published by the EDPS in its capacity as market surveillance authority under the AI Act, and not in its capacity as a data protection authority.

The EDPS notes that it was designated as market surveillance authority for AI systems used or put into service by EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies under the AI Act (Art. 59, Regulation (EU) 2024/1689), and simultaneously designated as a notified body for conformity assessments of certain high-risk AI systems. The EDPS identifies four strategic pillars structuring its approach: supervision of AI systems used by EU institutions, covering both ex ante conformity assessment and ex post market surveillance activity; contribution to AI Act governance and regulatory coordination, including participation in the AI Board; institutional empowerment for trustworthy AI, including interinstitutional knowledge exchange and a proposed Trustworthy AI Centre of Excellence; and international engagement and exchange of best practices.

The EDPS states that the two-year timeframe reflects two factors: the evolving nature of the AI Act, which is expected to be complemented by harmonised standards and European Commission guidelines, and the potential changes to the AI Act envisaged in the 2025 Digital Omnibus legislative proposal (COM(2025) 836 final), which the EDPS finds may require corresponding adjustments to its current supervisory setup.
