The EDPS further notes that it established its Artificial Intelligence Unit in October 2024 following an AI Preparedness Strategy announced in May 2024, as preparatory steps toward operationalising the powers conferred by the AI Act.

rows.title: EDPS Issues Supervisory Guidance and Binding Decision on DPO Independence and Dismissal Consent in EU Institutions
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has adopted supervisory guidance and a binding decision establishing procedural requirements for the protection and dismissal of data protection officers across EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies, under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

On 18 December 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor issued Supervisory Guidance on the role of data protection officers in EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies. Building on that guidance, the EDPS adopted Decision 01/2026 on 16 January 2026, published in the Official Journal on 29 January 2026 (OJ L226/199), establishing binding Rules on the application of the requirement of prior EDPS consent for the dismissal of data protection officers before the end of their designation term.

The EDPS finds that the Supervisory Guidance clarifies its interpretation of the data protection officer's role, institutional positioning, guarantees of independence attached to the function, and the responsibilities entrusted to those officers under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. The EDPS states that the guidance addresses the designation of data protection officers and provides a practical framework reflecting the EDPS's current expectations for all EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies.

The EDPS established in Decision 01/2026 what it describes as a clear and uniform procedural framework that EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies are required to follow when seeking the EDPS's prior consent before dismissing a data protection officer prior to the end of their designation term. The EDPS notes that both instruments apply immediately and are to be reflected in the internal practices and decision-making of all EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies concerning the position and protection of data protection officers. Supervisor Wojciech Wiewiórowski stated that the measures are intended to ensure the independent, consistent and effective application of EU data protection law and to reinforce the data protection officer's position as an internal safeguard for the protection of personal data.

rows.title: EDPS and EDPB Oppose Proposed Changes to Personal Data Definition in Digital Omnibus Regulation
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor and the European Data Protection Board have jointly adopted an opinion on the European Commission's Digital Omnibus Regulation proposal, opposing proposed amendments to the definition of personal data under the GDPR while welcoming certain simplification measures.

On 20 April 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) and the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) published a Joint Opinion assessing the Digital Omnibus Regulation proposal against three criteria: whether it delivers real simplification and facilitates compliance, whether it provides greater legal certainty, and whether it affects individuals' fundamental rights under the GDPR, the EUDPR, the ePrivacy Directive, and the Data Acquis.

The EDPS and the EDPB find that the proposed changes to the definition of personal data go far beyond a targeted or technical amendment and do not accurately reflect Court of Justice of the European Union jurisprudence. The joint opinion states that these changes would result in significantly narrowing the concept of personal data and create legal uncertainty. The two authorities urge co-legislators not to adopt these amendments. The EDPS and the EDPB further find that entrusting the European Commission to determine by implementing act what no longer constitutes personal data after pseudonymisation directly affects the scope of EU data protection law and is not appropriate.

The EDPS and the EDPB find several elements of the proposal to be positive. The joint opinion welcomes the proposed increase to the risk threshold triggering mandatory data breach notification to supervisory authorities and the extension of the notification deadline, noting these measures would reduce administrative burden. The authorities also welcome proposed common templates for data breach reporting and data protection impact assessments, as well as a new derogation permitting processing of special categories of data for biometric authentication where verification means remain under the individual's sole control. On the ePrivacy Directive, the EDPS and the EDPB find that proposed requirements for automated and machine-readable indications of individuals' choices regarding cookie consent represent a constructive regulatory response to consent fatigue. The EDPS and the EDPB identify areas requiring further refinement, including the proposed derogation for incidental processing of sensitive data in artificial intelligence development and operation, provisions on abuse of data subject rights, and amendments to automated individual decision-making rules.

rows.title: EDPS Closes Enforcement Proceedings Against European Commission Over Microsoft 365 Following Remediation of Infringements
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has closed enforcement proceedings against the European Commission after determining that infringements identified in its Decision of 8 March 2024 concerning the Commission's use of Microsoft 365 have been remedied under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

On 11 July 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor formally concluded, by letter, that the European Commission had brought its use of Microsoft 365 into compliance with Regulation (EU) 2018/1725, following enforcement proceedings that originated in a supervisory investigation. The EDPS finds that the factual situation has substantially changed compared to that examined in the March 2024 Decision, which the EDPS had identified as containing a number of infringements and imposed corrective measures on the Commission.

The EDPS determines that the Commission addressed three principal areas of non-compliance. On purpose limitation, the EDPS finds that the Commission has explicitly specified the types of personal data processed and the purposes of processing, and has ensured, through updated contractual, technical and organisational measures, that Microsoft and its sub-processors process data solely on the basis of documented instructions and for specified purposes in the public interest. On transfers to third countries, the EDPS finds that transfers outside the EU and EEA are now limited to countries listed in the amended contract and rely either on adequacy decisions or the derogation for important reasons of public interest (Art. 50(1)(d) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725), complemented by binding instructions issued to Microsoft and its sub-processors. On disclosures and notifications, the EDPS finds that additional contractual provisions now ensure that only EU or Member State law, or third-country law providing essentially equivalent protection, may require Microsoft or sub-processors to omit notification of disclosure requests or to disclose personal data processed within and outside the EEA.

The EDPS received a compliance report from the Commission in December 2024 and held subsequent discussions with Commission services before receiving the Commission's letter of 3 July 2025 detailing additional measures implemented and scheduled by both the Commission and Microsoft. The EDPS notes that the closure of these proceedings relates to specific provisions of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 examined in the 2024 Decision and does not constitute an assessment of the Commission's overall compliance with other provisions of the Regulation. The Commission has made the compliance improvements to the Inter-Institutional Licensing Agreement with Microsoft available to other EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies contracting Microsoft 365 under that agreement.

rows.title: Belgian Data Protection Authority Issues Opinion on Draft Brussels Ordinance Governing Information Exchange on Public Road Infrastructure
rows.summary:
The Belgian Data Protection Authority has issued Opinion 44/2026, adopted on 12 March 2026, raising data protection concerns under the GDPR regarding a draft Brussels-Capital Region ordinance on the exchange of information relating to installations on public roads and regional domain, requested by the Minister for Mobility, Public Works and Road Safety.

On 12 March 2026, the Belgian Data Protection Authority (Autorité de protection des données, APD) adopted Opinion 44/2026 in response to a request received on 4 December 2025 from Minister Elke Van den Brandt. The opinion concerns a preliminary draft ordinance intended to replace the Brussels ordinance of 26 July 2013 and the government decree of 16 May 2014, both governing access to and exchange of information on underground cables and conduits. No fine was imposed in the context of this advisory opinion.

The APD identifies three categories of personal data processing that will arise under the draft ordinance: processing within the CICC system (Articles 4 et seq. of the draft), processing in connection with two cadastres managed by Paradigm covering fibre, ducts and physical infrastructures (Article 10 of the draft), and processing in the context of the investigation of infringements and the imposition of administrative sanctions (Articles 13 to 27 of the draft). The APD finds that the requestor did not answer its questions regarding the scope of personal data processing within the CICC system, and notes that the explanatory memorandum attributes responsibility for that processing to the federal legislator rather than addressing it within the draft ordinance itself.

The APD further finds that certain definitions used in the draft ordinance diverge from those in EU Regulation 2024/1309 of 29 April 2024 on measures to reduce the cost of deploying gigabit electronic communications networks, despite the explanatory memorandum stating that the draft aligns with that Regulation's definitions. The APD draws attention specifically to the concepts of "organisme du secteur public" (Art. 2, 14° of the draft) and "civiele werken" in the Dutch-language version (Art. 2, 21° of the draft). The APD also finds that both the text and the explanatory memorandum contain errors that impair legibility, citing among other examples an incorrect reference to a Council of State opinion, and emphasises the importance of clarity and precision in legislative texts as a matter of the principles of legality and foreseeability under the GDPR (Art. 5(1)(a) GDPR).

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion on Cybersecurity Act 2 Proposal and NIS 2 Directive Amendments, Citing Data Protection Concerns
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor, acting jointly under Art. 42(2) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725, have adopted Joint Opinion 4/2026 on the European Commission's proposals for a Cybersecurity Act 2 and amendments to the NIS 2 Directive, raising recommendations on personal data processing safeguards, ENISA's expanded role, and incident reporting mechanisms.

On 18 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor adopted their joint opinion following the Commission's formal consultation of 21 January 2026. The Commission had on 20 January 2026 issued a proposal for a Regulation on the EU Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA), covering the European cybersecurity certification framework and ICT supply chain security, alongside a proposal to amend Directive (EU) 2022/2555 through simplification measures aligned with the Cybersecurity Act 2 proposal.

The EDPB and EDPS find that the relationship between data protection and cybersecurity is double-sided: cybersecurity measures serve to protect personal data by limiting risks of unwanted access, modification or unavailability, yet certain cybersecurity measures can interfere with individuals' rights to privacy and data protection. The joint opinion notes that necessity and proportionality are required considerations alongside effectiveness. The EDPB and EDPS support the proposals' objective to strengthen ENISA's role and facilitate cybersecurity certification uptake, subject to the specific recommendations set out in the opinion, and welcome the objective of establishing mechanisms to make compliance with cybersecurity requirements more coherent.

The EDPB and EDPS strongly support the proposed single-entry point for personal data breach notifications (Art. 15 CSA2), finding that this would reduce administrative burden for organisations without diminishing protection for data subjects. The joint opinion recommends that the EDPS be added explicitly as a Union body with which ENISA would cooperate, and as a possible requestor of advice from ENISA, ensuring clear coordination and division of responsibilities. The EDPB and EDPS further find that where ENISA's future tasks as an information hub would require substantial processing of personal data, the essential elements of any large-scale processing and appropriate safeguards are required to be set out explicitly in the basic act governing those tasks. The joint opinion also recommends that Art. 66 of the CSA2 proposal be amended to provide for prior consultation with the EDPS before adoption of internal rules on personal data processing by ENISA's Management Board.

rows.title: EDPS Issues Orientations on Generative AI and Personal Data Protection for EU Institutions
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has published its first Orientations on generative artificial intelligence and personal data protection, providing EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies with practical guidance on processing personal data when using generative AI systems under the applicable EU data protection framework.

On 3 June 2024, the European Data Protection Supervisor published the document titled "First EDPS Orientations for EUIs using Generative AI", addressed to EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies, referred to collectively as EUIs, with the stated purpose of facilitating compliance with data protection requirements when those entities deploy or interact with generative AI systems.

The EDPS states that the Orientations are intended to provide practical advice and instructions on the processing of personal data in the context of generative AI use. The document, published in English and running to a file size of 5.17 MB, covers the intersection of generative AI capabilities and the obligations EUIs hold under the EU data protection legal framework, which for EUIs is principally Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 rather than the GDPR. The EDPS finds that EUIs require dedicated operational guidance given the particular legal framework governing their data processing activities, distinct from that applicable to private sector entities or EU member state public bodies.

The EDPS states that the Orientations address how personal data may be affected at various stages of generative AI system use, including during input, processing and output phases. The EDPS frames the document as a compliance facilitation tool, recognising that generative AI systems present novel questions about data minimisation, purpose limitation and accountability obligations applicable to EUIs (Arts. 4, 5 and 26 Regulation (EU) 2018/1725). The EDPS notes that the publication follows a period of increased institutional deployment of generative AI tools across the EU administration and reflects the EDPS's supervisory interest in ensuring that such deployments are assessed against existing data protection requirements before and during use.

rows.title: EDPS adopts decision requiring interest representatives to be registered in EU Transparency Register before meetings with senior staff
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has adopted a formal decision requiring that the Supervisor, the Head of the EDPS Secretariat, and all staff holding managerial responsibilities engage, in their official capacities, exclusively with interest representatives registered in the EU Transparency Register, with meeting information to be published publicly.

On 24 April 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor adopted its Decision on Transparency Measures in accordance with the Interinstitutional Agreement of 20 May 2021 between the European Parliament, the Council of the European Union and the European Commission on a mandatory transparency register, with the decision published on the EDPS website on 20 April 2026. The EDPS notes that no financial penalty is associated with this decision.

The decision, adopted with reference to Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 on the processing of personal data by Union institutions and bodies, and to the Interinstitutional Agreement at Article 11 thereof, establishes that scheduled bilateral encounters organised to discuss policy or legislation falling within EDPS competencies constitute meetings for the purposes of the new framework. The EDPS finds it appropriate that only those interest representatives registered in the Transparency Register, established under the 2021 Interinstitutional Agreement, qualify as eligible counterparts for official engagement by the Supervisor and senior staff.

The decision further requires that information on meetings held between the Supervisor, the Head of the EDPS Secretariat, and staff holding management functions with interest representatives, including participants and subjects addressed, be published in the EDPS Agenda. The EDPS states that this publication obligation is intended to enhance institutional accountability and foster public understanding of the authority's operations. The EDPS also notes that the decision is intended to lay down the foundations for an improved transparency policy for the EDPS as a Union entity.

To complement the decision, the EDPS has made available a Public Access to Documents Register, allowing the public to consult documents for which access has been granted, alongside resources explaining the process for exercising public access rights under the applicable framework.

rows.title: EDPS Issues Opinion on Proposed EPPO and OLAF Access to VAT Information Under Amended Regulation (EU) No 904/2010
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has issued Opinion 1/2026, finding that proposed direct access by the European Public Prosecutor's Office and the European Anti-Fraud Office to VAT information is necessary but requires additional data protection safeguards, under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 (Art. 42(1) EUDPR).

On 7 January 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) published Opinion 1/2026 on the European Commission's Proposal for a Council Regulation amending Regulation (EU) No 904/2010 (COM(2025) 685 final), which was itself issued on 14 November 2025. The Proposal seeks to grant the European Public Prosecutor's Office (EPPO) and the European Anti-Fraud Office (OLAF) direct and centralised access to VAT information at Union level, with the stated aim of combating Missing Trader Intra-Community (MTIC) fraud.

The EDPS finds that the proposed access is necessary to achieve the objective of effectively combating cross-border VAT fraud, noting the targeted nature of the access and the specific categories of information concerned. The EDPS states that these categories relate primarily to VAT numbers and business-to-business transactions. However, the EDPS stresses that personal data processing for administrative purposes and for criminal law enforcement purposes are legally distinct, and the EDPS finds that this separation is required to be carefully reflected in the Proposal. The EDPS further finds that the Proposal is required to clearly explain the exceptional nature of granting law enforcement bodies direct access to administrative databases, and the EDPS considers that further clarification is warranted to prevent the measure being misconstrued as a precedent for broader application.

On technical and organisational safeguards, the EDPS determines that certain parameters governing data access are required to be defined at the level of the basic act itself, rather than left entirely to implementing acts as currently envisaged. The EDPS recommends that the Proposal incorporate additional safeguards to ensure compliance with the principles of purpose limitation, data minimisation and accountability under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. The EDPS also calls for ex-post evaluation and review mechanisms to be included in the legislative text to enable ongoing oversight of the measure's data protection implications.

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion on European Biotech Act Proposal, Raising Data Protection Concerns
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor, in Joint Opinion 3/2026 adopted on 10 March 2026, have identified areas where the European Commission's proposed European Biotech Act requires amendment to ensure compliance with the General Data Protection Regulation.

On 10 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor adopted Joint Opinion 3/2026 in response to the European Commission's Proposal of 16 December 2025 for a Regulation establishing a framework for strengthening the Union's biotechnology and biomanufacturing sectors. The opinion addresses proposed amendments to the Clinical Trials Regulation, the Food Law Regulation, and the SoHO Regulation.

The EDPB and EDPS find that proposed amendments to Article 93 of the Clinical Trials Regulation require clarification on legal bases under Article 6 GDPR and the application of Article 9(2)(i) and (j) GDPR to special categories of data. The authorities find that the qualification of sponsors and investigators as controllers requires further precision to ensure accountability obligations are properly allocated. The EDPB and EDPS determine that proposed retention periods for personal data, including genetic data, and conditions for further processing for scientific research necessitate additional safeguards. The authorities find that the current drafting on technical and organisational measures under Article 32 GDPR is insufficient.

On provisions addressing artificial intelligence as a biotechnology enabler, the EDPB and EDPS find that verification of legitimate need requires alignment with existing GDPR principles, and that provisions governing AI use in clinical trials must be brought into conformity with data protection requirements. The authorities state that the identified provisions require amendment before adoption.

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion on European Commission Proposal to Amend GDPR Record-Keeping Obligations for SMEs and SMCs
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor have issued a Joint Opinion welcoming, with qualifications, the European Commission's proposed amendment to the record-keeping derogation under Art. 30(5) GDPR, whilst requesting clarifications on the employee threshold and the scope of the term 'organisation'.

On 9 July 2025, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) and the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) published Joint Opinion 01/2025 on the Commission's Proposal for a Regulation amending certain regulations, including the GDPR, as part of the fourth simplification Omnibus package. No fines are at issue in this opinion.

The Proposal seeks to modify Art. 30(5) GDPR, which provides a derogation from the obligation to maintain records of processing activities. The EDPB and EDPS note that the current derogation applies only to enterprises and organisations with fewer than 250 employees, subject to exceptions. Under the Commission's Proposal, the threshold would be extended to enterprises or organisations employing fewer than 750 persons, unless the processing is likely to result in a high risk to individuals' rights and freedoms within the meaning of Art. 35 GDPR. The Proposal also introduces definitions of SME and small mid-cap enterprise (SMC) into Art. 4 GDPR, and extends the scope of Art. 40(1) and Art. 42(1) GDPR, covering codes of conduct and certification, to SMCs.

The EDPB and EDPS find that the proposed modifications to Art. 30(5) are targeted and limited in nature and do not affect the core principles and obligations under the GDPR. Both bodies support the general objective of reducing administrative burden for SMEs and SMCs, provided that the protection of individuals' fundamental rights, including the rights to privacy and to the protection of personal data, is not diminished. The Joint Opinion notes, however, that the exemption in Art. 30(5) as drafted refers to 'enterprises employing fewer than 750 employees' without cross-referencing the newly introduced definitions of SME and SMC in Art. 4 GDPR, which also incorporate financial criteria. The EDPB and EDPS recommend that the provision refer to those definitions to ensure the exemption benefits only the intended categories of entity.

The Joint Opinion further requests that co-legislators clarify why a threshold of 750 employees is more appropriate under the GDPR than the 500-employee threshold initially considered, given that the Proposal affects legislation across other policy areas. The EDPB and EDPS also request that co-legislators clarify that the term 'organisation' within the scope of the proposed derogation under Art. 30(5) GDPR does not include public authorities and bodies.

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion on Cybersecurity Act 2 and NIS2 Directive Amendments
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor have jointly adopted an opinion on the European Commission's proposals for a Cybersecurity Act 2 and targeted amendments to the NIS2 Directive, broadly supporting the cybersecurity package while recommending stronger coordination with data protection authorities under Art. 42(2) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

On 20 January 2026, the European Commission published its cybersecurity package proposal, and on 21 January 2026 formally consulted the EDPB and the EDPS, requesting a joint opinion in accordance with Art. 42(2) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. The joint opinion was adopted on 19 March 2026.

Regarding the Cybersecurity Act 2 proposal, the EDPB and EDPS support the general objective of strengthening the role of the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) and facilitating uptake of cybersecurity certification. The joint opinion welcomes the clarification that ENISA's advice would be issued upon a prior request from the EDPB, establishing a clear division of responsibilities, and recommends that the EDPS be added as a possible requestor of ENISA advice. The joint opinion also recommends that where ENISA's Management Board adopts additional measures relating to the processing of personal data, such decisions be limited to technical details, and that the EDPS be consulted prior to adoption of such rules. The EDPB and EDPS further recommend that an explicit reference to the EDPS be added as an EU body with which ENISA cooperates.

On certification, the joint opinion finds that the scope of the European Cybersecurity Certification Framework and its relationship with GDPR certification requires further clarification. The EDPB and EDPS determine that ENISA is required to consult the EDPB before adopting certification schemes relating to the security of personal data processing, and that such schemes for products, services and processes likely used in data processing operations take into account security controls capable of demonstrating compliance with GDPR requirements. The joint opinion also supports establishing a single-entry point for notification of personal data breaches, consistent with the earlier EDPB-EDPS joint opinion on the Digital Omnibus Regulation Proposal, on the basis that this would reduce administrative burden without diminishing protection for individuals. Regarding the NIS2 amendments, the EDPB and EDPS welcome the designation of European Digital Identity Wallet and European Business Wallet providers as essential entities.

rows.title: EDPS Completes Ten Prior Consultations for Europol and Eurojust Within Legal Deadlines in 2025
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor concluded ten prior consultations submitted by Europol and Eurojust within applicable legal deadlines, confirming the mechanism as a binding oversight tool under EU data protection law for high-risk processing in the Area of Freedom, Security and Justice.

On 20 April 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor published details of its prior consultation practice in 2025, covering processing operations by Europol and Eurojust. The EDPS assessed and concluded all ten consultations within prescribed timeframes: six weeks under the Europol Regulation, extendable by one month, and two months under the Eurojust Regulation.

The EDPS finds that prior consultation obligations arise where planned processing is likely to result in high risk to individuals' rights and freedoms, even after mitigation measures are identified. The EDPS notes that the legal basis derives from Regulation (EU) 2018/1725, Directive (EU) 2016/680, and sector-specific regulations governing Europol and Eurojust. The EDPS determines that consultation is required where a Data Protection Impact Assessment indicates high risk or where processing involves high risk through new technologies, mechanisms or procedures.

The EDPS finds that prior consultations provide legal certainty to controllers by delivering guidance before high-risk processing commences, enable proactive risk identification, and facilitate harmonised interpretation of data protection principles. The authority notes that, following examination of a proposed operation, it issues a written opinion recommending modifications or safeguards, giving controllers actionable guidance before processing begins.

The EDPS sets out the urgency procedure under Europol's regulation. Where processing is particularly urgent to prevent crime or protect vital interests, the EDPS notes that Europol is permitted to initiate processing after launching prior consultation but before the standard deadline expires. Europol is required to notify the EDPS before starting processing, and the EDPS's advice is required to be considered, with processing adjusted where necessary. The EDPS finds this procedure preserves independent supervision without compromising operational capacity.

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Joint Opinion on Digital Omnibus AI Proposal Calls for Retained Safeguards on High-Risk Systems and Data Processing
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor have adopted a Joint Opinion on the European Commission's Digital Omnibus on AI proposal, supporting administrative simplification in principle whilst finding that several proposed changes risk undermining fundamental rights protections under the AI Act.

On 21 January 2026, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) and the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) published their Joint Opinion on the Commission's Digital Omnibus on AI proposal, which seeks to streamline implementation of certain harmonised rules under the AI Act. No financial penalties are at issue in this opinion.

The Joint Opinion finds that the proposal's extension of permissions to process special categories of personal data, including ethnicity and health data, for bias detection and correction to providers and deployers of any AI systems and models requires further restriction. The EDPB and EDPS recommend specifying that such data may be used for bias detection and correction only in circumscribed situations where the risk of adverse effects from bias is considered sufficiently serious. On registration obligations, the Joint Opinion advises against the proposed deletion of the requirement to register AI systems classified as high-risk even where providers self-assess their systems as non-high-risk, finding that this change would undermine accountability and create an incentive for providers to claim exemptions to avoid public scrutiny.

Regarding EU-level AI regulatory sandboxes, the EDPB and EDPS welcome their creation but recommend the direct involvement of competent data protection authorities in supervising data processing within those sandboxes. The Joint Opinion further recommends that the EDPB be afforded an advisory role and observer status at the European Artificial Intelligence Board to ensure consistency at EU level. The EDPB and EDPS state that the supervisory role of the AI Office with respect to AI systems based on general-purpose AI models must be clearly delineated and must not overlap with the independent supervisory function of the EDPS over AI systems developed or used by Union institutions, bodies, offices or agencies. The EDPB and EDPS also recommend maintaining a duty on AI providers and deployers to ensure AI literacy among staff, and express concern over the proposed postponement of core provisions applicable to high-risk AI systems, inviting co-legislators to consider whether the original timeline can be maintained for obligations including transparency requirements.

rows.title: EDPS Opinion on UN Convention against Cybercrime: EU Fundamental Rights Protections Must Be Preserved in International Data Transfers
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor issued an Opinion on 4 September 2025 on two Council proposals concerning the signing and conclusion, on behalf of the European Union, of the United Nations Convention against Cybercrime, finding that international law enforcement cooperation under the Convention must remain consistent with EU fundamental rights guarantees on data protection and privacy, under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

On 4 September 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) published Opinion 23/2025, addressing Commission proposals to authorise both the signing and the conclusion of the United Nations Convention against Cybercrime on the EU's behalf. The EDPS notes that the Convention is designed to establish common global rules for preventing and combating cybercrime and for collecting electronic evidence in criminal investigations and proceedings.

The EDPS welcomes the Convention's provision that States Parties are not obliged to transfer personal data where such transfers would breach their applicable data protection laws. The EDPS underlines, however, that EU Member States, when implementing and applying the Convention, are required to verify in each individual case whether the conditions set out in Chapter V of the Law Enforcement Directive are satisfied before transferring personal data to a third country. The EDPS notes the heterogeneous nature of the legal systems of United Nations member states with respect to fundamental rights protections, and the EDPS finds that this heterogeneity makes case-by-case verification particularly critical.

The EDPS further finds that competent authorities of Member States are required to ensure that personal data transfers to third-country parties to the Convention remain fully consistent with international human rights obligations and the fundamental rights of the individuals concerned. The EDPS notes that where those conditions are not met, Member States have grounds available to refuse cooperation and are expected to exercise them. The EDPS also recommends that the practical effects of the Convention be carefully assessed once in operation, and that data protection experts be involved in any future reviews of the Convention's text. The EDPS additionally finds that any future proposals to introduce offences incompatible with EU law or values within the Convention framework are to be firmly opposed.

rows.title: EDPS Issues Opinion on Proposed EU Return Regulation, Citing Data Protection Risks for Migrants and Children
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has published Opinion 9/2025 on the European Commission's proposal for a Regulation establishing a common system for the return of third-country nationals staying illegally in the EU, identifying a series of data protection concerns and recommending amendments to the text.

On 28 May 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor issued its opinion following the Commission's publication on 11 March 2025 of the proposal (COM(2025) 101 final), which aims to complement the legislative framework of the Pact on Migration and Asylum adopted in May 2024 and to repeal Directive 2008/115/EC, Council Directive 2001/40/EC and Council Decision 2004/191/EC. No financial penalty is associated with this opinion.

The EDPS acknowledges the need for more effective enforcement of EU and national laws in migration and asylum but finds that data protection represents one of the last lines of defence for vulnerable individuals, including migrants and asylum seekers approaching EU external borders. The EDPS considers that the proposed comprehensive approach is required to be based on full respect for the fundamental rights of persons seeking international protection and other migrants, including their rights to data protection and privacy under the applicable Union framework. In view of the proposal's impact on those fundamental rights, the EDPS finds that an in-depth fundamental rights impact assessment is warranted.

The EDPS recommends aligning the information about data subjects' rights to be provided under Article 24 of the proposal with the relevant provisions of already adopted legal acts in the area of migration and asylum. The EDPS further finds that the limitation of information about the factual reasons for a return decision to the essence of the grounds on which it is taken must not be applied as a rule but only in exceptional cases where strictly necessary, such as where disclosure would be contrary to State security interests, in order to preserve access to an effective remedy. The EDPS also highlights the need for additional safeguards where data relating to criminal convictions of third-country nationals are transferred to third countries, and recommends that the conditions under which personal data of children may be transferred to the third country of return be further specified in the text.

rows.title: Italian Garante Issues Guidelines on Tracking Pixels in Email Communications
rows.summary:
Italy's Garante per la protezione dei dati personali has adopted guidelines governing the use of tracking pixels in email communications, establishing legal requirements applicable to platform operators, service providers and intermediaries under the GDPR (Reg. (EU) 2016/679) and the e-Privacy Directive (2002/58/CE as amended by 2009/136/CE).

On 17 April 2026, the Garante, in plenary session with President Pasquale Stanzione, Vice-President Ginevra Cerrina Feroni and member Agostino Ghiglia, adopted Provvedimento n. 284, published under doc. web n. 10241943 and pending publication in the Gazzetta Ufficiale. The Garante describes tracking pixels as very small, often transparent images of a single pixel embedded in email messages, and finds them to be a category of tracking tool that is particularly invasive due to their concealed nature, which renders them unidentifiable by the data subject.

The Garante notes a marked increase in the use of tracking and behavioural monitoring tools across digital environments, alongside a growing volume of requests received daily from individuals concerning the existence, characteristics and effects of such tools. The guidelines are framed within a body of prior regulatory instruments, including the Garante's 2021 cookie and tracking tool guidelines (Provvedimento n. 231 of 10 June 2021), the 2015 online profiling guidelines (Provvedimento n. 161 of 19 March 2015), and the EDPB Guidelines 2/2023 of 7 October 2024 on the technical scope of Art. 5(3) of the e-Privacy Directive. The Garante also references EDPB Opinion 05/2019 on the interrelationship between the e-Privacy Directive and the GDPR, and WP29 Opinion 05/2014 on anonymisation techniques.

The Garante determines that tracking pixels, alongside other tracking instruments such as cookies, fingerprinting, web beacons, scripts, JavaScript, mouseflow tools and widgets, require specific protective measures that account for their distinct technical characteristics. The Garante states that the capacity of individuals to exercise control over their personal data, particularly in digital contexts, is a prerequisite for trust in the digital ecosystem. The guidelines apply to operators deploying tracking pixels in electronic mail communications and are directed at ensuring that such processing meets the requirements of the GDPR and the Italian Data Protection Code (d.lgs. n. 196/2003, as amended by d.lgs. n. 101/2018).
rows.body_name: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rows.title: Garante Issues Opinion on Draft Decree Governing Telematic Agency Register Platform at Italy's Directorate General for Motorisation
rows.summary:
The Garante per la protezione dei dati personali has issued an opinion on a draft decree prepared by the Directorate General for Motorisation of the Italian Ministry of Infrastructure and Transport, governing the telematic platform used for registration in the Electronic Register of Telematic Agencies (ReAT), on the basis of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR) and Legislative Decree No. 196 of 30 June 2003 (the Italian Data Protection Code).

On 17 April 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali adopted the opinion, registered as provvedimento No. 232 of 26 March 2026, following consultations requested by the Ministry of Infrastructure and Transport through notes dated 14 November 2025, 19 February 2026 and 20 March 2026. The opinion concerns the platform established at the data processing centre of the Directorate General for Motorisation, which handles the timing and modalities for the submission of registration applications by telematic agencies seeking inscription in the ReAT.

The Garante notes that the ReAT framework derives from Law No. 177 of 25 November 2024, which established the register for firms providing professional consultancy services for the circulation of motor vehicles pursuant to Law No. 264 of 8 August 1991. The Garante further notes that, under Decree No. 200 of 16 June 2025 issued by the Head of Department, registration in the ReAT constitutes a necessary condition for access to and operation within the automated information systems of the Directorate General for Motorisation. The Garante finds that applications for ReAT registration are submitted via a dedicated web application accessed through SPID level 2 or the Electronic Identity Card (CIE), and that suspension, revocation or cancellation of ReAT registration precludes the agency from accessing those systems.

The Garante finds that the draft decree addresses the specific procedural aspects delegated by Decree No. 200 of 2025, namely the timing and modalities for submission of registration requests through the telematic platform. The Garante's opinion was adopted in the session attended by President Pasquale Stanzione, Vice-President Ginevra Cerrina Feroni, Member Agostino Ghiglia, and Secretary General Luigi Montuori, in application of the applicable provisions of the GDPR (Art. 36 and Art. 58) and the Italian Data Protection Code.
rows.body_name: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rows.title: China's TC 260 Publishes Draft Technical Document on AI Application Ethics and Safety Guidance for Public Comment
rows.summary:
China's National Technical Committee 260 on Information Security Standardization (TC 260) has released version 1.0 of its draft technical document, "Guidelines for AI Application Ethics and Safety" (人工智能应用伦理安全指引), for public consultation, with feedback sought ahead of a 26 April 2026 deadline.

On 17 April 2026, TC 260 published the draft technical document in accordance with the Committee's procedures for the development of technical documents, as set out in its internal work programme rules. The document has been made available for public comment, with all responses required to reach the TC 260 Secretariat no later than 24:00 on 26 April 2026. The Secretariat is contactable via the designated liaison, Lü Feixiao, at the China Electronics Standardization Institute, Beijing.

TC 260 notes that the document is a technical guidance file rather than a mandatory standard. The draft sets out an ethics and safety framework intended to guide organisations deploying artificial intelligence applications, addressing considerations that TC 260 identifies as relevant to responsible AI use within China's information security standardisation landscape. The accompanying compilation note, published alongside the draft, provides the drafting rationale and scope of the document, though the full substantive provisions of the guidance remain subject to the ongoing consultation process.

TC 260 states that the publication follows its broader programme of AI-related standardisation activity, which has previously included technical specifications on large language model data safety and personal information protection in AI systems. TC 260 does not characterise the document as legally binding, and the guidance is framed as a voluntary reference for entities developing or deploying AI applications. Interested parties are directed to submit written comments to the Secretariat at the China Electronics Standardization Institute, No. 1 Andingmenwai Street, Beijing, postal code 100007, or by email to lvfx@cesi.cn.
rows.body_name: National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization

rows.title: Mecklenburg-Vorpommern LfDI Warns Medical Practices of Fraudulent Research Requests Targeting Children's Health Data
rows.summary:
The Landesbeauftragter für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit Mecklenburg-Vorpommern (LfDI MV) has issued a public warning to medical practices, in particular paediatric practices, following a reported attempt by unknown persons posing as researchers to obtain medical data, including photographs, relating to minors.

On 17 April 2026, the LfDI MV published a press notice stating that it had received a report indicating that an association was contacting doctor's practices by e-mail under the guise of a research project and requesting the transfer of children's medical data. The authority notes that initial investigations suggest the incident is not isolated.

The LfDI MV states that, according to the person who submitted the report, the physician was invited by e-mail to participate in an alleged study. The authority finds that the requests sought photographs from treatment documentation, in some cases accompanied by additional information such as age, diagnosis or date of treatment. The LfDI MV notes that the submitted research design documents appeared deceptively authentic, but that closer examination revealed references to websites containing pornographic content and only vague statements regarding data processing.

The LfDI MV finds that the disclosure of patient data, in particular photographic material relating to minors, is permissible only under strict legal and ethical conditions, with medical confidentiality, data protection and the protection of children carrying the highest priority. The authority states that credible medical research is generally identifiable by the presence of a verifiable ethics committee opinion, including a reference number and date, transparently named research institutions with officially verifiable contact details, and a coherent data protection and consent framework, particularly where minors are involved (Art. 9 GDPR). The authority further states that credible projects do not operate under time pressure or with opaque, immediate demands.

The LfDI MV advises that indicators of fraudulent requests include unclear or changing institutional details, unverifiable contact routes, use of free e-mail providers or lookalike domains, requests to upload data to unknown platforms, disproportionate data demands such as requests for all photographs including metadata, and abbreviated references to anonymisation. The authority states that any practice that has inadvertently responded to such a request and transferred data is required to report the incident as a data breach to the LfDI MV or the competent supervisory authority in the relevant federal state and to file a report with the police.

rows.title: Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium Sets 18 April 2026 Compliance Deadline for Essential Entities Under NIS2
rows.summary:
The Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium has confirmed that essential entities operating under the Belgian NIS2 framework face a binding compliance deadline of 18 April 2026, requiring demonstration of active cybersecurity risk-management measures under the Belgian NIS2 Law of 26 April 2024, which entered into force on 18 October 2024.

On 16 April 2026, the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB) published guidance detailing what essential entities must have in place by 18 April 2026. The CCB states that this deadline derives from a formal request issued by its inspection service, under which all essential entities are required to submit specific information and supporting evidence to enable ex-ante supervision and verification of their cybersecurity posture.

The CCB sets out three compliance pathways, each with distinct requirements. Under the CyberFundamentals (CyFun®) pathway, the CCB finds that entities are required to obtain, or be actively in the process of obtaining, at least a Basic or Important verification, or to hold a signed agreement with an accredited assessment body. Under the ISO/IEC 27001 pathway, the CCB states that entities are required to submit their certification scope, Statement of Applicability and most recent internal audit report by 18 April 2026, with full certification to be completed by April 2027. The CCB notes that entities opting for direct inspection are required to provide a self-assessment and relevant supporting documentation and to formally request an inspection, with the CCB stating that this pathway may lead directly to supervisory measures.

The CCB determines that the 18 April 2026 deadline constitutes a binding regulatory obligation and not a procedural formality. The CCB states that failure to submit complete or timely information may result in administrative measures or financial penalties, as well as further supervisory action. The CCB notes that the Belgian NIS2 Law is designed to strengthen cybersecurity across essential services through risk management, incident reporting and supervision, reflecting the obligations established under the EU NIS2 Directive.

rows.title: Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium Sets 18 April 2026 Compliance Deadline for Essential Entities Under NIS2
rows.summary:
The Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium has issued a formal inspection request requiring all essential entities operating under the Belgian NIS2 framework to demonstrate active implementation of cybersecurity risk management measures by 18 April 2026, under the Belgian NIS2 Law of 26 April 2024.

On 16 April 2026, the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB) published guidance confirming that the 18 April 2026 deadline constitutes a binding regulatory obligation for essential entities, not a procedural formality. The CCB states that all essential entities are required to provide specific information and supporting documentation to enable ex ante supervision and verification of their cybersecurity posture, following a formal request issued by the CCB's inspection service.

The CCB sets out three recognised compliance pathways that essential entities are required to follow. Under the CyberFundamentals (CyFun®) pathway, the CCB finds that entities must have obtained, or be in the process of obtaining, at least a "Basic" or "Important" level verification, or hold a signed agreement with an accredited assessment body. Under the ISO/IEC 27001 pathway, the CCB requires entities to submit the certification scope, a Statement of Applicability, and the most recent internal audit report, with full certification to be completed by April 2027. The third pathway involves direct inspection, under which the CCB requires entities to submit a self-assessment and supporting documentation and to formally request an inspection, a route the CCB notes may lead directly to supervisory measures.

The CCB states that these requirements derive from the Belgian NIS2 Law, which entered into force on 18 October 2024, and which the authority describes as aimed at strengthening the cybersecurity of essential services through risk management, incident notification and supervision. The CCB further states that failure to submit complete information within the prescribed timeframe is liable to result in administrative measures, financial penalties, or additional supervisory action. No fine amounts are specified in the published guidance.

rows.title: EDPB Adopts Scientific Research Guidelines and Approves Europrivacy Certification at 118th Plenary
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board adopted guidelines on processing personal data for scientific research purposes, established a sprint team to finalise anonymisation guidelines, and approved Europrivacy certification criteria as a European data protection seal at its 118th plenary session on 15 and 16 April 2026.

On 15 and 16 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) held its 118th plenary session, during which it adopted Guidelines 1/2026 on the processing of personal data for scientific research purposes under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). The EDPB opened the guidelines for public consultation until 25 June 2026.

The EDPB finds that determining whether processing qualifies as scientific research requires consideration of six key factors: a methodical and systematic approach; adherence to ethical standards; verifiability and transparency; autonomy and independence; the objectives of the research; and the potential to develop or apply existing scientific knowledge in a novel way. The EDPB determines that where research activities satisfy these factors, they may be recognised as scientific research for GDPR purposes, and controllers must justify why activities that do not satisfy them merit that classification.

The EDPB states that processing for scientific research purposes is considered compatible with the original purpose of data collection under Article 5(1)(b) GDPR, meaning controllers are not required to conduct a compatibility test. However, the EDPB notes that controllers must ensure the legal basis for the original processing is also appropriate for the subsequent research processing. The EDPB clarifies that broad consent may be relied upon where research purposes are not fully known at data collection, provided researchers respect applicable ethical standards and implement additional safeguards. The EDPB additionally clarifies the application of data subject rights, including the right to erasure and the right to object, and notes that restrictions may apply where processing is necessary for a task carried out in the public interest.

The EDPB established a dedicated sprint team to complete work on forthcoming anonymisation guidelines by summer 2026, and adopted opinions approving Europrivacy certification criteria as a European data protection seal, one of which the EDPB determines may be used as a data transfer mechanism under the GDPR.

rows.title: EDPB launches public consultation on harmonised European DPIA template
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board has opened a public consultation on a draft harmonised template for documenting data protection impact assessments, with the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés inviting stakeholders to contribute before the 9 June 2026 deadline.

On 16 April 2026, the CNIL published a notice confirming that the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) has developed a draft common template intended to assist controllers in documenting the results of their data protection impact assessments (DPIAs), as required under Art. 35 GDPR for processing operations likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals. The consultation runs until 9 June 2026.

The CNIL notes that the draft template was developed under the Helsinki Action Plan framework and comprises two parts: a DPIA template presented in tabular form for completion by controllers, and an explanatory document to support its use. The EDPB's stated objective, as reported by the CNIL, is to offer a common format harmonised at European level for formalising the expected outputs of a DPIA.

The CNIL clarifies that existing DPIAs completed by controllers remain valid and that no remedial action is required. Where the context of a processing operation or its associated risks evolve, the CNIL states that the DPIA may be updated using existing tools. The CNIL further notes that new DPIAs may continue to be conducted without any obligation to adopt the new template. The CNIL states that its own existing resources, including its models, methodology, knowledge bases and PIA software, will remain fully available alongside whatever template the EDPB adopts following the consultation.

The CNIL encourages controllers, data protection officers, professional federations, experts and other interested parties to submit observations on the draft. The CNIL states that contributions received will enable the EDPB to improve the operational character of the future European DPIA template. The authority also notes that additional tools, including guides, methods and software, may subsequently be developed in collaboration with other data protection authorities within the EDPB framework.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: European Commission Proposes DMA Compliance Measures Requiring Google to Share Search Data with Third Parties
rows.summary:
The European Commission has sent preliminary findings to Google proposing measures under the Digital Markets Act (DMA) requiring the company to share search engine data with third-party search engines on fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory terms.

On 16 April 2026, the European Commission issued proposed measures to Google addressing its obligations as a gatekeeper under the DMA. The proposals set out how Google is required to provide third-party search engines with access to search data, including ranking, query, click and view data, on fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory terms.

The Commission determines that the proposed measures cover six areas: the eligibility of data beneficiaries to receive search data, including artificial intelligence chatbots with search functionalities; the scope of search data Google is required to share; the means and frequency of data sharing; measures to ensure the anonymisation of personal data; parameters for setting fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory prices for search data; and processes governing beneficiaries' access to search data. The Commission finds that the aim of these measures is to enable third-party online search engines to optimise their search services and contest Google Search's position in the market.

To assess the effectiveness of the proposed measures, the Commission has opened a public consultation inviting interested parties to submit comments, with the consultation opening on 17 April 2026. The Commission's preliminary findings do not constitute a final determination of non-compliance, and the proposed measures remain subject to revision following the consultation process. The Commission notes that the eligibility criteria for data beneficiaries extend to AI-enabled services with search functionalities, reflecting the Commission's assessment of the evolving competitive landscape in online search.

rows.title: EU Special Panel on Child Online Safety Convenes Second Meeting, Reviewing Age Verification App and International Approaches
rows.summary:
The Special Panel on child online safety, advising European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen, convened its second meeting on 16 April 2026 to examine existing EU regulatory frameworks and international measures aimed at protecting minors online.

On 16 April 2026, the Special Panel met to review current EU rules and initiatives relevant to child online safety, drawing on expertise from advisers to Member States. The meeting also examined international approaches, including Australia's legislated minimum age requirement for social media access. The session followed the Commission's presentation, on 15 April 2026, of a finalised EU age verification application, which the Commission describes as the most privacy-preserving and user-friendly tool of its kind for protecting children online.

The panel's discussion encompassed a range of existing EU instruments and the degree to which they address the protection of minors across online platforms. Experts advising Member States contributed analysis of national and cross-border measures, enabling the panel to assess gaps and areas of alignment. The Commission's age verification application was presented as a harmonised European solution, with President von der Leyen stating that Member States are encouraged to begin customising the application so that it can be made available to citizens. The Commission describes the application as meeting what it characterises as the highest privacy standards globally.

President von der Leyen stated that protecting children online requires a harmonised European approach, and that the age verification tool is intended to assist parents in protecting children across platforms. The Commission's position, as reflected in the panel's proceedings, is that coordinated EU-level action is necessary to achieve consistent protection for minors in the digital environment. The Special Panel continues to advise the President in its capacity as an expert body, and its work feeds into the Commission's broader policy development on child online safety under applicable EU frameworks, including the Digital Services Act (Regulation (EU) 2022/2065).

rows.title: Estonia's Data Protection Inspectorate Reports 5% Rise in Data Breach Notifications in Q1 2026
rows.summary:
Estonia's Data Protection Inspectorate (AKI) has published its first-quarter 2026 breach notification statistics, finding that organisations submitted 68 data breach notifications between January and March 2026, representing a 5 per cent increase compared with the same period in 2025.

On 15 April 2026, the Data Protection Inspectorate published its Q1 2026 breach notification report, covering notifications received under the applicable data protection framework. The Inspectorate reports that no fines were reported in connection with this statistical release. AKI finds that 19 notifications were submitted in January, 23 in February, and 26 in March, indicating a steady increase across the quarter.

AKI finds that 31 of the 68 incidents involved the public sector, including state authorities, local governments, and entities or companies administered by them. The Inspectorate identifies several illustrative cases from the period. These include a medical record error in which a blood test was incorrectly registered under another patient's name, causing inaccurate data to appear in a health portal. AKI also identifies an e-commerce server compromise in which weakly protected infrastructure was used to install cryptomining software, with the attacker also gaining access to customer order data. A further case involved a misconfigured analytics service provider server that exposed client profiles, which were subsequently downloaded by a third party. Additional incidents noted by AKI include an account compromised via a phishing email granting access to file resources, a management system breach using credentials stolen from employee devices, and an SMS phishing attack used to obtain authentication data from employees.

AKI identifies information-stealing malware, citing examples such as RedLine and Vidar, as one of the more concealed threats on the current cyber threat landscape. The Inspectorate finds that such malware is frequently downloaded to personal devices via unlicensed software, malicious advertisements, or fraudulent links generated with artificial intelligence assistance. AKI further finds that these tools steal not only passwords but also session cookies, enabling attackers to bypass multi-factor authentication by hijacking active sessions. The Inspectorate also identifies risks associated with AI-assisted code generation, known as vibe coding, noting that several national cybersecurity authorities have found that while such tools accelerate development, they may introduce security vulnerabilities that developers without software expertise are unlikely to detect, and that insecure generated code can permit unauthorised data access or expose sensitive information.

rows.title: EDPB adopts Opinion 11/2026 on Belgian SA draft decision approving Kuwait Petroleum Group Controller BCRs under Art. 47 GDPR
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board has adopted Opinion 11/2026 under Article 64(1)(f) GDPR, assessing the Belgian supervisory authority's draft decision to approve the Controller Binding Corporate Rules of the Kuwait Petroleum Group as a transfer mechanism for international personal data flows.

On 15 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board adopted Opinion 11/2026, exercising its function under Article 63 and Article 64(1)(f) GDPR to ensure consistent application of the regulation across the European Economic Area where a supervisory authority proposes to approve Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) within the meaning of Article 47 GDPR. No financial penalty is associated with this opinion.

The EDPB notes that, pursuant to Article 46(1) GDPR, in the absence of an adequacy decision under Article 45(3) GDPR, a controller may transfer personal data to a third country only where appropriate safeguards are in place and where enforceable data subject rights and effective legal remedies are available. The EDPB finds that a group of undertakings engaged in a joint economic activity may provide such safeguards through legally binding BCRs that expressly confer enforceable rights on data subjects and satisfy the requirements set out in Article 47(2) GDPR. The EDPB states that the opinion evaluates whether the Kuwait Petroleum Group's submitted BCRs meet those requirements and align with EDPB Recommendations 1/2022 on the application for approval and on the elements and principles to be found in Controller BCRs, adopted on 20 June 2023, which the EDPB notes supersede Article 29 Working Party documents WP256 rev.01 and WP264.

The EDPB states that the scope of the opinion is confined to consideration of whether the BCRs afford appropriate safeguards as required by Article 47 GDPR and the Recommendations. The EDPB notes that the opinion does not address other GDPR obligations referenced within the BCRs, nor does it address any supplementary measures that an exporter subject to the GDPR may be required to adopt depending on the circumstances of a transfer, in accordance with the Court of Justice of the European Union judgment in Case C-311/18. The EDPB further recalls that responsibility for assessing whether supplementary measures are necessary in any given transfer context rests with the exporter.

rows.title: FCA Consults on Perimeter Guidance for UK Cryptoasset Regime Ahead of October 2027 Implementation
rows.summary:
The Financial Conduct Authority has launched a consultation, CP26-13, on perimeter guidance clarifying which cryptoasset activities will fall within the scope of the United Kingdom's forthcoming regulatory regime, established under The Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Cryptoassets) Regulations 2026 (SI 2026/102), made by Parliament on 4 February 2026.

On 15 April 2026, the Financial Conduct Authority published consultation paper CP26-13, inviting feedback on its interpretation of five regulated cryptoasset activities: issuing qualifying stablecoin, operating trading platforms, dealing and arranging deals in qualifying cryptoassets, safeguarding cryptoassets, and staking. The FCA states the consultation closes on 3 June 2026, with a final policy statement due in autumn 2026.

The FCA notes that the full cryptoasset regulatory regime is due to come into force in October 2027, with policy statements on the substantive rules to be published in summer 2026. The FCA states that the authorisation gateway opens on 30 September 2026, at which point firms will be able to begin applying for authorisation under the new regime. The FCA finds that the perimeter guidance consultation complements the series of rule-making consultations already conducted, which addressed stablecoin issuance and custody (CP25/14), prudential requirements (CP25/15, CP25/42), application of the FCA Handbook (CP25/25, CP26/4), regulated cryptoasset activities (CP25/40), and admissions, disclosures and market abuse (CP25/41).

The FCA determines that, until the new regime comes into force, cryptoassets remain largely unregulated in the United Kingdom except for financial promotions and financial crime purposes. The FCA notes that further consultations are planned later in 2026, covering decentralised finance guidance, operational resilience guidance for firms using distributed ledger technology, and updates to the Financial Crime Guide relevant to cryptoasset firms. To support prospective applicants ahead of the gateway opening, the FCA states it is hosting a series of authorisation-focused webinars, including sessions on anti-money laundering regulations and the Senior Managers and Certification Regime.

rows.title: NCSC CEO Warns of 'Perfect Storm' for UK Cyber Security Amid AI Advances and Geopolitical Tensions
rows.summary:
The UK National Cyber Security Centre has stated that the United Kingdom faces a convergence of rapid technological change and geopolitical tensions creating a 'perfect storm' for cyber security, with the NCSC finding that the majority of nationally significant incidents now originate from nation-state actors.

On 22 April 2026, Dr Richard Horne, CEO of the National Cyber Security Centre, part of signals intelligence agency GCHQ, delivered a speech at the CYBERUK conference in Glasgow in which the NCSC set out its assessment of the current and emerging threat landscape facing the United Kingdom.

The NCSC finds that the number of incidents it handles remains 'fairly steady', but notes a material shift in their origin. The authority states that the majority of nationally significant incidents it is currently handling originate directly or indirectly from nation states. The NCSC further states that Russia is applying lessons learnt on the battlefield during the illegal invasion of Ukraine and moving them beyond the battlefield, and that cyber operations are now integral to conflict globally. The authority characterises cyberspace as existing in a state 'between peace and war', describing the cyber domain as constituting 'the home front'.

On the question of technological change, the NCSC states that frontier AI is rapidly enabling the discovery and exploitation of existing vulnerabilities at scale, illustrating how quickly it will expose where fundamentals of cyber security remain unaddressed. The authority finds that the definition of cyber security is expanding alongside the technology landscape, and that future cyber security will be required to cover robotics, autonomous systems, and technology physically integrated with the human body, all of which the NCSC describes as 'way beyond' how the discipline was understood a decade ago. The NCSC states that organisations failing to treat their technology base as core to their prosperity 'are no longer just naïve but are failing to grasp the reality of today's world'. The authority calls for a cultural shift within organisations, stating that cyber security is the responsibility of everyone, whether they sit on the Board or the IT help desk, and that cyber security is part of their mission.

rows.title: EDPB Adopts Opinion 9/2026 on Dutch SA Draft Decision Approving Jacobs Douwe Egberts Group Controller BCRs
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board has adopted Opinion 9/2026 under Art. 64(1)(f) GDPR, reviewing the Dutch Supervisory Authority's draft decision on the controller Binding Corporate Rules submitted by the Jacobs Douwe Egberts Group for approval as an international data transfer mechanism.

On 15 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board adopted Opinion 9/2026 pursuant to Art. 63 and Art. 64(1)(f) GDPR, which requires the EDPB to issue an opinion where a supervisory authority aims to approve Binding Corporate Rules within the meaning of Art. 47 GDPR. The opinion concerns the draft decision of the Dutch Supervisory Authority, acting as BCR Lead, regarding the controller BCRs of the Jacobs Douwe Egberts Group. The EDPB notes that no financial penalties are associated with this opinion.

The EDPB notes that, pursuant to Art. 46(1) GDPR, in the absence of an adequacy decision under Art. 45(3) GDPR, a controller or processor may transfer personal data to a third country only where appropriate safeguards are provided and enforceable data subject rights and effective legal remedies are available. The EDPB finds that a group of undertakings may provide such safeguards through legally binding BCRs that expressly confer enforceable rights on data subjects and fulfil the requirements set out in Art. 47(2) GDPR. The EDPB further notes that BCRs are intended to provide guarantees applying uniformly across all third countries, independently of the level of protection guaranteed in each jurisdiction.

The EDPB states that the scope of Opinion 9/2026 is limited to consideration of whether the BCRs submitted afford appropriate safeguards by meeting all requirements of Art. 47 GDPR and the EDPB Recommendations 1/2022 on the Application for Approval and on the elements and principles to be found in Controller Binding Corporate Rules, adopted on 20 June 2023, which the EDPB confirms supersede Article 29 Working Party working documents WP256 rev.01 and WP264. The EDPB states that the opinion does not address other GDPR obligations referenced in the BCRs beyond those relating to Art. 47 GDPR, nor does it address any supplementary measures an exporter subject to the GDPR may be required to adopt depending on the circumstances of specific transfers. The EDPB also recalls, by reference to the Court of Justice of the European Union judgment in Case C-311/18, that responsibility for compliance with transfer obligations rests with the exporting entity.

rows.title: FCA Publishes Open Finance Roadmap, Targeting Regulatory Framework by End of 2027
rows.summary:
The Financial Conduct Authority has published a roadmap setting out its vision for open finance in the United Kingdom, prioritising expanded consumer and business control over financial data and committing to develop a regulatory framework in collaboration with HM Treasury by the end of 2027.

On 14 April 2026, the Financial Conduct Authority published its open finance roadmap, outlining a programme to enable consumers and businesses to share financial data securely with a range of financial services providers. No fines or penalties are associated with this publication. The roadmap identifies potential applications across mortgages, investments, savings, pensions and credit access for small and medium-sized enterprises.

The FCA states that open finance will give financial services firms a more complete picture of consumers' and businesses' finances, enabling more personalised services, more competitive pricing and stronger fraud protection. The authority identifies SME credit access and mortgage management as priority areas for early exploration. To develop practical use cases, the FCA finds that engagement with industry, consumer groups and fellow regulators will be conducted during 2026 through the FCA's Smart Data Accelerator and its PRISM, Prioritisation and Real-world Insights Selection Matrix, Taskforce.

The roadmap notes that the FCA will consult on a proposed long-term regulatory framework for open banking before the end of 2026, distinct from the broader open finance framework that the authority is required to develop with HM Treasury by the end of 2027. The FCA states that firms will be supported to introduce open finance products earlier where they already have access to relevant data and appropriate permissions are in place. Research by Open Banking Limited and EY, cited in the roadmap's accompanying notes, estimates that the combined economic impact of open banking and open finance could reach £7.4 billion per year within five years, with full adoption potentially delivering £43 billion annually. The FCA does not adopt these figures as regulatory determinations but references them to illustrate the scale of the opportunity the roadmap is intended to address. Open banking currently has approximately 17 million users in the United Kingdom, representing close to one in three adults.

rows.title: CNIL Launches FantomApp Application to Educate Adolescents on Social Media Privacy
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has released FantomApp, a digital application directed at adolescents in France, designed to help young users understand and exercise their personal data rights in the context of social media platforms.

On 14 April 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published details of FantomApp, a tool aimed at raising awareness among teenagers about online privacy risks and the protections available to them under French and European data protection law, including the rights of access, rectification and erasure of personal data (Art. 15, 16 and 17 GDPR).

The CNIL presents the application as part of a broader educational programme targeting young people, consistent with the authority's established outreach activities in this area. The initiative follows the authority's earlier publication of "Agence Privacy", a graphic novel produced for the CNIL by Futur Composé, a subsidiary of the association Fréquence Ecoles, in which fictional teenage characters navigate online privacy challenges. The CNIL notes that this publication was deposited in April 2025 under ISBN 978-2-11-179444-3 and was produced under the Law of 16 July 1949 on publications intended for young audiences and licensed under Creative Commons BY-NC-ND 4.0.

The CNIL also notes that the FantomApp release sits alongside existing awareness materials, including the authority's "Cyber Réflexes" guidance, which the CNIL states sets out practical steps for internet users covering password security and timely software updates. The authority frames these resources collectively as tools to enable individuals, and adolescents in particular, to understand how to access their personal data, request corrections and seek deletion, and to know how to act when a problem arises involving their data online. The CNIL confirms that no financial penalties or enforcement proceedings are associated with this publication.
