rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CNIL publishes final recommendation on tracking pixels in emails under Article 82 of the Informatique et Libertés Act
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published its final recommendation on the use of tracking pixels in emails, clarifying the legal framework applicable to both private and public entities under Article 82 of the Informatique et Libertés Act.

On 14 April 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published the final version of its recommendation on tracking pixels in emails, following a public consultation launched in June 2025. No financial penalty is associated with this publication. The recommendation addresses all organisations, whether private or public, including companies, associations, public authorities and local bodies, that use tracking pixels in emails, as well as the technical service providers they engage.

The CNIL finds that tracking pixels, sometimes referred to as spy pixels or web beacons, are typically implemented as a one-pixel image embedded invisibly within an email. The CNIL determines that when such an image loads, it discloses to the sender that the recipient has read the message, thereby constituting a tracking method distinct from cookies but falling within the scope of Article 82 of the Informatique et Libertés Act. The CNIL notes that the use of tracking pixels in emails has grown in recent years and has generated an increasing number of complaints received by the authority, prompting it to clarify the applicable legal framework.

The CNIL determines that the recommendation serves three principal purposes: clarifying the role of each actor involved in the deployment of tracking pixels on a case-by-case basis; distinguishing between pixels that require prior consent and those that are exempt from that requirement under Article 82 of the Informatique et Libertés Act; and specifying the modalities for collecting, withdrawing and evidencing consent. The CNIL finds that the final version of the recommendation recognises an exemption from the consent requirement for individual deliverability measurement of emails linked to a service requested by the person concerned. The CNIL states that the recommendation complements both the European Data Protection Board's guidelines on technologies covered by the ePrivacy framework and the CNIL's existing recommendation on cookies and other trackers, while accounting for the technical and operational characteristics specific to email communications.

The CNIL states that its final recommendation was informed by consultations with professional associations, civil society organisations and data protection authorities, as well as a dedicated consultation on economic considerations, all of which contributed to several clarifications reflected in the published text.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CNIL Fines French Mobile Operator €15 Million and Issues Injunction for Data Security and Breach Notification Failures
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has imposed a €15 million (approximately USD 16.2 million) administrative fine and an injunction on a French mobile telephony operator for failures relating to data retention, data security, and the obligation to communicate a personal data breach to affected individuals, and a separate €5 million (approximately USD 5.4 million) fine and injunction on the same entity for breaching individuals' right of access.

On 8 January 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés issued two decisions against a mobile telephony operator. The first decision, carrying a €15 million fine, concerns three categories of breach: failures in data retention periods, a data security deficiency (Art. 32 GDPR), and a failure to communicate a personal data breach to the individuals concerned (Art. 34 GDPR). The second decision, carrying a €5 million fine, relates to failures in informing individuals of their rights and non-compliance with the right of access (Art. 15 GDPR). Both decisions include an injunction requiring the operator to bring its processing into compliance.

The CNIL's published sanctions list for 2025 records a series of decisions under its simplified procedure against smaller entities across multiple sectors. The CNIL determined that a specialist sports retail company incurred a €20,000 fine on 10 April 2025 for failures in data minimisation, retention periods, individual information, and data security in relation to video surveillance. The CNIL found that a dating website operator targeting individuals with shared political convictions received a €20,000 fine on 30 April 2025 for breaches covering retention periods, consent for the processing of sensitive data, individual information, controller-processor arrangements, data security, and failure to notify a breach to the supervisory authority (Art. 33 GDPR) or to the individuals concerned (Art. 34 GDPR).

The CNIL also records enforcement actions for non-cooperation and non-compliance with prior injunctions. The CNIL issued fines of €5,000 and €10,000 respectively on 3 April 2025 to a convenience retail company and a construction brokerage firm for deficient cooperation with the authority. The CNIL imposed astreinte liquidations of €4,000 each on an energy brokerage and a private security firm for failure to comply with earlier injunctions issued by the CNIL.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CNIL Fines NEXPUBLICA FRANCE €1,700,000 for Inadequate Data Security in PCRM Social Services Software
rows.summary:
France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has fined NEXPUBLICA FRANCE €1,700,000 (approximately USD 1,870,000) for failing to implement sufficient technical and organisational security measures in its PCRM user relationship management software, in breach of Article 32 of the GDPR.

On 22 December 2025, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés imposed a fine of €1,700,000 (approximately USD 1,870,000) on NEXPUBLICA FRANCE, formerly known as INETUM SOFTWARE FRANCE, following investigations into data security failures affecting its PCRM software package, a user relationship management tool used in the social services sector, including by Departmental Houses for the Disabled (Maisons départementales des personnes handicapées, or MDPH).

The CNIL's investigations were triggered in late November 2022, when customers of NEXPUBLICA FRANCE notified the authority of personal data breaches after users of the PCRM portal reported accessing documents belonging to third parties. The CNIL determined that the technical and organisational measures implemented by the company to ensure the security of personal data processed through PCRM were insufficient (Art. 32 GDPR). The restricted committee, the CNIL body responsible for issuing sanctions, found that the company had not complied with Article 32 requirements given the general weakness of the information system and the negligence shown in allowing structural security problems to persist.

The CNIL finds that the vulnerabilities identified in PCRM were largely attributable to a lack of knowledge of the state of the art and basic security principles, and that these vulnerabilities had been known to the company through multiple audit reports prior to the breaches. The CNIL notes that the flaws were corrected only after the data breaches occurred. The restricted committee considered these circumstances to be aggravated by the fact that NEXPUBLICA FRANCE specialises in IT systems and software consulting. In setting the fine, the CNIL took into account the company's financial capacity, its lack of familiarity with basic security principles, the number of individuals affected, and the sensitivity of the data processed, which included information revealing disability status. The restricted committee determined that the company had taken the necessary corrective measures following the data breaches and did not issue a compliance order.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: Court of Justice of the European Union Hears Challenge to Belgian Law on Blanket Retention of Communications Data
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union heard oral argument on 14 April 2026 in Case C-661/24, Académie Fiscale and Others, a preliminary reference concerning whether Belgian legislation requiring blanket retention of traffic and location data from all electronic communications users is compatible with EU law on privacy and personal data protection.

On 14 April 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union convened a hearing in Case C-661/24, referred by the Belgian Constitutional Court. The case concerns a Belgian law obliging operators of electronic communications services to retain traffic and location data from all users for a limited period, with the stated aims of preventing fraud and malicious use of networks and ensuring network security. No fine is at issue in this reference proceeding.

The Belgian Constitutional Court has asked the Court of Justice to determine whether national legislation of this character, requiring operators to retain certain categories of communications data across the entirety of a user base rather than in relation to targeted individuals or groups, is permissible under EU law. The referring court's question centres on the tension between the operational objectives cited by the Belgian legislature and the rights to privacy and the protection of personal data recognised under EU law, including under the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union.

Several professional organisations, fundamental rights associations and individuals brought proceedings before the Belgian Constitutional Court, arguing that the law results in overly broad data retention affecting all users of electronic communications services. The claimants contend that such a generalised retention regime undermines the right to private life and the right to the protection of personal data. The Court of Justice has previously addressed the compatibility of general and indiscriminate data retention obligations with EU law in a line of cases including La Quadrature du Net and Others, and the present reference invites the Court to assess how those principles apply to a retention scheme justified specifically by network security and anti-fraud objectives rather than national security grounds.
rows.body_name: General Court of the European Union

rows.title: Europol Coordinates Global Operation PowerOFF Action Week Targeting 75,000 DDoS-for-Hire Users Across 21 Countries
rows.summary:
The European Union Agency for Law Enforcement Cooperation coordinated a global enforcement action week under Operation PowerOFF, resulting in 4 arrests, the takedown of 53 domains, and the issuing of 25 search warrants targeting distributed denial-of-service-for-hire infrastructure across 21 participating countries.

On 13 April 2026, the European Union Agency for Law Enforcement Cooperation announced the conclusion of the coordinated action week, which directed enforcement and prevention measures against over 75,000 identified criminal users of DDoS-for-hire services. No financial penalties are reported in connection with this action. Participating countries included Australia, Austria, Belgium, Brazil, Bulgaria, Denmark, Estonia, Finland, Germany, Japan, Latvia, Lithuania, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, Norway, Poland, Portugal, Sweden, Thailand, the United Kingdom, and the United States.

Europol finds that leading up to the action week, a series of operational sprints gathered experts from national authorities to act against high-value target users of DDoS-for-hire platforms, referred to as booter services. Europol determined that seized databases enabled Europol experts to provide national counterparts with data on over 3 million criminal user accounts, which in turn supported the coordinated actions carried out during the action week. Europol's operational support included analysis of seized datasets, identification and geolocation of users, dissemination of intelligence packages to all EU Member States and countries with operational agreements, crypto-tracing expertise, forensic assistance, and the establishment of a command post during the action days.

Europol further finds that DDoS-for-hire is among the most accessible forms of cybercrime, enabling individuals with limited technical knowledge to execute attacks using step-by-step tutorials. Europol notes that motivations among users range from curiosity and hacktivism to financial gain through extortion or disruption of competitors. As Operation PowerOFF entered its prevention phase, Europol coordinated campaigns including the creation of targeted search engine advertisements directed at young people seeking DDoS-for-hire tools, the removal of over 100 URLs advertising such services from search engine results, and the transmission of warning messages via blockchains used by criminals to process payments related to their illegal activities.
rows.body_name: European Union Agency for Law Enforcement Cooperation

rows.title: Philippines NPC issues Advisory 2026-01 on data scraping of publicly available personal data
rows.summary: The Philippine National Privacy Commission issued Advisory No. 2026-01 on 13 April 2026, providing guidelines on the data scraping of publicly available personal data. The Advisory addresses the scope of the Data Privacy Act in relation to data scraping practices and conditions for lawful processing.

rows.title: Italy's ACN Publishes NIS Compliance Determinations for New Entities and Platform Access Rules
rows.summary:
Italy's Agenzia per la Cybersicurezza Nazionale (ACN) has published two determinations establishing compliance timelines for entities newly registered under the NIS framework in 2026 and updating access rules for the ACN digital platform, including provisions on the listing of relevant suppliers.

On 13 April 2026, the Agenzia per la Cybersicurezza Nazionale published Determination 127434/2026 and Determination 127437/2026, both examined at the eighth meeting of the NIS Roundtable and made available on ACN's institutional website. ACN states that no financial penalties are associated with these determinations.

ACN states that the first application phase under Article 42 of the NIS Decree concluded at the end of 2025. Determination 127434/2026 establishes the compliance timelines applicable to entities entered for the first time in the NIS register in 2026. ACN finds that the obligation to notify significant incidents, as defined under the baseline specifications set out in Determination 379907/2025, begins on 1 January 2027 for these new entities. Consequently, ACN notes that the designation of a CSIRT contact point is required by the end of 2026. ACN states that the adoption of baseline security measures is required by July 2027 for these newly registered entities.

Determination 127437/2026 updates the terms of use and access to the ACN digital platform and, ACN notes, introduces Article 18 concerning the listing of relevant suppliers. ACN states that this exercise is necessary to identify, from among suppliers, those to be designated as important or essential entities, with the aim of promoting an adequate level of cybersecurity across the supply chains of NIS entities, as provided under Article 3, paragraph 9, letter f) of the NIS Decree. ACN states that the determination also introduces, at Articles 20 and 21, procedural rules governing the listing and categorisation of activities and services in accordance with Article 30 of the NIS Decree.

ACN further states that a determination containing the categorisation model will be published the following week, together with supporting materials to assist entities in conducting a simplified business impact analysis (BIA) during May and June 2026, developed through the sectoral roundtables. ACN notes that the dedicated NIS web area on its institutional site will be updated in the coming days to reflect these developments.

rows.title: EU Agency for Fundamental Rights publishes Article 52 Charter page on scope and interpretation of rights and principles
rows.summary:
The EU Agency for Fundamental Rights has published a dedicated reference page for Article 52 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union, which sets out the scope and interpretation of the Charter's rights and principles, including the conditions under which limitations on those rights are permissible under EU law.

On 10 April 2026, the EU Agency for Fundamental Rights made available its page for Article 52 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union, which governs the scope and interpretation of the Charter's rights and principles.

The Charter provides that any limitation on the exercise of rights and freedoms recognised by the Charter must be provided for by law and respect the essential content of those rights and freedoms. The Charter states that, subject to the principle of proportionality, limitations are permissible only where they are necessary and genuinely meet objectives of general interest recognised by the Union or protect the rights and freedoms of others. The Charter determines that where Charter rights correspond to rights guaranteed by the European Convention on Human Rights, their meaning and scope are the same as those laid down by that Convention, whilst Union law may provide more extensive protection.

The FRA page also addresses the distinction between rights and principles within the Charter. The Charter provides that provisions containing principles may be implemented by legislative and executive acts of Union institutions and member states when implementing Union law, and their invocation before a court is admissible only for interpreting and reviewing the legality of such acts. The FRA notes that determining whether a given matter falls within the scope of Union law, as required by the Charter, is not straightforward in practice.

rows.title: EU Commission publishes first monitoring results under revised Code of Conduct on Countering Illegal Hate Speech Online+
rows.summary:
The European Commission has published the results of the first monitoring exercise conducted under the revised Code of Conduct on Countering Illegal Hate Speech Online+, covering five signatory platforms and incorporating both independent reporter data and platform self-assessments, within the co-regulatory framework of the Digital Services Act (Art. 45 DSA).

On 10 April 2026, the European Commission published the findings from the inaugural monitoring cycle under the revised Code+, which ran from early November to mid-December 2025. The Commission formally endorsed the Code as a code of conduct within the meaning of Article 45 DSA on 20 January 2025, at the request of signatories, integrating it into the DSA co-regulatory framework along with the European Board for Digital Services.

The monitoring exercise, structured as a mystery-shopping stress-test of up to six weeks, assessed whether signatory platforms reviewed the majority, defined as at least 50 per cent, of notifications from monitoring reporters within 24 hours. Of the 12 signatories to the Code+, only five, namely Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, X and YouTube, received relevant notifications from monitoring reporters during the reporting period. The Commission finds that these platforms maintained their commitment to reviewing the majority of notifications within 24 hours and provided systematic feedback to reporters. However, the Commission also finds that all platforms covered by the exercise, with the exception of X, classified a considerable share of cases as either disputed or errors. The Commission notes that a significant proportion of error cases appears attributable to incorrect reporting channels used by monitoring reporters.

Seven of the 12 signatories, Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, TikTok, YouTube, X and Snapchat, have been designated as Very Large Online Platforms or Very Large Online Search Engines under the DSA on the basis of having 45 million or more monthly users in the EU. The Commission states that for these entities, adherence to Code+ commitments is subject to independent annual auditing, and the Code serves as a relevant benchmark for assessing compliance with the risk assessment framework set out in Article 35 DSA. Comprehensive self-assessment reports from all 12 signatories have been made publicly available alongside the monitoring exercise results, in keeping with the Code+'s transparency and accountability commitments.

rows.title: EDPB Annual Report 2025: 414 Cross-Border Cases Initiated and EUR 1.15 Billion in National Fines Recorded
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board published its 2025 annual report on 9 April 2026, setting out the body's activities over the preceding year, including cross-border enforcement data, new guidelines, adequacy opinions, and international cooperation work conducted under its 2024–2027 strategy.

On 9 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board released its annual report covering 2025, as reported by the Estonian Data Protection Inspectorate. According to the EDPB report, data protection authorities across EU member states imposed a combined total of EUR 1.15 billion in fines at national level during the year. The EDPB also recorded 414 cross-border cases initiated and 1,299 one-stop-shop procedures under Art. 60 GDPR, of which 572 resulted in a final decision.

The EDPB states that the body adopted three new sets of guidelines in 2025, addressing pseudonymisation, blockchain technologies, and the intersection of the Digital Services Act with the GDPR. According to the report, the EDPB and the European Data Protection Supervisor issued a joint opinion on the European Commission's proposal to amend the EU legal framework, including certain provisions of the GDPR. The EDPB additionally adopted five adequacy-related opinions concerning the United Kingdom, Brazil, and the European Patent Organisation, alongside recommendations on the legal basis for mandatory user account creation on e-commerce platforms and recommendations relating to the World Anti-Doping Agency's 2027 World Anti-Doping Code.

According to the report, a central development of the year was the adoption of the Helsinki Declaration, which the EDPB states is directed at increasing clarity, support, and engagement to make GDPR compliance more accessible and to strengthen cooperation among stakeholders. EDPB Chair Anu Talus is quoted in the report as stating that the rapidly expanding EU digital regulatory framework has made the data protection ecosystem more complex, and that the EDPB focused on increasing legal certainty and making compliance more achievable in practice. The report also documents the EDPB's continued international engagement, including participation in the G7 Data Protection Authorities Roundtable, the Global Privacy Assembly, and a second online meeting in December 2025 with commissioners and supervisory authority representatives from adequacy decision countries and organisations.

rows.title: China TC 260 Opens Public Consultation on Draft National Standard for Blockchain System Security Implementation
rows.summary:
China's National Technical Committee 260 on Information Security Standardization (TC 260) has released a draft national standard titled "Cybersecurity Technology: Blockchain System Security Implementation Guide" for public consultation, with a submission deadline of 8 June 2026.

On 9 April 2026, the Secretariat of the National Technical Committee 260 on Information Security Standardization published a notice inviting relevant organisations and experts across China to submit comments on the draft standard. The draft, versioned as of 23 March 2026, has been developed following work by the standard's drafting units. TC 260 states that the consultation period is intended to ensure the quality of the standard prior to finalisation.

The notice identifies Zhang Boran as the designated contact person for the consultation process, with responses required to be submitted to the TC 260 Secretariat no later than 8 June 2026. TC 260 notes that the draft standard and its accompanying compilation explanatory notes, dated 25 March 2026, are both available for review by interested parties. The Secretariat is located at the China Electronics Standardisation Institute, 1 Andingmen East Street, Beijing.

TC 260 states that the draft standard falls within the committee's broader programme of developing cybersecurity technical standards under China's national standardisation framework. TC 260 states that the standard addresses security implementation requirements specific to blockchain systems, though the notice does not specify which underlying legislation or existing standards the draft is intended to support or supplement. No further detail on the substantive technical requirements contained within the draft is disclosed in the consultation notice itself. Parties wishing to provide input are directed to submit written feedback via the TC 260 Secretariat's online platform before the stated deadline.
rows.body_name: National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization

rows.title: Partnership on AI Case Study: Italian Union SLC-CGIL Secures Company-Level AI Agreement with Konecta Italia
rows.summary:
Partnership on AI has published a case study examining Italy's first company-level AI agreement, signed in March 2025 between business process outsourcing firm Konecta Italia and the communications union SLC-CGIL (Sindacato Lavoratori della Comunicazione), covering approximately 7,000 employees.

On 9 April 2026, Partnership on AI published the case study, based on interviews with senior representatives of SLC-CGIL, documenting how the agreement was negotiated and what protections it established for call centre workers subject to AI deployment.

Partnership on AI finds that the agreement was facilitated by Italy's existing labour policy framework, which includes sector-level bargaining provisions and laws requiring negotiation over the introduction of new technology tools. Partnership on AI notes that these preexisting legal structures gave SLC-CGIL meaningful leverage during negotiations, enabling the union to secure protections that would not have been readily achievable in jurisdictions with less prescriptive technology bargaining obligations. Partnership on AI finds that unions operating within comparable frameworks can use existing worker data protections as a foundation for new demands relating to AI training data collection and associated surveillance risks.

Partnership on AI determines that the agreement addresses short-term surveillance concerns by establishing safeguards governing how worker data is collected and used for AI training purposes. Partnership on AI further notes that the parties framed AI explicitly as a tool rather than a replacement for workers, a definitional approach which Partnership on AI finds establishes a basis for managing displacement risks within the governance structure of the agreement. Partnership on AI also finds, however, that longer-term risks, particularly those relating to generative AI, data use beyond initial training, and structural job loss, remain difficult to address through collective bargaining alone. Partnership on AI concludes that even where unions are unable to prevent the introduction of AI systems, participation in ongoing governance processes represents an achievable and documented outcome of negotiation, as illustrated by the terms secured by SLC-CGIL in this agreement.

rows.title: Partnership on AI Case Study: Pennsylvania Government and SEIU Local 668 Reach Binding AI Protections Agreement for State Employees
rows.summary:
The Partnership on AI has published a case study examining a binding agreement reached in March 2025 between the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania and SEIU Local 668, establishing AI protections for nearly 10,000 state employees and creating a formal labour-management collaboration structure for AI governance.

On 9 April 2026, the Partnership on AI published the case study detailing how Pennsylvania Governor Josh Shapiro issued a letter in March 2025 outlining eight AI protections and provisions for state employees, the majority of whom hold union-represented positions. Simultaneously, the Governor's Office of Administration negotiated a binding side letter agreement with SEIU Local 668, the full text of which appears as an annex to the case study.

The Partnership on AI finds that the side letter formally establishes a Generative AI labour and Management Collaboration Group, described in the case study as a mechanism for ongoing AI governance as technology evolves. The Partnership on AI notes that the agreement was reached through direct engagement with officials from the Pennsylvania Office of Administration and the president of SEIU Local 668, drawing on perspectives from both the employer and union sides of the negotiation. The Partnership on AI identifies that defining AI as a tool rather than a replacement for workers formed a foundational element of the negotiations, with the case study noting this framing as central to addressing displacement risks while enabling organisational innovation.

The Partnership on AI further finds that the agreement offers broader lessons for public sector AI governance. The case study identifies seven key insights, including the value of building on established labour-management trust, involving workers as subject matter experts in policy development, and pursuing high-level agreements capable of extending protections across multiple unions through what the case study terms patterning or impact bargaining. The Partnership on AI notes that SEIU Local 668's approach included proactive engagement even in instances where unions had not been formally invited to participate in AI discussions, and that the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania's leadership prioritised worker involvement from the outset to support accessible and efficient AI-supported public services.

rows.title: EU AI Office publishes two reports on AI adoption and policymaking on first anniversary of AI Continent Action Plan
rows.summary:
The EU AI Office has published a report on AI adoption in public administration and a Joint Research Centre Science for Policy brief on a decade of EU AI policymaking, marking one year since the European Commission launched its AI Continent Action Plan.

On 9 April 2026, the European Commission published the two documents to coincide with the first anniversary of the AI Continent Action Plan, which is structured around five pillars: computing infrastructure, data, skills, AI adoption, and simplification of AI rules. No fines are associated with this publication.

The AI Office reports that, over the past twelve months, delivery against the Plan has included 19 AI Factories now operational alongside 13 AI Factory antennas, and a call for expression of interest on AI Gigafactories that attracted 76 responses across 60 sites in 16 Member States. The Commission further notes the publication of its Data Union Strategy in November, focused on unlocking data for AI development, and the launch of the European Legal Gateway Office in February 2026 during the AI Impact Summit in New Delhi as part of efforts to attract AI talent. The Apply AI Strategy, published in October 2025, is recorded as having generated dozens of calls worth up to one billion euros across strategic sectors. The Commission also notes its November proposal for an AI Omnibus aimed at simplifying rules and providing legal certainty, alongside the launch of an AI Act Service Desk to assist businesses and organisations in implementing the AI Act.

The report on advancing AI adoption in EU public administration, aligned with the fourth pillar of the Plan, presents a framework structured around three core activities: anchoring AI adoption in EU policies, regulations and principles; adapting the capabilities of public administrations; and applying AI in high-impact domains. The Joint Research Centre's Science for Policy brief, the AI Office states, provides an interpretative analysis of EU AI policymaking since 2018, drawing on collaboration with EU institutions and Member States. The brief finds that the EU has progressively constructed a comprehensive approach intended to foster innovation and competitiveness while safeguarding fundamental rights, market integrity and trust, rather than relying on a single policy instrument.

rows.title: CERT-EU Threat Landscape Report 2025: 174 Threat Actors Tracked, Nine Significant Incidents Responded to Across EU Institutions
rows.summary:
CERT-EU's annual Threat Landscape Report 2025, published on 8 April 2026, finds that the cyber threat environment facing Union entities grew more complex in 2025, with threat actors diversifying methods, expanding targets, and increasing use of artificial intelligence in their operations.

On 8 April 2026, CERT-EU, the cybersecurity body serving EU institutions, bodies and agencies, released its Threat Landscape Report 2025, covering cyber threats observed and incidents handled throughout the year. The report does not carry a monetary penalty but constitutes an authoritative assessment of the threat environment relevant to EU institutional cybersecurity obligations under Regulation (EU) 2023/2841.

CERT-EU reports that it tracked 174 threat actors in 2025, an increase from 110 in the prior year, and determines that Union entities had critical exposure to five of those actors. CERT-EU finds that cyberespionage and prepositioning remained the dominant motive, accounting for 38% of observed activity. CERT-EU states it responded to nine significant incidents during the year, seven of which the authority attributes to vulnerability exploitation, including two zero-day vulnerabilities. CERT-EU reports that 198 software products used by Union entities were targeted, representing an 80% increase compared to 2024, and identifies edge devices, including firewalls, VPNs and network appliances, as the highest-impact entry points.

CERT-EU finds that social engineering techniques expanded beyond email channels in 2025, with voice phishing, AI-generated deepfakes, OAuth abuse and ClickFix attacks all recorded as growing vectors. For the first time, the report analyses threats directed at 90 partner organisations, with CERT-EU determining that public administrations accounted for 60% of that recorded activity. CERT-EU notes that global events, including elections, armed conflicts and sanctions, continued to fuel cyber operations against Union entities and their broader ecosystem. The report includes ten prioritised recommendations covering measures such as phishing-resistant multi-factor authentication and end-to-end encryption for sensitive communications, alongside strategic foresight directed at the 2026 threat environment.

rows.title: Polish Supreme Administrative Court Upholds UODO Order Against Debt Funds for Unlawful Processing of Heir's Personal Data
rows.summary:
Poland's Supreme Administrative Court has dismissed the cassation appeal of BEST Towarzystwo Funduszy Inwestycyjnych S.A., confirming that debt collection entities lacked a legal basis under GDPR to process the personal data of a deceased debtor's potential heir in order to pursue repayment of the deceased's obligations.

On 8 April 2026, the Naczelny Sąd Administracyjny (Supreme Administrative Court) dismissed the cassation appeal brought by BEST Towarzystwo Funduszy Inwestycyjnych S.A. against the judgment of the Provincial Administrative Court in Warsaw of 17 November 2022, which had itself upheld the 2021 decision of the President of the Urząd Ochrony Danych Osobowych (UODO), Poland's data protection authority. No financial penalty was reported in the underlying decision; the authority's order required deletion of the applicant's personal data.

The President of UODO determined that BEST S.A. and BEST III NSFIZ, represented by BEST Towarzystwo Funduszy Inwestycyjnych, had no legal basis permitting them to process the complainant's personal data for the purpose of enforcing a claim that originated with a deceased individual. The authority concluded that the mere death of a debtor does not render that person's descendants automatically liable for the debtor's obligations, and that the processing of heir data on that assumption therefore lacked a lawful basis within the meaning of Article 6 GDPR. The Supreme Administrative Court confirmed this reasoning in full.

The President of UODO further found that neither entity was entitled to determine independently the circle of heirs to a deceased person's estate. The authority stated that establishing who has acquired an inheritance, and to what extent, is a matter reserved for common court proceedings specifically convened to determine the acquisition of inheritance. Only a formal legal determination of that acquisition would, in the President of UODO's view, establish with sufficient certainty the identity of those liable for the testator's debts. The authority also noted a broader pattern in complaints received by UODO, whereby creditors direct repayment demands at individuals who may not have accepted the inheritance or who may have expressly rejected it, in circumstances where no lawful ground for processing their personal data exists.

rows.title: HaDEA publishes overview of EU-funded AI projects under Horizon Europe, Digital Europe and EU4Health programmes
rows.summary:
The European Health and Digital Executive Agency has published an overview of its portfolio of AI-related projects funded under the Horizon Europe, Digital Europe and EU4Health programmes, detailing how those projects contribute to the European Commission's stated goal of positioning the EU as a hub for human-centric and reliable artificial intelligence under the AI Act framework.

On 8 April 2026, the European Health and Digital Executive Agency (HaDEA) published the overview, which sets out the Commission's investment of EUR 200 billion to boost AI development across Europe, including EUR 1 billion annually allocated to the Horizon Europe and Digital Europe programmes. The publication references the AI Act, adopted in 2024 as the world's first comprehensive legal framework on AI, and notes the establishment of the European AI Office to support coherent implementation and enforcement of that regulation.

HaDEA's overview notes that the agency funds and manages hundreds of AI-related projects spanning healthcare diagnostics, manufacturing efficiency, earth observation and agricultural sustainability. Among the projects highlighted is COMPASS AI, co-funded under the EU4Health programme and launched in October 2025. HaDEA states that COMPASS AI brings together clinicians, patients, AI developers, regulators and policymakers to identify challenges and develop evidence-based guidelines for AI deployment in clinical settings. The agency notes the project will validate those guidelines through four pilot studies conducted in real-life clinical environments and will deliver policy recommendations to the Commission aimed at accelerating AI adoption in healthcare at scale.

HaDEA also highlights the concluded Horizon Europe project PRAESIIDIUM, which the agency states combined mechanistic biomedical modelling with AI and real-world electronic health records to enable personalised prediction of prediabetes risk. The agency notes that PRAESIIDIUM used machine learning to produce a faster version of an existing physiological model capable of predicting glucose levels with equivalent accuracy over longer timeframes while requiring reduced computing resources. HaDEA further states the project collected data from a Canadian primary care database covering more than 300,000 patients to develop models predicting individual risk of prediabetes or diabetes from electronic health records. The publication also references the AI Continent Action Plan, adopted in April 2025, and the Apply AI Strategy as the policy frameworks within which these funded projects operate.
rows.body_name: European Health and Digital Executive Agency

rows.title: CERT-EU Publishes Cyber Threat Intelligence Framework for EU Institutions and Bodies
rows.summary:
CERT-EU has published its Cyber Threat Intelligence Framework, establishing the analytical and operational standards the body uses to classify, assess, and prioritise malicious cyber activities affecting European Union institutions, bodies, offices, and agencies and their ecosystem.

On 8 April 2026, CERT-EU released the framework under TLP:CLEAR classification, making it available to its constituents and the broader public. CERT-EU states that the framework sets out a shared reference model intended to support consistent reporting, alerting, and awareness-raising on cyber threat intelligence across Union entities and their designated operational contacts, including primary operational contacts (POCs) and local cybersecurity officers (LCOs).

CERT-EU states that the framework introduces core concepts including malicious activities of interest (MAIs), ecosystem mapping, threat and counter-threat categories, threat domains, threat levels, threat actor levels, tactics, techniques and procedures (TTPs), and scoring mechanisms for adversaries and mitigation. CERT-EU defines an MAI as any adversarial cyber activity with a potential impact for Union entities or their ecosystem, encompassing confirmed compromise, suspicious attempts, adversarial resource development, and reconnaissance activities. CERT-EU notes that all components are aligned with recognised intelligence and cybersecurity standards, including practices drawn from EU cybersecurity regulations, FIRST, NATO, and threat intelligence industry good practices.

The framework also introduces the concept of an ecosystem to address threat visibility beyond constituent networks alone. CERT-EU determines that limiting monitoring to activity within constituent networks would risk missing relevant threats, while attempting to analyse all malicious activity across cyberspace would exceed available resources. CERT-EU defines the ecosystem concept as a set of components reflecting constituent exposure across countries of operation, sectors of activity, geopolitical events of interest, partners, providers, and systems and software. CERT-EU notes that targeting any of these components, for example by breaching a cloud service provider or managed service provider, can affect Union entities through service disruption, loss of data confidentiality, or malicious access to systems. CERT-EU states that the framework may evolve in response to regulatory changes and stakeholder feedback.

rows.title: CNIL publishes 2026 work programme covering AI governance, cross-domain consent and sector-specific GDPR guidance
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published its 2026 work programme, setting out the guidance documents, public consultations and operational resources it intends to produce for public and private sector organisations seeking compliance with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and certain provisions of the EU AI Act (AIA).

On 7 April 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published its indicative work programme for the year, confirming planned outputs across marketing and e-commerce, artificial intelligence deployment, data protection officer support, and the social housing sector. The CNIL notes no fines are associated with this publication.

On consent management, the CNIL states it will launch work on cross-domain consent, examining the conditions under which a single consent can be collected across multiple sites or media, including those belonging to the same group, following earlier recommendations on multi-device consent. The CNIL notes the objective is to reduce repeated consent requests for users, particularly within media groups and multi-brand environments, whilst preserving privacy and freedom of choice in line with consent requirements under the GDPR (Art. 7 GDPR). The authority also states it will clarify the conditions for deploying automatic transcription and analysis tools in call centres and video-conferencing software.

On artificial intelligence, the CNIL states it will finalise guidance on AI use in the workplace and health sectors, addressing algorithmic bias risks and the safeguards required to protect the rights of employees and patients. The CNIL further states it is preparing to be designated as market surveillance authority under the AIA, in addition to its existing role as a fundamental rights protection authority, and that it will clarify the consequences of applying the GDPR to AI models that are not anonymous (Art. 4(1) GDPR). The CNIL notes a public consultation on AI in healthcare, conducted jointly with the Haute Autorité de Santé, is running until 16 April 2026. The CNIL also states it will produce operational content for data protection officers, developed in coordination with DPO associations, covering governance, roadmap planning and documentation organisation, reflecting the advisory role assigned to DPOs (Art. 38 GDPR). The CNIL notes the work programme is indicative and subject to change in response to developments.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: IRDAI Issues Revised Information and Cyber Security Guidelines for All Insurers and Intermediaries
rows.summary:
The Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India has issued the IRDAI Information and Cyber Security Guidelines, 2026, replacing its 2023 framework and setting revised minimum standards and governance requirements applicable to all regulated entities in the Indian insurance sector.

On 6 April 2026, the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI) published Circular Ref No. IRDAI/GA&HR/CIR/MISC/51/4/2026, addressed to all insurers, insurance intermediaries and the Insurance Information Bureau of India (IIB). The circular supersedes the earlier guidelines issued under Circular Ref IRDAI/GA&HR/GDL/MISC/88/04/2023 dated 24 April 2023. IRDAI states that no financial penalty is associated with this guidance instrument.

IRDAI states that the revised guidelines have been issued in view of the evolving threat landscape, industry feedback, and recommendations from various IRDAI committees. The authority finds that the updated framework is intended to enable the insurance industry to further strengthen its defences and related governance mechanisms to address emerging cyber threats. The guidelines, attached as Annexure B to the circular, set out the minimum standards and governance mechanisms to be adopted by all regulated entities, with a summary of major changes from the prior framework provided separately as Annexure A.

IRDAI states that all insurers, including Foreign Reinsurance Branches, and all insurance intermediaries, covering brokers, corporate agents, web aggregators, third-party administrators, insurance marketing firms, insurance repositories, insurance self-network platforms, corporate surveyors, motor insurance service providers and common service centres, along with the IIB, are required to strictly adhere to the guidelines and ensure compliance from the current financial year. The circular was issued under the signature of A. R. Nithiyanantham, Executive Director (GA&HR), at IRDAI's offices in the Financial District, Hyderabad.

rows.title: CERT Polska Annual Report 2025: Incident Handling, APT Monitoring and Fraud Campaigns Across Polish Cyberspace
rows.summary:
CERT Polska, operating within the structures of NASK National Research Institute and executing tasks of CSIRT NASK under the Act on National Cybersecurity System, published its Annual Report 2025 on 3 April 2026, setting out the team's incident-handling activities, threat intelligence findings and operational projects across the preceding year.

On 3 April 2026, CERT Polska released its Annual Report 2025, covering the full calendar year 2025, which also marked the team's 30th anniversary since its establishment in 1996. The report is published under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International Licence by the National Research Institute NASK, Warsaw.

The report details CERT Polska's statutory responsibilities under Article 26 of the Act of 5 July 2018 on the National Cybersecurity System, which CERT Polska finds include monitoring cybersecurity threats and incidents at the national level, responding to reported incidents, coordinating incident handling, performing advanced malware and vulnerability analyses, developing detection tools, and conducting awareness-raising activities. CERT Polska states it coordinates incidents reported by a range of entities including public finance sector units, research institutes, digital service providers and commercial companies performing public service tasks.

With respect to threats, CERT Polska reports on widespread fraud campaigns, mobile malware, ransomware attacks and the observed activities of advanced persistent threat groups. CERT Polska also reports on key vulnerabilities and data leaks identified during the year. Operationally, the report covers the Warning List, SMS fraud countermeasures, coordinated vulnerability disclosure processes under the team's #BezpiecznyPrzemysł programme, web application audits, and security analyses of public sector mobile applications. The report further describes CERT Polska's contribution to Poland's Presidency of the Council of the European Union and participation in international exercises including Locked Shields 2025, alongside ongoing development of its own tools and services, among them Artemis, MWDB, n6, AIPITCH, PERUN, FETTA, DNS4EU and the moje.cert.pl platform.

rows.title: CNIL publishes 2026 municipal elections observatory report covering 739 reports and four investigations
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published its observatory report for the March 2026 French municipal elections, recording 739 signalements and 81 complaints processed during the campaign period, with four formal investigations and one simplified procedure initiated.

On 3 April 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published its post-election assessment covering the first and second rounds held on 15 and 22 March 2026. The CNIL notes that its signalement platform was opened on 30 January 2026 and remained active through the second round. The CNIL reports that no fines have been reported at this stage.

The CNIL finds that 739 signalements were received across the period, with SMS prospecting accounting for the largest share at 405 reports (63 per cent), followed by postal mail at 98 (16 per cent), email at 85 (13 per cent), telephone canvassing at 45 (7 per cent), and social media prospecting at 9 (1 per cent). The CNIL further notes an overall decrease in the volume of signalements and complaints compared with the 2020 municipal elections. Of the 81 complaints processed, the CNIL finds that the principal grounds concerned the origin of personal data used by candidates, and in certain cases, particularly involving incumbent candidates, suspected détournement de finalité, meaning data collected for one purpose being used for political canvassing.

The CNIL determines that four formal investigations have been opened alongside one simplified procedure, the latter targeting a candidate who allegedly failed to respond to a data subject rights request. The CNIL notes that decisions adopted under the simplified procedure are non-public. The authority states that these elections were the first to fall under the EU Regulation on the transparency and targeting of political advertising, which entered into application on 10 October 2025, introducing obligations supplementary to those under the GDPR (Art. 5, Art. 6 GDPR) concerning the use of personal data for online targeting of political advertising. In preparation, the CNIL states it published six practical guidance sheets and a seven-point responsible campaign guide for political actors, as well as dedicated resources and a FAQ for voters setting out their data rights.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CNIL Publishes Retention Period Reference Framework for HR Personal Data Processing
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published a reference framework setting out retention periods for personal data processed in the context of human resources management, applicable to all private and public employer organisations whose personnel are subject to French labour law, under the storage limitation principle (Art. 5(1)(e) GDPR).

On 2 April 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published its reference framework on retention periods for HR data processing activities. The document carries no fine or penalty and involves no currency amounts. The CNIL notes that the framework was developed in collaboration with major professional networks and associations across both the public and private sectors, with the aim of providing an operational tool to assist data protection officers, GDPR referents, HR staff and information systems departments in identifying appropriate retention periods.

The CNIL states that the framework covers the most frequent HR processing activities, organised by treatment category. These include recruitment, personnel administration, remuneration management, security of persons and assets, management of company vehicles, listening to and recording telephone conversations in the workplace, collective labour relations, workplace accident management, litigation and pre-litigation management. The CNIL notes that the framework does not cover retention periods for individual files of public servants, which the authority states are governed separately by the arrêté of 21 December 2012. The framework specifies retention durations in months to align with the formulations used in applicable legislation, though the CNIL notes these may be converted to days within software applications.

The CNIL determines that the framework constitutes "soft law" and compliance with it is not mandatory as a general matter. However, the authority states that certain retention periods listed within the framework are legally binding, as they derive from legislative or regulatory texts, including the Labour Code, the Social Security Code, the General Tax Code, the Highway Code and the Internal Security Code, and that data controllers are not permitted to deviate from those specific periods. The CNIL further notes that the framework addresses French national law only, and that organisations established in multiple member states are required to verify whether other national legislation applies to their operations. The framework is presented as a complement to existing CNIL guidance, including the recruitment guide and the broader HR reference framework previously published by the authority.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: Tanzania PDPC Reminds Mwanza Content Creators of Personal Data Protection Obligations Under the Personal Data Protection Act
rows.summary:
The Personal Data Protection Commission of Tanzania convened a session in Mwanza on 1 April 2026 to remind online content creators in the Lake Zone region of their obligations under the Personal Data Protection Act, Cap. 44, including the requirement to obtain consent from individuals featured in their content and to register with the Commission before the voluntary registration deadline of 8 April 2026.

On 1 April 2026, the Personal Data Protection Commission (PDPC) of Tanzania, through its Head of Public Relations and Communications, Mr Innocent Mungy, addressed content creators in Mwanza city on the application of the Personal Data Protection Act, Cap. 44 to their day-to-day activities. No financial penalties were announced at this session; the engagement was outreach and awareness in nature.

The PDPC states that content creators are directly involved in the collection, processing and distribution of personal data through their ordinary activities. Mr Mungy indicated, on behalf of the Commission, that creators are required to obtain the consent of individuals before featuring them in content and to use personal data only for the purposes for which it was collected, in accordance with Cap. 44. The Commission notes that personal data covered by these obligations includes names, telephone numbers, photographs, locations and financial information. The PDPC further states that failure to protect such data can result in loss of audience trust, reputational harm and exposure to legal consequences under the Act.

The session also served as a reminder to content creators to register with the PDPC ahead of the voluntary registration period closing on 8 April 2026. The Commission states that failure to comply with registration requirements under Cap. 44 may result in adverse legal consequences for non-compliant entities. The PDPC frames registration as a prerequisite for demonstrating accountability in personal data handling, consistent with the obligations applicable to data processors and controllers operating in Tanzania.

rows.title: Togo: IPDCP Issues First CCTV Declaration Receipts and Pictograms Under Online Registration System
rows.summary:
On 1 April 2026, the Instance de Protection des Données à Caractère Personnel (IPDCP) processed the first declarations submitted through its new online registration platform for video surveillance and video protection systems, issuing official receipts and regulatory pictograms to the initial applicants.

The IPDCP recorded the first online declarations through the national public services portal, service-public.gouv.tg, following the platform's official launch on 27 March 2026. The authority notes that no financial penalties are associated with this announcement; the development concerns the operationalisation of a compliance mechanism for video surveillance systems across Togo. Three initial requests, submitted by one company and two private individuals in connection with installations for private use, were processed end-to-end within an optimised timeframe, demonstrating the full operational capacity of the system. The IPDCP's President, Colonel BELEI Bédiani, conducted a formal handover of the first declaration receipts and regulatory pictograms to the applicants, marking the conclusion of the dematerialised process and the entry into force of a reinforced compliance mechanism.

The IPDCP determines that the pictogram constitutes a central and essential requirement for informing data subjects, guaranteeing transparency of processing operations and compliance with fundamental principles of personal data protection, including the right to information and respect for private life. All categories of data controllers are required to register their video surveillance systems through the dedicated platform, including public administrations, companies, non-governmental organisations, associations, and commercial establishments such as shops, bars, hotels, motels and restaurants, as well as private individuals. The online declaration process is designed to be straightforward and secure, forming part of a broader framework promoting responsible use of surveillance technologies in a manner respectful of fundamental rights and freedoms.
rows.body_name: Instance de Protection des Données à Caractère Personnel

rows.title: Togo's IPDCP launches online declaration platform for video surveillance devices
rows.summary:
Togo's Instance de Protection des Données à Caractère Personnel (IPDCP) has launched an online platform for the declaration of video surveillance and video protection devices, replacing paper-based administrative procedures with a digitalised system.

On 27 March 2026, the IPDCP formally inaugurated the platform at the Hôtel Sarakawa in Lomé. The event was attended by the IPDCP President, Colonel Bédiani Beléi, and by the Secretary General of the Ministry of Public Service Efficiency and Digital Transformation, M. Kassime Tidjani, representing Minister Cina Lawson. The IPDCP notes that no financial penalties or fines are associated with this measure.

The IPDCP states that the platform is designed to streamline declaration procedures for operators of video surveillance devices, reduce processing times, and increase the traceability of submitted files. According to the IPDCP President, the digital platform is intended to modernise administrative procedures while ensuring that the use of video surveillance devices remains responsible and compliant with the principles governing the protection of personal data. The authority notes that the platform incorporates a simplified declaration process, a reduced administrative burden for users, and enhanced operational traceability.

The IPDCP further notes that the launch included a practical demonstration for participants, covering the steps of the online declaration process. The authority indicates that the demonstration introduced two key compliance elements: a declaration receipt and a regulatory pictogram, both described by the IPDCP as essential to the lawful and regulated operation of video surveillance devices. The Secretary General, speaking on behalf of the Minister, stated that digitalisation of public services constitutes a strategic lever for bringing the administration closer to citizens and strengthening trust between the State and its users.

Interactive exchanges at the event allowed participants to raise questions, which the IPDCP states reflect its commitment to participatory governance and to facilitating effective adoption of the digital tools it makes available.
rows.body_name: Instance de Protection des Données à Caractère Personnel

rows.title: NIST and CAISI to Convene AI Incident Management Workshop in May 2026
rows.summary:
The National Institute of Standards and Technology, through its centre for AI Standards and Innovation, has announced a workshop on AI incident management to be held on 14 May 2026 at NIST's Gaithersburg campus, intended to inform updates to existing guidelines and the development of new recommendations under America's AI Action Plan.

On 31 March 2026, the National Institute of Standards and Technology, an agency within the U.S. Department of Commerce, published notice of a one-day workshop scheduled for 14 May 2026, running from 8:30 am to 5:30 pm EDT at the Gaithersburg, Maryland facility. NIST states that the event will bring together leaders from the AI and cybersecurity stakeholder communities, including AI developers, service providers, incident responders, critical infrastructure partners, academics, and government stakeholders.

NIST identifies a class of incidents in which AI systems function as both targets of attack and as sources of risk, particularly within critical infrastructure, cybersecurity, and national security contexts. The workshop agenda, as described by NIST, includes the presentation of a high-level NIST roadmap to advance AI incident response standards and practices, stakeholder engagement on existing playbooks and frameworks, exploration of definitions and taxonomies for AI-related incidents, and identification of gaps in current cybersecurity and AI risk management guidance. NIST further notes that the workshop will address emerging AI incident types beyond cybersecurity, including misuse scenarios.

NIST states that outcomes from the workshop will inform future work by its Information Technology Laboratory and the centre for AI Standards and Innovation, including updates to existing guidelines and the development of new recommendations. The authority describes this engagement as part of its effort to implement America's AI Action Plan and to support national and global alignment on AI incident response. No enforcement action, fine, or legal determination is associated with this announcement.
rows.body_name: US Center for AI Standards and Innovation

rows.title: Estonia's Data Protection Inspectorate Reports Record Complaints and Apotheka Fine in 2025 Annual Review
rows.summary:
Estonia's Data Protection Inspectorate (AKI) has published its 2025 annual report, recording a record number of complaints received during the year and highlighting the Apotheka customer data breach as the period's most prominent enforcement case, resolved out of court with a record fine under the GDPR.

On 31 March 2026, AKI published its annual report for 2025, detailing supervisory activity, enforcement outcomes and institutional priorities across the preceding calendar year. The report does not specify the exact fine amount in the source text, though AKI characterises the out-of-court settlement in the Apotheka matter as a record-level financial penalty. No EUR equivalent is provided in the source material.

AKI finds that the record volume of complaints received in 2025 reflects increased public awareness and growing expectations that the state respond to data protection violations more swiftly and consistently. Director General Pille Lehis notes in the report's foreword that the Inspectorate operates in an increasingly complex legal environment, in which new European Union digital regulations are bringing additional roles and expanded responsibilities to the authority. AKI states that the Apotheka case, concerning a leak of customer personal data, concluded in 2025 and exposed serious deficiencies in the implementation of security measures, engaging obligations under Art. 32 GDPR.

AKI identifies key supervisory themes for the year, including employee monitoring, the legal framework for sobriety testing, and the document registers of local authorities, where AKI finds that the public disclosure of sensitive personal data continues to occur. The Inspectorate also notes an increased need for preventive work, having organised regional training sessions, developed data protection networks, and directed particular attention to the education sector and the use of artificial intelligence in schools. AKI states that data protection is not solely a technical requirement but a matter of trust and transparency, and the report sets out a risk-based approach and strengthened preventive activity as the focus of the Inspectorate's new strategic period.

rows.title: NIST Convenes Workshop on AI Incident Management Standards and Practices
rows.summary:
The National Institute of Standards and Technology has announced a workshop on AI incident management, to be held on 14 May 2026 at its Gaithersburg campus, aimed at developing coordinated standards and guidance for responding to AI-related incidents across government, industry and academia.

On 31 March 2026, the National Institute of Standards and Technology, an agency within the U.S. Department of Commerce, published details of the forthcoming workshop, which is scheduled to run from 8:30 am to 5:30 pm EDT. NIST notes that no fines or enforcement actions are associated with this announcement. NIST states that the event will convene stakeholders from the AI and cybersecurity communities to initiate dialogue on scalable approaches to AI incident response.

NIST notes that AI systems are increasingly integral to critical infrastructure, cybersecurity and national security, and that a new class of incidents is emerging in which AI systems function as both targets and sources of risk. The workshop agenda, as set out by NIST, includes the presentation of a high-level NIST roadmap to advance AI incident response standards and practices, engagement with stakeholders on existing playbooks and frameworks, exploration of definitions, lifecycles and taxonomy of AI-related incidents, and identification of gaps in current cybersecurity and AI risk management guidance. NIST further states that the workshop will address emerging AI incident types beyond cybersecurity, including misuse scenarios.

NIST indicates that outcomes from the workshop will inform future work by the Information Technology Laboratory and the centre for AI Standards and Innovation, including updates to existing guidelines and the development of new recommendations, as part of efforts to implement America's AI Action Plan. The intended audience, as identified by NIST, includes AI developers, service providers, incident responders, critical infrastructure partners, academics, cybersecurity professionals and government stakeholders. NIST states that the event is intended to support both national and global alignment on AI incident management.
rows.body_name: National Institute of Standards and Technology

rows.title: TC260 Opens Public Consultation on Security Guidelines for OpenClaw-Class Agent Deployment
rows.summary:
China's National Technical Committee 260 on Information Security Standardization (TC260) has released a draft cybersecurity practice guide setting out security requirements for the deployment and use of OpenClaw-class intelligent agents, opening the document to public consultation until 15 April 2026.

On 31 March 2026, TC260's Secretariat issued notice Wangan Mi Zi [2026] No. 33, formally inviting public comment on the Cybersecurity Standards Practice Guide: Security Guidelines for the Deployment and Use of OpenClaw-Class Intelligent Agents (Consultation Draft). The Secretariat states that the guide was compiled to address security risks arising from the deployment and use of OpenClaw-class agents and to improve the management capabilities of organisations deploying such systems. The consultation is conducted in accordance with the TC260 Secretariat's document management procedures for cybersecurity standards practice guides.

TC260 identifies the purpose of the draft guide as mitigating risks specific to OpenClaw-class agent technologies, a category of AI agent systems. The Secretariat notes that the notice does not detail the substantive technical or administrative requirements contained within the draft, but the document is directed at relevant units and organisations engaged in the deployment and use of such agents. The draft guide is made available as a downloadable attachment alongside the consultation notice on the TC260 portal.

Stakeholders and relevant organisations wishing to submit comments or recommendations are required to do so by 15 April 2026. Responses are to be directed to the TC260 Secretariat, with the designated contact listed as Wang Hansheng, reachable at 010-64102730 or wanghs@cesi.cn. The Secretariat is based at the China Electronics Standardisation Institute, Andingmen East Street, Beijing.
rows.body_name: National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization

rows.title: ICO Publishes Updated Automated Decision-Making Guidance Reflecting Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 Changes
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has updated its detailed guidance on automated decision-making and profiling to reflect amendments to the UK GDPR introduced by the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025, consolidating obligations under the newly inserted articles 22A to 22D.

On 31 March 2026, the Information Commissioner's Office published a revised version of its automated decision-making guidance, incorporating changes to the UK GDPR resulting from the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025. The ICO states that the previous guidance remains available as a PDF and will be withdrawn once updated guidance is finalised following a concurrent consultation.

The ICO notes that the updated guidance addresses how organisations can determine whether their processing falls within the scope of the UK GDPR's article 22A provisions, which govern solely automated decisions with significant effects. The ICO states that automated decision-making, referred to throughout as ADM, is processing where a decision is based solely on automated processing, including profiling, with no meaningful human involvement, and produces a legal or similarly significant effect on a person, described in the UK GDPR as a significant decision (Art. 22A UK GDPR). The ICO clarifies that the term ADM provisions refers collectively to articles 22A to 22D of the UK GDPR.

The ICO states that the updated guidance clarifies when restrictions apply to an organisation's ability to carry out ADM and what conditions are required to be satisfied in those cases. The ICO notes that a new section has been added addressing the safeguards organisations are required to put in place and the rights individuals hold in relation to ADM that affects them. The ICO further notes that the guidance is aimed at data protection officers, compliance professionals and technical leads with oversight of ADM systems, including those deploying in-house tools or solutions provided by external vendors. The ICO states that this guidance will inform a statutory code of practice on artificial intelligence and ADM that the ICO will develop. The ICO notes that the guidance is specific to the UK GDPR ADM provisions and does not address part 3 or part 4 of the Data Protection Act 2018, which govern law enforcement and intelligence services processing respectively.

rows.title: BEREC Publishes Early Assessment of European Commission's Digital Networks Act Proposal, Identifying Concerns Over Centralisation and Legal Uncertainty
rows.summary:
The Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications has published an early assessment of the European Commission's proposed Digital Networks Act, welcoming its updated objectives whilst identifying concerns over centralisation, the Single Passport mechanism, spectrum management, and end-user protections.

On 30 March 2026, the Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications published its formal early assessment of the European Commission's proposal for a Digital Networks Act. No financial penalty is associated with this assessment. The document sets out BEREC's analysis across multiple regulatory domains, from general authorisation and spectrum management to universal service and institutional governance.

BEREC finds that the proposed Single Passport mechanism, which concentrates enforcement responsibilities in the Member State of first notification, risks forum shopping, uneven supervisory burdens and slower enforcement. BEREC determines that the proposal could introduce greater operational complexity, administrative burden and legal uncertainty without offering clear benefits over the existing authorisation regime. On spectrum management, BEREC finds that the proposed single market procedure, which includes a Commission veto, represents a notable trend towards centralisation. BEREC further notes that proposals for unlimited licence duration and automatic renewals risk favouring existing rights holders and reducing market contestability. BEREC supports EU coordination on satellite authorisation but states that alignment with ITU-R rules is required.

Regarding the Significant Market Power regime, BEREC welcomes its preservation alongside symmetric access tools, but finds that proposals including new sequencing rules for remedies, the potential removal of the Recommendation on Relevant Markets, and expanded Commission veto powers could reduce the ability of national regulatory authorities to address national market needs. On numbering resources, BEREC finds that key concepts such as an EU-wide numbering plan and pan-European numbering ranges require further clarification, and that additional analysis of demand, use cases and operational impacts is needed. BEREC also finds that the narrowing of end-user protection scope and limitations on Member States' ability to maintain or introduce stricter measures pose risks to consumer rights, accessibility and affordability.

On institutional matters, BEREC acknowledges the assignment of new roles to it under the draft legislation, including contributing to EU-level merger assessments and issuing guidance on ecosystem cooperation. BEREC states that proposed institutional changes must not dilute the existing technical expertise and autonomy underpinning the current two-tier structure, and that any reinforcement of the Office of Digital Networks' support function is required to enhance, rather than limit, the collective autonomy of national regulatory authorities.
rows.body_name: Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications

rows.title: Greece's National Cyber Security Authority to participate in Cyber Europe 2026 exercise targeting transport infrastructure resilience
rows.summary:
Greece's National Cyber Security Authority is participating in the design and delivery of Cyber Europe 2026, a two-day pan-European cyber crisis exercise scheduled for June 2026, focused on transport sector critical infrastructure under the auspices of the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA).

On 27 March 2026, the National Cyber Security Authority of Greece published an announcement confirming its active role in the national planning and implementation of Cyber Europe 2026. The authority states that the exercise carries no financial penalty element and involves no finding of breach; it is a cooperative preparedness exercise organised by ENISA in collaboration with national cybersecurity authorities and relevant European bodies across EU member states.

The National Cyber Security Authority states that the exercise is designed to strengthen the collective capacity of EU member states to prevent, detect and respond to complex cyber threats. The authority notes that the two-day exercise will centre on cyber crisis scenarios affecting the transport sector, with particular emphasis on critical infrastructure such as ports and railway networks. The authority further states that participants will have the opportunity to develop their skills and prepare effectively for scenarios reflecting the continuously evolving cybersecurity landscape.

The National Cyber Security Authority states that its participation confirms its commitment to reinforcing European cyber resilience. The authority notes that through its involvement it contributes to the development and implementation of strategies intended to ensure the protection of critical infrastructure in the transport sector and to strengthen overall security at both national and European Union level. The authority presents the exercise as a platform for cooperation among participating entities across the EU member state community.

rows.title: Estonian Data Protection Inspectorate Opens Own-Initiative Proceedings Over Website Publishing Personal Data Linked to Court Decisions
rows.summary:
Estonia's Data Protection Inspectorate (AKI) has opened own-initiative supervisory proceedings concerning the website ettevaatust.ee, which publishes personal data alongside references to court judgments, acting under its supervisory powers pursuant to the General Data Protection Regulation.

On 27 March 2026, the Andmekaitse Inspektsioon announced that it is aware of the website ettevaatust.ee and has initiated proceedings on its own motion. No fine has been issued at this stage.

AKI states that all possible further steps will be decided collectively in respect of the website as a whole.
