Accordingly, AKI finds that it will not open separate supervisory proceedings in response to each individual complaint submitted about the site. The authority notes, however, that this approach does not remove an individual's right to submit an objection or other request directly to the operator of the website, nor does it limit the right to bring proceedings directly before a court.

AKI states that individuals whose personal data have been published on the site are required, in the first instance, to contact the data controller, namely the website operator, with any questions, objections or requests relating to the disclosure of their data (Art. 77 GDPR). The authority finds that it is the data controller that bears the obligation and responsibility to explain the legal basis on which data are processed, what data protection conditions apply, and how the rights of data subjects are safeguarded, including the rights to rectification and erasure (Arts. 16 and 17 GDPR). AKI states that the data controller is required to respond to any such request without undue delay and in any event within one month (Art. 12(3) GDPR).

AKI directs individuals seeking further information about their rights to resources published on the authority's own website, covering access to personal data, rectification, erasure, and the submission of objections.

rows.title: LfDI Mecklenburg-Vorpommern and LKA MV Issue Joint Advisory on Extortion Attempts Following BDH Klinik Greifswald Cyber Attack
rows.summary:
The Landesbeauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit Mecklenburg-Vorpommern (LfDI MV) and the Landeskriminalamt Mecklenburg-Vorpommern (LKA MV) have jointly confirmed that patient data stolen in the February 2026 cyber attack on BDH-Klinik Greifswald is being actively used by perpetrators to extort former patients by email.

On 26 March 2026, the LfDI MV and LKA MV published a joint press release advising former patients of BDH-Klinik Greifswald that they may be targeted by unsolicited emails from those responsible for the February 2026 cyber attack on the clinic. The advisory follows an earlier LfDI MV press release dated 25 February 2026, which the authority notes warned citizens to exercise heightened caution following that attack.

The LfDI MV and LKA MV advise that the perpetrators are using stolen patient data to contact affected individuals across multiple channels, including email, telephone and messenger services, with the aim of extorting payments. The joint statement notes that the authorities consider a large volume of patient data to have already been published, and the authorities state that making payments to the perpetrators provides no guarantee that unlawfully published data will be deleted. The advisory further notes that the authorities state payment typically results in additional demands from the same perpetrators or from other criminal actors to whom the data may have been passed.

The LfDI MV and LKA MV set out four specific steps for affected individuals: to refrain from responding to any communications from the perpetrators, as doing so would confirm contact details and increase exposure to further extortion; to make no payments in response to demands; to avoid opening email attachments or clicking links contained in such messages, given the risk of malware execution or download; and to report any extortion attempt via the online reporting portal of the Mecklenburg-Vorpommern state police at portal.onlinewache.polizei.de, attaching the relevant email where possible, or alternatively at any local police station. The authorities state that further information on the criminal investigation cannot be disclosed for tactical reasons, and that press enquiries on that matter are to be directed to the Staatsanwaltschaft Rostock.

rows.title: China's TC 260 Establishes AI Safety Standards Working Group and International Cybersecurity Standards Study Group
rows.summary:
China's National Technical Committee 260 on Information Security Standardization (TC 260) has established two new subordinate bodies, the AI Safety Standards Working Group (WG9) and the Cybersecurity International Standards Study Group (SG1), following approval at the committee's first director's office meeting of 2026.

On 26 March 2026, TC 260 issued notice 20260327-网安字【2026】6号, formally constituting WG9 and SG1 with effect from 27 March 2026. According to the notice, no financial penalties are associated with this administrative decision.

TC 260 states that WG9 is mandated to survey the current landscape and development trends in AI safety standardisation, develop proposals for an AI safety standards framework, and conduct research and drafting of AI safety standards. The notice designates Zhou Bowen, Director of the Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, as chair of WG9, with four deputy chairs drawn from the National Computer Network Emergency Response Technical Team and Coordination Centre, the China Academy of Information and Communications Technology's AI Research Institute, the Ministry of Public Security's Third Research Institute, and the China Information Security Evaluation Centre.

TC 260 states that SG1 is responsible for tracking developments in international cybersecurity standardisation, conducting comparative analysis between domestic and international cybersecurity standards, and organising research and development of China's proposals for international cybersecurity standards. The notice designates Lin Ning, a researcher at the China Electronics Standardisation Institute (CESI), as chair of SG1, with deputy chairs from CESI's International Cooperation Division and Cybersecurity Research Centre, Huawei Technologies' chief network security standards office, and Beijing University of Technology. The notice confirms that full membership lists for both groups are attached as an annex to the official document.
rows.body_name: National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization

rows.title: Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium Reports 70% Rise in Incident Notifications and Faster Exploitation Windows in 2025 Annual Review
rows.summary:
The Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium recorded a 70% increase in total incident notifications in 2025, attributing the rise in part to expanded reporting obligations under NIS2 and DORA alongside sustained growth in hostile cyber activity across Belgian organisations.

On 26 March 2026, the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB) published its annual cyber threat review covering the 2025 reporting period. The CCB recorded 635 total incident notifications, of which 556 were classified as cyber-related, representing a 58% year-on-year increase in cyber incidents. The CCB reports that no fines were issued in connection with this report.

The CCB finds that account compromise was the most frequently reported incident category, accounting for 144 cases, with credential theft and abuse identified as the primary drivers. The CCB states that the Safeonweb BePhish platform processed approximately 10 million suspicious emails during the year, which the CCB states reflects the industrialisation of phishing campaigns. The authority notes that attackers increasingly combined email with messaging platforms, deployed CEO fraud scenarios to trigger urgent payments, and introduced techniques such as ClickFix and FileFix, in which users are manipulated into executing malicious commands directly.

On ransomware, the CCB notes that incident volumes remained relatively stable at 105 cases in 2025 compared with 109 in 2024, but finds that impact has intensified, with attackers combining data encryption, data exfiltration and escalating extortion tactics. The CCB finds that following the takedown of LockBit, the ransomware ecosystem has fragmented, with groups including Qilin, Akira and Clop identified among those most active against Belgian targets. The CCB also finds that Belgium was among the European countries most frequently targeted by pro-Russian hacktivist groups through distributed denial-of-service attacks, with group NoName057(16) launching five coordinated campaigns, often timed around geopolitical events. The authority states that the CCB's Red Button coordination procedure, enabling real-time response between victims, internet service providers, hosting providers and authorities, contributed to limiting the operational impact of these attacks.

The CCB further finds that the average time-to-exploit window between vulnerability disclosure and active exploitation fell to five days in 2025, with approximately one-third of vulnerabilities exploited within 24 hours. The authority states that the CCB conducted 103 emergency response operations and issued 32,005 targeted spear warnings, a 42% increase on the prior year.

rows.title: Italian Garante Reprimands Municipality for Unlawful Disclosure of Employee Sick-Leave Certificate
rows.summary:
The Garante per la protezione dei dati personali has issued a reprimand against an Italian municipality for disclosing the full electronic sick-leave certificate of its secretary-general to external and internal recipients without a valid legal basis, in violation of Arts. 5(1)(a), 5(1)(c), 6 and 9 of the GDPR and Art. 2-ter of the Italian Privacy Code (d.lgs. 196/2003).

On 26 March 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali adopted decision no. 201, finding that the municipality had processed health-related personal data unlawfully when it attached a copy of the complainant's electronic sick-leave certificate to a formal request addressed to the Prefettura and to the secretary-general of a second municipality, seeking authorisation for a temporary replacement during the complainant's absence.

The Garante finds that the municipality transmitted the certificate to recipients that the complainant contended had no entitlement to access it, and that the document was also accessed internally by a protocol officer and staff in the secretariat. During the investigation, the municipality acknowledged that attachment of the certificate had been an error, stating that the employee who prepared the communication had been assigned from another office as a temporary substitute and that previous requests of the same kind had never included such attachments (Art. 5(1)(c) GDPR). The municipality further represented that the Prefettura, as the body responsible for managing the register of municipal secretaries and the relevant personnel files, was authorised to process the complainant's personal data, and that the certificate had been forwarded not to the general address of the second municipality but solely to the certified email address of the replacement secretary. The municipality also contended that the certificate, by its design, contained no diagnostic information and disclosed only a generic attestation of absence.

The Garante finds that these circumstances do not eliminate the unlawfulness of the processing. The Garante determines that transmission of the certificate to external recipients and its internal circulation occurred in the absence of an adequate legal basis and in breach of the principles of lawfulness, fairness, transparency and data minimisation (Arts. 5(1)(a) and 5(1)(c) GDPR). The Garante finds that the processing of data classifiable as health data required a specific legal basis under Art. 9 GDPR, which was not established on the facts. The Garante notes that the municipality did not request a hearing before the authority during the proceedings conducted under Art. 166(5) of the Privacy Code.
rows.body_name: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rows.title: Italian Garante Fines Fix It S.R.L.S. for Unlawful Marketing Emails and Failure to Respond to Access Request
rows.summary:
The Garante per la protezione dei dati personali has issued a corrective decision against Fix It S.r.l.s., operator of the platform www.megamta.com, finding violations of the GDPR and the Italian Privacy Code arising from unsolicited commercial communications sent without a valid legal basis and a failure to respond to a data subject access request.

On 26 March 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali adopted decision no. 211, registering the matter under document reference 10241455. The proceedings originated from a complaint lodged on 27 September 2025 by an individual who reported receiving an unauthorised commercial email from Fix It S.r.l.s., a company registered in Borgo Veneto, Padua, and who alleged that his access request submitted on 30 July 2025 had received no reply.

The Garante finds that Fix It S.r.l.s. processed the complainant's personal data for marketing purposes without being able to demonstrate a lawful basis for that processing, in breach of Art. 5(1)(a), Art. 5(2), and Art. 6 GDPR, as well as Art. 130 of the Italian Privacy Code (Legislative Decree 196/2003, as amended). The Garante determines that the company's general assertion that its platform is intended solely to send communications to recipients who have accepted and confirmed they wish to receive them from MegaMTA does not constitute adequate evidence that the complainant's consent was obtained and relied upon as the legal basis for the processing in question. The Garante further finds that the company failed to respond to the complainant's access request within the required timeframe, in breach of Art. 12 and Art. 15 GDPR.

In its defence submissions of 23 February 2026, Fix It S.r.l.s. contended that it had not received the complainant's email exercising his rights and noted that the complainant had unsubscribed from its systems on 21 September 2025, after which no further commercial communications were sent. The company also indicated that data access is available at any time through the personal area of the MegaMTA portal, accessible via its email communications. The Garante determines that these arguments are insufficient to exclude liability for the contested violations, noting that no specific information was provided regarding the legal basis underpinning the processing of the complainant's data, and that, in the absence of such evidence, the processing is to be regarded as unlawful (Art. 5(1)(a) GDPR).
rows.body_name: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rows.title: Tanzania PDPC Sets 8 April 2026 Deadline for Mandatory Registration, Warns of Fines Up to TZS 5 Billion (approx. EUR 1.75 million / USD 1.9 million)
rows.summary:
Tanzania's Personal Data Protection Commission has announced that the voluntary registration period for entities collecting and processing personal data closes on 8 April 2026, after which the PDPC states enforcement under the Personal Data Protection Act (Cap. 44) will commence, carrying fines of up to TZS 5 billion (approximately EUR 1.75 million / USD 1.9 million) for non-compliant organisations.

On 26 March 2026, the Personal Data Protection Commission (PDPC) issued a final public reminder to all public and private institutions in Tanzania, stating that the voluntary registration window expires on 8 April 2026. The PDPC states that enforcement proceedings will begin from 9 April 2026, at which point any entity found to have failed to register in the PDPC system will face legal liability under the Personal Data Protection Act, Cap. 44.

Speaking at a press conference, PDPC Director General Dr Emmanuel Lameck Mkilia stated that the government had provided sufficient time since April 2024, when President Samia Suluhu Hassan officially launched the Commission. The PDPC notes that the registration deadline was extended on two separate occasions, with the final extension determined by the Minister for Communications and Information Technology, Angellah Jasmine Mbelwa Kairuki, who set the current deadline of 8 April 2026.

The PDPC states that penalties under Cap. 44 are tiered by category of offender. For individuals, the Commission notes that sanctions include a fine of up to TZS 20 million (approximately EUR 7,000 / USD 7,600) or a custodial sentence of up to ten years. For institutions and companies, the PDPC states that fines range from TZS 1 million to TZS 5 billion (approximately EUR 350 to EUR 1.75 million / USD 380 to USD 1.9 million), depending on the nature of the violation. The PDPC confirms that the law applies across all sectors, including government ministries and departments, banks, insurance companies, hospitals, schools, universities, telecommunications companies, industries, the tourism sector, hotels, transport companies, construction firms, political parties, trade unions, religious organisations, and non-governmental organisations.

rows.title: Italian Garante Orders Removal of Judicial Document Posted on Facebook, Finds Unlawful Processing of Criminal Data
rows.summary:
The Garante per la protezione dei dati personali has ruled against Nicolò Cristaldi, finding that his publication of a criminal penalty decree on his personal Facebook profile constituted unlawful processing of judicial personal data in breach of the GDPR and applicable Italian law, and ordering removal of the content.

On 26 March 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali issued Provvedimento n. 215, following a complaint lodged on 30 July 2025 under Art. 77 GDPR by an individual identified as the subject of a criminal penalty decree. The Garante finds that Cristaldi published on his Facebook profile a document dated 21 July 2025 communicating the issuance of a decree of criminal conviction. The complainant argued that the publication constituted unlawful processing of judicial data without valid legal basis and was damaging to his reputation. An authority information request was sent to Cristaldi on 9 September 2025 pursuant to Art. 157 of the Italian Privacy Code, but received no response. The Garante finds that failure to respond constitutes an independent administrative infringement.

In defensive submissions filed on 12 February 2026, assisted by the Guardia di Finanza's Nucleo speciale privacy, Cristaldi stated he had not viewed the certified email sent by the authority, as it had been addressed to his company and subsequently deleted by the company's accountant. Cristaldi contended that the publication was lawful as an exercise of the right to report on matters of public interest, given the judicial proceedings involving the complainant. The Garante, however, finds that the publication breached the principles of lawfulness, fairness and data minimisation under Art. 5(1)(a) and (c) GDPR, as well as Art. 137 of the Code and Art. 6 of the Deontological Rules for journalistic processing of personal data, on the basis that the content exceeded what the principle of essentiality of information permits in journalistic activity.
rows.body_name: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rows.title: Garante Reprimands Il Gazzettino for Failure to Respond to Data Subject Rights Request
rows.summary:
Italy's Garante per la protezione dei dati personali has issued a reprimand against Il Gazzettino S.p.A. for failing to respond to a data subject's rights request within the statutory timeframe, finding a violation of Articles 5(2), 12(2) and (3), and 24(1) and (2) of the GDPR.

On 26 March 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali adopted Provvedimento n. 216, addressing a complaint filed pursuant to Art. 77 GDPR and formalised on 14 October 2025. The complainant, a general practitioner, alleged that Il Gazzettino S.p.A. had published an article identifying her by full name in connection with her refusal to continue treating two patients. The complainant submitted a preliminary request to the data controller on 29 July 2025, to which she received no response prior to filing her complaint.

The Garante finds that Il Gazzettino S.p.A. failed to provide any response to the complainant's exercise of rights within the one-month period required under Art. 12(3) GDPR. The authority noted the controller's explanation that the delay resulted from the complainant's use of a certified email address rather than the dedicated privacy contact address, compounded by the request arriving during the summer holiday period and the illness and death of the journalist responsible for the article. The Garante determined that these circumstances do not constitute sufficient justification for the failure to respond, as the controller bears accountability for ensuring that rights requests are managed effectively regardless of the channel used.

The authority acknowledged that following notification of the complaint, the controller removed the complainant's name from the digital archive version of the article and subsequently took the article offline entirely. The Garante finds that the inclusion of the complainant's full name was not essential to the informational purpose pursued by the article. The Garante determined that the decision takes the form of a reprimand under Art. 58(2)(b) GDPR with no financial penalty recorded.
rows.body_name: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rows.title: CJEU Rules Directive 2012/29/EU on Victims' Rights Inapplicable Where Criminal Offence Occurred Outside EU Jurisdiction
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union has ruled, in Case C-239/24 (Aurnois), that Directive 2012/29/EU establishing minimum standards on the rights, support and protection of victims of crime does not apply where the underlying criminal offence was committed outside the European Union and proceedings are conducted under a Member State's extraterritorial jurisdiction.

On 26 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union (Third Chamber) delivered its judgment in response to a request for a preliminary ruling from the Cour d'appel de Bruxelles, made by decision of 12 December 2023. The case concerns proceedings initiated in Belgium by FP and LD, family members of SX, a Saudi national and women's rights activist, against several leaders of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia for crimes against humanity allegedly committed against her. No financial penalties were at issue in this matter.

The Court finds that the central question concerns the interpretation of Article 2(1)(a)(i) of Directive 2012/29/EU, which defines a 'victim' as a natural person who has suffered harm directly caused by a criminal offence. The Court notes that Recital 13 of the Directive expressly states that the instrument applies in relation to criminal offences committed in the Union and to criminal proceedings that take place in the Union, and that it confers rights on victims of extraterritorial offences only in relation to criminal proceedings taking place in the Union. The Court determines that this framing is material to the scope of the definition of 'victim' within the meaning of Article 2(1)(a).

The Court further finds that Directive 2012/29/EU was adopted on the legal basis of Article 82(2) TFEU, which confers EU legislative competence to establish minimum rules in criminal matters to the extent necessary to facilitate mutual recognition of judgments and judicial cooperation having a cross-border dimension. The Court determines that where a Member State exercises extraterritorial criminal jurisdiction over offences committed entirely outside the Union, the legislative competence underpinning the Directive does not extend to require that Member State to apply the Directive's victim-protection standards in such proceedings. The Court accordingly rules that Directive 2012/29/EU is inapplicable in circumstances where the criminal offence forming the basis of proceedings was not committed within the territory of the European Union.

rows.title: German Data Protection Authorities Issue Joint Guidance on Tenant Data Collection by Landlords
rows.summary:
The German Federal and State Data Protection Authorities, coordinated under the leadership of the North Rhine-Westphalia Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information (LDI NRW), have published joint guidance setting out which personal data landlords are permitted to collect from prospective tenants at each stage of the rental process, establishing a nationally applicable framework under the GDPR.

On 24 March 2026, the Datenschutzkonferenz, the body comprising all German federal and state data protection authorities, released Version 2.0 of its guidance on the collection of self-disclosures from prospective tenants. The ten-page document carries national validity across all German jurisdictions and does not involve a financial penalty.

The LfDI Mecklenburg-Vorpommern finds that the guidance distinguishes permissible data collection according to the stage at which information is requested within the rental process. The authority notes that at a property viewing, landlords are generally not permitted to request information on a prospective tenant's financial circumstances. The authority determines that once a landlord is considering a specific applicant, that person is required to answer questions concerning any opened insolvency proceedings. Where a tenant has been selected for a tenancy, the authority finds that landlords may additionally request evidence of creditworthiness, but only such evidence as is strictly necessary for the conclusion of the rental agreement (Art. 6(1)(b) GDPR). The authority finds that comprehensive credit reports obtainable from credit reference agencies, which frequently contain additional information beyond what is necessary, may not be demanded.

The authority finds that the guidance addresses two central legal questions that must govern any data collection by landlords: whether the landlord has a legitimate interest in the data, or whether collection is necessary for the performance of the rental contract (Art. 6(1)(f) and Art. 6(1)(b) GDPR), and whether the prospective tenant's right to data protection precludes collection. The authority clarifies the conditions under which collected data is required to be erased. Sebastian Schmidt, LfDI Mecklenburg-Vorpommern, noted that the guidance creates legal certainty for landlords and clarity for prospective tenants across Germany. Schmidt presented the guidance to housing industry representatives at the annual Regional Conference on the Housing Sector in Mecklenburg-Vorpommern in Rostock on 25 March 2026.

rows.title: UK NCSC Publishes Guidance on Cloud-Hosted SCADA for Operational Technology Organisations
rows.summary:
The UK National Cyber Security Centre has published guidance on cloud-hosted Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) systems, setting out three critical assessment areas for operational technology organisations considering cloud migration.

On 24 March 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) published new guidance addressing the migration of SCADA solutions to cloud environments by operational technology (OT) organisations. The guidance carries no associated fine or enforcement action and does not reference a specific legislative basis, operating instead as technical and risk-management guidance directed at OT operators, including critical national infrastructure (CNI) operators.

The NCSC notes that, through engagement with industry, it has observed a clear shift in attitudes towards using cloud platforms for industrial applications. Where cloud adoption was previously dismissed by many OT organisations due to perceived risks, the NCSC finds that organisations in this sector are increasingly exploring cloud-based solutions. The NCSC states that any migration decision is required to be informed by each organisation's unique risk profile and specific technical requirements.

The NCSC identifies three critical areas that OT organisations are required to assess before deciding on a SCADA cloud migration: understanding business drivers and cloud opportunities; organisational readiness; and technology and cloud solution suitability. The guidance further identifies particular challenges facing OT organisations, including reliance on legacy equipment, on-premises solutions, and monolithic software packages, all of which the NCSC finds can complicate the path to secure cloud implementation. The NCSC notes that securing a cloud platform is a problem shared with information technology functions and states that the new guidance is intended to be used alongside its existing cloud security guidance collection and its using-cloud-services-securely guidance. The guidance was developed with input from industry bodies, CNI operators, and vendors within the SCADA community.

rows.title: AEPD Sets Out Personal Data Breach Notification Requirements Under Art. 33 GDPR
rows.summary:
The Agencia Española de Protección de Datos has published guidance clarifying the obligations of data controllers to notify personal data breaches to the supervisory authority under Article 33 of the GDPR, including the applicable 72-hour deadline and the conditions determining AEPD's territorial competence.

On 24 March 2026, the Agencia Española de Protección de Datos published updated guidance on its website setting out the procedural and substantive requirements for breach notification to the supervisory authority. No fine was associated with this publication.

The AEPD states that a personal data breach constitutes a security incident resulting in the accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to, personal data processed by a controller. The AEPD notes that such incidents may cause physical, material or immaterial harm to individuals. The AEPD states that Article 33 GDPR obliges controllers to notify the competent supervisory authority when a breach is likely to pose a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons, and that where the risk is high, controllers are additionally required to communicate the breach to affected individuals in accordance with Article 34 GDPR. The AEPD further states that the notification deadline is 72 hours from the moment the organisation becomes aware of the breach.

The AEPD states that the conditions under which it is the competent authority for private-sector controllers are: where the controller's sole establishment is in Spain; where the controller has multiple EU establishments and its main establishment is in Spain; or, where the controller has no EU establishment, where affected individuals are located in Spain. For public-sector entities, the AEPD states that public administrations are generally required to notify it of breaches, with exceptions applying to certain autonomous communities in relation to public-sector entities under their own competence.

The AEPD states that notifications are required to be submitted electronically via the breach notification form on the Sede Electrónica, in order to meet the requirements of Article 33.3 GDPR. The AEPD further states that where a controller determines no risk to individuals exists, the controller nonetheless remains obligated to document the breach, its effects and any corrective measures taken, enabling the AEPD to verify compliance with Article 33 GDPR. The AEPD notes that timely notification constitutes evidence of a controller's diligence, while failure to notify where the obligation applies is classified as an infringement under the GDPR.
rows.body_name: Agencia Espanola de Proteccion de Datos

rows.title: ANSSI Opens Public Consultation on Reference Architectures for Security Supervision Doctrine
rows.summary:
France's Agence nationale de la sécurité des systèmes d'information (ANSSI) has launched a public call for comments on the reference architectures underpinning its forthcoming security supervision doctrine, targeting a broad range of ecosystem stakeholders, with a response deadline of 7 May 2026.

On 19 March 2026, ANSSI published a consultation document and accompanying online questionnaire inviting responses from across the cybersecurity ecosystem. The call for comments addresses the architectural and technical components of security supervision, which ANSSI defines as the totality of means and activities contributing, within the shortest possible timeframe, to the detection and qualification of a security incident on a supervised perimeter, and to the selection of an appropriate response.

ANSSI identifies four categories of intended respondents: beneficiaries of the Agency's support, including operators of vital importance (OIV), operators of essential services (OSE), ministries, and regulated entities in general; security supervision service providers, in particular those operating as PDIS (prestataires de détection des incidents de sécurité) and MSSP service providers; editors of software and hardware supervision solutions, including those already qualified or seeking recognition by ANSSI; and any other actor with relevant expertise in security supervision. ANSSI states that the consultation document presents a range of reference architectures intended to cover the principal information system contexts subject to supervision, with ANSSI noting that the forthcoming guides will address cloud hosting, service delegation, and technological diversity among supervision products.

ANSSI states that the consultation feeds into two technical guides currently being drafted, titled 'Architectures and Deployment' and 'Technologies and Implementation', which form part of a broader 'Security Supervision' collection. ANSSI further states that two guides in the collection, one at strategic level and one at operational level, were already published in 2025. ANSSI further states that the security supervision guides will serve as the basis for a new PDIS qualification framework. ANSSI clarifies that the consultation does not call into question existing regulation or the current PDIS v2.0 qualification framework, both of which remain applicable. Responses are to be submitted via an online questionnaire no later than 7 May 2026.

rows.title: CJEU Rules Article 15 GDPR Access Requests Made Solely to Engineer Compensation Claims May Be Refused as Abusive
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union has ruled that a first request for access to personal data under Article 15 GDPR may be refused as abusive where the controller demonstrates the request was made to artificially create conditions for a compensation claim under Article 82 GDPR, rather than to verify lawfulness of processing.

On 19 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union delivered its judgment in Case C-526/24 (Brillen Rottler) in response to a reference from the Local Court, Arnsberg, Germany. The case concerned an individual who subscribed to the newsletter of Brillen Rottler, an optician, thirteen days before submitting an access request under Article 15 GDPR. Brillen Rottler refused the request, citing evidence that the individual had systematically subscribed to newsletters of multiple companies before lodging access requests and subsequent compensation claims.

The Court finds that a first access request may qualify as abusive within Article 12(5) GDPR where the controller demonstrates the request was made not to verify lawfulness of processing but to artificially engineer conditions for compensation. The Court determines that relevant circumstances include whether the data subject provided personal data without obligation to do so, the time elapsed between provision and the access request, and the overall conduct of the data subject. The Court states that numerous access requests to various controllers followed by compensation claims may be considered in establishing abusive intent.

The Court further finds that a data subject claiming compensation under Article 82 GDPR for infringement of the right of access is required to demonstrate actual material or non-material damage. The Court determines that compensation cannot be awarded where the data subject's own conduct is the determining cause of the damage alleged.

rows.title: CJEU Rules First Access Request Cannot Be Refused as Excessive Under GDPR
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union has ruled that a controller cannot refuse a first-time access request under Article 15 GDPR on grounds of being excessive, and that a data subject may claim compensation for non-material damage in the form of loss of control over personal data.

On 19 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union (Fourth Chamber) delivered its judgment in Case C-526/24, Brillen Rottler GmbH & Co. KG v TC, following a request for a preliminary ruling lodged by the Amtsgericht Arnsberg on 31 July 2024. The proceedings arose from an optician company's refusal to provide access to an individual's personal data, with the company contending that the request was excessive within the meaning of Article 12(5) GDPR.

The Court finds that Article 12(5) GDPR, read in conjunction with Article 15(1) GDPR, does not permit a controller to refuse a first access request by characterising it as excessive. The Court determines that the concept of an "excessive" request presupposes a repetitive character or manifest lack of good faith, neither of which can be established on the basis of a single, initial request alone. The Court further determines that a controller seeking to invoke excessive character bears the burden of demonstrating that the request manifestly meets that threshold.

On liability, the Court addresses Article 82(1) GDPR and finds that unlawful refusal of an access request may, in principle, give rise to non-material damage in the form of loss of control over personal data, as contemplated by recital 85 GDPR. The Court notes that the mere infringement of the GDPR does not automatically establish the existence of damage: the data subject is required to demonstrate that actual non-material damage was suffered as a result of the infringement. The Court affirms that compensation under Article 82(1) GDPR serves a compensatory rather than punitive function, and that national courts must assess the existence and extent of damage in each case.

rows.title: CJEU Advocate General: Unlicensed Sports Betting Operator May Owe Refunds to German Player
rows.summary:
Advocate General Nicholas Emiliou of the Court of Justice of the European Union has issued an Opinion in Case C-530/24 (Tipico), finding that EU law does not preclude Germany from imposing civil-law consequences, including refund obligations, on a sports betting operator that offered services without the required national licence.

On 19 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union published the Advocate General's Opinion in Case C-530/24, arising from a German consumer's claims against Maltese operator Tipico to recover stakes wagered and lost on Tipico's German website between 2013 and 9 October 2020. Tipico held a Maltese licence at the relevant time but not a German licence, as required under German law.

The Advocate General finds that a Member State licensing requirement for the provision of gambling services is compatible with the freedom to provide services, and that national authorities, including civil courts, are entitled to enforce that requirement against operators providing unlicensed services. The Advocate General finds that courts may apply civil-law consequences provided for under national law, including nullity of contracts with clients, which may give rise to an obligation to refund stakes. The Advocate General finds that such consequences are proportionate to the consumer-protection objective and contribute to dissuading operators from circumventing licensing systems.

The Advocate General identifies one exception. Where authorised sources within national authorities provided an operator with precise, unconditional and consistent assurances that the licensing requirement would not be enforced, the Advocate General finds it would be disproportionate to impose civil-law consequences for breach of that requirement. The Advocate General finds that in such circumstances, no fault could be imputed to the operator, and any liability for consumer harm could only be borne by the public authorities that gave those assurances. The Advocate General confirms that whether such assurances were in fact given is a matter for the German Federal Court of Justice to determine. The Opinion is not binding on the Court of Justice.

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion on Cybersecurity Act 2 and NIS2 Amendment Proposals
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor have adopted a Joint Opinion on the European Commission's proposals for a Cybersecurity Act 2 and targeted amendments to the Network and Information Security 2 Directive, identifying areas of support and recommendations to align the proposals with data protection requirements under the GDPR and Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

On 19 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor published their Joint Opinion following a formal consultation request from the Commission dated 21 January 2026. The Commission's underlying cybersecurity package was published on 20 January 2026 with the stated aims of strengthening cybersecurity across the EU and reducing compliance burdens for organisations.

Regarding the Cybersecurity Act 2 proposal, the EDPB and EDPS express support for the reinforced role of the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA), the facilitation of cybersecurity certification uptake, and measures addressing risks to ICT supply chains. The Joint Opinion welcomes the provision under which ENISA's advice would be issued upon a prior request from the EDPB, and recommends that the EDPS also be designated as a possible requestor of such advice. The EDPB and EDPS find that the scope of the European Cybersecurity Certification Framework and its relationship with GDPR certification requires further clarification, and that ENISA is required to consult the EDPB before adopting certification schemes relating to the security of processing of personal data.

On the proposed NIS2 amendments, the EDPB and EDPS welcome the designation of European Digital Identity Wallet and European Business Wallet providers as essential entities. Consistent with their earlier Joint Opinion on the Digital Omnibus Regulation Proposal, the EDPB and EDPS express support for a single-entry point for personal data breach notifications, and find that such a mechanism would reduce administrative burden for notifying organisations without diminishing the level of protection afforded to individuals.

rows.title: CJEU Advocate General: EU Member States May Exclude Telecoms Equipment on National Security Grounds
rows.summary:
Advocate General Tamara Ćapeta has issued an Opinion in Case C-354/24 (Elisa Eesti) proposing that the Court of Justice of the European Union find that Member States may exclude telecommunications hardware and software where the manufacturer poses a national security risk, provided any exclusion remains subject to judicial review and proportionality assessment under EU law.

On 19 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union published Advocate General Ćapeta's Opinion following a request for a preliminary ruling from the Administrative Court, Tallinn, in proceedings brought by Estonian telecommunications provider Elisa Eesti AS. In 2022, Elisa Eesti applied for authorisation to use hardware and software from Huawei in its 2G-4G and 5G networks. The Estonian authorities refused, classifying Huawei as a high-risk manufacturer, and Elisa Eesti challenged that decision.

The Advocate General proposes that Member States may exclude third-country telecommunications equipment manufacturers on national security grounds. However, the Advocate General finds that any such decision is required to be subject to judicial review, including proportionality assessment. The Advocate General finds that a decision cannot rest on general suspicion and must involve a specific assessment of the intended equipment and associated risks. The Advocate General notes that the European Electronic Communications Code provides for security requirements for national telecommunications networks, and that competent authorities may draw on risk assessments conducted by EU institutions and other bodies, including the Commission's 2023 Communication on the Implementation of the 5G Cybersecurity Toolbox.

On property rights, the Advocate General observes that a restriction on equipment use on security grounds constitutes a limitation on use of property within Article 17(1) of the Charter of Fundamental Rights. The Advocate General states that a company is not entitled to compensation unless the national court finds the burden arising from the restriction is disproportionately heavy. The Opinion is not binding on the Court of Justice, which will deliver judgment at a later date.

rows.title: EDPB Launches 2026 Coordinated Enforcement Framework Action on Transparency and Information Obligations Under GDPR
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board has launched its 2026 Coordinated Enforcement Framework action, directing 25 data protection authorities across Europe, including the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés, to assess controller compliance with transparency and information obligations under Articles 12, 13 and 14 of the GDPR.

On 19 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board announced the launch of the Coordinated Enforcement Framework (CEF) action for 2026, having selected the topic at its October 2025 plenary. The EDPB notes that no fines have been issued at this stage. The initiative forms part of the EDPB's 2024–2027 Strategy, which the EDPB identifies as addressing coordinated enforcement as a key mechanism for streamlining cooperation among data protection authorities across the European Economic Area.

The EDPB states that the 2026 action will focus on the obligations requiring controllers to inform individuals when their personal data is being processed (Art. 12, 13 and 14 GDPR). The Board notes that this right to be informed is a core element of the broader principle of transparency and is intended to ensure that individuals retain greater control over their personal data. Participating data protection authorities, including the CNIL, are described as preparing to contact controllers from a range of sectors, either through enforcement actions or fact-finding exercises. The EDPB notes that where fact-finding exercises are conducted, the relevant authority may decide to undertake additional follow-up actions where circumstances require.

During the second half of 2026, the EDPB states that participating authorities will share and discuss their findings collectively, with the aim of aggregating the results of national actions and generating deeper insight into transparency compliance across the EU. The EDPB notes that a consolidated report will subsequently be drafted and submitted for adoption by the Board, and that this report is intended to enable targeted follow-up actions at both national and EU levels. The EDPB notes that the 2026 CEF action follows previous coordinated exercises covering cloud-based services in the public sector, the designation and position of data protection officers, the implementation of the right of access (Art. 15 GDPR), and the right to erasure (Art. 17 GDPR).
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: EDPB Launches 2026 Coordinated Enforcement Action on Transparency Obligations, with CNIL as Coordinator
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board has launched its 2026 Coordinated Enforcement Framework action focusing on transparency and information obligations under the GDPR, with the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés designated as coordinator for the initiative.

On 19 March 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published details of the European Data Protection Board's Coordinated Enforcement Framework (CEF) action for 2026, which concerns the obligations on controllers to inform data subjects of the existence and conditions of processing their personal data, as set out in Articles 12, 13 and 14 of the GDPR. The CNIL notes that no financial penalties have been issued at this stage; the action concerns supervisory coordination and examination across participating authorities.

The CNIL notes that 25 European data protection authorities will participate in the 2026 CEF action. The CNIL states that the participating authorities are to contact controllers across various sectors throughout Europe, either by way of questionnaire or formal investigation. The CNIL states that, where investigations are conducted, authorities may also decide to pursue additional follow-up measures. The CNIL notes that transparency and the right to information are considered essential elements enabling data subjects to exercise greater control over their personal data (Arts. 12, 13 and 14 GDPR).

The CNIL states that during the second half of 2026, participating authorities will exchange and examine their findings in order to consolidate the results of their national actions and deepen shared understanding of the subject. The CNIL states that a consolidated report will then be prepared and submitted for adoption by the EDPB, with a view to enabling targeted follow-up at both national and European Union level. The CNIL notes that the CEF forms a key element of the EDPB's strategic plan for 2024 to 2027, and follows previous coordinated actions covering cloud services in the public sector (2023), the designation and role of data protection officers (2024), implementation of the right of access (2025), and implementation of the right to erasure (2026, as previously announced).
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CJEU Rules Police May Collect Biometric Data Only Where Strictly Necessary
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union has ruled that national police authorities may collect biometric data during criminal investigations only where strictly necessary under Article 10 of Directive 2016/680, and that every such decision requires a stated reason.

On 19 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union delivered its judgment following a reference for a preliminary ruling from the Court of Appeal, Paris. The case arose from the detention in May 2020 of an individual, HW, who was held in Paris for organising an unnotified demonstration. During police custody, HW refused to consent to the collection of fingerprints and photographs, leading to his conviction and a fine of EUR 300, even though he was acquitted of the underlying offence.

The Court finds that biometric data, including fingerprints and photographs, constitute sensitive personal data attracting enhanced protection under EU law. The Court determines that reasonable grounds for suspecting an offence do not alone justify collecting such data. The Court states that every decision to gather identification data must contain a clear statement of reasons, enabling the data subject to understand the basis for the measure and to exercise the right to an effective remedy. The Court finds that national legislation rendering such collection systematic, without permitting the competent authority to assess necessity in each case, is contrary to EU law.

On the question of penalties for refusal, the Court holds that lawfulness depends on whether the underlying collection satisfies the strictly necessary condition. The Court finds that a criminal penalty for refusal is not incompatible with EU law, provided the penalty observes the principle of proportionality as laid down by the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union.

rows.title: Brazil Enacts ECA Digital Regulations, Imposing New Obligations on Platforms Serving Children and Adolescents
rows.summary:
Brazil's federal government has regulated Lei nº 15.211/2025, known as the Estatuto Digital da Criança e do Adolescente (ECA Digital), establishing a framework of obligations for digital platforms, online services and virtual environments accessible to children and adolescents, with enforcement responsibility assigned to the Autoridade Nacional de Proteção de Dados (ANPD).

On 18 March 2026, at a ceremony at the Palácio do Planalto in Brasília, President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva signed three decrees implementing the new law. The first decree regulates the ECA Digital itself. The second creates the Centro Nacional de Proteção à Criança e ao Adolescente within the Federal Police, centralising reporting mechanisms and expanding the state's capacity to respond to digital crimes. The third strengthens the ANPD, designating it as the authority responsible for regulating, supervising and ensuring compliance with the new rules. The government also announced the allocation of BRL 100 million (approximately EUR 17 million or USD 18 million) through Finep to support the development of technological solutions for child protection in digital environments.

The Secretaria de Governo Digital sets out that the ECA Digital establishes obligations for applications, games, social networks and other digital services operating in Brazil. Among the measures the legislation introduces are the replacement of self-declaration with reliable age verification systems, the prompt removal of illegal content, and the adoption of default safety configurations for accounts held by minors. The law also addresses practices the government identifies as harmful to younger users, including manipulative design intended to encourage compulsive platform use and loot box mechanics associated with gambling-like reward systems. The legislation further regulates the commercial activities of child influencers, requiring judicial authorisation for commercial content, and strengthens parental supervision instruments.

The Ministra da Gestão e da Inovação em Serviços Públicos, Esther Dweck, noted that the decree provides for the public sector to act not only as regulator and supervisor but also as a promoter of implementation solutions. The ministry states it is committed to working alongside state-owned technology companies Serpro and Dataprev to develop public technological solutions supporting ECA Digital implementation. The ANPD's reinforced mandate, the government states, encompasses data protection oversight and the definition of technical parameters for the law's application, extending the existing framework of Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados to the specific context of minors in digital environments.

rows.title: Cyber Security Agency of Singapore and Japan's METI Sign Mutual Recognition Memorandum of Cooperation for IoT Cybersecurity Labelling Schemes
rows.summary:
The Cyber Security Agency of Singapore and Japan's Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry have entered into a Memorandum of Cooperation providing for mutual recognition of their respective IoT cybersecurity labelling schemes, with Japan becoming the fifth country to hold such an arrangement with Singapore.

On 18 March 2026, the Cyber Security Agency of Singapore (CSA) and Japan's Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry (METI) signed the Memorandum of Cooperation (MoC), formalising mutual recognition between Singapore's Cybersecurity Labelling Scheme (CLS) and Japan's Japan Cyber STAR (JC-STAR) scheme. The agreement was signed by Ms Rahayu Mahzam, Minister of State for Digital Development and Information, and Mr Ino Toshiro, State Minister of Economy, Trade and Industry of Japan. CSA states that no financial penalties are associated with this instrument.

CSA notes that under the arrangement, smart devices that have obtained cybersecurity labels under either the CLS or JC-STAR will be mutually recognised across both jurisdictions, allowing manufacturers to apply for the other country's labelling scheme through a streamlined process. The CSA confirms that the arrangement applies to a range of smart devices, including smart home assistants, home automation and alarm systems, IoT gateways, and hubs that connect multiple devices. CSA states that the MoC is to take effect on 1 June 2026.

CSA describes the CLS as a voluntary scheme, with a mandatory exception for Wi-Fi routers, for which obtaining CLS Level 1 is required. Under the CLS, CSA notes that smart devices are rated according to their levels of cybersecurity provisions, with ratings displayed as one to four stars, each additional star indicating progressively more rigorous testing and security assessments. CSA states that since the CLS launched in 2020, it has received applications for over 1,000 products, ranging from routers to smart lighting to smart cameras.

CSA confirms that Japan is the fifth nation to enter into such a mutual recognition arrangement with Singapore, following Finland, Germany, the Republic of Korea, and the United Kingdom. The CSA notes, however, that Finland discontinued its own cybersecurity labelling scheme on 31 July 2025, following the mutual recognition arrangement established with Singapore in 2021. The CSA states that the cooperation is intended to enhance cybersecurity standards for IoT devices across both nations, provide market access for manufacturers, and give consumers greater confidence in the security of their smart devices.

rows.title: World Anti-Doping Agency enters compliance agreement with Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada over athletes' sensitive data use
rows.summary:
The World Anti-Doping Agency has entered into a compliance agreement with the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada, committing to restrict the use of athletes' sensitive personal information to anti-doping purposes only, following an investigation under the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act.

On 17 March 2026, the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada announced that the World Anti-Doping Agency, headquartered in Montreal, Quebec, had concluded a compliance agreement with the Office following an investigation launched by Privacy Commissioner Philippe Dufresne into a complaint. No financial penalty is reported in connection with this matter.

The investigation arose from a complaint alleging that personal information disclosed by WADA to international sporting federations was being used to assess athletes' sex-based eligibility without their knowledge or consent. The Office of the Privacy Commissioner finds that this use of the data fell outside the purposes for which the information was originally collected, raising concerns under PIPEDA regarding purpose limitation and consent. The Office of the Privacy Commissioner notes that WADA became subject to PIPEDA in 2015 following international pressure to ensure that its holdings of sensitive personal information were subject to proper oversight in Canada.

Under the compliance agreement, WADA has committed to implementing remedial measures designed to help ensure that international sport federations and other anti-doping organisations receiving personal information under WADA's control do not use that information for purposes unrelated to anti-doping. The agreement covers data that WADA holds about athletes globally and that is shared with third-party organisations within the anti-doping framework. The Office of the Privacy Commissioner finds that the commitments made by WADA address the concerns identified during the investigation. Privacy Commissioner Dufresne stated that WADA is entrusted with highly sensitive personal information belonging to thousands of athletes from around the world, and welcomed WADA's commitment to take steps to help ensure that this information is only used for the purposes for which it was collected.
rows.body_name: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada

rows.title: EDPS Publishes Compass Document Setting Out AI Act Supervisory Approach for EU Institutions, 2026–2027
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has published its Compass document outlining how it will exercise its role as market surveillance authority and notified body for AI systems used or put into service by European Union institutions, bodies, offices and agencies under Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (the AI Act).

On 17 March 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor published the document titled "Towards Trustworthy AI in the EU Public Administration: The EDPS Compass for its New Role under the AI Act 2026–2027", setting out the authority's strategic approach to supervising EU institutions' AI systems. The EDPS states that the document is published expressly in the EDPS's capacity as market surveillance authority under the AI Act, and not in its capacity as a data protection authority.

The EDPS notes that its expanded mandate under the AI Act builds on earlier preparatory steps, including the publication of its AI Preparedness Strategy in May 2024 and the establishment of a dedicated Artificial Intelligence Unit in October 2024. The EDPS identifies four strategic pillars guiding the authority's action: supervision of EU institutions' AI systems, including both ex ante supervision as a notified body conducting conformity assessments of high-risk AI systems and ex post supervision as a market surveillance authority; contribution to AI Act governance and regulatory coordination, including participation in the AI Board; institutional empowerment for trustworthy AI, encompassing interinstitutional knowledge exchange and the establishment of a Trustworthy AI Centre of Excellence; and international engagement and exchange of best practices.

The EDPS states that the two-year timeframe of 2026 to 2027 reflects two specific factors: the evolving nature of the AI Act, which the authority notes is to be complemented by harmonised standards and European Commission guidelines, and the possible amendments to the AI Act envisaged under the 2025 Digital Omnibus legislative proposal (COM(2025) 836 final), which the authority finds may require adjustments to the current supervisory setup. The EDPS states that it intends to pursue its supervisory responsibilities grounded in principles of impartiality, integrity, transparency, pragmatism and responsible innovation, with the stated objective of enhancing legal and procedural clarity in the implementation of the AI Act through a cooperative approach with EU institutions.

rows.title: German DSK Calls for Targeted GDPR Amendments in Response to European Commission Digital Fitness Check
rows.summary:
Germany's Conference of Independent Data Protection Authorities (Datenschutzkonferenz, DSK), coordinated under the Mecklenburg-Vorpommern LfDI, has published a formal opinion calling for targeted amendments to the General Data Protection Regulation in response to the European Commission's Digital Fitness Check, citing the need to strengthen data subject rights, extend liability to hardware and software manufacturers, and reduce administrative burdens on small and medium-sized enterprises.

On 17 March 2026, the DSK, comprising the independent federal and state data protection authorities of Germany, published its position paper setting out specific proposed modifications to the GDPR.

The DSK finds that core principles in Art. 5 and Art. 6 GDPR have proved effective and are required to remain intact. The GDPR's relationship to other EU digital legislation, including the AI Act, the Digital Services Act, and the Data Act, demonstrates its continuing centrality to European data law. On administrative simplification, the DSK recommends removing the obligation to communicate data protection officer contact details to supervisory authorities under Art. 37(7) GDPR. The DSK also calls for greater clarification of biometric data definitions and proposes modifications to access rights allowing balancing against third-party rights in justified cases.

The DSK finds that Art. 25 GDPR currently targets users rather than manufacturers and providers. Data protection responsibility is required to be extended to these actors, aligning with the Cyber Resilience Act and AI Regulation. On child protection, the DSK calls for prohibiting personalised advertising to children and implementing child-appropriate default settings on social networks. The DSK recommends establishing specific legal bases for AI model development and operation, and finds that information and access rights concerning AI-based personal data processing are required to be strengthened.

rows.title: Germany Enacts KRITIS-Dachgesetz Transposing EU Critical Entities Resilience Directive into National Law
rows.summary:
Germany's Federal Ministry of the Interior has published the KRITIS-Dachgesetz, a federal statute transposing Directive (EU) 2022/2557 on the resilience of critical entities (the CER Directive) into national law, establishing uniform physical protection requirements for operators of critical infrastructure.

On 16 March 2026, the Federal Republic of Germany promulgated the Act on the Implementation of Directive (EU) 2022/2557 and on Strengthening the Resilience of Critical Installations (KRITIS-Dachgesetz) in the Federal Law Gazette (BGBl-1/2026/66). The legislative procedure was initiated on 1 September 2025. The Federal Ministry of the Interior states that the law does not involve financial penalties as its primary instrument, and no fine amounts are applicable to this legislative update.

The Federal Ministry of the Interior states that the law introduces uniform federal rules for the physical protection of critical infrastructures, with the stated purpose of strengthening economic resilience and, by extension, security of supply for the population. The ministry sets out four core regulatory elements in the enacted text: provisions for identifying operators of critical installations and critical entities of particular European significance; the conduct of national risk analyses and risk assessments for critical services; minimum requirements for resilience-enhancing measures to be implemented by operators of critical installations; and the introduction of an incident reporting regime.

The ministry notes that the legislative process involved consultation submissions from a broad range of industry associations and public bodies, including Deutsche Bahn AG, the Bundesverband der Energie- und Wasserwirtschaft e.V., the Deutsche Krankenhausgesellschaft, the Bundesverband deutscher Banken, and the Deutscher Städtetag, among others. The ministry states that the enacted statute applies the CER Directive framework at federal level, ensuring that operators across sectors including energy, water, transport, banking and health are subject to consistent national standards for physical resilience, complementing the cybersecurity obligations established under separate legislation transposing Directive (EU) 2022/2555 (NIS 2).

rows.title: Egypt's Ministry of Communications and Information Technology Publishes National AI Governance Framework Guide
rows.summary:
Egypt's Ministry of Communications and Information Technology, acting through the National Council for Artificial Intelligence, Quantum Computing, and Emerging Technologies, has published the Guide to Egypt's National AI Governance Framework (Version 2.0), authorised on 15 March 2026 by Minister Raafat Hindy, establishing a formal governance architecture for artificial intelligence across Egypt's public and private sectors.

On 15 March 2026, the National Council for Artificial Intelligence, Quantum Computing, and Emerging Technologies released the Guide, prepared by the Egyptian centre for Responsible AI (ECRAI). The document underwent a structured drafting process beginning in August 2025, incorporating a public consultation phase in January 2026 and feedback integration from government bodies, private sector participants and international organisations before final release. The Guide states that no financial penalties are associated with this publication.

The Framework sets out a governance philosophy the Guide terms the "State as Orchestrator," under which the state is described as simultaneously enabling AI adoption through infrastructure, data provision and capacity building, while establishing regulatory protections for high-risk systems. The Guide defines a hierarchy of instruments: the overarching Framework suite, which encompasses the National Strategy, the Guide itself, National Guidelines for Trustworthy and Responsible AI, and future sectoral regulations. The Guide characterises itself as the strategic document responsible for defining what is governed, including risk tier classifications, a "Dual-Check" compliance logic, and an institutional architecture for enforcement and oversight. The Framework states it applies to all entities operating AI systems within Egypt's public and private sectors.

The Guide further describes Egypt's approach as a "Strategic Hybrid" model, combining elements of enabling innovation with targeted safeguards for applications assessed as high-risk. According to ECRAI, compliance pathways include ethical impact assessments and technical audits. The document positions Egypt as a conduit between the Arab region, Africa and the global digital economy, and the Guide states that AI is recognised as a strategic national priority within Egypt's constitutional framework. The Guide is classified as a public document with applicability to all sectors.
rows.body_name: Ministry of Communications and Information Technology

rows.title: HmbBfDI Issues €775,000 in GDPR Fines for First Nine Months of 2025, Including €492,000 Against Financial Firm Over Automated Decision Failures
rows.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) has imposed a total of approximately €775,000 in fines under the General Data Protection Regulation across 15 concluded proceedings in the first nine months of 2025, with the largest penalty of €492,000 levied against an unnamed financial sector company for failures relating to automated individual decision-making (Art. 22 GDPR).

On 30 September 2025, the HmbBfDI published an interim review disclosing that the financial sector company had rejected credit card applications from several customers with sound credit ratings using fully automated, algorithm-based processes without human intervention. When affected customers requested explanations for the rejections, the HmbBfDI finds that the company failed to adequately fulfil its statutory information and access obligations, including the requirement to provide meaningful information about the logic involved in automated decision-making (Art. 13, 14 and 15 GDPR). The HmbBfDI notes that such automated procedures are permissible only under strict conditions and carry heightened obligations for controllers. The HmbBfDI states that the company's extensive cooperation throughout both the administrative and penalty proceedings, and the substantial improvements it made to its processes for handling data subject rights in automated decision-making, were given considerable weight as mitigating factors in the calculation of the fine. The company accepted the penalty notice.

Beyond the financial sector case, the HmbBfDI finds that enforcement activity focused on two further areas. The HmbBfDI determined that three companies were fined in the lower five-figure range each for sending promotional emails to customers without valid consent (Art. 6(1) GDPR). A retail company received a fine of €195,000 after it repeatedly and over a prolonged period failed to respond to data subject rights requests within the required time limits following a postal advertising campaign conducted through third-party service providers (Art. 12 GDPR). The HmbBfDI imposed six fines on employees of the Hamburg police and other public authorities who conducted unauthorised queries of personal data held in official databases, and one hospital employee was fined for accessing a colleague's patient records without any involvement in that person's treatment. Thomas Fuchs, the HmbBfDI, states that where companies systematically fail to respond adequately to information and access requests, a perceptible sanction is required, and that this applies in particular to opaque structures such as address trading or complex decision algorithms, and increasingly to the use of artificial intelligence.
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.title: HmbBfDI calls on German federal government to designate AI market surveillance authorities after EU AI Act deadline passes
rows.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) has called on the German federal government to designate competent market surveillance authorities under the EU AI Act, following Germany's failure to meet the 2 August 2025 designation deadline imposed on EU member states.

On 31 July 2025, the HmbBfDI, Thomas Fuchs, issued a public appeal to the federal government to act without delay in designating the AI market surveillance authorities required under the EU AI Regulation (AI Act). The HmbBfDI notes that Germany has not enacted a national AI Act implementing law, leaving the designation process incomplete as the statutory deadline expired.

The HmbBfDI finds that EU member states were required to designate competent market surveillance authorities by 2 August 2025 under the AI Act. The authority notes that Germany's failure to meet this deadline means that businesses and public bodies currently lack a binding point of contact for questions relating to AI Act compliance. Thomas Fuchs states that this situation represents a disadvantage for Germany as a location for AI innovation. The HmbBfDI further notes that the AI Act imposes demanding requirements on designated authorities, including independence and adequate technical, financial and human resources, specifically staff with expertise in artificial intelligence, data law and product safety law.

The HmbBfDI observes that the AI Act already defines the role of data protection supervisory authorities in certain sectors. Under Article 74(8) of the AI Act, the HmbBfDI notes that data protection supervisory authorities are designated as the competent market surveillance authorities for high-risk AI systems deployed in areas particularly sensitive to fundamental rights, including law enforcement, migration and the administration of justice. The HmbBfDI notes that responsibility for AI systems used in other areas of the public sector, such as education, remains unresolved pending national implementing legislation. Despite the absence of a national legal framework, the HmbBfDI states that it is proactively building internal competence and training staff for the assessment of AI systems in preparation for the moment of formal statutory designation. The authority additionally notes that new transparency obligations for AI model providers under the AI Act apply from 2 August 2025.
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.title: HmbBfDI assumes Data Act supervisory role for personal data from connected devices from 12 September 2025
rows.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) has confirmed its supervisory competence under the EU Data Act for matters involving personal data, with the regulation entering into application on 12 September 2025, on the basis of Art. 37(3) Data Act.

On 9 September 2025, the HmbBfDI published a press release setting out the new rights and oversight responsibilities arising from the Data Act's application date of 12 September 2025. The authority notes that the Data Act creates access rights to sensor data generated by connected devices, ranging from electric toothbrushes to wind turbines, for both users of those devices and eligible third parties, provided the conditions set out in the Data Act are met, data protection law does not preclude access, and trade secrets are preserved.

The HmbBfDI states that, under Art. 37(3) Data Act, data protection authorities are designated as the competent supervisory body for Data Act compliance where personal data are involved. The authority finds that its remit covers, in particular: access to personal data held by manufacturers; switching between cloud and data processing service providers; the protection of confidentiality through technical and organisational measures at the receiving party; and transparency obligations. The HmbBfDI notes that it is able to enforce these rights by means of orders and that infringements may, in part, be subject to fines. Alternatively, the authority states, rights may be pursued independently through civil courts. The HmbBfDI has published detailed guidance on the rights and obligations arising from the Data Act, its interaction with data protection law, and supervisory enforcement.

The authority confirms that any natural or legal person may lodge a complaint with the HmbBfDI where there are grounds to believe that a Hamburg-based undertaking has infringed their Data Act rights in relation to personal data. The HmbBfDI notes that each complaint is handled by the departmental unit responsible for data protection supervision of the relevant controller, in line with the objective in Art. 37(3) Data Act of ensuring consistent assessment of data use under both the GDPR and the Data Act. The authority further notes that its Data Act supervisory competence is confined to cases involving personal data, and that, for non-personal data, the German federal legislature has yet to designate a competent authority, meaning such claims remain limited to the civil courts for the time being.
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion on European Biotech Act Data Protection Safeguards
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor have jointly adopted an opinion on the European Commission's proposed European Biotech Act, identifying areas where the proposal requires stronger data protection safeguards for health and genetic data in clinical trials.

On 12 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) and the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) adopted Joint Opinion 3/2026 on the Commission's Proposal for a European Biotech Act. The proposal seeks to streamline the regulatory framework and update rules under the Clinical Trials Regulation (CTR).

The EDPB and EDPS support the proposal's objective of addressing fragmentation in CTR application and welcome establishing a single legal basis for personal data processing by sponsors and investigators. However, the EDPB and EDPS find that the sensitivity of health and genetic data processed in clinical trials requires a high standard of protection, and the joint opinion sets out recommendations to ensure proposed simplifications do not lower protections for trial participants.

The EDPB and EDPS find the proposal is required to specify whether actors involved in funding and conducting clinical trials act as sole or joint data controllers. On data retention, the authorities find the mandatory 25-year minimum retention period is required to apply only to the clinical trial master file, not to all personal data processed during a trial. Regarding further processing of trial data for other clinical trials or scientific research, the EDPB and EDPS find the Biotech Act is required to clearly define purposes and safeguards for such processing. The EDPB and EDPS also find the Biotech Act is required to ensure sponsor obligations complement existing AI Act requirements.
