On technical and organisational measures, the authorities find the CTR is required to explicitly mandate pseudonymisation where directly identifiable personal data need not be processed.

rows.title: Court of Justice of the EU Rules Hosting Providers Bear GDPR Responsibility for User-Published Content in Russmedia Case
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union ruled on 2 December 2025 that a hosting provider can bear joint controllership under the GDPR for unlawful content published by users on its platform.

On 2 December 2025, the Court of Justice of the European Union delivered its judgment in Case C-492/23 Russmedia. The case concerned an advertisement placed on an online marketplace without consent, using a woman's photograph and telephone number to offer alleged sexual services. The Court finds that the hosting provider bore data protection responsibility for user-published content on the platform even without direct influence over the advertisement's content.

The Court determined that decisions taken in connection with operating the platform, specifically concerning the purposes and means of processing personal data, were sufficient to engage GDPR obligations under Article 4(7) GDPR. The Court notes that the hosting provider's terms of service reserved rights to use, distribute, transmit, reproduce, modify, translate, pass to partners and delete published content at any time without providing reasons. The Court finds that a hosting provider, acting as controller, is required to identify content containing special category data under Article 9(1) GDPR before publication, verify whether the person whose data appears in an advertisement is the publishing user, and confirm that a valid legal basis or consent exists where that is not the case.

The Court notes that the liability privilege under Article 6 DSA, which applies once a hosting provider gains actual knowledge of unlawful content via the notice-and-action mechanism, does not limit or reduce data subjects' rights under the GDPR. The Court determines that GDPR responsibility arises prior to publication and does not depend on notification of a violation.
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.title: AAIP and Over 60 Data Protection Authorities Issue Joint Statement on AI-Generated Imagery and Privacy
rows.summary:
Argentina's Agencia de Acceso a la Información Pública (AAIP), alongside more than 60 data protection and privacy authorities worldwide, has issued a joint statement establishing foundational principles for the development and use of artificial intelligence systems that generate realistic images and videos of identifiable individuals without their knowledge or consent.

On 12 March 2026, the AAIP published the joint declaration, which was produced under the framework of the Global Privacy Assembly's International Enforcement Cooperation Working Group. Participating authorities include offices from Albania, Australia, Belgium, Brazil, Canada, Colombia, France, Germany, Ghana, Israel, Italy, Kenya, Mexico, New Zealand, Nigeria, Norway, Portugal, Singapore, Spain, Switzerland, the United Kingdom, Uruguay and dozens of additional jurisdictions.

The declaration sets out expectations directed at organisations and institutions that develop or deploy audiovisual content generation tools using AI systems. The AAIP and co-signatories identify four principal areas of concern. First, the statement finds that developers and deployers are required to implement security measures to prevent the misuse of personal information and the generation of non-consensual intimate images, with particular attention to content depicting children and adolescents. Second, the declaration states that organisations are required to make transparent the capabilities, security measures, acceptable uses and consequences of misuse of their AI systems. Third, the authorities find that effective and accessible mechanisms must be provided to enable individuals to request the removal of content involving their personal information, with rapid responses to such requests. Fourth, the statement addresses specific risks to minors, finding that organisations are required to provide clear, age-appropriate information for children, parents, guardians and educators.

The AAIP notes that the statement is directed at systems generating content that represents identifiable persons without their knowledge or consent, a category the declaration identifies as presenting distinct risks to personal privacy and dignity. The declaration does not impose binding legal obligations but articulates shared regulatory expectations across the participating jurisdictions.
rows.body_name: Agencia de Acceso a la Informacion Publica

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion on European Biotech Act, Backing Harmonisation but Calling for Stronger Health Data Safeguards
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor have adopted a Joint Opinion on the European Commission's proposal for a European Biotech Act, supporting harmonisation of clinical trial rules while calling for specific safeguards for health and genetic data processed in that context.

On 12 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) and the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) published Joint Opinion 3/2026 on the Commission's proposed European Biotech Act. No fines are associated with this opinion. The EDPB and EDPS note that the proposal under review seeks to strengthen the EU's biotechnology and biomanufacturing sectors, primarily in the area of health, by streamlining the existing regulatory framework and updating rules governing clinical trials.

The EDPB and EDPS find that the proposal's objective of fostering EU competitiveness and reducing fragmentation in the application of the Clinical Trials Regulation is supported. The Joint Opinion specifically welcomes the proposal's aim to establish a single legal basis for the processing of personal data by sponsors and investigators, which the authorities find would improve legal clarity across member states (Art. 9 GDPR). The Joint Opinion notes that the current divergence in how the Clinical Trials Regulation is applied across jurisdictions has created inconsistency that the proposed measure is intended to address.

At the same time, the EDPB and EDPS underline that health and genetic data processed in the context of clinical trials are among the most sensitive categories of personal data and require a high standard of protection (Art. 9 GDPR). The Joint Opinion sets out several recommendations directed at the EU legislature, with the EDPB and EDPS finding that the proposed simplifications must not result in a lowering of the level of protection afforded to clinical trial participants. The authorities find that any harmonised legal basis introduced by the European Biotech Act is required to be accompanied by specific and proportionate safeguards commensurate with the sensitivity of the data involved. The opinion does not specify the precise text of those recommended safeguards but indicates that the existing framework under the GDPR provides the baseline against which adequacy is to be assessed.

rows.title: Privacy Commissioner of Canada Tables Special Report on ArriveCAN App Investigation, Finding No Privacy Act Contraventions but Identifying Contracting Shortcomings
rows.summary:
The Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada has tabled a Special Report in Parliament following an investigation into the ArriveCAN application, finding no contraventions of the Privacy Act but identifying shortcomings in contracting practices at the Canada Border Services Agency.

On 12 March 2026, the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (OPC) tabled its Special Report on the ArriveCAN investigation in Parliament. The investigation was initiated following a complaint against the Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA) relating to contracting practices during the development of the app, which was launched during the COVID-19 pandemic to digitise the collection of traveller information and expedite border processing.

The OPC finds no evidence that personal information collected through the ArriveCAN app was used or disclosed in contravention of the Privacy Act, which governs the personal information handling practices of federal institutions. The OPC further finds that all ArriveCAN-related contracts permitting access to personal information included appropriate clauses describing security requirements and outlining specific safeguards.

Notwithstanding the absence of contraventions, the OPC identifies certain shortcomings in the CBSA's contracting practices. The OPC's Report of Findings sets out steps the OPC expects the CBSA to take when contractors perform work on its behalf. These include ensuring that security requirements are rigorously and accurately assessed and completed within a reasonable time prior to contract award, ensuring that task descriptions in task authorisations clearly and accurately define the work to be performed so that privacy and security requirements are accurately identified, proactively managing security clearances and renewal processes with rigour and strong oversight, and restricting contractor permissions and access to personal information to what is strictly necessary. The OPC notes that the CBSA accepted the Commissioner's recommendations and agreed with the objective of strengthening privacy and security practices within its contracting framework. The CBSA also indicated its commitment to ensuring that its policies and procedures uphold standards of accountability, transparency and protection of personal information. Privacy Commissioner Philippe Dufresne stated that the findings represent an opportunity to raise awareness among all federal institutions about best practices in contracting to ensure strong privacy protections.
rows.body_name: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada

rows.title: HmbBfDI Sets Out GPAI Provider Obligations Under EU AI Act from 2 August 2025
rows.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) has published guidance confirming that new transparency and compliance obligations for providers of general-purpose AI (GPAI) models under the EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689) entered into force on 2 August 2025.

On 31 July 2025, the HmbBfDI issued a statement setting out the scope and timeline of obligations now binding on GPAI model providers under the AI Act. The authority notes that these rules build upon the prohibitions on certain AI practices applicable since February 2025 and the AI literacy requirement already in force.

The HmbBfDI sets out that, for GPAI models placed on the European market after 2 August 2025, providers are required to create technical documentation, declare compliance with EU copyright law, publish a detailed summary of training data used, and provide information on model functionality. For high-capability models presenting systemic risk, the authority notes that providers are additionally required to test for vulnerabilities and risks and to report serious security incidents. The HmbBfDI observes that the European Commission has published guidelines to assist providers in implementing these requirements.

The HmbBfDI finds that the new obligations produce a corresponding empowerment for companies and public authorities that integrate GPAI models into their own AI systems, allowing those deployers to demand from model providers the information necessary for their own compliance. The HmbBfDI states that this enables deployers to better assess the GPAI models they use, minimise risks, and fulfil data protection due diligence obligations. On the implementation timeline, the authority confirms that providers of models already available before 2 August 2025 benefit from a transition period running until 2 August 2027. For enforcement, the HmbBfDI notes that the AI Office, situated within the Commission, is able to launch investigations and demand corrective measures from August 2025, while the Commission's power to impose fines and other sanctions under the AI Act applies only from 2 August 2026.
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.title: ANSSI Cyber Threat Overview 2025: France Recorded 3,586 Security Events with State-Linked and Criminal Actors Targeting Key Sectors
rows.summary:
France's Agence nationale de la sécurité des systèmes d'information has published its Panorama de la cybermenace 2025, finding that French organisations remained under sustained pressure from both state-linked and cybercriminal attackers throughout the year.

On 11 March 2026, the Agence nationale de la sécurité des systèmes d'information (ANSSI) published its annual cyber threat overview for 2025, documenting 3,586 security events handled across varying levels of mobilisation. ANSSI finds this figure represents an 18 per cent decrease compared to 2024, attributing the reduction in part to elevated reporting volumes recorded during the Paris Olympic and Paralympic Games between May and August 2024.

ANSSI reports that of the total events, 2,209 constituted notifications and 1,366 were characterised as incidents. The agency finds that four sectors bore the greatest exposure: education and research (34 per cent), ministries and local authorities (24 per cent), health (10 per cent), and telecommunications (9 per cent). On cybercriminal activity, ANSSI notes a rise in data exfiltration incidents, though it finds that only 42 per cent of the 460 events characterised as possible data leaks were confirmed as actual data breaches. The agency determines that, while data theft does not always produce substantial direct gain for attackers, it generates considerable incident response costs for victim entities.

ANSSI finds that the attack methods targeting French interests continue to be linked to Russia and China, principally for espionage and pre-positioning purposes. The agency also finds that targeting of critical infrastructure, particularly in the telecommunications and energy sectors through sabotage attempts, remains a priority for state-linked actors, a threat it explored in a separate threat assessment on mobile equipment published in November 2025. ANSSI further finds that a growing convergence between state and criminal actors, involving shared capabilities and the cross-adoption of techniques, is complicating attribution efforts. The agency additionally finds that edge devices remain a favoured target owing to the volume of vulnerabilities affecting such equipment, placing continued demands on its incident response teams. ANSSI notes that the transposition of the NIS 2 Directive and the entry into force of the Cyber Resilience Act are regulatory frameworks whose baseline security measures the agency considers essential to improving the protection of French entities.

rows.title: CNIL and HAS Sign Partnership Agreement on Digital Health and AI Data Protection
rows.summary:
France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés and the Haute Autorité de santé signed a formal partnership agreement on 10 March 2026 to coordinate guidance on personal data protection and the use of artificial intelligence across the health, social and medico-social sectors.

On 10 March 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés and the Haute Autorité de santé formalised a partnership convention, published by the CNIL on 11 March 2026. The agreement carries no financial penalty element and involves no fine. The CNIL and the HAS jointly commit to sharing expertise and issuing coordinated guidance for health professionals, establishments and digital solution providers operating within the French health system.

The CNIL and the HAS identify four principal areas of collaboration under the agreement. These are: the promotion of personal data protection and fundamental rights in connection with digital tools, including AI-embedded solutions; the operational adaptation of European regulatory requirements to the specific context of the French health system; the improvement of data protection and health data security practices among sector actors; and the integration of digital and AI requirements into the certification and evaluation processes for health and medico-social establishments. The partnership also provides, where relevant, for the two institutions to issue joint positions to assist professionals in applying recommendations within clinical practice.

The CNIL and the HAS state that a first joint recommendation is expected during the second quarter of 2026. That recommendation will address the appropriate use of artificial intelligence in care settings and is directed at health professionals and establishments. The two authorities state it will specify the applicable legal and regulatory framework. The CNIL notes, in the statement attributed to its president Marie-Laure Denis, that the sustainable development of digital health and AI depends on patient and professional trust, grounded in data protection guarantees and clear delineation of responsibilities. The HAS is described as an independent public authority with scientific character holding functions that include evaluating digital medical devices, issuing professional best practice recommendations, and measuring care quality and patient safety in health establishments.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: AgID Publishes Draft Guidelines for AI System Development in Italian Public Administration
rows.summary:
Italy's Agenzia per l'Italia Digitale (AgID) has issued a draft version of guidelines governing the development of artificial intelligence systems within the Italian public administration, issued pursuant to the D.P.C.M. of 12 January 2024 on the Three-Year Plan for Information Technology in Public Administration 2024-2026.

On 11 March 2026, AgID published Version 1.0 of the draft guidelines, titled "Linee Guida per lo sviluppo di sistemi di Intelligenza Artificiale nella pubblica amministrazione", opened for public consultation. AgID states the document does not carry a financial penalty and is not a final binding instrument at this stage.

AgID sets out seven general principles drawn from Law 132/2025 that the guidelines state are applicable to the development and procurement of AI systems across the public administration. According to AgID, these principles cover: the protection of fundamental rights, transparency, proportionality and non-discrimination (Principle 1); the quality, accuracy, reliability and transparency of data and processes (Principle 2); human-centredness, explainability and harm prevention (Principle 3); the protection of democratic and institutional processes and sovereignty (Principle 4); coherence with the European regulatory framework, specifically the EU AI Act (Principle 5); cybersecurity and resilience across the system lifecycle (Principle 6); and universal accessibility and inclusion (Principle 7).

The draft guidelines also establish a reference logical architecture for AI systems in the public administration. AgID defines a layered AI stack encompassing an Energy Layer, a Chip Layer, an Infrastructure Layer, an AI Model Layer and an Application Layer. AgID further identifies a reference AI architecture model comprising AI models, heterogeneous data sources across cloud, on-premises and hybrid environments, application-level tools and an orchestrator component. AgID classifies AI system families to which the guidelines apply, including statistical or data-driven AI, machine learning, neural networks, deep learning and generative AI. AgID addresses the role of data in AI system development, including training data and operational data, as a distinct dimension of the reference architecture.

rows.title: Agenzia per l'Italia Digitale Publishes Draft Guidelines on AI Procurement for Italian Public Administrations
rows.summary:
Italy's Agenzia per l'Italia Digitale (AgID) has published draft guidelines on the procurement of artificial intelligence systems by public administrations, issued pursuant to the D.P.C.M. of 12 January 2024 establishing the three-year plan for public administration information technology 2024–2026.

On 11 March 2026, the Agenzia per l'Italia Digitale published Version 1.0 of the draft Linee guida per il procurement di IA nella Pubblica Amministrazione for public consultation. AgID states that the guidelines apply to all entities falling within the scope of Article 2, paragraph 2, of the Codice dell'Amministrazione Digitale (CAD), collectively referred to throughout the document as PA.

AgID sets out that the guidelines address the modalities for procuring AI systems with particular reference to regulatory compliance and organisational impact. AgID notes that the document is structured around five principal areas: the general framework for acquiring AI systems; metrics, costs, monitoring and contract management; and procurement instruments and inter-administrative cooperation. AgID identifies distinct families of AI systems for procurement purposes and introduces a Levelised Cost of AI (LCOAI) methodology as an alternative to traditional economic appraisal approaches, which AgID notes have limitations when applied to AI lifecycle costs.

AgID addresses cybersecurity risk management for AI procurement, including attack taxonomies and security objectives, and sets out considerations relating to hardware neutrality, the use of accelerators, and system portability. AgID also addresses the role of data in procurement decisions and the implications of architectural choices on cost, control and risk exposure. AgID notes that the document includes provisions on interoperability and cooperation between public administrations, as well as good practices applicable across the full contract lifecycle. AgID presents the guidelines as a draft open to public consultation prior to formal adoption.

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS call for data protection safeguards in European Biotech Act
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and European Data Protection Supervisor have adopted a joint opinion on the proposed European Biotech Act, calling for specific protective measures for health and genetic data in clinical trials whilst supporting the harmonisation objective.

On 10 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) and the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) adopted Joint Opinion 3/2026. The opinion responds to the European Commission's proposal for a European Biotech Act, which aims to streamline the regulatory framework and update rules on clinical trials.

The EDPB and EDPS find that the proposal's goal of addressing fragmentation in clinical trials regulation is supported. The two bodies welcome the aim of establishing a unified legal basis for processing personal data by sponsors and investigators, which the EDPB and EDPS find would improve legal clarity across the EU. However, the EDPB and EDPS determine that the sensitivity of health data and genetic data in clinical trial contexts requires a high standard of protection and that proposed simplifications must not reduce protection afforded to trial participants.

The joint opinion sets out several recommendations. The EDPB and EDPS find that the proposal is required to clarify whether entities involved in funding and conducting clinical trials act as independent or joint controllers to ensure clear allocation of responsibilities. The EDPB and EDPS find that the mandatory 25-year data retention period is required to refer explicitly to main clinical trial documentation only, not all personal data processed during a trial. The EDPB and EDPS find that the European Biotech Act is required to specify clearly the purposes and safeguards where data are reused for other clinical or scientific research. The EDPB and EDPS find that pseudonymisation is required whenever direct identification of participants is unnecessary, and that regulatory sandboxes are required to provide an explicit legal basis for personal data processing, including derogations for special category data.

rows.title: Bremen LfDI Annual Report 2025: Complaints Rise 48%, Digital Sovereignty Identified as Key Data Protection Concern
rows.summary:
The Bremen Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information (LfDI Bremen) published its eighth annual report under the GDPR on 16 March 2026, recording a 48 per cent increase in complaints and identifying reliance on cloud services outside the GDPR's territorial scope as a continuing concern under the regulation (Art. 3 GDPR).

On 16 March 2026, the LfDI Bremen, Dr Timo Utermark, presented the authority's annual report covering the 2025 reporting year. No fines are reported in the published source material. The authority states that digital sovereignty is becoming increasingly important for the effective protection of personal data, and the report notes that the use of cloud services where the retention of personal data within the GDPR's scope of application cannot be guaranteed remains a serious problem.

The LfDI Bremen records that 765 complaints were submitted to the authority in 2025, compared with figures implying approximately 517 in the prior year, representing the 48 per cent increase. The authority finds that video surveillance continued to account for a substantial share of complaints at 19 per cent. The number of reported personal data breaches also increased, rising from 205 to 260 in the reporting year.

The authority identifies several specific breach categories across the reporting period. At Bremen District Court, the LfDI Bremen finds that multiple employees used their professionally established access to the residents' registration register to conduct queries unrelated to their official duties. The authority also finds that a cyberattack on a hospital group operating facilities in Bremen resulted in the compromise of personal data belonging to both patients and employees (Art. 32 GDPR). In addition, the report records recurring failures by companies in the handling of employee sick notes, including cases where unauthorised persons accessed such records, where sick notes, in some instances including diagnoses, were published in calendars, and where records were not stored appropriately (Art. 5(1)(f) GDPR). The authority further notes instances where images of children were published on social media by one separated parent without the consent of the other, rendering such publication unlawful.

Regarding supervisory jurisdiction, the LfDI Bremen states that the inadequate legal framework governing its supervisory competence over the Bremen Public Prosecution Office remains a serious deficit. The authority records that the Bremen Senate, following pressure from the Commissioner, committed during the reporting year to preparing corresponding legislative provisions to address this gap.

rows.title: NTRA and EG-CERT Launch Internet Safety Awareness Campaign in Egypt
rows.summary:
Egypt's National Telecom Regulatory Authority, acting through its national cybersecurity centre EG-CERT, launched a public awareness campaign on safe internet use, targeting multiple segments of Egyptian society and coinciding with Safer Internet Day in February 2026.

On 25 February 2026, the National Telecom Regulatory Authority (NTRA) of Egypt, operating under the Ministry of Communications and Information Technology, launched the campaign alongside the debut of the digital citizenship and online protection platform www.wa3i.net. The NTRA reports that the launch was attended by the Minister of Communications and Information Technology, Eng. Raouf Handey, as well as the Chairperson of the National Council for Childhood and Motherhood, representatives from UNICEF Egypt, and the resident representative of the United Nations Development Programme, together with leadership from the Ministry and the National Council for Childhood and Motherhood.

The NTRA states that the campaign is designed to promote a culture of safe and responsible technology use across social media platforms, raise awareness of the importance of protecting personal data, and reduce risks associated with the digital environment. The authority notes that campaign materials focus on reinforcing values of respect and positive engagement on digital platforms, emphasise the active role of parents in monitoring their children's internet use, and highlight the importance of responsible use of social media. The NTRA further states that the initiative forms part of its broader efforts to address growing challenges in the digital world, equipping users with practical guidance on personal data protection, countering electronic fraud, mitigating cybersecurity risks, and building a society capable of engaging more consciously with technology.

The NTRA describes the wa3i.net platform as among the first integrated Arabic-language platforms specialising in digital citizenship and online protection, providing an interactive and educational space with Arabic content dedicated to targeted demographic groups, including children, adolescents, youth, parents, teachers, and older persons, categorised by age group.
rows.body_name: Ministry of Communications and Information Technology

rows.title: German Federal Administrative Court upholds Rheinland-Pfalz LfDI order requiring private health insurer to obtain consent before processing diagnoses for preventive programme recommendations
rows.summary:
Germany's Bundesverwaltungsgericht has upheld a 2022 order by the Rheinland-Pfalz Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit requiring a private health insurer to obtain insured persons' consent before analysing diagnosis data from submitted invoices to recommend preventive health programmes, finding no adequate legal basis for the processing under Art. 9 GDPR.

On 6 March 2026, the Bundesverwaltungsgericht issued its judgment in appeal proceedings (Az. 6 C 7.24), confirming the position of the Rheinland-Pfalz LfDI, Prof. Dr. Dieter Kugelmann, who had issued a formal warning and order to the unnamed private health insurer in 2022. No financial penalty is recorded in this matter; the case concerns a corrective order rather than an administrative fine.

The LfDI found that the insurer was processing health data contained in reimbursement invoices, including diagnoses relating to conditions such as back problems, diabetes and asthma, to identify and recommend tailored preventive programmes to policyholders. The LfDI determined that this analysis constituted a processing purpose beyond that for which the data were originally submitted, namely cost reimbursement, and that no sufficient legal basis existed for such secondary use of special category health data under Art. 9 GDPR without the data subjects' consent. The Verwaltungsgericht Mainz and the Oberverwaltungsgericht Koblenz had each initially accepted the insurer's arguments against the order, before the matter proceeded to the federal level on revision.

The Bundesverwaltungsgericht determined that private health insurers are required to obtain explicit consent from insured persons before processing diagnosis information derived from invoices for any purpose beyond reimbursement, including the recommendation of preventive or coaching programmes. Prof. Dr. Kugelmann stated that insured persons must be able to rely on the fact that their health data are not processed for other purposes without their consent and that they are adequately informed, characterising the ruling as providing legal certainty for the processing of patient data. The authority states that the judgment confirms the LfDI's practice and strengthens the right of self-determination of insured persons with respect to their health data (Art. 9 GDPR).

rows.title: CNIL Closes Injunction Against KASPR Following Full Compliance with December 2024 Orders
rows.summary:
France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has closed the injunction it imposed on data intelligence company KASPR in December 2024, following the company's demonstration of full compliance with remediation orders issued alongside a fine of €240,000 (approximately USD 261,000), on the basis of multiple violations of the General Data Protection Regulation.

On 4 March 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés adopted a deliberation formally closing the injunction it had pronounced against KASPR on 5 December 2024, at which time the authority's restricted committee had also imposed a fine of €240,000 (approximately USD 261,000). The original decision had further provided that non-compliance with the injunction would expose KASPR to a daily penalty of €10,000 per day of delay.

The CNIL determined in December 2024 that KASPR had committed four categories of breach. First, the CNIL found that the company collected contact details of LinkedIn users who had expressly chosen to limit the visibility of their profiles (Art. 5(1)(a) GDPR). Second, the CNIL found that KASPR retained personal data for five years from each data update, which typically occurred when an individual changed position or employer, constituting a breach of storage limitation requirements (Art. 5(1)(e) GDPR). Third, the CNIL determined that data subjects were not informed of the collection of their data prior to 2022, and that from 2022 onwards the information provided was available only in English, in breach of transparency obligations (Art. 13 and 14 GDPR). Fourth, the CNIL found that when individuals exercised their right of access to identify the source of their data, KASPR provided only the response that data had been collected from publicly accessible sources, falling short of its obligations under Art. 15 GDPR.

In response to the injunction, the CNIL finds that KASPR, within the prescribed timeframes, provided evidence of effective remedial measures. The CNIL notes that the company chose to erase its entire database and cease all data collection from LinkedIn. The CNIL reports that it also removed the automatic renewal mechanism for data retention periods triggered by profile updates. The CNIL confirms that KASPR now provides information to data subjects in all official languages of the European Union and has responded to the access requests submitted by complainants. On the basis of this demonstrated compliance, the CNIL's restricted committee determined not to enforce the daily penalty and closed the injunction.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: NTIA Recommends FCC Seek Public Comment on GPS Signal Protection Rules for Direct-to-Device Satellite Equipment
rows.summary:
The National Telecommunications and Information Administration has issued a formal recommendation to the Federal Communications Commission calling for updated guidance to protect the GPS L1 signal at 1575.42 MHz from interference caused by Mobile Satellite Service Direct-to-Device user equipment operating in the 1610–1660.5 MHz band.

On 6 March 2026, the National Telecommunications and Information Administration, acting through its Office of Spectrum Management, transmitted a recommendation letter to the FCC addressing interference risks posed by Direct-to-Device, or D2D, user equipment operated by Mobile Satellite Service providers. The letter requests that the FCC initiate a public comment process on new or updated guidance designed to protect the GPS L1 signal, which operates at 1575.42 MHz, from emissions generated by D2D devices functioning in the adjacent 1610–1660.5 MHz band.

NTIA recommends that the FCC seek public input on emission limits applicable to D2D user equipment, with a view to establishing protections sufficient to prevent harmful interference to GPS L1 operations. The recommendation identifies a specific operational restriction: NTIA further recommends that D2D devices unable to satisfy certain emission limits be rendered incapable of operating aboard aircraft. NTIA indicates that the letter does not specify the precise numerical emission thresholds that would trigger this restriction, indicating that the parameters are intended to be developed through the FCC's forthcoming public comment process.

NTIA states that the recommendation reflects its role as the principal federal adviser on spectrum policy and its responsibility for coordinating federal government spectrum use with the FCC's oversight of non-federal users. NTIA identifies the GPS L1 frequency as widely used across aviation, navigation, timing, and critical infrastructure applications, and NTIA's letter identifies the proximity of MSS D2D operational frequencies as a potential source of out-of-band emissions affecting that band. NTIA notes that no fine or enforcement action is associated with this recommendation, which is advisory in nature and directed at initiating FCC rulemaking or guidance activity.

rows.title: Bremen LfDI Publishes Eighth Annual GDPR Report Covering 2025 Activity
rows.summary:
The Bremen Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information, Dr Timo Utermark, has published his eighth annual report under Art. 59 GDPR, covering supervisory activity across 2025 and addressing fines, artificial intelligence, digital sovereignty and police law reform.

On 16 March 2026, the Landesbeauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit der Freien Hansestadt Bremen released the report to the Bremische Bürgerschaft and the President of the Senate, as required under Art. 59 GDPR. The report, with an editorial deadline of 31 December 2025, was prepared by the Commissioner's office in Bremerhaven.

The LfDI sets out the authority's enforcement activity in the area of fines (Chapter 3), covering two specific cases: one involving GPS tracking by a former intimate partner, and one concerning unauthorised data queries made by an employee exploiting their professional position. The LfDI notes procedural guidance on fines and identifies a new fining competence arising from the EU Regulation on the Transparency and Targeting of Political Advertising. No fine amounts are disclosed in the portions of the source text available.

The LfDI addresses the relationship between artificial intelligence and data protection law (Art. 59 GDPR), examining the training of AI systems and the implementation of the EU AI Act in Germany. The report also considers the supervisory jurisdiction of the authority in relation to the Data Act and the Political Advertising Regulation. On digital sovereignty, the LfDI examines the continued use of Microsoft 365 by public bodies and finds this raises questions about compliance with data protection requirements applicable to public sector controllers. The report further covers an online interpreter service, the use of fax in Bremen's public administration, notifications of data protection officers under Art. 37(7) GDPR, video surveillance of detention cells and waste disposal sites, and a review of the police-held personal marker for psychological and behavioural disorders. The LfDI also sets out its formal opinion on the draft Bremen Police Act, addressing provisions on the examination of electronic storage media, electronic monitoring bracelets and location data collection.

rows.title: CNIL and HAS Launch Public Consultation on Draft Guide for AI Use in Healthcare Settings
rows.summary:
France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés and the Haute Autorité de Santé have jointly published a draft guide on the use of artificial intelligence systems in care settings, open to public consultation until 16 April 2026, under the framework of the EU AI Act, the Medical Devices Regulation and the In Vitro Diagnostic Medical Devices Regulation.

On 5 March 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés and the Haute Autorité de Santé launched a public consultation on a draft guide titled "Accompagner le bon usage des systèmes d'intelligence artificielle en contexte de soins". The guide was produced by a multidisciplinary working group co-piloted by both authorities, drawing on targeted stakeholder hearings and a systematic review of existing materials. The authorities note that no fine is associated with this publication.

The CNIL and HAS note that 65 per cent of French public health establishments already use AI systems, citing data from the Fédération Hospitalière de France. The draft guide, the authorities state, pursues two objectives: to clarify the applicable legal and regulatory framework and the obligations binding health professionals and structures, and to establish best-practice recommendations for deployment of AI systems in care that is compliant with regulation, ethical and secure. The guide covers all AI systems with a direct impact on patient care, regardless of their classification under the EU AI Act, the Medical Device Regulation or the In Vitro Diagnostic Medical Devices Regulation, and applies to all actors in the healthcare sector, including hospitals and independent practitioners.

The CNIL notes that the consultation document comprises twelve fiches: ten covering the various stages of AI system deployment and use, from acquisition to deinstallation, and two generic fiches addressing governance and the specific characteristics of generative AI systems. The CNIL and HAS state that the guide classifies its best-practice recommendations into three levels: standard recommendations reflecting consensus applicable in the majority of contexts, advanced recommendations constituting levers for continuous improvement depending on available resources, and reflexes to be adopted systematically to avoid non-compliant, inappropriate or dangerous conduct. The authorities confirm the guide does not cover the development phase of AI systems, for which the CNIL has published separate dedicated content, nor AI systems used by healthcare actors without direct impact on patient care, such as human resources or billing management. Contributions are accepted exclusively via an online form and the consultation closes on 16 April 2026.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: Privacy Commissioner of Canada addresses AI governance and deepfake risks at Victoria Summit
rows.summary:
The Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada has outlined its approach to AI governance, deepfake regulation and trust-based data protection frameworks, with Commissioner Philippe Dufresne citing expanding investigations and a new joint international declaration on AI-generated content.

On 5 March 2026, Commissioner Philippe Dufresne delivered keynote remarks at the Victoria International Privacy and Security Summit, setting out the Office's current enforcement and policy priorities across AI governance, children's privacy, cybersecurity and law reform, without announcing a specific fine or enforcement decision.

The Commissioner notes that in January 2026 he expanded the Office's existing investigation into the social media platform X to include its Grok chatbot, following concern about the platform being used to create and distribute explicit synthetic images of individuals, including children, without consent. The Commissioner states that the use of personal information without consent to generate deepfakes, including intimate images, represents a growing phenomenon that poses serious risks to individuals' fundamental right to privacy. The Office identifies this as among the emerging risks associated with the rapid advancement of AI capabilities, referencing the second International AI Safety Report, released on 3 February 2026 and authored by more than 100 experts under Professor Yoshua Bengio, which the Commissioner notes characterises accessible AI tools as having "substantially lowered the barrier to creating harmful synthetic content at scale."

The Commissioner also states that the Office signed a joint declaration on AI-generated content with more than 50 global data protection authorities, convened by the Global Privacy Assembly's enforcement collaboration working group. The declaration, the Commissioner notes, identifies fundamental principles that the Office determines are required to guide organisations developing and deploying AI content generation systems, including implementing safeguards to prevent misuse of personal information, ensuring meaningful transparency about AI system capabilities and acceptable uses, and taking steps to address specific risks to children. The Commissioner further states that a survey conducted by the Office found that nine in ten Canadians express concern about the protection of their privacy, and that trust in how personal information is handled is directly affecting consumer behaviour, including decisions to withhold personal information, alter privacy settings and disengage from organisations that have experienced a breach.

rows.title: CNIL Publishes 2026 Edition of Tables Informatique et Libertés, Consolidating Decisional Practice Under GDPR and French Data Protection Law
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published the 2026 edition of its Tables Informatique et Libertés, a thematically organised reference document consolidating jurisprudence and decisional practice under the General Data Protection Regulation and the French loi Informatique et Libertés.

On 2 March 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés released version 2.3 of the Tables, dated 20 February 2026. The document covers rulings from a range of authorities across extended time periods: the Court of Justice of the European Union (2003–2025), the European Court of Human Rights (2017–2025), the Conseil d'État (1978–2025), the Cour de cassation (1998–2025), the Conseil Constitutionnel (1978–2025), the European Data Protection Board (2018–2025), and CNIL decisions themselves (2021–2026). The CNIL states that no fines are associated with this publication.

The CNIL states that the Tables serve two distinct objectives. First, the document is intended to support consistent internal application of doctrine within the authority, which the CNIL notes has grown substantially in staffing in recent years and handles a high volume of matters annually, including approximately 20,000 written information requests and around 1,500 complex legal consultation requests from data controllers and professional federations. The CNIL finds that internal consistency in the application of its doctrine is a fundamental requirement of equal treatment as a public service obligation. Second, the Tables are directed at external audiences, including data protection officers, lawyers, consultants and academics, to whom the CNIL's decisional positions, particularly those arising from corrective measures and complaint-handling that do not result in published decisions, have not previously been accessible.

The CNIL notes that while decisions of its restricted formation (formation restreinte) are partly public, a large volume of its practical positions, taken through legal reminders, formal notices (mises en demeure) and complaint rejections, remains without external publication. The Tables are structured thematically, covering principles, legal bases (Art. 6 GDPR), individual rights, security obligations (Art. 32 GDPR), international transfers and sanctions. The CNIL states the document is intended to be updated on a regular basis, and invites professionals to submit corrections or additional references directly to the authority.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: Ecuador's Superintendencia de Protección de Datos Personales Publishes Version 2 of Risk Management and Impact Assessment Guide
rows.summary:
Ecuador's Superintendencia de Protección de Datos Personales (SPDP) has published the second version of its Guide on Risk Management and Impact Assessment of Personal Data Processing, establishing a structured methodology for controllers and processors to comply with the Ley Orgánica de Protección de Datos Personales (LOPDP).

On 2 March 2026, the Superintendencia de Protección de Datos Personales, through its Intendencia General de Innovación Tecnológica y Seguridad de Datos Personales, released the document authored by Luis Enríquez Álvarez, Intendente General de Innovación Tecnológica y Seguridad de Datos Personales, and edited by Vanessa Hervás Novoa, adviser to the SPDP, with the support of Superintendent Fabrizio Peralta-Díaz. The SPDP notes that no fines or enforcement orders are associated with this publication.

The SPDP establishes that the LOPDP is grounded in risk management principles and that the purpose of risk management is to reduce uncertainty for controllers and processors, enabling informed decisions for the effective protection of the rights and freedoms of personal data subjects. The SPDP states that risk management is not a default compliance exercise involving checklists or unsupported risk estimates, but rather the construction of a risk compliance culture requiring reliable data, calibrated expert opinion, meaningful metrics and appropriate risk models. According to the SPDP, the guide sets out both quantitative and qualitative methods adaptable to different circumstances of controllers and processors, with impact assessment methods grounded in the development of real risk scenarios capable of affecting data subjects' rights and freedoms.

The SPDP describes that the guide's structure covers fundamental principles including risk management foundations, integration of information security risks with rights-and-freedoms risk management, the role of best practice standards, justification of risk rationales, risk compliance principles, audit typologies encompassing permanent, legal, organisational and technical audits, and categories of personal data security breaches covering confidentiality, integrity and availability. According to the SPDP, the procedural section addresses each stage of the risk management process: context establishment, risk identification, risk analysis, risk evaluation including data protection impact assessments (DPIA), and risk treatment, together with an annexed DPIA format template. The SPDP notes the guide is designed to be scalable to micro, small and medium-sized enterprises as well as larger organisations.
rows.body_name: Superintendencia de Proteccion de Datos Personales

rows.title: CSA Mandates Cyber Trust Mark Certification for Critical Information Infrastructure Owners, Auditors and Licensed Cybersecurity Providers
rows.summary:
The Cyber Security Agency of Singapore has announced mandatory Cyber Trust Mark certification requirements for Critical Information Infrastructure Owners, CII auditors, and licensed cybersecurity service providers, with tiered deadlines running to end-2027, under the Cybersecurity Act (s. 49).

On 2 March 2026, the Cyber Security Agency of Singapore (CSA) announced, at the Ministry of Digital Development and Information Committee of Supply Debates 2026, that Critical Information Infrastructure Owners (CIIOs), approved auditors conducting audits for CIIOs, and licensed cybersecurity service providers engaged in penetration testing and managed security operations centre monitoring services are required to obtain certification under the Cyber Trust Mark (CTM) framework. No financial penalties were announced in connection with this requirement; the announcement concerns certification obligations rather than enforcement action.

CSA states that CIIOs are required to attain CTM Level 5, the highest tier of the five-tier certification framework, for non-CII systems under their control that support business operations and services. CSA notes that CIIOs are granted a two-year grace period, with a compliance deadline of end-2027. CSA states that CII auditors are required to obtain CTM Level 5 at the organisational level for systems supporting their business operations, with a one-year grace period ending by end-2026. CSA notes that the CTM framework was enhanced in a prior period to incorporate domains covering Cloud security, Operational Technology security, and Artificial Intelligence security, reflecting what the agency describes as a fast-evolving digital landscape. The framework comprises five cybersecurity preparedness tiers, each encompassing between 10 and 22 domains.

For licensed cybersecurity service providers, CSA states that the requirement to obtain CTM Promoter (Tier 3) certification was implemented in February 2026 following a four-week public consultation conducted from 22 September 2025 to 21 October 2025. CSA received 17 responses to that consultation, with the agency noting that the majority of respondents welcomed the proposal to raise baseline cyber hygiene for providers with access to client data and systems. CSA finds that the measure is intended to reduce risks arising from cyber supply chain interdependencies. Licensed providers are granted a grace period until 31 December 2026 to obtain the required certification under s. 49 of the Cybersecurity Act, which governs the licensing of penetration testing and managed security operations centre monitoring services in Singapore.

rows.title: Denmark's Centre for Cyber Security Issues Guidance on Security Clearance Circular Amendments Effective 1 March 2026
rows.summary:
The Danish Agency for Society Security has published guidance for public authorities on amendments to Circular No. 10338 (the Security Circular), following changes introduced by the Ministry of Society Security and Emergency Preparedness, with the amended rules entering into force on 1 March 2026.

On 2 March 2026, the Danish Agency for Society Security (Styrelsen for Samfundssikkerhed) published a guidance document directed at public authorities that employ security-cleared personnel. The guidance explains how authorities are to apply the amended provisions of Circular No. 10338 when engaging new staff. The Agency notes that no financial penalties are associated with this regulatory development.

The Agency's guidance sets out that the amended circular introduces a mechanism allowing a public authority to grant a security clearance to an individual on the basis of an existing security clearance held by that person in another capacity. The Agency explains that, as a result, the employing authority is no longer required to submit a new security investigation request to the Danish Security and Intelligence Service (Politiets Efterretningstjeneste, PET). The guidance provides a step-by-step process for authorities seeking to rely on an existing clearance in this manner.

The Agency further notes that the amended circular introduces a suspension mechanism for renewal applications. The Agency states that where an authority submits a request to renew an existing security clearance before that clearance expires, the expiry of the clearance is suspended for a period of up to 12 months from its expiry date while the renewal application is being processed. The Agency describes the guidance as intended to assist authorities in understanding both elements of the amended circular, covering the transfer of an existing clearance and the suspension of clearance expiry. Authorities with questions regarding the guidance are directed to contact the Agency at raadgivning@samsik.dk.

rows.title: ANSSI and CNIL Launch Call for Interest in GDPR Audit Tool Testing for AI Models Under PANAME Project
rows.summary:
ANSSI, in partnership with the CNIL, PEReN and the IPoP project under France's PEPR Cybersecurity programme led by Inria, has issued a call for expressions of interest from public and private entities wishing to participate in the testing phase of a GDPR audit tool for artificial intelligence models, developed under the PANAME (Privacy Auditing of AI Models) initiative.

On 2 March 2026, ANSSI published the call for expressions of interest, noting that the PANAME project was launched jointly in June 2025 by the four partners. The call is open from 26 February 2026 to 28 March 2026 and is directed at all public and private entities established in European Union member states, including companies, startups, research laboratories and institutions that use or develop AI models.

The PANAME project aims to develop a software library, available in whole or in part as open-source, to standardise the way in which the privacy of AI models is evaluated. The project states that the tool is intended to enable efficient and lower-cost implementation of certain technical tests relating to the extraction of information from training data, tests that AI ecosystem actors may be required to carry out in order to assess the status of an AI model under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). The project notes that this addresses compliance obligations relevant to AI model development and deployment under EU data protection law (Regulation (EU) 2016/679).

Following an initial phase of technical specification and library development, the PANAME project members state that a testing phase with administrations and companies is now being initiated to ensure that the tool's development remains aligned with real-world use contexts. The project indicates that feedback gathered during this phase will be used to enrich the tool's functionalities and to confirm that it enables effective GDPR compliance assessment. A workshop is to be organised for entities that respond to the call.

rows.title: Croatian DPA Fines Real Estate Agency EUR 100,000 for Storage, Lawfulness and Security Failures
rows.summary:
The Croatian Personal Data Protection Agency (AZOP) has imposed an administrative fine of EUR 100,000 on a real estate agency acting as a data controller for multiple infringements of the General Data Protection Regulation, including unlawful retention of client data, processing of identification documents without legal basis, and failure to implement adequate technical and organisational measures.

On 19 February 2026, the Agencija za zaštitu osobnih podataka issued its decision following a supervisory procedure during which AZOP identified three distinct GDPR violations. AZOP levied a fine of EUR 100,000 against the unnamed real estate agency in respect of infringements of Articles 5(1)(e), 5(1)(c), 6(1) and 32(4) GDPR.

AZOP finds that the controller retained personal data belonging to 11,887 clients beyond the period for which those data were required, in breach of the storage limitation principle (Art. 5(1)(e) GDPR). The agency's paper archive contained forms titled Mediation Agreement for the Purchase of Real Estate, Mediation Agreement for the Rental/Lease of Real Estate, and Broker's Sheet, concluded between 23 September 2010 and 31 December 2019 at the latest. AZOP further determines that the controller processed, without a demonstrated legal basis, 914 copies of personal identification and financial documents, including 898 identity card copies, 6 passport copies, 3 bank card copies, 2 driving licence copies, 2 foreign passport copies, and individual copies of a health insurance card, a minor child's identity card, and a residence permit, contrary to Article 6(1) in conjunction with Article 5(1)(c) GDPR. AZOP notes that during the inspection the company's director stated that bank card copies were not collected, yet such copies were found within the mediation documentation, which AZOP determines further confirms a lack of oversight over processing operations.

With regard to technical and organisational measures, AZOP establishes that the controller failed to ensure adequate training and supervision of employees with access to personal data, in breach of Article 32(4) GDPR. AZOP finds that data protection training was conducted irregularly and to an insufficient extent. AZOP concludes that the non-compliance resulted from the controller's negligent conduct and that no harm to data subjects was established in this specific case.

rows.title: CNPD Joins 61 Data Protection Authorities in Joint Declaration on Privacy Risks of AI-Generated Content
rows.summary:
Cape Verde's Comissão Nacional de Proteção de Dados has co-signed a joint declaration alongside 60 other data protection authorities worldwide, setting out requirements for organisations that develop or deploy AI content-generation systems, with particular concern for risks to children.

On 27 February 2026, the Comissão Nacional de Proteção de Dados (CNPD) published its endorsement of the Joint Declaration on AI-Generated Images, which was originally released on 23 February 2026 and coordinated by the International Enforcement Cooperation Working Group (IEWG) of the Global Privacy Assembly (GPA). The declaration represents the unanimous position of 61 authorities and carries no associated financial penalty.

The CNPD, together with co-signatories, finds that AI systems capable of generating realistic images and videos of identifiable individuals without their knowledge or consent present serious risks to data protection and privacy. The declaration notes particular concern regarding harm to children depicted in such content. The authority finds that the declaration establishes fundamental requirements that organisations developing or using AI content-generation systems are required to observe.

Among the requirements set out, the declaration states that organisations are required to implement security measures to prevent the misuse of personal information and the generation of non-consensual intimate images and other harmful material, especially where children are depicted. The declaration further states that organisations are required to ensure effective transparency regarding system functionality, permitted uses, security measures, and procedures applicable in cases of misuse. The declaration states that organisations are additionally required to provide effective and accessible mechanisms enabling data subjects to request the removal of harmful content involving personal data, and to respond promptly to such requests. The declaration also states that organisations are required to address risks specific to children, implement enhanced safeguards, and provide age-appropriate information for children, parents, guardians and educators.

The CNPD notes that co-signatories intend to share information on their respective approaches to these concerns, including in the areas of enforcement, general policy measures, and education.

rows.title: IMY Sets 2026 Supervisory Priorities: AI in Public Sector, Children's Data and Law Enforcement Tools
rows.summary:
The Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection (IMY) has published its supervisory and guidance priorities for 2026, identifying AI use in the public sector, data protection for children and young people, and law enforcement tools as the three focus areas under the GDPR.

On 23 February 2026, IMY announced that these priority areas were selected following the authority's assessment that they present elevated privacy risks or that increased knowledge is needed to improve compliance. No financial penalties are associated with this priority-setting announcement.

IMY finds that the public sector's growing adoption of AI warrants particular attention because citizens engaging with public services frequently have no alternative provider to turn to, making it harder to avoid data processing by AI systems. Where those systems handle sensitive personal data, IMY finds that heightened demands apply with respect to privacy protection (Art. 9 GDPR). During 2026, IMY states it will follow up on how public bodies use AI and will provide support to help them deploy the technology in a manner consistent with data protection obligations.

On children and young people, IMY notes that minors are frequent users of digital services, including the internet, social media and apps, often without a full understanding of the associated risks. IMY further notes that children's personal data merits specific protection under the GDPR (Recital 38 GDPR). The authority states it will work to raise awareness among young people themselves and among those who process data on their behalf, including guardians and schools.

Regarding law enforcement, IMY finds that certain tools used by law enforcement agencies, including secret coercive measures and the collection of biometric data, involve restrictions on individuals' right to privacy. IMY states that its 2026 supervision and guidance activity will examine these tools from a data protection perspective. Director General Eric Leijonram is quoted as stating that IMY prioritises areas where the authority's efforts can make the most difference, reflecting its assessment that risks are greater in some areas than others.

rows.title: Brandenburg DPA Joins EDPB Coordinated Enforcement Action on Right to Erasure, Examining Ten Housing Companies
rows.summary:
The Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz Brandenburg participated in a European Data Protection Board coordinated enforcement action examining compliance with the right to erasure under Article 17 GDPR, selecting ten larger housing companies based in Brandenburg for review.

On 18 February 2026, the European Data Protection Board published the results of its 2025 Coordinated Enforcement Framework action on the right to erasure (Art. 17 GDPR). A total of 32 supervisory authorities across Europe participated, sending a standardised questionnaire to controllers. The EDPB notes that 764 bodies, companies and other controllers responded in total, including 60 in Germany. German participating authorities included those from Baden-Württemberg, Brandenburg, Mecklenburg-Vorpommern, Lower Saxony, North Rhine-Westphalia, Rhineland-Palatinate, and the Federal Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information.

The EDPB finds that controllers faced several recurring challenges in implementing the right to erasure. These included the absence of clear internal rules on when and how to delete personal data, a lack of internal staff training, and legal uncertainty regarding the application of exemptions and retention periods. The EDPB further identifies uncertainty among controllers concerning the deletion of personal data held in back-up systems and the requirements for anonymisation. The findings have been incorporated into a final report, which includes recommendations derived from the review. A German annex to the report additionally sets out a range of best practices.

Regarding Brandenburg's participation, Commissioner Dagmar Hartge states that the housing companies examined demonstrated a high level of cooperation, and that the results underline the importance of establishing internal deletion routines. Hartge states that tenants and prospective tenants are required to be able to rely on their personal data not being processed without authorisation. The Landesbeauftragte for Brandenburg notes that it will follow up by sending individual advisory letters to the housing companies that participated in the questionnaire, with the aim of raising awareness of the conditions governing the right to erasure. The 2025 coordinated action on the right to erasure is the fourth initiative under the CEF, following earlier actions in 2023 on data protection officers and in 2024 on the right of access.

rows.title: MLCommons Publishes Benchmarking Methodology for Jailbreak Attacks on Large Language Models
rows.summary:
MLCommons, in collaboration with researchers from institutions including the Alan Turing Institute, Google DeepMind, NVIDIA, Meta, and the Infocomm Media Development Authority of Singapore, has published a methodology paper proposing a taxonomy-driven framework for benchmarking single-turn jailbreak attacks on large language models, with reference to ISO/IEC 42001 as a governance alignment standard.

On 16 February 2026, MLCommons released the paper titled "A Robust, Defensible, and Reproducible Methodology for Benchmarking Single-Turn Jailbreak Attacks on Large Language Models", authored by a multi-institutional team across academia, industry and government bodies. The paper builds on prior MLCommons infrastructure, including AILuminate v1.0 and the MLCommons Jailbreak Benchmark v0.5, and addresses what the authors identify as structural limitations in existing jailbreak evaluation practice.

The paper finds that current jailbreak benchmarks exhibit three categories of deficiency. First, the paper finds that many benchmarks lack defensibility, as attack selection proceeds without a formal taxonomy, meaning external auditors cannot verify whether a benchmark meaningfully represents the relevant attack surface, which the paper states undermines governance credibility. Second, the paper finds that coverage gaps exist across attack families, noting that encoding-based obfuscation, semantic-preserving perturbations, and compositional instruction strategies are frequently underrepresented in evaluation corpora, leading to what the paper characterises as misleading robustness estimates. Third, the paper finds that reproducibility is compromised where categories overlap, operate at inconsistent abstraction levels, or lack deterministic placement rules, weakening inter-annotator agreement and making cross-organisational comparisons unstable.

In response to these findings, the paper introduces what it describes as a mechanism-first, benchmark-operational taxonomy of single-turn jailbreak attacks. The paper states that organisations deploying large language models in safety-critical, enterprise, and regulated environments require evaluation methodologies that are reproducible, interpretable, and defensible to auditors and governance bodies. The paper frames resistance to single-turn inference-time jailbreak attacks, defined as prompt manipulations designed to bypass safety controls, as an operational and governance requirement rather than a research concern, and positions the proposed taxonomy as a foundation for evaluations that can withstand regulatory scrutiny and support auditability in line with ISO/IEC 42001.

rows.title: Florida Attorney General Annual Report: 186 Notices of Alleged Violation Issued Under Florida Digital Bill of Rights in 2025
rows.summary:
The Florida Attorney General's Department of Legal Affairs issued 186 Notices of Alleged Violation to controllers under the Florida Digital Bill of Rights during the 2025 calendar year, with no penalties issued or collected, according to the authority's annual enforcement report published pursuant to s. 501.72(4), Florida Statutes.

On 10 February 2026, the Florida Attorney General's Office published its Florida Digital Bill of Rights Annual Enforcement Report covering the period from 1 January 2025 to 31 December 2025. According to the report, no financial penalties in any currency were issued or collected during the reporting period.

The Department of Legal Affairs reports receiving 1,496 consumer complaints and inquiries during the reporting period. Of these, 685 were closed as falling outside the scope of the Florida Digital Bill of Rights, while 811 were placed under active review as potentially falling within the categories of consumer relief set out under s. 501.705(2), Florida Statutes. The report notes that 130 complaints fell under two or more categories and were counted in each relevant category. The largest volume of complaints under active review related to consumer rights to confirm whether a controller is processing personal data and to access that data, accounting for 730 complaints. Further categories included deletion of personal data (97), correction of inaccuracies (74), portability (60), opting out of processing (53), opting out of collection of sensitive data including precise geolocation data (35), and opting out of collection via voice or facial recognition features (15).

On enforcement outcomes, the Department reports that 568 complaints were closed following initial review due to legal insufficiency, and 57 remain under initial review to determine what action, if any, is to be taken. The report states that 60 initial inquiries were made to potential controllers to assess whether they fall within the scope of enforcement under the Act (s. 501.72(4)(b), Fla. Stat.). Of the 186 Notices of Alleged Violation issued to controllers, the Department reports that 64 were resolved without litigation. According to the report, one active litigation is ongoing alleging violations of s. 501.701 et seq., Florida Statutes, against an alleged controller.

rows.title: CNIL 2025 Annual Sanctions Review: 83 Decisions and EUR 486.8 Million in Fines
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published its 2025 sanctions review, recording 83 sanctions and cumulative fines of EUR 486,839,500 across enforcement actions concerning cookies and trackers, employee video surveillance, and processor obligations under the GDPR.

On 9 February 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés released its annual enforcement balance sheet for 2025, detailing 259 decisions in total: 83 sanctions, 143 formal notices, 31 reminders of legal obligations, and 2 warnings. The CNIL finds that the EUR 486,839,500 in total fines reflects enforcement across its ordinary and simplified sanctioning procedures. The CNIL notes that of the 83 sanctions, 78 were fines, three were astreinte liquidation decisions, and two were reprimands.

The CNIL determines that 16 sanctions were issued by the restricted formation under the ordinary procedure, with 67 issued under the simplified procedure introduced in 2022. The CNIL notes that four of the ordinary-procedure decisions were adopted in cooperation with European counterparts through the one-stop-shop mechanism. On cookies and trackers, the CNIL finds that 21 entities were sanctioned for violations including depositing trackers without user consent, providing insufficient information for informed consent, and failing to give effect to user refusal or withdrawal of consent. The CNIL reports that the restricted formation imposed fines of EUR 325 million and EUR 150 million on two entities.

On employee surveillance, the CNIL determines that 16 organisations were sanctioned for unlawful video monitoring of staff, including continuous filming of checkout and office employees and the use of concealed cameras outside exceptional circumstances. The CNIL finds that under the simplified procedure, the three principal grounds for sanction were inadequate data security, failure to cooperate with the CNIL, and non-compliance with data subject rights requests, each recorded against 14 organisations.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: Japan AI Safety Institute Publishes Japanese-Language Guide to AI System Attack Types and Security Impacts
rows.summary:
The Japan AI Safety Institute (J-AISI), operating under the Information-technology Promotion Agency (IPA), has published a Japanese-language guidance document cataloguing eleven categories of known attacks against AI systems, mapping each to confidentiality, integrity and availability impacts.

On 6 February 2026, J-AISI published "Securing AI Systems: A Guide to Known Attacks and Impacts (Japanese Version)", authored by Naoto Kiribuchi, Kengo Zenitani and Takayuki Semitsu of J-AISI and IPA. The document is presented as a Japanese translation and detailed version of a previously published J-AISI reference, itself corresponding to an arXiv technical paper (arXiv:2506.23296v1). No financial penalties are associated with this publication.

J-AISI identifies eleven principal attack types applicable to both predictive and generative AI systems, designated attacks A through K in the document. These include model extraction attacks, training data inference, model poisoning, data poisoning, evasion attacks, sponge attacks, prompt theft, prompt injection, code injection, fine-tuning attacks and rowhammer attacks. J-AISI maps each attack type to one or more of three security impact categories: information disclosure, system compromise and computational resource exhaustion, which the document aligns explicitly with the confidentiality, integrity and availability (CIA) triad.

J-AISI states that the guide is directed at researchers, developers, security practitioners and policymakers, including those without specialist AI security expertise, with the stated purpose of supporting recognition of AI-specific risks and implementation of effective countermeasures. The document notes that J-AISI finds existing academic surveys have addressed either predictive or generative AI systems in isolation, and that a practically oriented guide covering both system types, with explicit linkage between attack mechanisms and security impacts, addresses a gap in available resources. J-AISI further identifies, for each attack type, the attacker capabilities required as preconditions for a successful attack, and organises referenced attack literature by data modality and AI model type to assist practitioners conducting risk assessments and red-team exercises. The document references established international frameworks including NIST AI 100-2e2025 and MITRE ATLAS as part of its contextual analysis.

rows.title: CNIL Publishes Economic Analysis Work Programme for 2026–2028
rows.summary:
France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published its economic analysis work programme for 2026 to 2028, setting out eight strategic workstreams focused on personal data business models and the economic impact of the authority's own decisions.

On 2 February 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published its work programme for the mission d'analyse économique covering the period 2026 to 2028. The programme carries no fine or enforcement measure and involves no currency figures. The CNIL established the economic analysis unit in early 2023, following a prefiguration year in 2022, to develop sustained in-house expertise on the economics of personal data regulation.

The CNIL states that the unit conducts impact studies, sectoral analyses and quantitative work to inform the authority's decisions, including the production of recommendations and guidelines. The CNIL notes that the programme states that the unit also supports cooperation with other regulators, linking data protection with competition and innovation policy. The CNIL finds that the underlying premise of its approach is that individuals who are better informed and have greater control over their choices will have stronger trust in the digital economy, a condition the authority considers necessary for that economy's development.

The CNIL notes that prior workstreams covered the intersection of data protection and competition law, including a contribution to the mission led by Bruno Lasserre whose conclusions were published in November 2024, the economic foundations of sanction amounts, privacy-preserving business models, the secondary data market including data brokers, and the costs and benefits of the data protection officer role (Art. 37–39 GDPR). On GDPR economic impact, the CNIL finds that compliance costs for businesses were real but largely temporary and lower than anticipated, that research provides no evidence the regulation reduced turnover or profitability, and that innovation effects include a positive component through compliance-driven innovation.
