The CNIL identifies eight workstreams for 2026 to 2028 that include continued work on competition and contestability in digital markets including participation in the Digital Markets Act high-level group, further quantification of GDPR benefits for businesses and individuals, the integration of economic and quantitative elements into sanction-setting methodology, and analysis of the economic impact of generative artificial intelligence across its value chain.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: Bermuda PrivCom Co-Sponsors Two AI Resolutions at 47th Global Privacy Assembly in Seoul
rows.summary:
The Office of the Privacy Commissioner for Bermuda co-sponsored two resolutions on artificial intelligence adopted at the 47th Global Privacy Assembly in Seoul, South Korea, addressing the use of personal data in AI training and the role of human oversight in automated decision-making systems.

On 29 January 2026, the Office of the Privacy Commissioner for Bermuda (PrivCom) published details of its co-sponsorship of two resolutions passed at the 47th Global Privacy Assembly (GPA), which convened in Seoul in September 2025. The GPA brings together more than 130 data protection and privacy authorities worldwide. No financial penalties are associated with the resolutions.

The first resolution, submitted by the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner and co-sponsored by PrivCom alongside authorities including the Italian Garante, the CNIL, the Data Protection Commission of Ireland, and the European Data Protection Supervisor, addresses the collection, use and disclosure of personal data to pre-train, train and fine-tune AI models. The resolution affirms that AI training is required to comply with existing privacy and data protection laws, warns against the indiscriminate scraping of publicly available personal information without a lawful condition for processing, and calls for stronger cross-border enforcement cooperation among data protection authorities to oversee global AI systems. The resolution also promotes engagement with the OECD, the Council of Europe, and UN bodies to advance AI governance frameworks. The GPA underscores, in this resolution, that privacy constitutes a fundamental human right.

The second resolution, submitted by the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada on behalf of the GPA Working Group on Ethics and Data Protection in Artificial Intelligence, addresses human oversight of automated decision-making systems. The resolution reaffirms that individuals must not be subject to fully automated decisions without meaningful human review and safeguards. The GPA finds that oversight is required to be effective, informed, and empowered rather than symbolic or procedural, and warns that automation risks undermining transparency, explainability, and accountability where individuals cannot understand or challenge system outputs. The resolution calls for clear governance frameworks enabling human intervention, override, or audit of algorithmic decisions, and highlights the need for protections where decisions affect employment, access to services, vulnerable persons, or other fundamental rights.

rows.title: IMY Fines Sportadmin SEK 6 Million for Inadequate Security Following Data Breach Affecting 2.1 Million Individuals
rows.summary:
Sweden's Integritetsskyddsmyndigheten (IMY) has imposed an administrative fine of SEK 6 million (approximately EUR 530,000) on sports administration platform Sportadmin following a cyber attack that exposed personal data relating to more than 2.1 million individuals, finding a violation of Article 32 of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

On 28 January 2026, the Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection (IMY) published its decision against Sportadmin i Skandinavien AB, imposing a fine of SEK 6 million (approximately EUR 530,000) after a supervisory review initiated in response to a cyber attack that occurred in January 2025. IMY finds that the attacker gained access to data concerning more than 2.1 million individuals and subsequently published that data on the Darknet.

IMY finds that the compromised data primarily concerned children and young people and included names, contact details, personal identity numbers, and information about the sport and sports club each individual was associated with. IMY's decision notes that the leaked data also included sensitive health data and, to some extent, data relating to persons with protected identity, meaning individuals whose personal data is held as confidential. IMY's review identified both technical and organisational deficiencies at Sportadmin. IMY finds that, for a period prior to the attack, Sportadmin was aware of certain weaknesses in its systems and of areas carrying elevated risks of attack, and is deemed not to have done enough to address them.

IMY further finds that Sportadmin lacked the routines required to detect deficiencies in existing security measures and did not operate a system capable of detecting intrusions and attempted intrusions in real time (Art. 32 GDPR). IMY determines that, had such measures been in place, Sportadmin would have been better positioned to prevent the incident or limit the resulting damage. IMY concludes that Sportadmin's failure to maintain a level of security appropriate to the personal data it processed constitutes a violation of Article 32 GDPR, providing the basis for the SEK 6 million administrative fine.

rows.title: Philippines NPC opens applications for Accountability Agent under APEC and Global CBPR Systems
rows.summary: The Philippine National Privacy Commission opened applications for recognition as an Accountability Agent under the APEC Cross-Border Privacy Rules (CBPR) System and the Global CBPR System. Initial application period: 27 January – 27 February 2026 (extended to 20 March 2026); nominations period extended to 16 April 2026. Separate applications are required for APEC and Global CBPR — recognition under one does not constitute recognition under the other. Accountability Agents certify whether participating organisations meet CBPR requirements.

rows.title: BEREC Welcomes European Commission Digital Networks Act Proposal, Confirms Advisory Role in Legislative Process
rows.summary:
The Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications (BEREC) has issued a statement welcoming the European Commission's proposal for a Digital Networks Act (DNA) and confirming its intention to provide technical expertise and advice throughout the forthcoming legislative process.

On 22 January 2026, BEREC published a press release stating its position on the Commission's DNA proposal, which consolidates several existing instruments including the European Electronic Communications Code (EECC), the BEREC Regulation, the Open Internet Regulation, the ePrivacy Directive and the Radio Spectrum Policy Programme. No fines were imposed; the statement concerns a legislative development rather than an enforcement action.

BEREC finds the DNA proposal represents a transition in the EU regulatory framework, aimed at ensuring fitness-for-purpose in light of technical and market developments. BEREC notes the proposal introduces provisions intended to facilitate the transition to fibre, high-quality 5G and future network technologies while preserving effective competition. BEREC's statement indicates it shares the Commission's assessment that core principles of the EECC, in particular ex-ante market regulation, are required to be maintained where market failures persist, on the basis that this approach ensures regulatory predictability and safeguards end-user welfare and rights. BEREC further states the future regulatory framework is required to respond to developments including digitalisation, virtualisation of network functions, integration of cloud solutions into networks, and the need to strengthen security and resilience.

BEREC confirms it will conduct a thorough examination of the Commission's proposals and will remain available to provide advice and support to legislators. As part of its engagement, BEREC notes it will hold its annual Stakeholder Forum on 31 March 2026, at which Executive Vice-President Henna Virkkunen will deliver a keynote address and panellists will discuss the evolution of the regulatory framework in light of the DNA proposal. BEREC additionally plans a public workshop in the first quarter of 2026 on migration to fibre-optic networks and copper switch-off, covering competitive dynamics following switch-off and the needs of vulnerable end-users, with the date and format to be confirmed.
rows.body_name: Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications

rows.title: EDPB Adopts Law Enforcement Directive Evaluation Report and Processor BCR Recommendations
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board adopted, on 15 January 2026, a contribution to the European Commission's evaluation of the Law Enforcement Directive and Recommendations 1/2026 on processor binding corporate rules, consolidating earlier framework documents.

On 15 January 2026, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) formally adopted two instruments: a report supporting the Commission's forthcoming evaluation of Directive 2016/680 and Recommendations 1/2026 on processor binding corporate rules (BCR-ST). The Commission is required to submit its evaluation report to the European Parliament and the Council no later than 6 May 2026.

In its report, the EDPB finds that data protection authorities have requested clarification on the Law Enforcement Directive's scope, particularly its relationship with the GDPR where overlaps arise, and greater attention to challenges posed by new technologies, including artificial intelligence, in law enforcement. The EDPB finds that law enforcement authorities are required to use such tools in strict compliance with the directive's provisions, ensuring necessity, proportionality and adequate safeguards. The EDPB further finds that the role of data protection officers in law enforcement contexts requires strengthening to ensure consistent application of rules, and that supervisory authorities require additional financial and human resources to fulfil tasks arising from recent legal instruments.

Regarding Recommendations 1/2026, the EDPB determines that these consolidate WP265 and WP257 into a single revised document governing BCR-ST approvals. The EDPB finds that BCR-ST constitute a transfer tool under Article 46 GDPR, available to groups of undertakings acting on behalf of controllers outside the group. The EDPB notes that the recommendations clarify that a processor member holding BCR-ST is not required to conclude a separate sub-processing contract with other group members covered by those rules.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CNIL Publishes Synthesis of Public Consultation Responses on Multi-Device Consent Recommendation
rows.summary:
France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published its synthesis of contributions received during the public consultation on its draft recommendation concerning the collection of multi-device consent, confirming the final scope and key conditions of the framework under the RGPD and the loi Informatique et Libertés.

On 15 January 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published the synthesis of responses to its public consultation, which was launched on 24 April 2025. No financial penalties are associated with this publication. The document sets out the ten contributions received, from three professional associations, one civil society association, and six private companies, and details the CNIL's responses to the most frequently raised concerns.

On the question of scope, the CNIL determines that the recommendation will not be extended to cover consent for the use of offline data, such as purchase data processed via customer relationship management platforms. The CNIL finds that consent collected through a consent management platform generally covers the processing of such offline data for the purposes to which users consent, provided that the CNIL determines individuals receive adequate information to ensure the validity of that consent. The CNIL also determines that non-logged-in environments remain outside the scope of the recommendation, on the basis that the CNIL finds extending multi-device consent to such environments would require intrusive processing to recognise a single user across devices and raises specific questions that the recommendation is not designed to address.

On the legality of multi-device consent, the CNIL finds that neither the RGPD nor the loi Informatique et Libertés precludes consent from being given across multiple devices. The CNIL states, however, that the lawfulness of any such arrangement depends on the conditions of its implementation as detailed in the recommendation. The CNIL further determines that a data controller may choose to offer only a multi-device consent model, provided the conditions set out in the recommendation are met. Regarding the information banner recommended for display after a user authenticates on a new device, the CNIL states that this measure is intended to ensure the informed character of consent by recommending a staged information approach, at the point of collection and subsequently upon connection of a new device, and that it is not intended to impose a new act of consent collection at that stage.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CAISI Issues Request for Information on Security of AI Agent Systems
rows.summary:
The centre for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) at the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) has published a Request for Information seeking input on the secure development and deployment of AI agent systems, with responses to inform voluntary guidelines and best practices.

On 12 January 2026, the centre for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI), operating within the U.S. Department of Commerce's National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), published a Request for Information (RFI) in the Federal Register (docket no. NIST-2025-0035) concerning security considerations for artificial intelligence agent systems. No fines or penalties are associated with this publication. The comment period closes on 9 March 2026.

CAISI identifies AI agent systems as capable of planning and taking autonomous actions that affect real-world systems or environments. CAISI distinguishes between security risks common to software generally, such as authentication vulnerabilities and memory management flaws, and risks specific to AI agent systems. CAISI identifies the latter category as including threats arising from AI model outputs interacting with adversarial data, such as indirect prompt injection, risks from the use of models subject to data poisoning, and the risk that models may take actions harmful to security even in the absence of adversarial inputs, including through specification gaming or misaligned objectives.

CAISI frames these security challenges as both a barrier to current adoption and a potential risk to public safety and national security as AI agent systems become more widely deployed. The RFI poses questions covering the nature and evolution of unique security threats affecting AI agent systems, methods for improving security during development and deployment, gaps in existing cybersecurity approaches when applied to agent systems, methods for measuring agent security, and interventions in deployment environments to constrain and monitor agent access. CAISI states that responses from AI agent deployers, developers, and computer security researchers will inform future voluntary guidelines and best practices, and will contribute to CAISI's ongoing research and evaluations of agent security. Respondents are encouraged to submit concrete examples, case studies, and actionable recommendations based on direct experience with AI agent systems. Comments are to be submitted via www.regulations.gov under docket no. NIST-2025-0035.
rows.body_name: US Center for AI Standards and Innovation

rows.title: Japan's Personal Information Protection Commission Sets Out Triennial Review Reform Policy for APPI
rows.summary:
Japan's Personal Information Protection Commission has published the outline of its policy for amending the Act on the Protection of Personal Information, derived from the triennial review obligation under Article 10 of the supplementary provisions of the 2020 amended APPI, covering consent exemptions, children's data protections, biometric data restrictions, enforcement powers and a new surcharge mechanism.

On 9 January 2026, the Personal Information Protection Commission published the outline of its system reform policy based on the triennial review provision (Supplementary Provisions, Art. 10) inserted by the 2020 amendment to the Act on the Protection of Personal Information. The Commission states that the reform reflects rapid developments in information and communications technology as well as international trends.

The Commission determines that several consent requirements are to be revised. The Commission states that the policy provides that third-party provision of personal data and acquisition of publicly available sensitive personal information will not require the data subject's consent where the data is used solely for the creation of statistical information, including AI development classified as statistical processing. The Commission also finds that consent requirements for purpose-of-use violations, acquisition of sensitive personal information and third-party transfers are to be relaxed where handling clearly does not harm the rights and interests of the individual concerned. The Commission states that where the data subject is under 16 years of age, consent and notification obligations are to be directed explicitly to the legal representative, and a duty provision requiring priority consideration of the best interests of the minor is to be introduced.

On enforcement, the Commission determines that facial feature data and equivalent information will be subject to mandatory disclosure obligations, and that opt-out third-party transfers of such data will be prohibited. The Commission further states that a surcharge mechanism is to be introduced requiring payment of an amount equivalent to financial gains derived from serious violations that infringe individual rights and interests through large-scale, malicious handling of personal information. The Commission also states that the policy provides for revised order requirements to enable faster correction of violations and new penalties for malicious-purpose provision of personal information databases and fraudulent acquisition of personal information.

rows.title: ASIC Sets Out Enforcement and Surveillance Framework for Illegal Phoenix Activity
rows.summary:
The Australian Securities and Investments Commission has published details of its regulatory framework targeting illegal phoenix activity, encompassing engagement, surveillance and enforcement tools directed at company directors, service providers and registered liquidators under Australian corporations law.

On 7 January 2026, the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) released a detailed statement of its current approach to identifying, disrupting and taking action against illegal phoenix activity. No fine or penalty was announced in connection with this publication. ASIC states that its regulatory focus is directed at director, service provider and registered liquidator misconduct that harms creditors and consumers and reduces business confidence.

ASIC describes illegal phoenix activity as occurring when company directors abandon a company or transfer its business to a new entity without paying true or market value, leaving debts with the old company, which is then placed into liquidation or abandoned. ASIC finds that where a liquidator is appointed in such circumstances, the absence of recoverable assets means creditors cannot be paid. ASIC states it uses a combination of engagement, surveillance and enforcement tools to address this conduct.

On engagement, ASIC states it shares and refers information to other government agencies, including the Serious Financial Crime Taskforce and the Phoenix Taskforce, and exchanges intelligence with the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) as part of the Data Fusion Project to detect illegal phoenix activity. ASIC also states it supports and implements legislative tools introduced to combat this activity and contributes to law reform and policy.

On surveillance, ASIC states it operates a Phoenix Surveillance Campaign, working with the ATO to identify high-risk directors and jointly conducting meetings with directors considered at risk of engaging in illegal phoenix activity. ASIC also states it conducts registered liquidator surveillance, including consideration of whether to appoint a reviewing liquidator funded from the Assetless Administration Fund. On enforcement, ASIC states it takes civil and criminal action, administrative action including director disqualification, and pursues prosecution of directors through the Assistance for External Administration programme, alongside grant funding to liquidators from the Assetless Administration Fund and administrative action against registered liquidators.
rows.body_name: Australian Securities and Investments Commission Press Releases

rows.title: CJEU Rules Body Camera Footage in Ticket Inspections Constitutes Direct Data Collection Under Art. 13 GDPR
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union has ruled in Case C-422/24 (Storstockholms Lokaltrafik) that personal data obtained through body cameras during ticket inspections constitutes data collected directly from the data subject, triggering the immediate information obligations set out in Art. 13 GDPR, rather than the indirect collection obligations under Art. 14 GDPR.

On 18 December 2025, the Court of Justice of the European Union delivered its preliminary ruling in response to questions referred by a Swedish national court concerning a dispute between a Stockholm public transport company and the Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection (IMY). IMY had fined the company for breaching several GDPR provisions, including the obligation to provide adequate information to passengers filmed by ticket inspectors equipped with body cameras. The fine amount in local currency and euro equivalent was not disclosed in the press release issued by the Court.

The Court finds that the classification of data collection as direct does not require the data subject to knowingly provide data or take any particular action. The Court determines that data obtained by observing a data subject is to be regarded as collected directly from that person, placing the processing squarely within Art. 13 GDPR. By contrast, the Court notes that indirect collection under Art. 14 GDPR applies only where the controller is not in direct contact with the data subject and obtains the data from a third-party source. The company had argued that its collection was indirect, a classification that would have altered both the timing and scope of its transparency obligations and, in its view, rendered the fine unjustified.

The Court further finds that the Art. 13 GDPR information obligation in cases of direct collection may be fulfilled through a multi-layered approach, consistent with European Data Protection Board Guidelines 3/2019 on the processing of personal data through video devices, adopted on 29 January 2020. The Court determines that the most important information, including the identity and contact details of the controller, the purposes and legal basis of the processing, data recipients, retention periods, and data subject rights of access and erasure, may be indicated on a warning sign, while remaining mandatory information is required to be provided in an easily accessible place. The Court does not itself resolve the underlying national dispute, which is referred back to the Swedish court for determination in accordance with this ruling.

rows.title: Vietnam National Assembly Passes New Law on Cybersecurity, Effective July 2026
rows.summary:
Vietnam's National Assembly has passed a new Law on Cybersecurity, consolidating two predecessor statutes into a single instrument comprising eight chapters and 45 articles, with the Ministry of Public Security designated as the lead coordinating authority.

On 10 December 2025, the National Assembly of the Socialist Republic of Vietnam passed the Law on Cybersecurity, with 434 of the 443 deputies present voting in favour. The law will take effect from 1 July 2026. No financial penalties are specified in the source material.

Presenting the report on the draft law, Minister of Public Security General Luong Tam Quang stated that the Ministry of Public Security, as the drafting body, developed the 2025 Law on Cybersecurity by merging the 2018 Law on Cybersecurity and the 2015 Law on Cyberinformation Security. The Minister noted that the consolidation does not alter the functions or responsibilities of existing ministries and sectors, nor does it introduce new policies. The Ministry further reviewed and incorporated relevant provisions of the United Nations Convention against Cybercrime, known as the Hanoi Convention, into the domestic legislative framework, adding provisions on international cooperation in combating cybercrime and on cybersecurity protection measures, in response to recommendations from National Assembly deputies.

Under the Government's assignment, the Ministry of Public Security is designated as the standing agency coordinating the National Cybersecurity Incident Response Alliance, which comprises relevant authorities and enterprises. According to the source, the Ministry's role is to ensure swift and effective response to cybersecurity incidents and threats to information systems of ministries, sectors, localities and key enterprises. The Ministry is also required to ensure the collection of data and electronic evidence in accordance with the Criminal Procedure Code to support the investigation and handling of cybercrime. Minister Luong Tam Quang characterised ensuring cybersecurity as a critical, regular, urgent and strategic task, describing it as a responsibility shared by the entire political system and the Vietnamese people.

rows.title: New Brunswick Ombud Submits 2025 Legislative Review Memorandum on Right to Information and Privacy Protection Act
rows.summary:
The Office of the Ombud of New Brunswick has submitted a formal memorandum to the Minister of Finance and Treasury Board calling for amendments to the Right to Information and Protection of Privacy Act (RIPA/LDIPVP) across definitions, oversight powers, artificial intelligence governance and exceptions to disclosure.

On 12 September 2025, Ombud Marie-France Pelletier addressed her memorandum to the Honourable René Legacy, Minister of Finance and Treasury Board, in her capacity as the designated statutory oversight body under s. 64.1(1) of the Act. The memorandum sets out recommendations arising from the Office's supervisory experience under the LDIPVP and from research into best practices observed across other Canadian provinces and territories. No fines are associated with this submission.

The Ombud identifies three areas for reform. Part 1 addresses existing statutory provisions, and the Ombud recommends that the definition of "government body" be expanded, that the definition of "personal information" be broadened, and that definitions relating to artificial intelligence be introduced into the Act. The Office also recommends amendments to response timelines under Article 11, refinements to the power to disregard requests under Article 15, including resolution of inconsistencies between English and French text, and revised treatment of mandatory exceptions relating to Executive Council confidences and historical Cabinet documents under Article 17. The Ombud finds that the current framework for third-party privacy protections under Article 21 and commercial interest exceptions under Article 22 also warrant revision.

Part 2 addresses the General Regulation, with the Ombud recommending that the definition of privacy breach be expanded to capture lost or stolen information under Article 4.2, and that the Office be notified of all court recourses and appeals under Articles 5 and 7, including a formal appeal procedure initiated by the Ombud. Part 3 raises broader policy matters: the Office recommends that it be consulted on draft legislation capable of affecting access or privacy rights, that it be granted intervener status in recourse and appeal proceedings, and that it receive recommendation or order-making powers. The memorandum further identifies obligations to document decisions involving artificial intelligence and recommends introduction of a public interest override provision governing exceptions to disclosure.
rows.body_name: Office of the Access to Information and Privacy Commissioner (New Brunswick)

rows.title: BEREC Adopts Strategy 2026–2030 and Work Programme 2026, Covering Connectivity, Competition and Digital Networks Act Preparation
rows.summary:
The Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications has adopted its Strategy 2026–2030 and accompanying Work Programme 2026, consolidating its previous strategic frameworks and setting priorities around connectivity, competition, open digital ecosystems and end-user empowerment across the European Union.

On 9 December 2025, the Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications (BEREC) adopted the BEREC Strategy 2026–2030, which updates and consolidates three preceding strategies, namely the Strategy 2021–2025, the Medium-Term Strategy for Relations with Other Institutions 2022–2025 and the Medium-Term Strategy for International Cooperation 2022–2025, together with the Action Plan 2030. BEREC notes that no financial penalties are associated with this adoption. BEREC notes that the strategy may be revised ahead of 2030 if substantial regulatory changes occur, particularly in relation to its mandate and the forthcoming review of the legal framework.

BEREC determines that the new strategic cycle will be implemented under a renewed mandate. BEREC identifies priorities including the expansion of gigabit broadband connectivity, the rollout of 5G across all EU regions, and reinforcing end-user empowerment in electronic communications. BEREC notes that the Work Programme 2026, adopted by the Board of Regulators, builds on mandatory tasks set out in the European Electronic Communications Code (EECC) and anticipates work related to the forthcoming Digital Networks Act proposal. BEREC states its commitment to providing advice to the European Parliament, the Council and the European Commission on electronic communications and digital services, and to enhancing the consistent and harmonised application of regulation to develop the internal market.

Alongside the strategy adoption, BEREC approved a draft report on switching and termination of contracts for public consultation, with stakeholder contributions invited by 20 January 2026. BEREC states that the draft report examines the switching process, identifies disincentives and challenges for end users, and addresses applicable end-user rights throughout that process. BEREC also launched for public consultation updated Guidelines on Geographical Surveys of Broadband Network Deployment, in support of national regulatory authorities applying Art. 22 EECC, with contributions expected by 9 February 2026. BEREC finds that the updated guidelines aim to provide clearer guidance, foster greater harmonisation and improve comparability of survey results across Member States. The chairmanship of BEREC passes from Mr Robert Mourik of COMREG to Mr Marko Mišmaš of AKOS for 2026.
rows.body_name: Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications

rows.title: Nigeria Data Protection Commission Participates in Youth Internet Governance Forum and National Symposium on Crisis Communication Data Privacy
rows.summary:
The Nigeria Data Protection Commission (NDPC) participated in two separate public engagement events in December 2025, addressing artificial intelligence data governance and the protection of personal data in crisis communication systems.

On 8 December 2025, the NDPC published details of its representation at the Nigeria Youth Internet Governance Forum (NYIGF) and a National Symposium on Digital Innovation in Crisis Communication, both of which involved NDPC officers contributing to panel discussions on data protection themes. The NDPC states that no fines were imposed in connection with either event.

At the NYIGF, the NDPC was represented by Mr Abubakar Mahmud, an officer from the Commission's IT and Cybersecurity Department, who participated in a panel session titled "Artificial Intelligence Data Governance and Regulation." The NDPC notes that during the session Mr Mahmud highlighted that the Commission has translated relevant materials, though the source text does not specify the full content of those translations or the complete scope of his remarks. The NDPC states that the forum provided a platform for engagement with young people on questions of AI data governance and the regulatory framework governing data processing in Nigeria.

At the National Symposium on Digital Innovation in Crisis Communication, the NDPC was represented by Mrs Adaobi Nwankwo, Head of the Commission's Innovation Unit, who participated in a panel session themed "Leveraging Emerging Technologies to Transform Crisis Communication: Data Privacy and Digital Trust in Crisis Communication Systems." The NDPC notes that the symposium was organised to examine how emerging technologies intersect with data privacy obligations in the context of crisis communication systems. Mrs Nwankwo's participation placed the Commission's regulatory perspective within broader discussions on digital trust and the safeguarding of personal data in systems used during crisis scenarios.

Both engagements reflect the NDPC's continued public outreach activities under its mandate to promote awareness of data protection obligations across sectors and demographic groups in Nigeria.

rows.title: FRA Paper Sets Out Fundamental Rights Framework for Live Facial Recognition Technology in EU Law Enforcement
rows.summary:
The European Union Agency for Fundamental Rights published a focus paper examining the fundamental rights implications of live facial recognition technology used by law enforcement and border-management authorities across EU member states, with reference to protections under the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights and applicable data protection law.

On 27 November 2019, the European Union Agency for Fundamental Rights released the paper, which analyses the deployment of facial recognition technology, defined as the comparison of digital facial images to determine whether they depict the same person, in law enforcement contexts. The Agency notes that no fines were imposed and no monetary penalties are associated with this publication.

The Agency finds that examples of national law enforcement authorities in the EU actively using live facial recognition technology remain sparse, though several authorities are testing its potential. The paper focuses specifically on live facial recognition, which the Agency describes as the comparison of footage obtained from video surveillance cameras with images held in databases. The Agency finds that facial images constitute sensitive data under EU law, given their status as a form of biometric data, and notes that such images are also relatively easy to capture in public spaces without the knowledge of individuals concerned.

The Agency finds that accuracy of matching is improving but that the risk of errors remains real, particularly for certain minority groups, raising concerns under equality and non-discrimination provisions. The paper further identifies as a central fundamental rights challenge the fact that individuals whose images are captured and processed may be unaware that processing is occurring, and are therefore unable to challenge potential misuses. The Agency finds this inability to contest processing raises concerns relating to the rights to privacy, data protection, and effective remedy. The paper situates these considerations within broader EU frameworks governing biometric data processing and the use of technology in law enforcement, including the Law Enforcement Directive (Directive 2016/680), which governs the processing of personal data by competent authorities for law enforcement purposes.

rows.title: Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium Launches Second HackTheGovernment Initiative for Federal IT Systems
rows.summary:
The Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB) has launched the second edition of its HackTheGovernment initiative, inviting ethical hackers to identify and report vulnerabilities across federal public sector IT infrastructure under coordinated vulnerability disclosure principles.

On 13 November 2025, the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium announced the opening of HackTheGovernment2025, running from 12 to 26 November 2025. The CCB states that the initiative carries no associated fine or penalty mechanism and is framed as a national cybersecurity coordination programme rather than an enforcement action.

The CCB states that participating ethical hackers are invited to test selected government systems, with named targets including the CCB itself, the defence Department, and FPS BOSA. The CCB notes that all findings identified during the two-week period are to be handled under coordinated vulnerability disclosure principles, with the CCB coordinating the process to ensure that any identified issues are addressed by the relevant public services. The CCB states that the programme is open to both emerging cybersecurity talent and experienced practitioners.

The CCB characterises collaboration with the ethical-hacking community as a strategy for enhancing public sector digital resilience, framing cybersecurity as a national responsibility rather than an isolated technical matter. The CCB further notes that the initiative is designed to encourage cooperation between government institutions, the cybersecurity community, and citizens, with the stated aim of identifying vulnerabilities before malicious actors are able to exploit them. The CCB positions the programme as building on coordinated vulnerability disclosure as an organising framework, emphasising openness and trust between public institutions and the broader security community as central to its operation.

rows.title: Kenya ODPC Issues Guidance Notes on Processing Personal Data for Historical and Statistical Purposes
rows.summary:
The Office of the Data Protection Commissioner of Kenya has published Guidance Notes on Historical and Statistical Purposes, setting out the lawful bases, obligations and exemptions applicable to data controllers processing personal data for historical and statistical ends under the Data Protection Act, 2019.

On 6 November 2025, the Office of the Data Protection Commissioner (ODPC) published its Guidance Notes on Historical and Statistical Purposes, providing a framework for data controllers and processors operating in Kenya. No financial penalty is associated with this publication.

The ODPC identifies seven lawful bases under which personal data may be processed for historical and statistical purposes, including consent, legal obligation, public interest, performance of a task by a public authority, functions of a public nature, legitimate interests, and a dedicated historical and statistical purposes basis. The ODPC notes that further processing of personal data originally collected for other purposes may be permissible where it is compatible with the original processing purpose, subject to a compatibility assessment by the data controller.

The ODPC sets out twelve categories of obligation applicable to data controllers engaged in such processing. These include data minimisation, data quality, transparency and communication, data security, purpose limitation, storage limitation, accountability and compliance, respect for data subject rights, the conduct of Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs), data breach notification, and governance of arrangements with data processors or other data controllers. The ODPC notes that data subjects retain rights in respect of their personal data even where processing is undertaken for historical or statistical purposes, and that exemptions from certain data subject rights are available only where their exercise would seriously impair or render impossible the achievement of the historical or statistical purpose. The ODPC further states that anonymisation and secure data handling are required before publication of outputs derived from such processing, and that a Public Interest Assessment Framework must be applied where public interest is relied upon as the lawful basis. The guidance grounds its legislative framework in Article 31 of the Constitution of Kenya, 2010, and the Data Protection Act, 2019, and includes a compliance checklist at Annex A for use by data controllers.
rows.body_name: Office of the Data Protection Commissioner

rows.title: Czech NÚKIB Co-Seals Three-Part Guidance Series on Modern Defensible Architecture with Australian Signals Directorate and International Partners
rows.summary:
The National Cyber and Information Security Agency of the Czech Republic (NÚKIB) has co-sealed three guidance publications on modern defensible architecture alongside the Australian Signals Directorate and a coalition of international cybersecurity authorities, providing baseline design principles, senior decision-maker guidance, and investment planning frameworks for organisations seeking to strengthen their cyber resilience.

On 30 October 2025, the National Cyber and Information Security Agency of the Czech Republic announced its co-sealing of the publication series, which carries no fine or penalty element. Co-signatories include the Canadian Centre for Cyber Security, the National Cyber Security Centre of New Zealand, the Japan Computer Emergency Response Team, Japan's National Cybersecurity Office and National Police Agency, the National Intelligence Service of the Republic of Korea, and Germany's Bundesamt für Sicherheit in der Informationstechnik.

The series comprises three distinct publications. The first, Foundations for Modern Defensible Architecture, sets out a baseline collection of secure design and architecture activities intended to prepare organisations to adapt to current and emerging cyber threats. NÚKIB notes that each Foundation represents an organisational goal or capability intended to facilitate adoption of zero trust architecture and technologies. The second publication, Modern Defensible Architecture for Senior Decision Makers, is directed at executive-level audiences and addresses the contemporary threat landscape, presenting key factors and questions for consideration prior to investment in and implementation of modern defensible architecture. The third publication, Investing in Modern Defensible Architecture, targets ICT managers and enterprise architects, and NÚKIB describes it as a resource for developing an investment roadmap to present to those responsible for cyber security and information technology investment decisions.

Lukáš Kintr, Director of NÚKIB, states that the documents represent an important step toward enabling organisations to practically and proactively design and build IT environments that minimise risks to critical systems. NÚKIB further states that the fundamentals of modern defensible architecture, together with the recommendations on decision-making and investment planning contained in the series, are consistent with the long-term security standards the agency promotes. The full publication series is hosted on the Australian Signals Directorate's website.

rows.title: Centre for Cyber Security Denmark issues guidance on cybersecurity for surveillance equipment across procurement, operation and disposal
rows.summary:
The Centre for Cyber Security Denmark (CFCS) has published guidance setting out concrete recommendations for public and private organisations on the cybersecure procurement, configuration, operation and disposal of surveillance equipment, citing vulnerabilities inherent in internet-connected IoT devices.

On 28 February 2023, the Centre for Cyber Security Denmark published the guidance document "Cybersikkerhed i overvågningsudstyr", addressed primarily to staff responsible for managing the procurement and operation of surveillance equipment, as well as to senior management responsible for strategic decisions in this area. No fine or enforcement action is associated with this publication.

The guidance defines surveillance equipment as encompassing the surveillance camera together with its firmware and software, and extends its recommendations to the network infrastructure to which such equipment is connected. The Centre for Cyber Security finds that surveillance equipment connected to the internet is categorised as IoT devices, and that all IoT devices may contain vulnerabilities that a threat actor can exploit. The guidance notes that internet-connected surveillance equipment carries a risk of unauthorised third-party access to camera feeds, and that such equipment can be used by a malicious actor as an entry point into an organisation's broader network infrastructure.

The Centre for Cyber Security further finds that some surveillance equipment collects sensitive data, including images of persons, products or industrial processes, and that a risk assessment is therefore required. The guidance sets out six overarching recommendations: organisations are required to ensure that information security within supplier relationships is adequate; software is kept updated; networks are segmented; the physical location of surveillance equipment is registered; default passwords on surveillance equipment are changed; and surveillance equipment is disposed of securely. The guidance addresses both the operational staff who implement these measures and the leadership level responsible for setting organisational policy on surveillance equipment.

rows.title: CJEU Advocate General: Prior Judicial Authorisation Not Required for Competition Email Seizures
rows.summary:
Advocate General Medina has opined that national competition authorities are not required to obtain prior judicial authorisation before seizing business emails during competition investigations, provided adequate procedural safeguards and subsequent judicial review are in place.

On 23 October 2025, Advocate General Laila Medina delivered a supplementary Opinion in three joined cases (C-258/23, C-259/23 and C-260/23) arising from seizures of employee business emails conducted by the Portuguese Competition Authority. The companies under investigation contested the seizures, arguing that correspondence secrecy as a fundamental right required authorisation from an investigating judge rather than the Public Prosecutor's Office.

Advocate General Medina distinguished the present cases from the Grand Chamber judgment in Bezirkshauptmannschaft Landeck (C-548/21), which held that access to personal data on a mobile telephone for criminal investigations is subject to prior judicial review. Advocate General Medina found that competition authority seizures target business information concerning legal persons and do not afford uncontrolled access to a comprehensive picture of private life in the manner that mobile telephone access does.

Regarding the interference with the fundamental right to protection of personal data under Article 8 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights, Advocate General Medina found that the principle of proportionality is complied with where certain procedural safeguards are ensured, in addition to obligations under the General Data Protection Regulation (EU) 2016/679, and subject to subsequent judicial review both during and at the close of investigation. Advocate General Medina found that prior judicial authorisation would be required only where seizures are carried out at a person's private residence or to incriminate a natural person under criminal law. Directive (EU) 2019/1 permits Member States to provide for a prior authorisation mechanism issued by a judicial authority. The Advocate General's Opinion is not binding on the Court of Justice, which will deliver judgment at a later date.

rows.title: NÚKIB Director Visits Australia for Bilateral Cyber Security Consultations
rows.summary:
The Czech National Cyber and Information Security Agency (NÚKIB) conducted its first official leadership visit to Australia in October 2025, holding bilateral consultations with Australian counterpart institutions across Canberra and Sydney on topics including critical infrastructure threats, artificial intelligence and secure technologies.

On 15 October 2025, NÚKIB confirmed that Director Lukáš Kintr and Roman Račka, Director of the Cabinet, undertook a working visit to Australia to advance bilateral cooperation in cyber security. The visit, described by NÚKIB as the first official visit of the agency's leadership to Australia, included engagement with representatives across several Australian government bodies.

In Canberra, the NÚKIB delegation held consultations with the Australian Cyber Security Centre, which operates under the Australian Signals Directorate. A bilateral meeting took place with the National Cyber Security Coordinator, Lieutenant General Michelle McGuinness CSC. The delegation also participated in a roundtable at the Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade, chaired by Hugh Watson, Acting Ambassador for Cyber Affairs and Critical Technologies, and met with senior officials from the Department of Home Affairs and the Department of Cyber Security. The official programme in Canberra concluded with a visit to the Australian Strategic Policy Institute and a reception for the Australian cyber community hosted at the Czech Embassy, opened by Ambassador Jana Tyrer.

The delegation subsequently visited Sydney to observe the operations of the Joint Policing Cybercrime Coordination Centre, led by the Australian Federal Police. NÚKIB notes that the Centre operates as a model of inter-agency collaboration between law enforcement and the private sector. Discussions across the visit addressed threats to critical infrastructure from state-sponsored actors, the security implications of artificial intelligence development, trustworthy technology standards, the protection of energy infrastructure and the security of connected vehicles. NÚKIB Cyber Attachée for the Indo-Pacific region, Veronika Kolek Netolická, noted that the visit represents a step towards deepening a partnership established when the Czech Republic appointed its first Cyber Attaché to the region in 2023, and referenced the NATO Cyber Champions Summit, which the Czech Republic is scheduled to host in 2026.

rows.title: Norwegian Communications Authority Backs DSA Prohibition on Dark Patterns and Manipulative Marketing Techniques
rows.summary:
Nkom has submitted a consultation response supporting the introduction of prohibitions on manipulative digital marketing techniques under the Digital Services Act (DSA), including countdown timers, spin-the-wheel mechanics and subliminal imagery, applicable to all digital platforms operating in Norway regardless of their location.

On 14 October 2025, Nkom published its position ahead of the DSA's implementation into Norwegian law, identifying a range of dark pattern practices that the regulation is intended to prohibit. No fines are attached to this consultation statement. According to the Institute for Advertising and Media Statistics (IRM), Norway's digital advertising market was estimated at approximately 19 billion Norwegian kroner (approximately EUR 1.6 billion) in 2024.

Nkom finds that the practices at issue, including images or videos displayed too quickly for conscious perception, gamification mechanisms such as spin-the-wheel features, time-limited offers with countdown timers creating artificial urgency, and automatic enrolments, constitute manipulative design that limits users' ability to make free and informed choices. Kamilla Sharma, Director of the Market and Services Division at Nkom, states in the authority's published response that such techniques mislead consumers not because of any lack of knowledge on the part of users, but because the technology is advanced, draws on large volumes of personal data, and exploits human vulnerability.

Nkom notes in its consultation response that the DSA also imposes greater responsibility on the largest digital platforms, including Meta, Snapchat, Amazon and Temu, for the manner in which third-party advertisers operate on their platforms. Nkom finds that particular protection is afforded to vulnerable groups, especially minors, and states that behavioural advertising directed at children and young people, which involves platforms collecting data on minors' online behaviour for targeting purposes, is among the practices the regulation addresses. Sharma states that technology exploiting the vulnerabilities of minors conflicts with rights to equal treatment and consumer protection. Nkom's position is that the proposed rules would apply across all digital platforms irrespective of where those platforms are established.

rows.title: Norwegian Communications Authority publishes survey on AI regulation and confirms supervisory role under EU AI Act
rows.summary:
The Norwegian Communications Authority (Nkom) has published survey results finding that 70 per cent of Norwegian adults support strict laws governing the use of artificial intelligence by individuals and companies, as Norway moves to implement the EU AI Act through domestic legislation.

On 1 October 2025, Nkom released the survey findings alongside a statement confirming its designated supervisory responsibility for the practical application of the AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689) in Norway. The survey, conducted between 19 and 30 June 2025 among 1,062 persons aged 18 and over, was weighted by gender, age and region to reflect the national population. Nkom notes a margin of error of at least plus or minus five percentage points at a 95 per cent confidence level.

Nkom finds that 69.6 per cent of respondents, rounded to 70 per cent, agreed or strongly agreed with the statement that there are strict laws and rules governing how artificial intelligence can be used by individuals and companies, whilst only 8 per cent disagreed. The authority notes that women expressed support for strict regulation at a rate seven percentage points higher than men. Nkom's divisional director Espen Slette states that the survey reflects public perception of AI as a powerful tool carrying both utility and risk.

Nkom confirms that the consultation deadline for the proposed Norwegian AI Act, implementing the EU AI Act into national law, expired on 1 October 2025. The authority notes that the AI Act applies differentiated requirements depending on the risk category of each AI use case. Nkom further states that, in partnership with AI Norway, it is establishing a regulatory AI sandbox where companies may test new solutions in controlled environments prior to market release.

rows.title: OECD and European Commission Release Harmonised Framework for Measuring AI Investment Across EU and Comparator Economies
rows.summary:
The OECD and the European Commission have jointly published a harmonised methodology for quantifying artificial intelligence investment across the European Union and benchmarking it against the United States, the United Kingdom, Canada, and Japan, providing a standardised basis for evaluating national and regional AI strategies.

On 26 September 2025, the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development and the European Commission released "Advancing the measurement of investments in artificial intelligence", a report establishing a two-step measurement framework designed to isolate AI-related spending from broader information and communications technology or research and development expenditure. The framework draws on macroeconomic data from Eurostat and the EUKLEMS and INTANProd databases, combined with AI intensity coefficients derived from patent data and education statistics.

The report defines AI investment broadly, encompassing not only research and development but also complementary assets including skills development, data infrastructure, computing hardware, and organisational capital. The framework notes that AI functions as a general-purpose technology whose economic footprint extends well beyond software development, and the methodology is constructed accordingly to capture this wider scope of spending.

Applying the framework to available data, the report finds that total AI investment across the EU27 reached approximately EUR 257 billion in 2023, with estimates ranging from EUR 220 billion to EUR 294 billion depending on assumptions applied. The report notes this figure largely met the EUR 20 billion annual investment target set under the European Union Coordinated Plan on Artificial Intelligence, which was launched in 2018 and revised in 2021 with a 2030 horizon. The framework finds that private investment accounts for 73 per cent of total AI spending, with skills development comprising the largest single category at 41 per cent, followed by data and equipment at 37 per cent, research and development at 13 per cent, and other intellectual property products at 9 per cent. The report identifies Germany, France, and the Netherlands as leading in overall investment, while Ireland ranks highest on a per capita basis. The framework also finds a notable acceleration in AI investment across all categories in the post-2019 period, particularly in software, development, and AI-enabling infrastructure. The European Commission concurrently launched its Apply AI Strategy, which incorporates a European AI Observatory intended to use the new measurement framework as one of its primary data sources for monitoring AI development and trends across sectors.

rows.title: ENISA Threat Landscape 2025: Public Administration Most Targeted EU Sector, Ransomware Remains Leading Cybercrime Tool
rows.summary:
The European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) has published its annual Threat Landscape report for 2025, identifying public administration as the most targeted sector in the EU and ransomware as the most impactful cybercrime tool, drawing on incident data collected across the reporting period.

On 24 September 2025, ENISA published the ENISA Threat Landscape 2025 Booklet (catalogue number TP-01-25-025-EN-N), covering the threat environment facing EU Member States and organisations. No fines or penalties are associated with this report.

ENISA finds that public administration accounted for 38.2% of all recorded incidents, with distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks representing 94.8% of incidents within that sector and ransomware particularly affecting municipalities. The transport sector ranked second at 7.5% of incidents, with air, logistics, and maritime subsectors identified as focus areas, the latter notably targeted by state-nexus intrusion sets. ENISA determines that essential entities represented 53.7% of the total number of recorded incidents across all sectors. ENISA notes that digital infrastructure and services, including Digital Services Providers, remain high-value targets despite accounting for only 2.2% of recorded incidents, particularly where cyber dependencies are leveraged as launchpads for follow-up attacks.

ENISA identifies phishing as the dominant initial intrusion vector, accounting for approximately 60% of cases and encompassing malspam, vishing, and malvertising. ENISA reports that vulnerability exploitation represented 21.3% of initial access vectors, with 68% of those cases leading to malware deployment as a follow-up activity. ENISA finds that ransomware recorded an 11% decrease compared to the previous Threat Landscape edition, yet remained the most impactful cybercrime tool, with the Akira and SafePay strains among the most deployed. ENISA reports that hacktivism dominated incident volume in the EU, driven primarily by low-impact DDoS campaigns, with only 2% of such incidents resulting in service disruption. ENISA attributes over 60% of hacktivist claims to the group NoName057(16), sustained through its DDoSia platform. ENISA reports that among state-nexus intrusion sets, APT28, APT29, and Sandworm are the most active in the EU, targeting public administration, defence, and telecommunications entities, while the Matryoshka Information Manipulation Set is identified as the most active actor in Foreign Information Manipulation and Interference campaigns. ENISA further finds that both state-aligned intrusion sets and cybercriminal operators are increasingly leveraging artificial intelligence for productivity and optimisation of malicious activities.

rows.title: Brazilian Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation Publishes Updated Overview of National AI Strategy Established Under Portaria MCTI nº 4.617
rows.summary:
The Brazilian Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation has published an updated overview of the Estratégia Brasileira de Inteligência Artificial, the national artificial intelligence strategy established by Portaria MCTI nº 4.617 of 6 April 2021 and amended by Portaria MCTI nº 4.979 of 13 July 2021, setting out the framework's objectives, governance structure and nine thematic pillars.

On 23 September 2025, the Brazilian Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation published the updated page for the Estratégia Brasileira de Inteligência Artificial, referred to as the EBIA. The Ministry states that the strategy was instituted under Portaria MCTI nº 4.617 and subsequently amended, with no financial penalties attached to the instrument.

The Ministry states that the EBIA is aligned with the OECD principles endorsed by Brazil and is founded on five core principles for responsible AI governance: inclusive growth, sustainable development and well-being; human-centred values and equity; transparency and explicability; robustness, safety and security; and accountability. The Ministry notes that the strategy was developed through three stages, comprising engagement of specialist consultancy, national and international benchmarking, and a public consultation conducted between 12 December 2019 and 3 March 2020 via a federal government electronic platform, which the Ministry states received approximately 1,000 contributions that informed the final document.

The Ministry states that the EBIA establishes nine thematic axes structured across two categories. The Ministry identifies three cross-cutting axes covering legislation, regulation and ethical use; AI governance; and international aspects. Six vertical axes address qualifications for a digital future; workforce and capacity building; research, development, innovation and entrepreneurship; application in productive sectors; application in the public sector; and public security. The Ministry states that under Portaria MCTI nº 4.617, the Ministry is required to create governance instances to prioritise, implement, monitor and update the strategic actions defined in the EBIA; coordinate actions enabling its implementation; invite public, private and academic institutions to support strategic actions; prepare evaluation reports on implementation; and assess the periodicity for updating the strategy. The Ministry states that governance of the EBIA is composed of the MCTI itself, the Rede MCTI/EMBRAPII de Tecnologias e Inovação Digital, and invited institutions.
rows.body_name: Brazilian Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation

rows.title: DSK Issues Guidance on Transparency Obligations for Third-Country Data Transfers in Medical Research
rows.summary:
The Konferenz der unabhängigen Datenschutzaufsichtsbehörden des Bundes und der Länder (DSK) has issued recommendations setting out information obligations applicable to transfers of personal data to third countries in medical research under the GDPR.

On 18 September 2025, the DSK published guidance on information obligations for third-country data transfers in medical research. The DSK determines that controllers planning such transfers must fulfil transparency obligations under Art. 13(1)(f) and Art. 14(1)(f) GDPR. The DSK states data subjects must be informed of the planned transfer, destination country or countries, and the applicable transfer mechanism under Chapter V GDPR, such as an adequacy decision, standard contractual clauses or binding corporate rules.

Where transfer is based on appropriate safeguards under Art. 46 GDPR, the DSK finds controllers are required to state that no adequacy decision covers the destination country and to provide a reference to specific safeguards in place, including how data subjects may obtain copies.

Where transfer relies on derogations under Art. 49(1) GDPR, the DSK determines data subjects must be informed that protection in the third country may be non-existent and is not essentially equivalent to EU levels. The DSK finds the specific derogation must be identified by reference to the relevant subparagraph. For transfers based on important public interest under Art. 49(1)(d) and Art. 49(4) GDPR, the DSK finds the public interest ground must be identified in outline. Where transfer is based on explicit consent under Art. 49(1)(a) GDPR, the DSK states data subjects must be informed in transparent and specific terms about the absence of adequate safeguards and all known deficiencies in the third country's data protection framework.

rows.title: Japan Enacts Cyber Defence Legislation for Critical Infrastructure
rows.summary:
Japan's Cabinet Secretariat National Cyber Policy Office has enacted the Act on Prevention of Damage Caused by Unauthorised Acts Against Important Computers (Act No. 42 of 2025) and its accompanying arrangement act (Act No. 43 of 2025), establishing a legal framework for active cyber defence of critical electronic systems.

On 23 May 2025, the Cabinet Secretariat National Cyber Policy Office promulgated the legislation following parliamentary deliberation and amendment. The bills were approved by cabinet on 7 February 2025. The authority notes that the legislation implements commitments made under the National Security Strategy adopted by cabinet decision on 16 December 2022, which set a target of raising Japan's cyber security response capacity to a level at or above that of major Western nations.

The authority finds that the legislation addresses three core areas: enhanced public-private cooperation, the use of communications information, and access and neutralisation measures. Under the public-private cooperation provisions, operators of critical infrastructure are required to notify the competent minister upon introducing specified important computers and to report incidents affecting those systems to both the competent minister and the Prime Minister. The authority finds that operators of qualifying critical infrastructure must file asset notifications setting out product names and related details, and must report cybersecurity incidents or events capable of causing such incidents. The legislation provides that the Prime Minister and the competent minister for computer supply businesses may provide vulnerability information to suppliers of computers or programmes used in critical systems.

The authority notes that the legislation includes a provision requiring that powers under the act be exercised only to the minimum extent necessary for the stated purposes and that the confidentiality of communications and other constitutional rights must not be unduly restricted. The arrangement act provides for neutralisation measures by police against confirmed attack servers, subject to prior approval by an independent body and direction by the Commissioner-General of the National Police Agency.

rows.title: Czech Government Approves National Cyber Security Strategy Effective 2026, Led by NÚKIB
rows.summary:
The Czech Government has approved a new National Cyber Security Strategy, effective from 2026, prepared by the National Cyber and Information Security Agency and replacing the strategy in force since 2021.

On 3 September 2025, the Czech Government formally adopted the new National Cyber Security Strategy (NCSS), with the National Cyber and Information Security Agency (NÚKIB) identified as the lead authority responsible for the document. No financial penalties are associated with this measure.

NÚKIB states that the strategy was developed with contributions from dozens of organisations across both the public and private sectors, including through public consultations. The agency notes that the NCSS responds to a deteriorating global security environment and recent technological changes, and that it emphasises the need for more intensive cooperation at national and international levels. NÚKIB further states that implementation will be carried out through an action plan currently being prepared, which will assign specific tasks to responsible institutions. The implementation of those tasks is to be evaluated annually and the results submitted to the Czech Government.

NÚKIB identifies three main strategic areas: securing strategic infrastructure, including strengthening resilience and enhancing the state's ability to detect and counter cyber threats; whole-of-society preparedness and development, covering digital competences, the cyber security workforce, and innovation; and international cooperation, encompassing an active role for Czechia within the EU, NATO and other international organisations. According to NÚKIB, Czechia is required in the coming years to make greater use of new technologies to secure organisations, prepare for potential transitions to crisis states, and improve working conditions for cyber security experts in public administration. The agency notes that the strategy also identifies the need to develop secure alternatives to risky technologies, build new platforms for information sharing, and pursue an internationally coordinated approach to deterring malicious state actors. NÚKIB Director Lukáš Kintr stated that the strategy confirms Czechia's capacity to respond to the current security environment and noted that threats and vulnerabilities must be detected and addressed in advance, with Russia and China named as examples of malicious state actors.

rows.title: Court of Justice Sets Aside General Court Ruling on EDPS Decision Against Single Resolution Board Over Pseudonymised Data Processing
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union (First Chamber) has set aside the General Court's judgment of 26 April 2023 that had annulled the European Data Protection Supervisor's revised decision finding that the Single Resolution Board failed to fulfil its obligations relating to the processing of personal data under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

On 4 September 2025, the Court of Justice of the European Union delivered its judgment in Case C-413/23 P, ruling on an appeal brought by the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) against the General Court's judgment in SRB v EDPS (T-557/20). The proceedings originated from five complaints (Cases 2019-947, 2019-998, 2019-999, 2019-1000 and 2019-1122) submitted by shareholders and creditors of a banking institution in the context of the Single Resolution Board's procedure for granting compensation following the resolution of that institution. The CJEU notes that no financial penalty was imposed in this matter.

The Court examined two core questions of interpretation under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. First, the Court considered the concept of 'personal data' under Art. 3(1) of that Regulation in the context of pseudonymised data transmitted to a third party. The Court's analysis addressed whether data pseudonymised by the SRB and transmitted to an external valuer retained the character of personal data in the hands of that third party, having regard to all means reasonably likely to be used to re-identify the natural persons concerned, including available technology and objective factors such as costs and time required for identification. Second, the Court addressed the concept of 'pseudonymisation' as defined in Art. 3(6) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725, and the consequent obligations under Art. 15(1)(d) concerning the controller's obligation to inform data subjects of the recipients or categories of recipients of their personal data.

The Court finds that Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 is to be interpreted homogeneously with the GDPR where its provisions follow the same principles, consistent with recital 5 of that Regulation. The Court finds that the General Court erred in its assessment of the EDPS's revised decision of 24 November 2020, which the EDPS had determined found that the SRB failed to comply with its information obligations under Art. 15(1)(d) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 when transmitting pseudonymised data to a third-party valuer. The EDPS was supported in the appeal by the European Data Protection Board as intervener, while the European Commission intervened in support of the SRB.

rows.title: Czech NÚKIB Joins International SBOM Cybersecurity Guidance Issued by CISA, NSA and 16 Partner Authorities
rows.summary:
The Czech National Cyber and Information Security Agency (NÚKIB) has joined a joint international guidance document on Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for cybersecurity, co-authored with the U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA), the U.S. National Security Agency (NSA), and fifteen additional national authorities across Europe, Asia-Pacific and North America.

On 3 September 2025, the full title of the document, "A Shared Vision of Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for Cybersecurity", was published under TLP:CLEAR, meaning disclosure is not limited and distribution carries no foreseeable restriction. NÚKIB notes that it has been an active member of the Global SBOM Forum, within which the document was developed, on behalf of the Czech Republic since early 2025. No financial penalties or enforcement measures are associated with this publication.

According to NÚKIB, the document presents SBOM as a machine-readable record of all components and libraries used in software development, functioning as a supply chain transparency mechanism. The authoring organisations state that widespread SBOM adoption is directed at producers, procuring organisations, and operators of software, with the stated aim of strengthening security, reducing risk, and decreasing costs. NÚKIB notes that when a vulnerability is identified in a component, SBOM enables rapid identification of its location and the taking of targeted remediation measures, with direct relevance to the protection of critical infrastructure.

The guidance further emphasises, according to NÚKIB, that SBOM adoption is an essential step toward aligning software development with the secure-by-design principle, under which security is integrated from the outset of development. According to the document, there is a call for the alignment of technical standards across countries and sectors to ensure interoperability and consistent large-scale implementation. Lukáš Kintr, Director of NÚKIB, is quoted in the authority's announcement as characterising SBOM as key to producing secure and resilient software, noting that modern software frequently consists of hundreds of components from varied sources and libraries, and that SBOM introduces transparency into that environment for both citizens and institutions.

rows.title: Czech National Cybersecurity Authority Issues High-Level Warning on Data Transfers and Remote Administration Linked to People's Republic of China
rows.summary:
The Czech National Cyber and Information Security Agency (NÚKIB) has issued a formal cyber security warning, rated High, concerning the transfer of system and user data to the People's Republic of China and the remote administration of technical assets carried out from PRC territories, pursuant to Section 12(1) of Act No. 181/2014 Coll. on Cyber Security.

On 3 September 2025, the National Cyber and Information Security Agency published the warning under file reference 350-647/2025-E, addressed to all authorities and persons obliged to implement security measures under the Cyber Security Act. No financial penalty is associated with this warning. NÚKIB determines the threat level as High, meaning the threat is rated as likely to very likely, and NÚKIB finds that obliged persons are required to evaluate the threat within their risk management processes at that same level, in accordance with Section 5(1)(d) of Decree No. 82/2018 Coll. on security measures.

NÚKIB concludes that the transfer of system and user data to the PRC, to PRC Special Administrative Regions, or to entities based in those territories, as well as remote administration of technical assets performed from or by entities in those territories, constitutes a cyber security threat. The Agency states that its conclusion is based on facts established during the exercise of its powers, supplemented by unclassified and classified information obtained from domestic and foreign partners.

NÚKIB finds that the legal environment of the PRC directly affects the trustworthiness of companies subject to it. The Agency identifies several PRC legal instruments as contributing factors to the threat assessment. These include the National Security Law of 2015, which the Agency notes imposes a general duty on all Chinese citizens and organisations to assist state authorities in matters of state security. NÚKIB further identifies the National Intelligence Law of 2017, and the Agency notes that Article 7 of which requires every citizen and organisation to support national intelligence activities and maintain confidentiality on related classified matters. The Agency also references the Counter-Espionage Law of 2014, noting under Article 6 that it applies extraterritorially and that, owing to broad and vague definitions, PRC state authorities can require companies to provide almost any information, with companies holding de facto no control over the information they possess and no access to independent judicial review. NÚKIB additionally notes that a 2023 amendment to that law further expanded its scope.

rows.title: Hamburg DPA Fines Debt Collection Firm €900,000 for Retaining Personal Data Up to Five Years Beyond Deletion Deadlines
rows.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) has imposed a fine of €900,000 (EUR 900,000) on a Hamburg-based debt collection service provider for retaining personal data without a legal basis for up to five years beyond applicable deletion deadlines, in breach of Art. 5(1)(a) and Art. 6(1) GDPR.

On 12 November 2024, the HmbBfDI announced the outcome of a sector-wide audit of companies with a strong market presence in the debt collection industry.
