The HmbBfDI notes that Hamburg is a leading European location for this sector and that data processed about defaulting debtors tends to be particularly sensitive, as it is regularly shared with third parties including credit reference agencies and address investigation services.

The HmbBfDI conducted the audit independently of individual complaints, examining how debtors' data was stored and processed across the sector. The authority sent detailed questionnaires to the companies examined and requested documents including records of processing activities, lists of security measures, and sample correspondence. The HmbBfDI subsequently conducted on-site inspections at a number of the companies. The HmbBfDI finds that for the majority of those audited, there was a high degree of professionalism and sensitivity, and the authority notes that improvements in transparency towards data subjects were achieved through dialogue, with particular focus on the formulation of meaningful data access responses under Art. 15 GDPR and on timely access procedures.

In one case, the HmbBfDI determines that the company stored a six-figure number of personal data records without any legal basis until at least mid-November 2023, with some records remaining in the company's database up to five years after the expiry of the statutory retention period, in violation of Art. 5(1)(a) and Art. 6(1) GDPR. The HmbBfDI finds that although the data were not passed to third parties during this period, their continued retention was unlawful. The HmbBfDI states that the fine of €900,000 is final and legally binding. The HmbBfDI notes that the company admitted the violation, accepted the fine, and cooperated professionally with the authority during the remediation process, which the authority states was taken into account when setting the penalty amount. The HmbBfDI further notes that a second audited company was found to have comparable deficiencies relating to deletion obligations, and that proceedings against that company remain ongoing.
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.title: HmbBfDI Annual Report 2023: Complaint Cases Rise 20%, Data Breach Notifications Hit 925
rows.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) published its 32nd annual data protection report for 2023, recording a 20 per cent increase in complaint cases, 925 data breach notifications, and findings that deletion failures occur across multiple sectors.

On 15 April 2024, HmbBfDI Commissioner Thomas Fuchs presented the 2023 annual data protection report to Hamburg Parliament President Carola Veit. The HmbBfDI reports that complaint proceedings increased to 2,537 cases in 2023, compared with 2,160 in the prior year, representing a rise of approximately 20 per cent. The authority attributes this growth largely to complaints relating to products and services offered by Meta and Google, reflecting HmbBfDI's lead supervisory role in Germany for those companies. The HmbBfDI reports that data breach notifications also increased, with 925 reports recorded in 2023 compared with 859 the previous year. Of those, the authority notes that 235 concerned hacker attacks, up from 227 in 2022. The HmbBfDI initiated 20 fine proceedings during the reporting year.

The HmbBfDI finds that failures by organisations to implement adequate deletion practices constitute a recurring, cross-sectoral problem. The authority observes that personal data, including copies of identity documents and financial records, is frequently retained after the purpose for which it was collected has lapsed, in breach of the storage limitation principle (Art. 5(1)(e) GDPR) and the requirement to erase data without undue delay (Art. 17 GDPR). The authority identifies this pattern across sectors ranging from debt collection services to the hospitality industry. The report also addresses AI supervision: Commissioner Fuchs states that data protection supervisory authorities are the appropriate bodies to oversee compliance with the EU AI Regulation, given their existing familiarity with IT systems processing personal data.
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.title: HmbBfDI Confirms Court Ruling on Guest Checkout Exception for Online Marketplaces
rows.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) has confirmed that the Hanseatisches Oberlandesgericht Hamburg upheld a ruling that online marketplace operators such as Otto.de are not required to offer guest checkout where compensatory data minimisation measures are in place (Art. 5(1)(c) GDPR).

On 12 March 2025, the HmbBfDI published a press release reporting that the Hanseatisches Oberlandesgericht Hamburg ruled on 27 February 2025 (Az. 5 U 30/24) in favour of Otto.de, dismissing an action by Verbraucherzentrale Nordrhein-Westfalen seeking to compel guest ordering. The court confirmed a first-instance judgment of the Landgericht Hamburg.

The HmbBfDI finds that a general rule established by the Konferenz der unabhängigen Datenschutzaufsichtsbehörden des Bundes und der Länder (DSK) on 24 March 2022 requires online retailers to offer guest ordering to comply with the data minimisation principle. The authority notes that compelling customers to create permanent accounts is typically incompatible with data protection law, as such accounts contain unnecessary personal data and expose information to security risks.

The HmbBfDI determines that Otto.de satisfied conditions for an exception to the guest checkout obligation as an online marketplace hosting third-party sellers. The authority finds that Otto.de demonstrated that customer service, including returns organisation, operates more efficiently through a unified customer account. The HmbBfDI notes that Otto.de adopted alternative data minimisation measures, including automated account deletion following inactivity and data removal after warranty expiry. The authority states that whilst the DSK resolution is not binding on courts, both judicial decisions treated it as soft law. The HmbBfDI finds that other platforms claiming marketplace status must substantiate that claim with concrete usage data, as general assertions do not establish entitlement to the exception.
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.title: CNIL Publishes GDPR Recommendations for AI System Development
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published its first set of recommendations on the application of the General Data Protection Regulation to the development of artificial intelligence systems, covering purpose definition, controller responsibilities, and training data use.

On 22 July 2025, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published guidance directed at designers and developers of AI systems, addressing how GDPR obligations apply during the development phase, defined as conception, dataset compilation, and model training, prior to deployment. No fine was issued; the publication is a guidance document carrying no monetary penalty.

The CNIL determines that the requirement to establish a lawful purpose (Art. 5(1)(b) GDPR) applies to AI development and is not incompatible with the nature of machine learning, including systems that may develop unanticipated characteristics during training. The authority finds that purpose must be determined, explicit, and legitimate, and that an objective stated as broadly as "development and improvement of an AI system" is insufficient. For general-purpose AI systems, the CNIL states that developers are required to specify the type of system being built, its technically foreseeable capabilities, foreseeable high-risk functionalities, functionalities excluded by design, and known deployment conditions such as open-source release, commercialisation, or API availability. For AI developed for scientific research purposes, the authority finds that a less detailed initial purpose definition is permissible, provided that supplementary information is added as the project progresses.

The CNIL further determines that the authority finds organisations using personal data for AI training are required to identify their role under the GDPR, whether as controller, joint controller, or processor, as this determines the applicable compliance obligations (Art. 4(7), Art. 26, Art. 28 GDPR). The authority notes that the recommendations have been elaborated to complement the EU AI Act, adopted in summer 2024, given that both instruments apply concurrently when personal data are processed during AI development. The CNIL states that a checklist document has been made available alongside the recommendations to assist professionals in verifying compliance at each development stage.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: France PEReN and International Network Publish Joint Evaluation Report on Agentic AI Safety Testing Methodologies
rows.summary:
France's Pôle d'Expertise de la Régulation Numérique (PEReN), alongside AI Safety Institutes and government-mandated offices from Singapore, Japan, Australia, Canada, the European Commission, Kenya, South Korea and the UK AI Security Institute, has published a joint evaluation report advancing methodologies for testing AI agents across risk domains including sensitive information leakage, fraud and cybersecurity.

On 17 July 2025, the International Network published the report, titled "Advancing Methodologies for Agentic Evaluations Across Domains", following a joint testing exercise conducted across the participating jurisdictions. The exercise was structured around two strands: leakage of sensitive information and fraud, led by Singapore's AI Safety Institute, and cybersecurity evaluations, led by the UK AI Security Institute.

The report notes that, given the nascency of agentic testing, test results and individual model capabilities are not the primary focus of the exercise. Rather, the network finds that the intent is to identify methodological issues in conducting agentic testing in order to inform future evaluation efforts. The exercise assessed two key aspects of agent safety testing: how safe models are when operating as agents, and how effective models are as judges of agent behaviour. The network finds that two models were tested as agents, with two further models serving as judges, and that human annotation was subsequently conducted with judge-LLM evaluations available as a reference point.

Across the leakage and fraud strand, the report finds that tasks covering two risk categories were assessed in eight languages, with agents provided with a selection of tools they could call to complete assigned tasks. The network finds that agent trajectories, comprising task prompts, tool calls with reasoning and final outputs, were evaluated on a pass or fail basis tied to specific scenarios. For malicious tasks, the report identifies two failure scenarios: complete execution of a harmful activity and partial execution of a harmful activity. The report finds that metrics centred on pass rates for tested models and discrepancy rates between judge-LLM evaluations and human annotation. Language-specific analysis was conducted across Farsi, French, Hindi, Japanese, Kiswahili, Korean, Mandarin Chinese and Telugu.

rows.title: CNIL Sets Out GDPR Requirements for Bank App Call-Detection Anti-Fraud Tools
rows.summary:
France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published guidance stating that banking applications which detect whether a customer is engaged in a telephone call during a transaction must comply with the General Data Protection Regulation, requiring freely given and informed consent before accessing call-related data.

On 15 July 2025, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published a public information notice addressing the collection of telephone call data, specifically call existence and call duration, by certain banking applications. The CNIL determines that such data constitutes personal data and that its collection is therefore subject to the RGPD (GDPR), including its consent requirements under Art. 7 GDPR.

The CNIL finds that the purpose of these tools is to detect in real time whether a customer may be acting under the influence of a fraudster during sensitive operations such as adding a new payee or initiating a transfer. The authority notes that the mechanism does not listen to or record the content of telephone conversations, but detects only whether a call is in progress. The CNIL determines that, where a bank deems a situation abnormal, the application may display an alert or, depending on the bank's configuration, block the operation entirely.

The CNIL states that banks are required to obtain free and informed consent before accessing call-related information, and that refusal of consent must not result in degraded service or increased charges. Where a customer declines, the authority finds that access to non-sensitive features such as account consultation must remain available within the application, while higher-risk functions such as transfers or payee additions may be restricted. The CNIL recommends that commonly used operations, including transfers, remain accessible via the bank's website, with less frequent operations available by telephone or in branch. The authority further states that customers who have given consent are entitled to withdraw it at any time via a clear and accessible mechanism, such as a link within the application. The CNIL acknowledges that banks operating under European anti-fraud obligations may lawfully refuse certain erasure or objection requests where retention is legally required, and states that banks must clearly explain any such refusal. Customers who remain dissatisfied are directed to lodge a complaint with the CNIL.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: Centre for Cyber Security Denmark Issues Sector-Specific NIS 2 Guidance for Danish Municipalities
rows.summary:
The Centre for Cyber Security Denmark, operating through the Agency for Public Security (Styrelsen for Samfundssikkerhed), has published sector-specific guidance on the application of the Danish NIS 2 Act to the country's 98 municipalities, addressing scope, supervisory arrangements and risk-based security measures.

On 1 July 2025, the Agency for Public Security published the first edition of its sector-specific NIS 2 guidance for municipalities, forming part of Denmark's implementation of the EU NIS 2 Directive. The agency states that the guidance does not carry binding legal force; the agency states that it is the supervisory authority in each case that determines whether an entity meets its obligations under the NIS 2 Act.

The agency notes that Danish municipalities are established as independent entities with their own political and administrative leadership, and that their responsibilities span multiple sectors, from schools and childcare institutions to technical and environmental services. The agency finds that entities associated with a municipality to varying degrees, including municipal companies, independent care homes and similar bodies, may fall within the scope of the NIS 2 Act if they provide services within one or more of the sectors listed in Annexes I and II of the Act. The agency further finds that such entities are subject to the competent authority for the sector in which they operate, rather than to the municipal supervisory framework directly.

On the question of scope, the agency explains that a municipality has relatively broad discretion in how it organises its service delivery. Some tasks are carried out within the municipality itself, while others are handled by affiliated entities such as joint municipal communities (section 60 communities) covering waste incineration and natural gas supply, or by limited companies. The agency notes that an entity under the NIS 2 Act is defined as a natural or legal person established and recognised under national law, capable of exercising rights and assuming obligations in its own name, encompassing businesses, associations, organisations and public authorities. The agency states that it does not address NIS 2 legislation applicable to the telecommunications, energy or financial sectors, and directs readers to the cross-sectoral NIS 2 guidance for general measures, including requirements for cybersecurity risk management under a risk-based approach.

rows.title: Centre for Cyber Security Denmark Rates Cyber Espionage and Cybercrime Threats to Danish Space Sector as Very High
rows.summary:
The Centre for Cyber Security Denmark, operating under the Agency for Societal Security (SAMSIK), has published its first threat assessment for the Danish space sector, rating the threats from both cyber espionage and cybercrime as VERY HIGH, with the assessment explicitly contextualised within the NIS2 Directive, which SAMSIK notes also covers the space domain.

On 26 June 2025, SAMSIK published the threat assessment titled "Cybertruslen mod Rumsektoren" (The Cyber Threat Against the Space Sector), marking the first time the authority has produced a dedicated assessment for this sector. SAMSIK states the document is intended to support risk owners in the Danish space sector and forms part of the basis for risk assessment work across the sector.

SAMSIK assesses the threat from cyber espionage against the Danish space sector as VERY HIGH. The authority finds that foreign states, in particular Russia and China, conduct cyber espionage against space sector organisations with the aim of gaining insight into other states' space activities, acquiring new and innovative technology, and obtaining knowledge of national defence capabilities. SAMSIK highlights examples of cyber espionage directed at space organisations in Japan and the United States as illustrative of this pattern. The authority further finds that the dual-use nature of space technology, serving both civilian and military purposes, sustains a persistent foreign state interest in the sector.

SAMSIK assesses the threat from cybercrime against the Danish space sector as VERY HIGH, with the authority finding that criminal actors primarily employ extortion techniques, including compromising networks and encrypting valuable data, or stealing data and threatening to publish it. The authority assesses the threat from cyber activism, principally distributed denial-of-service attacks, as MEDIUM, a level SAMSIK attributes to cyber activists likely placing lower symbolic value on the space sector compared with other sectors. The authority assesses the threat from destructive cyber attacks as MEDIUM. SAMSIK finds that this threat comes primarily from Russia, which the authority states has demonstrated increased willingness since early 2024 to employ hybrid methods with destructive effects against European NATO members, including destructive cyber attacks. The authority assesses the threat from cyber terrorism against the Danish space sector as NONE, finding that militant extremists currently lack the requisite capabilities and that intent is extremely limited.

rows.title: ENISA publishes updated European Cybersecurity Skills Framework consolidating 12 professional role profiles for EU workforce alignment
rows.summary:
The European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) has published updated materials for the European Cybersecurity Skills Framework (ECSF), consolidating 12 cybersecurity professional role profiles intended to serve as the EU reference point for defining and assessing cybersecurity skills across the public and private sectors.

On 26 June 2025, ENISA published the current state of the ECSF, a practical framework tool originally presented at the first ENISA Cybersecurity Skills Conference in September 2022. The framework carries no monetary penalty and involves no fine amounts. ENISA describes the ECSF as an open European tool designed to establish a common understanding of cybersecurity professional role profiles, alongside clear mappings to the skills, knowledge and competences required for each.

ENISA finds that the ECSF summarises cybersecurity-related roles into 12 distinct profiles, each individually analysed according to responsibilities, skills, synergies and interdependencies. The agency states that the framework facilitates recognition of cybersecurity skills and supports the design of cybersecurity-related training programmes. The Commission's April 2023 Communication on a Cybersecurity Skills Academy designated the ECSF as the basis upon which the Academy will define and assess relevant skills, monitor the evolution of skill gaps and provide indications on emerging needs.

ENISA notes that the 3rd Skills Conference, hosted under the Hungarian Presidency of the Council of the EU, confirmed the framework's maturity and versatility, demonstrating how the ECSF supports workforce planning aligned with obligations under the NIS2 Directive, adapts to the AI domain and enables skills attestation for cybersecurity professionals. The agency further states that the ECSF 2025 workshop showcased how EU co-funded projects are leveraging the framework across diverse sectors and use cases, supporting harmonisation of cybersecurity skills and professional roles across the EU. ENISA has also published a mapping between the ECSF and ESCO, the multilingual classification of European Skills, Competences and Occupations, as well as an application of the ECSF to the aviation sector developed by the European Union Aviation Safety Agency. ENISA notes that NIS2 obligations are mapped to ECSF roles within the accompanying documentation, and the framework is available in interactive, xlsx and JSON formats.

rows.title: ICO Places PECR Guide Under Review Following Data (Use and Access) Act Coming into Force
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has placed its Guide to the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations under review following the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 coming into force in the United Kingdom.

On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office announced that its Guide to the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations is subject to review in light of the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025, which came into law on that date. The ICO notes that the guidance may be subject to change and directs organisations to its Plans for New and Updated Guidance page for information on which guidance will be updated and when.

The ICO states that the guide is intended for organisations that send electronic marketing messages by phone, fax, email or text, use cookies, or provide electronic communication services to the public. The guide explains how to apply the Regulations through practical examples and answers to frequently asked questions. The ICO confirms that, pending the review, the existing guidance remains the current reference point for compliance with the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations.

The ICO's announcement reflects the broader legislative change introduced by the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025, which the authority identifies as the basis for placing existing PECR guidance under review. The ICO has not specified a timetable for the completion of that review but indicates that updates will be communicated through its dedicated guidance plans page. The ICO has not announced any amendments to the substantive obligations under the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations in connection with this review notice.

rows.title: ICO Places Children's Code Guidance Under Review Following Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 Coming into Force
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has placed its Children's code guidance and associated resources under formal review following the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 coming into force on 19 June 2025, with the ICO noting that existing materials may be subject to change.

On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office announced that its Children's code guidance, which covers the age-appropriate design code of practice for online services likely to be accessed by children, is under review as a consequence of the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 entering into law on that date. The ICO notes that no financial penalty is associated with this announcement.

The ICO states that the Children's code sets out fifteen standards with which providers of information society services likely to be accessed by children, including apps, online games, and social media sites, are required to conform. The ICO notes that the guidance applies to organisations of all sizes and across all sectors. The ICO states that resources available under the existing framework include a self-assessment risk toolkit enabling organisations to assess how both the UK GDPR and the Children's code apply to their digital services, Data Protection Impact Assessment templates, and sector-specific guidance covering edtech, the digital news industry, and games designers.

The ICO further states that its published Children's Code Strategy, together with a progress update issued in March 2025, sets out the authority's enforcement priorities and actions taken to date in relation to children's privacy online. The ICO's Commissioner's opinion on age assurance details how the Commissioner expects businesses to meet the code's age-appropriate application standard and sets out expectations for age assurance data protection compliance. The ICO also notes that it has co-signed a joint statement on a common international approach to age assurance, establishing shared principles across signatory regulators. The ICO states that its plans for new and updated guidance page is the reference point for information on which guidance will be updated and the timeline for those updates.

rows.title: ICO Publishes AI and Data Protection Risk Toolkit Version 1.1
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has published version 1.1 of its AI and data protection risk toolkit, providing a practical spreadsheet-based resource for organisations processing personal data through AI systems under the UK GDPR.

On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) released an updated iteration of its AI and data protection risk toolkit, available for download as a Microsoft Excel file. The publication coincides with the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 coming into force on the same date. The ICO notes that its broader AI and data protection guidance is currently under review in light of that legislation and may be subject to change, with updates to be signalled via the ICO's Plans for New and Updated Guidance page.

The ICO states that the toolkit is designed to provide practical support to organisations seeking to reduce risks to individuals' rights and freedoms arising from their own AI systems. The resource is framed as supplementary material sitting alongside the ICO's existing guidance on AI and data protection, which the ICO states addresses obligations under the UK GDPR including requirements relating to lawful basis, data minimisation, purpose limitation, and accountability (Art. 5, Art. 6, Art. 24 UK GDPR). The ICO presents the toolkit as a means of helping organisations work through data protection risk considerations in a structured, documented format consistent with the accountability principle.

The ICO confirms it is no longer seeking case study submissions from organisations in connection with the toolkit, contrary to an earlier call for contributions referenced in accompanying guidance materials. The toolkit is offered as a downloadable spreadsheet compatible with Microsoft Excel or equivalent programmes, and the ICO notes that organisations on networks that restrict access to video platforms such as Vimeo may be unable to view associated instructional video content and are advised to use an alternative device. The ICO states that no specific compliance deadline or enforcement context is attached to the toolkit's publication.

rows.title: ICO Publishes Updated Guidance on AI and Data Protection, Adding New Chapters on Fairness and Transparency
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has published updated guidance on artificial intelligence and data protection under the UK GDPR, restructuring existing content and introducing new chapters on fairness, transparency, and lawfulness in AI systems.

On 15 March 2023, the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) released a revised version of its Guidance on AI and Data Protection, with the document remaining subject to further review following the Data (Use and Access) Act coming into law on 19 June 2025. The ICO states that the guidance carries no financial penalty and imposes no fines; it is a compliance guidance instrument addressing obligations under the UK GDPR.

The ICO states that the update was issued in response to requests from UK industry for clarification on fairness requirements in AI, and that it delivers on a commitment under the ICO25 strategic plan to help organisations adopt new technologies whilst protecting individuals and vulnerable groups. The ICO finds that the guidance has been restructured around data protection principles, which provides a more editorially coherent and operationally sustainable framework given the pace of technological development.

The ICO notes that the revised guidance introduces several new and reorganised chapters. The ICO states that a standalone chapter on transparency sets out how the transparency principle (Art. 5(1)(a) UK GDPR) applies to AI, with detailed explainability content retained within the ICO's separate Explaining Decisions Made with AI product. The ICO notes that a new chapter on lawfulness includes added content on AI-generated inferences, affinity groups, and special category data (Art. 9 UK GDPR). The ICO states that it also introduces a dedicated fairness chapter covering the distinction between data protection fairness, algorithmic fairness, bias, and discrimination, as well as technical approaches to bias mitigation and the relationship between automated decision-making safeguards and fairness considerations (Art. 22 UK GDPR). The ICO notes that a new Annex A addresses fairness across the full AI lifecycle, from problem formulation through to decommissioning, identifying sources of bias arising from underlying assumptions, target variable selection, and reliance on quantifiable proxies. The ICO states that statistical accuracy content, previously located within the lawfulness and fairness chapter, has been relocated to a dedicated accuracy chapter reflecting its closer alignment with the accuracy principle (Art. 5(1)(d) UK GDPR). The ICO notes the guidance will continue to be updated to reflect forthcoming regulatory reform and the UK government's pro-innovation approach to AI regulation.
